title: fix(cli): fix arrow keys in interactive picker on Windows
body:
## Summary

- Arrow keys, Enter, and Ctrl+C didn't work in `deno approve-scripts` and `deno outdated --interactive` on Windows (only j/k worked)
- Root cause: Windows Terminal enables `ENABLE_VIRTUAL_TERMINAL_INPUT` by default, which makes Windows deliver arrow keys as VT escape sequences (ESC[A/ESC[B) instead of `VK_UP`/`VK_DOWN` key events. Crossterm's Windows backend reads via `ReadConsoleInput` expecting `VK_*` codes, so those keys were silently dropped
- Fix: clear `ENABLE_VIRTUAL_TERMINAL_INPUT` when entering raw mode for the interactive picker, restore it on exit
- Regular character keys (j/k) were unaffected because they produce `u_char` values regardless of VT input mode

Fixes #32971

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p deno` passes
- [ ] Manual testing on Windows with Windows Terminal
- [ ] CI

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(cli): fix arrow keys in interactive picker on Windows

Windows Terminal enables ENABLE_VIRTUAL_TERMINAL_INPUT by default,
which causes arrow keys, Enter, and Ctrl+C to be delivered as VT
escape sequences instead of VK_* key events. Crossterm's Windows
backend reads via ReadConsoleInput expecting VK_* codes, so those
keys were silently dropped.

Fix: clear ENABLE_VIRTUAL_TERMINAL_INPUT when entering raw mode for
the interactive picker, and restore it on exit.

Fixes #32971

title: fix(cli): parse --frozen flag for deno update and deno outdated
body:
## Summary

- `outdated_parse()` was missing a call to `lock_args_parse()`, so `--frozen`, `--lock`, and `--no-lock` flags were never parsed for `deno update` and `deno outdated`
- This caused `deno update --frozen=false` to fail with "The lockfile is out of date" because `frozen_lockfile` remained `None` (defaulting to true)
- Add unit tests for `--frozen=false` and `--frozen` on both commands

Fixes #32969

## Test plan

fix(napi): fix napi_escape_handle double-call and napi_throw/clear cycle

Two fixes for NAPI bugs found by the new test coverage:

1. napi_escape_handle: Track whether escape has been called via a
   heap-allocated bool in napi_open_escapable_handle_scope. Return
   napi_escape_called_twice on second call instead of silently
   succeeding. Previously the escapable handle scope was entirely a
   no-op with no state tracking.

2. napi_throw: Only store the exception in env.last_exception, do not
   also call scope.throw_exception(). The NAPI callback wrapper
   (call_fn) already checks last_exception after each callback and
   throws via V8 then. Throwing in both places made it impossible to
   clear the exception with napi_get_and_clear_last_exception since
   V8 would still have a pending exception after clearing last_exception.

title: fix(node/http): stop leaking TCP wrappers on HTTPS upgrade with createConnection TLSSocket
body: Fixes a Node-compat HTTP/TLS bug where `https.request()` could leak native `TCP` wrappers when `createConnection` returns a pre-created `TLSSocket` and the request upgrades the connection. This is the same pattern used by `npm:ws` - create the TLS socket first with `tls.connect()`, then pass it to `https.request({ createConnection })`. This patch: - reuses the original encrypted `TLSSocket` during the upgrade path instead of creating a replacement socket - skips the sock-init workaround for sockets that are already encrypted - waits for `secureConnect` before flushing the request on pre-created `TLSSocket`s - preserves the TLS-upgrade state needed when the handle is reinitialized ## User-visible improvement With the attached repro, RSS no longer grows linearly with each upgraded connection. Before this patch: - RSS grows from 102 MB to 349 MB over about 4,053 connections in a comparable ~5 minute run After this patch: - RSS grows from 96 MB to 118 MB over about 3,920 connections in a comparable ~5 minute run - heap usage stays roughly flat - the process reaches a steady state instead of continuing to climb Script used can be found at https://github.com/magurotuna/tls_then_upgrade/blob/main/client.ts
author: magurotuna

commits.message: fix
test

fix: use Duplex type for server upgrade socket, flatten test promise

The upgrade callback's socket parameter is typed as Duplex, not Socket.
Also flatten the nested promise to avoid a dangling unresolved promise
on test cleanup.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: only reuse socket via kReinitializeHandle for encrypted TLS sockets

The kReinitializeHandle path should only be used when the socket was
provided via createConnection as an encrypted TLS socket. For plain
HTTP upgrades, create a fresh Socket to avoid dangling state that
prevents the close event from firing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(napi): add test coverage and fix escape_handle/throw bugs
body:
## Summary

- Add NAPI test coverage for handle scopes, references, and exception handling
- Fix two bugs discovered by the new tests

### New tests

**Handle scopes** (`handle_scope.rs` + `handle_scope_test.js`):
- `napi_open/close_handle_scope`
- `napi_open/close_escapable_handle_scope` + `napi_escape_handle`
- Nested handle scopes
- Double escape detection

**References** (`reference.rs` + `reference_test.js`):
- `napi_create_reference` with strong refcount
- `napi_get_reference_value` / `napi_delete_reference`
- `napi_reference_ref` / `napi_reference_unref` counting
- `napi_create_external` / `napi_get_value_external` roundtrip
- External values accessed through references

**Exceptions** (`exception.rs` + `exception_test.js`):
- `napi_is_exception_pending`
- `napi_get_and_clear_last_exception`
- Exception propagation through `napi_call_function`

### Bug fixes

**`napi_escape_handle` double-call panic**: The escapable handle scope was
a complete no-op with no state tracking. Calling `napi_escape_handle`
twice would silently succeed instead of returning
`napi_escape_called_twice`. Fixed by tracking escape state via a
heap-allocated bool created in `napi_open_escapable_handle_scope`.

**`napi_throw` + `napi_get_and_clear_last_exception` cycle broken**:
`napi_throw` was both storing the exception in `env.last_exception` AND
calling `scope.throw_exception()`. This made it impossible to clear the
exception with `napi_get_and_clear_last_exception` since V8 still had a
pending exception. Fixed by only storing in `env.last_exception`; the
callback wrapper (`call_fn`) already handles the V8 throw after the
callback returns.

## Test plan

- [x] `./x test-napi` passes: 72 passed, 0 failed

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: fix(ext/node): support numeric FDs in child_process stdio array
body:
## Summary

- Node.js allows passing raw file descriptors as numbers in the `stdio` array for `child_process.spawn()`, e.g. `spawn('cmd', { stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe', fd] })`. This previously failed with `serde_v8 error: invalid type; expected: enum, got: Number`
- Refactor `StdioOrRid` → `StdioOrFdOrRid` with a new `Fd(i32)` variant for raw file descriptors
- Numbers in `extra_stdio` now deserialize as `Fd` and are dup2'd to the target fd in the child process on Unix
- `as_stdio()` also handles `Fd` by dup'ing it into a `std::fs::File` for use with stdin/stdout/stderr

Towards https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32846
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): support numeric FDs in child_process stdio array

Node.js allows passing raw file descriptors as numbers in the `stdio`
array for `child_process.spawn()`, e.g.:
  spawn('cmd', { stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe', fd] })

Previously this failed with `serde_v8 error: invalid type; expected:
enum, got: Number` because `extra_stdio` was typed as `Vec<Stdio>`
which only accepts string variants.

Refactor `StdioOrRid` → `StdioOrFdOrRid` with a new `Fd(i32)` variant
for raw file descriptors. Numbers now deserialize as `Fd` and are
dup2'd to the target fd in the child process on Unix.

title: fix(ext/node): close libuv handle on HandleWrap.close() for new-style handles
body:
## Summary

- Fix file descriptor leak when closing TTY handles (e.g. from node-pty)
- `HandleWrap.close()` was only calling the JS-side `_onClose()` callback for new-style `uv_compat` handles, but never calling `uv_compat::uv_close()` — so `stop_tty()` never ran and the underlying FD was never closed
- Each PTY spawn via node-pty leaked its `/dev/ptmx` master FD
- Fix: call `uv_compat::uv_close()` for `Handle::New` handles in `HandleWrap.close()`

Towards https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32846
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): close libuv handle on HandleWrap.close() for new-style handles

When a TTY (or other new-style uv_compat handle) was closed via
HandleWrap.close(), only the JS-side _onClose() callback was called.
The actual uv_compat::uv_close() was never invoked, so stop_tty()
never ran and the underlying file descriptor was never closed.

This caused /dev/ptmx FD leaks when using node-pty: each PTY spawn
leaked its master FD because tty.ReadStream.destroy() never closed it.

Fix: call uv_compat::uv_close() for Handle::New handles before
running the JS close path. This matches Node.js behavior where
uv_close() is always called to shut down the libuv handle.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test(ext/node): add TTY fd leak regression test

Verify that destroying a tty.WriteStream properly closes the
underlying file descriptor. Creates 10 TTY streams under script(1)
(for a real PTY) and checks the fd count doesn't grow.

Without the HandleWrap.close() fix, this leaks 10 fds
(before=22 after=32).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use temp file and platform-specific script(1) args in TTY test

On Linux, script(1) requires -c to pass the command as a string,
while on macOS it takes the command as trailing arguments. Write the
helper to a temp file to avoid shell quoting issues.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/napi): pass valid env to tsfn call_js_cb after close race
body:
## Summary

- Fix SIGSEGV crash in native addons (e.g. node-pty) caused by a race condition in NAPI threadsafe function teardown
- When a tsfn is released while calls are still pending, `TsFn::drop` can run before a queued call is processed. Previously, the `call_js_cb` was invoked with `env=NULL`, crashing addons that dereference env without a null check (SIGSEGV at address 0x90)
- Fix captures the env pointer in the call closure so it's available after the TsFn is freed. The env is always valid (leaked via `Box::into_raw`, never freed) and V8 is alive (callback runs on V8 thread)
- Add regression test `test_tsfn_close_race` that spawns concurrent calling/releasing threads to exercise the race

Towards https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32846
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Remove em-dashes

commits.message:
fix(ext/napi): pass valid env to tsfn call_js_cb after close race

When a NAPI threadsafe function is released while calls are still
pending, there is a race where TsFn::drop runs before a queued call
is processed. Previously, the call_js_cb was invoked with env=NULL
in this case, causing native addons like node-pty to SIGSEGV when
they dereference env without a null check (crash at address 0x90).

Fix: capture the env pointer in the call closure so it remains
available after the TsFn is freed. The env is always valid because
it is leaked via Box::into_raw in op_napi_open and never freed.
V8 is also alive since the callback runs on the V8 thread.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test(ext/napi): add tsfn abort mode race test

Add test_tsfn_abort_race that exercises the same close-race scenario
as test_tsfn_close_race but using napi_tsfn_abort mode. Abort forces
immediate close regardless of thread_count, which is another path
to the is_closed=true state that previously passed null env.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore: replace em-dashes with plain punctuation in comments

title: fix(ext/node): drain all ready connections in poll_tcp_handle to prevent accept starvation
body:
## Summary

- Fix `node:net` accept starvation under high concurrency when mixed with Deno-native APIs (`Deno.listenTls()`, `Deno.connect()`)
- `poll_tcp_handle` now drains all ready connections from `poll_accept` in a loop before firing callbacks, instead of accepting only one at a time
- Fires the connection callback once per accepted stream (matching libuv semantics), with a safety check that stops if the callback doesn't call `uv_accept`

## Root cause

Under high concurrent load, `poll_tcp_handle` previously accepted one connection, fired the JS callback, and moved on. The callback's async work (e.g., `Deno.listenTls`) created pending tokio ops processed in Phase 2 of the event loop, delaying the next Phase 4 (I/O) iteration. Since tokio's reactor only re-polls epoll when the event loop returns `Pending`, connections that arrived in the same epoll batch were invisible until the next reactor turn — creating a feedback loop where accept throughput degraded to zero.

Closes #32941

## Test plan

- [x] New test `tcp_batch_accept_concurrent` — opens 8 concurrent connections before ticking the event loop, verifies all 8 are accepted in a single tick
- [x] All 59 `uv_compat` tests pass (58 existing + 1 new)
- [x] `./x fmt` and `./x lint` pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Fixes `node:net` accept starvation in uv_compat under high concurrency by changing `poll_tcp_handle` to drain all ready accepts in one poll and then invoke the connection callback once per accepted stream (with a guard to stop if the callback doesn’t consume the backlog).

**Changes:**
- Update `poll_tcp_handle` to batch/drain `poll_accept` results into an internal backlog before invoking callbacks.
- Invoke the connection callback once per backlog entry, stopping when the backlog no longer shrinks (ie. `uv_accept` wasn’t called).
- Add a regression test asserting multiple pre-tick connections are accepted in a single tick.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| libs/core/uv_compat/tcp.rs | Batch-accept loop and per-connection callback firing logic to avoid accept starvation. |
| libs/core/uv_compat/tests.rs | New regression test for batch-accept behavior under multiple pending connections. |

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): drain all ready connections in poll_tcp_handle to prevent accept starvation

Previously, poll_tcp_handle accepted only one connection per call, then
fired the JS callback. Under high concurrency with mixed node:net and
Deno-native I/O, tokio's reactor only re-polls epoll when the event
loop returns Pending. Connections that arrived in the same epoll batch
were invisible until the next reactor turn, but Phase 2 (pending ops
from Deno.listenTls/connect) kept the loop busy, delaying Phase 4
(I/O) and creating a feedback loop where accept throughput degraded
to zero.

The fix drains all ready connections from poll_accept in a loop before
firing callbacks. Each accepted stream goes into internal_backlog, then
the connection callback fires once per entry (matching libuv semantics).
If the callback doesn't call uv_accept (backlog doesn't shrink), we
stop to avoid spinning.

Closes #32941

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: address review issues in batch accept

- Fix use-after-free: heap-allocate accepted client handles so they
  survive until uv_close processes them in the close phase
- Set any_work = true when connection callbacks consume backlog entries,
  ensuring run_io() correctly reports work for pre-existing backlogs
- Rename test to tcp_batch_accept (sequential connects, not concurrent)
- Fix clippy: use MaybeUninit::uninit() instead of zeroed().assume_init()
  to avoid UB from zeroing NonNull fields in uv_tcp_t

title: fix(inspector): send executionContextDestroyed on process.exit()
body:
## Summary

- When `process.exit()` is called during an inspector debugging session, broadcast `Runtime.executionContextDestroyed` CDP notification to all connected sessions before exiting
- Wait for debugger sessions to disconnect after sending the notification, matching Node.js behavior
- Align the "waiting for disconnect" stderr message with Node.js's expected format (`"Waiting for the debugger to disconnect..."`)
- Enable `test-inspector-esm.js` and `test-inspector-console.js` node compat tests

## Details

Previously, `process.exit()` → `Deno.exit()` → `std::process::exit()` would terminate immediately, skipping the normal event-loop shutdown path where V8's `context_destroyed` sends `Runtime.executionContextDestroyed`. Debugger clients (like the Node.js test suite's inspector helper) rely on this notification to know when execution is complete.

The fix adds an `OpExitCallback` in the CLI worker that:
1. Checks if any inspector session with `wait_for_disconnect` is active
2. Broadcasts `Runtime.executionContextDestroyed` via session send callbacks (no V8 scope needed)
3. Prints the disconnect message matching Node.js format
4. Blocks until all sessions disconnect

This builds on #32714 which implemented the `NodeRuntime` CDP domain.

## Test plan

- [x] `./x test-compat test-inspector-esm` passes
- [x] `./x test-compat test-inspector-console` passes
- [x] `cargo test inspector_test` passes (existing inspector unit tests)
- [x] `./x test-compat test-inspector-open-dispose` passes (existing compat test)
- [x] `./tools/lint.js` and `./tools/format.js` pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Updates Deno’s inspector shutdown behavior to better match Node.js when `process.exit()` is invoked during an active debugging session, ensuring connected debugger clients receive an execution-context teardown signal and get time to disconnect cleanly.

**Changes:**
- Enable additional Node compat inspector tests (`test-inspector-esm`, `test-inspector-console`).
- Align the “waiting for disconnect” log message with Node.js.
- Add inspector APIs and CLI exit callbacks to broadcast `Runtime.executionContextDestroyed` and wait for inspector disconnect before `std::process::exit()`.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| tests/node_compat/config.jsonc | Enables the two inspector-related Node compat tests. |
| runtime/worker.rs | Updates the inspector-disconnect log message to Node’s expected wording. |
| libs/core/inspector.rs | Adds “broadcast context destroyed” and “wait for disconnect” helper methods on the inspector. |
| cli/worker.rs | Registers an additional `Deno.exit()` callback to notify debugger sessions and block until disconnect. |

commits.message:
fix(inspector): send executionContextDestroyed on process.exit()

When `process.exit()` is called during an inspector debugging session,
Deno previously called `std::process::exit()` immediately, skipping the
normal event-loop shutdown path. This meant the
`Runtime.executionContextDestroyed` CDP notification was never sent to
debugger clients, and the "Waiting for the debugger to disconnect..."
message was never printed.

This adds an `OpExitCallback` that broadcasts the context destroyed
notification to all connected inspector sessions and waits for them to
disconnect before exiting. Also aligns the disconnect message with
Node.js's expected format.

Enables `test-inspector-esm.js` and `test-inspector-console.js` node
compat tests which now pass.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: avoid busy-spin in wait_for_sessions_disconnect

Set `waiting_for_disconnect` flag so that `poll_sessions` parks the
thread while waiting for debugger clients to disconnect, instead of
returning immediately and spinning in a tight loop.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

revert busy-spin fix, add doc explaining why spinning is necessary

Parking the thread during wait_for_sessions_disconnect prevents the
async I/O reactor from processing WebSocket close frames, causing
the test-inspector-esm test to hang indefinitely. Unlike
wait_for_session (where new WS connections arrive via a separate
server thread), disconnect detection requires the main thread to
keep polling so the tokio reactor can deliver close events.

Added a doc comment explaining why the spin-wait is intentional.

title: fix: handle inner quotes in double-quoted env values in .env file
body:
## Summary

- Fix `--env-file` parsing of double-quoted values containing inner
  (unescaped) quotes, e.g. `KEY="foo'bar"baz`qux"`
- Fix `\n` escape expansion in double-quoted values with inner quotes,
  e.g. `KEY="foo\ni am "on" newline"`

**Root cause**: `find_char` found the first matching `"` after the opening
quote, which was an inner quote — truncating the value. The dotenv npm
package instead treats the outermost quotes as delimiters.

**Fix**: New `find_closing_quote` function tries the nearest matching
quote first. If there's non-whitespace content after it on the same line,
inner quotes are present — it falls back to the last matching quote on the
line instead.

Closes #32928

## Test plan
- [x] New unit tests in `deno_dotenv`: `inner_quotes_in_double_quoted_values`
  and `inner_single_quotes_preserved`
- [x] New spec tests `eval::env_file_inner_quotes` (both inner_quotes and
  inner_quotes_with_newline)
- [x] All 32 dotenv unit tests pass
- [x] All 12 env_file spec tests pass


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Fixes Deno’s `--env-file` dotenv parsing to correctly handle double-quoted values containing inner (unescaped) quotes, aligning behavior more closely with the `dotenv` npm package and preventing truncation/incorrect `\n` expansion in those cases.

**Changes:**
- Replace first-quote matching with a new `find_closing_quote` heuristic to prefer the “outermost” quote when inner quotes are detected.
- Add new Rust unit tests in `libs/dotenv` for inner-quote parsing and `\n` expansion behavior.
- Add new spec tests under `tests/specs/eval/env_file_inner_quotes/` to validate CLI behavior with `--env-file`.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `libs/dotenv/lib.rs` | Updates quote-closing detection and adds regression tests for inner quotes + newline escape expansion. |
| `tests/specs/eval/env_file_inner_quotes/env_file` | Adds `.env` fixture containing inner quotes and `\n` escape sequences. |
| `tests/specs/eval/env_file_inner_quotes/__test__.jsonc` | Adds spec tests validating `deno eval --env-file` outputs for the new fixture. |

commits.message:
fix(dotenv): handle inner quotes in double-quoted env values

When a double-quoted value contains inner (unescaped) quotes like
`KEY="foo'bar"baz`qux"`, the parser would stop at the first inner
quote instead of treating the outermost quotes as the delimiters.

Fix: introduce `find_closing_quote` which first tries the nearest
matching quote. If there's non-whitespace content after it on the
same line, inner quotes are present — fall back to the last matching
quote on the line. This matches the behavior of the dotenv npm package.

Also handles escaped newlines correctly within values that contain
inner quotes.

Closes #32928

  closer (matching Node.js/dotenv behavior)
- Rename .env to env_file in spec test to avoid .gitignore exclusion

improve find_closing_quote to handle inline comments with quotes

The backward scan now prefers a quote candidate followed by a `#`
comment (strong signal) over one followed by just whitespace/EOL. This
correctly handles the edge case where an inline comment contains the
same quote character, e.g. `KEY="a"b" # "comment"` now yields `a"b`
instead of including the comment text.

Also adds tests for same-type inner quotes and inline comments with
inner quotes, and clarifies existing test comments.

title: fix(ext/node): `fs.cp` across allowed dirs with ignored read permissions
body:
## Summary

- Fix `node:fs.cp` failing with `ENOENT` when copying between allowed
  directories while read permissions outside those directories are ignored
  (`"ignore": true` in deno.json permissions config)
- The `check_parent_paths` walk-up now handles `NotFound` from permission
  checks (ignored denials) the same way it handles `NotFound` from `stat` —
  by stopping the ancestor traversal

**Root cause**: When `fs.cp` was moved from JS to Rust (#32580, #32687), the
parent path validation started propagating ignored-permission `ENOENT` as a
hard error instead of treating it as a stop signal. The old JS code's
`safeStatFn` caught `NotFound` and returned `undefined`, gracefully stopping
the walk. The Rust code's `check_cp_path()?` propagated it before reaching
the stat call's own `NotFound` handler.

Closes #32923

## Test plan
- [x] New spec test `fs_cp_ignored_permissions` — copies a file using
  `fs.cp` (callback), `fs.promises.cp`, and `fs.cpSync` across directories
  with limited read permissions and `ignore: true`
- [x] All existing `fs_cp` spec tests pass


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Fixes a regression in the Rust-backed `node:fs.cp` implementation where ancestor-path validation could incorrectly treat *ignored* permission denials as fatal `ENOENT`, breaking copies between otherwise-allowed directories when `permissions.default.read.ignore: true` is set.

**Changes:**
- Update `check_parent_paths` ancestor traversal to treat ignored-permission `NotFound` from permission checks as a stop signal (like `stat`’s `NotFound`).
- Add a new node spec regression test covering `fs.cp` (callback), `fs.promises.cp`, and `fs.cpSync` under ignored read permissions.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 9 out of 10 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `ext/node/ops/fs.rs` | Stops parent traversal when permission checks return `NotFound` due to ignored read permissions, preventing erroneous hard failures. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/main.ts` | New regression script exercising `fs.cp` variants under a permissions config using `ignore: true`. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/main.out` | Expected output for the new regression test. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/__test__.jsonc` | Spec test definition running the new script with `-P` permissions config. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/deno.json` | Permissions configuration reproducing the ignored-read-permissions scenario. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/dir1/data.txt` | Source fixture file for the copy. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/dir2/data_cb.txt` | Destination fixture file used by callback copy variant. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/dir2/data_promise.txt` | Destination fixture file used by promise copy variant. |
| `tests/specs/node/fs_cp_ignored_permissions/dir2/data_sync.txt` | Destination fixture file used by sync copy variant.

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): `fs.cp` across allowed dirs with ignored read permissions

When `fs.cp` was moved from JS to Rust, the `check_parent_paths` function
walks up the destination's parent directory chain calling `check_cp_path()`
at each level. With `"ignore": true` permissions, denied reads become
`ENOENT` errors. The old JS code handled this gracefully (safeStatFn caught
NotFound and stopped walking), but the Rust code propagated the error via
`?` before reaching the stat call's own NotFound handler.

Fix: catch NotFound from the permission check and treat it the same as a
non-existent directory — stop walking and return Ok(()).

Closes #32923

title: fix(ext/node): defer uv_write callbacks to prevent re-entrancy panic
body:
## Summary

- `uv_write` in the uv compat layer was firing write callbacks synchronously when `try_write` succeeded fully or failed. This caused a `RefCell` double-borrow panic ("RefCell already borrowed") when the caller (e.g. `StreamWrap.write_buffer`) held an `OpState` borrow during the `uv_write` call, and the synchronous callback re-entered JS which called another op borrowing `OpState`.
- Changed both TCP and TTY `uv_write` implementations to always queue completed writes with a pre-determined status, deferring callbacks to `poll_tcp_handle`/`poll_tty_handle` in the event loop. This matches real libuv behavior where `uv_write` never fires callbacks synchronously.
- Added a `status` field to `WritePending` to support pre-completed entries that skip I/O in the drain loop.

Closes #32891

## Test plan

- [x] Added regression test `uv_write_callback_is_deferred` verifying callback does not fire synchronously from `uv_write`
- [x] All 58 uv_compat tests pass
- [x] Node.js unit tests pass (buffer, net, http, http2, tty)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): defer uv_write callbacks to event loop to prevent re-entrancy panic

uv_write was firing write callbacks synchronously when try_write succeeded,
causing a RefCell double-borrow panic when callers (e.g. StreamWrap ops)
held an OpState borrow during the call. This matches real libuv behavior
where uv_write never fires callbacks synchronously.

Closes #32891

title: fix(ext/console): handle throwing Symbol.toStringTag getter gracefully
body:
## Summary

When `console.log` formats an object, it accesses `Symbol.toStringTag` which may be a getter that throws. For example, [quick-lru](https://www.npmjs.com/package/quick-lru)'s `Symbol.toStringTag` getter calls `JSON.stringify(this)` which fails on circular structures. This caused an `[Internal Formatting Error]` instead of printing the object.

Fix by wrapping the `Symbol.toStringTag` access in a try-catch, matching Node.js behavior (see `lib/internal/util/inspect.js` line 1221-1225).

Before:
```
[Internal Formatting Error] TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON
```

After:
```
<ref *1> Bot {
  chatEventEmitter: BotChatEmitter { bot: [Circular *1] },
  cache: QuickLRU(0) {}
}
```

Fixes #32894

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit test with a `Symbol.toStringTag` getter that throws
- [x] `./x test-unit console_test` passes
- [x] Verified with the exact reproducer from the issue (quick-lru@7.0.0)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: fix(ext/web): AbortSignal.any() memory leak with long-lived signals
body:
## Summary

`AbortSignal.any()` leaked memory when called repeatedly with a long-lived parent signal (common pattern: a global shutdown signal combined per-request). The parent signal's `dependentSignals` collection grew unboundedly because dead `WeakRef` wrappers were never cleaned up.

- Replaced custom `WeakRefSet` (which accumulated dead `WeakRef` objects in an array that was never compacted) with a standard `SafeSet<WeakRef>`
- Added `FinalizationRegistry` to automatically remove dead entries from source signals' `dependentSignals` when dependent signals are garbage collected
- This matches the approach used by Node.js (`dependantSignalsCleanupRegistry` in `lib/internal/abort_controller.js`)

Before (memory grows linearly):
```
100000 - 75.53 MiB
500000 - 135.24 MiB
1000000 - 167.59 MiB
2000000 - 216.48 MiB
3000000 - 281.54 MiB
```

After (memory stabilizes):
```
100000 - 145.59 MiB
500000 - 187.75 MiB
700000 - 186.25 MiB
1000000 - 186.75 MiB
```

Fixes #24842
Closes #32898

## Test plan

- [x] Reproducer from #24842 shows stable memory instead of linear growth
- [x] `AbortSignal.any()` basic functionality verified (abort propagation, already-aborted signals, timeout signals)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(web): AbortSignal.any() memory leak with long-lived signals

When AbortSignal.any() is called repeatedly with a long-lived parent
signal (e.g. in a loop), the parent signal's dependentSignals collection
grew unboundedly because dead WeakRef wrappers were never cleaned up.

Fix by replacing the custom WeakRefSet with a standard SafeSet of
WeakRefs and using FinalizationRegistry to remove dead entries when
dependent signals are garbage collected. This matches the approach
used by Node.js.

Also switched sourceSignals from WeakRefSet to SafeSet<WeakRef> for
consistency, since the same iteration patterns apply.

Fixes #24842

title: fix(ext/node): fix Buffer.concat truncating buffers larger than 4GB
body:
## Summary

- Replace `| 0` (32-bit integer truncation) with `MathTrunc()` in `checked()`, `_allocUnsafe()`, and `fromString()` in the Node.js Buffer polyfill
- This fixes `Buffer.concat` and `Buffer.allocUnsafe` silently truncating buffers with sizes exceeding 2^32 (e.g. `(2**32 + 5) | 0` → `5`)

Closes #32895

## Test plan

- [x] Added tests for `Buffer.allocUnsafe` and `Buffer.concat` with >4GB sizes (marked `ignore: true` due to memory requirements)
- [x] Existing buffer tests pass (`./x test-node buffer`)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): fix `Buffer.concat` truncating buffers larger than 4GB

The `checked()` function and `_allocUnsafe()` used `| 0` to truncate
lengths to integers, which converts numbers to 32-bit signed integers.
This caused any buffer size > 2^32 to wrap around (e.g. `(2**32 + 5) | 0`
becomes `5`). Replace `| 0` with `MathTrunc()` to support large buffers.

Closes #32895

title: fix(ext/node): return all own properties from VM context property enumerator

- VM context's property enumerator callbacks were using `GetPropertyNamesArgs::default()` which only returns enumerable, non-symbol properties
- This caused `Reflect.ownKeys()`, `Object.getOwnPropertyNames()`, and `Object.getOwnPropertySymbols()` to return incomplete results inside VM contexts
- Fix by using `OwnOnly` + `ALL_PROPERTIES` filter so non-enumerable properties and symbols are included
- Also fixes a V8 crash in `test-vm-global-property-enumerator` (was a fatal `Check failed: IsName` panic)

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR fixes Node.js `vm` context property enumeration in Deno’s Node-compat layer so that “own keys” queries inside VM contexts return complete results (including non-enumerable properties and symbols) and avoids a V8 crash seen in the VM global property enumerator path.

**Changes:**
- Update VM context named property enumerator to collect *own* non-index keys with `ALL_PROPERTIES` (incl. non-enumerable + symbols).
- Update VM context indexed property enumerator to collect *own* numeric keys (incl. non-enumerable) and keep them as numbers.
- Enable the corresponding Node-compat VM tests in `tests/node_compat/config.jsonc`.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `tests/node_compat/config.jsonc` | Enables additional `parallel/test-vm-*` coverage related to `ownKeys`/`getOwnProperty*` behavior in VM contexts. |
| `ext/node/ops/vm.rs` | Adjusts V8 `GetPropertyNames` argument configuration for VM context enumerator callbacks to include all own properties and prevent invalid key types from reaching V8. |

commits.message:
fix(node/vm): return all own properties from VM context property enumerator

The VM context's named and indexed property enumerator callbacks were
using `GetPropertyNamesArgs::default()` which filters to only enumerable
properties and skips symbols. This caused `Reflect.ownKeys()`,
`Object.getOwnPropertyNames()`, and `Object.getOwnPropertySymbols()`
to return incomplete results when called on a VM context's global object.

Fix by using `OwnOnly` collection mode with `ALL_PROPERTIES` filter, so
non-enumerable properties and symbols are included.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): route uncaught exceptions through _fatalException
body:
## Summary
- Route `uncaughtExceptionHandler` through `process._fatalException` so that `setUncaughtExceptionCaptureCallback` is respected for unhandled rejections
- Install the unhandled rejection callback in `synchronizeListeners` when a capture callback is active (not just when event listeners exist)
- Use proper Node.js error classes (`ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE`, `ERR_UNCAUGHT_EXCEPTION_CAPTURE_ALREADY_SET`) in `setUncaughtExceptionCaptureCallback`
- Enable 4 newly passing node_compat tests

## Test plan
- [x] `./x test-compat test-process-exception-capture.js`
- [x] `./x test-compat test-process-exception-capture-should-abort-on-uncaught.js`
- [x] `./x test-compat test-process-exception-capture-should-abort-on-uncaught-setflagsfromstring.js`
- [x] `./x test-compat test-process-exception-capture-errors.js`

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): route uncaught exceptions through _fatalException for capture callback support

`uncaughtExceptionHandler` was directly emitting `uncaughtException` events,
bypassing `process._fatalException` and its check for the capture callback set
via `process.setUncaughtExceptionCaptureCallback`. Also ensures
`synchronizeListeners` installs the unhandled rejection callback when a capture
callback is active, and uses proper Node.js error classes (ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE,
ERR_UNCAUGHT_EXCEPTION_CAPTURE_ALREADY_SET).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: chore: update deno_doc crate
body:
Major restructuring of the DocNode format (v2), addressing long-standing design issues with how documentation nodes are modeled and serialized. this also bumps the JSON schema version to 2.

The output now is a `Record<string, Document>` instead of `DocNode[]`.
For a complete view of the JSON schema changes, see https://jsr.io/@deno/doc/diff/0.194.1...0.196.0. 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
##  Structural changes
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - Symbol-based model: Declarations are now grouped into Symbols that explicitly model declaration merging (e.g., interface Foo + var Foo), replacing implicit name-based grouping via (name, kind) tuples. The additional benefit is that function overloads are all under a single symbol.
  - Imports and module docs separated from DocNode: Import and ModuleDoc are no longer DocNodeDef variants, they're modeled separately so DocNode always represents an actual declaration                                            
  - TsTypeDef is now a proper enum: Replaces the flat struct with ~20 Option fields (manually-tagged union) with a real Rust enum, eliminating invalid states and reducing memory waste                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
## Correctness & consistency fixes                                                                                                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - Location.line is now 0-indexed: Both line and column are consistently 0-indexed                                                                                                                                                   
  - DocNode.is_default is bool instead of Option<bool>: the three-state ambiguity is gone now that imports are modeled separately
  - DeclarationDef simplified: Removed redundant structure                                                                                                                                                                            
  - TsTypeRefDef includes type resolution: Type references now carry resolution info (scope, location of definition), so consumers don't need to reimplement symbol resolution for linking                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
## New capabilities                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - JSDoc type parsing: TypeScript types declared in JSDoc comments (@param {string} x, @property {Date} d) are now parsed into proper TsTypeDef structures
  - Smaller serde output: Optimized serialization to reduce JSON output size
author: crowlKats

commits.message: chore: update deno_doc crate
test fixes
fix test
fix

title: fix(audit): respect package.json overrides in vulnerability checks
body:
## Summary

Closes #32871

When `package.json` overrides force a transitive dependency to a patched version, `deno audit` was still reporting it as vulnerable. For example:

```json
{
  "dependencies": { "@vitest/ui": "^4.1.0" },
  "overrides": { "flatted": "3.4.2" }
}
```

The override correctly resolves `flatted` to `3.4.2` (visible in the lockfile), but `deno audit` still reported the vulnerability for `flatted < 3.4.2`.

### Root cause

The npm audit API returns advisories based on the declared dependency tree, not the resolved versions after overrides. Deno was passing all advisories through without checking if the actually-installed version falls within the vulnerable range.

### Fix

After receiving advisories from the npm audit API, filter out any where no installed version actually falls within the `vulnerable_versions` range. Uses the finding paths to identify actual package names and cross-references with the resolution snapshot.

## Test plan

- [x] Added `tests/specs/audit/overrides` spec test
- [x] All 12 audit tests pass (11 existing + 1 new)


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Fixes `deno audit` false positives when `package.json` `overrides` (or similar resolution behavior) forces a transitive dependency to a non-vulnerable version, by filtering npm audit advisories against the actually-resolved versions in the `NpmResolutionSnapshot`.

**Changes:**
- Filter npm audit advisories where no resolved/installed package version matches the advisory’s `vulnerable_versions` range.
- Add a spec regression test covering an overridden transitive dependency that would otherwise be reported as vulnerable.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| cli/tools/pm/audit.rs | Filters advisories using the resolution snapshot to avoid reporting vulnerabilities for packages that are resolved to patched versions via overrides. |
| tests/specs/audit/overrides/__test__.jsonc | Adds a regression spec test that runs `install` then `audit` for an overrides scenario. |
| tests/specs/audit/overrides/package.json | Defines a dependency that pulls a vulnerable transitive package, then overrides it to a patched version. |
| tests/specs/audit/overrides/audit.out | Asserts the audit output reports no known vulnerabilities after overrides are applied. |

commits.message:
fix(audit): respect package.json overrides in vulnerability checks (#32871)

When package.json overrides force a transitive dependency to a
patched version, `deno audit` was still reporting it as vulnerable.
This happened because the npm audit API returns advisories based on
the declared dependency tree, not the resolved versions after
overrides are applied.

Fix: after receiving advisories from the npm audit API, filter out
any where no installed version actually falls within the advisory's
vulnerable_versions range. Uses the finding paths to identify the
actual package names and cross-references with the resolution
snapshot's installed versions.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: move TTY guard from readline to process.stdin.setRawMode

The readline [kSetRawMode] should match Node.js and only check if
setRawMode is a function (not check isTTY). The real issue is that
Deno's process.stdin.setRawMode is always defined (unlike Node where
it only exists on TTY stdin), so it needs to guard against calling
Deno.stdin.setRaw() on non-TTY streams.

This fixes the original ENOTTY error while keeping the node_compat
test-readline-set-raw-mode.js passing.

title: fix(ext/node): update process.versions.napi to 9
body:
## Summary

Closes #30091

`process.versions.napi` was hardcoded to `"8"` but the actual NAPI implementation (`ext/napi/js_native_api.rs`) defines `NAPI_VERSION: u32 = 9`. This mismatch caused `node-pre-gyp` and similar tools to reject NAPI v9 native modules (like `get-windows`) with:

> This Node instance does not support builds for Node-API version 9

### Changes
- Update `process.versions.napi` from `"8"` to `"9"` in `ext/node/polyfills/_process/process.ts`
- Add value assertion in `tests/unit_node/process_test.ts` to prevent future drift

## Test plan
- [x] `./x test-node process` passes
- [x] `deno eval "console.log(process.versions.napi)"` prints `9`


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): update process.versions.napi to 9 (#30091)

process.versions.napi was hardcoded to "8" but the actual NAPI
implementation (ext/napi/js_native_api.rs) supports version 9.
This mismatch caused node-pre-gyp and similar tools to reject
NAPI v9 native modules even though Deno can run them.

Also adds a value check in the process test to prevent future
drift between the reported and actual NAPI versions.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): don't fire upgrade event for h2c requests
body:
## Summary

HTTP requests to a `node:http` server hang when:
1. The server has an `"upgrade"` listener (e.g., Vite for WebSocket HMR)
2. A browser/client sends an HTTP/2 cleartext upgrade (`Upgrade: h2c`)

The server was calling `upgradeHttpRaw()` and emitting the `"upgrade"` event
for h2c requests. The listener (expecting WebSocket) ignores h2c, leaving the
upgraded connection hanging indefinitely.

Fix: skip the `"upgrade"` event for `Upgrade: h2c` requests, treating them as
regular HTTP/1.1 requests instead. h2c is a protocol-level upgrade not meant
for user-space upgrade handlers.

Closes #32857

## Reproduction

```json
// deno.json
{ "imports": { "vite": "npm:vite@7.3.1" } }
```

```bash
deno run -A npm:vite
curl --http2 http://localhost:5173/  # hangs before fix, 200 after
```

## Test plan
- [x] `curl --http2` to Vite dev server returns 200 (was hanging)
- [x] `curl --http2-prior-knowledge` still works (HTTP/2)
- [x] Regular HTTP/1.1 still works

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): don't fire upgrade event for h2c requests

When a browser sends an HTTP/2 cleartext (h2c) upgrade request to a
node:http server that has an "upgrade" listener (e.g. Vite with WebSocket
HMR), the server was calling upgradeHttpRaw and emitting the upgrade
event. The listener (expecting WebSocket) would ignore h2c, leaving the
connection hanging indefinitely.

Fix by skipping the upgrade event for h2c upgrades, treating them as
regular HTTP/1.1 requests instead. This matches the expected behavior
where h2c is a protocol-level upgrade not meant for user-space handlers.

Closes #32857

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: add regression test for h2c upgrade hang

Verifies that an HTTP request with `Upgrade: h2c` headers is handled
as a normal HTTP/1.1 request and returns 200, even when the server
has an "upgrade" listener (e.g. for WebSocket).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use case-insensitive check for h2c upgrade header

The Upgrade header value is case-insensitive per RFC 7230, so compare
with toLowerCase() to handle variants like "H2C" or "H2c".

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: refactor(core): clean up timer implementation, build web timers on Node Timeout
body:
## Summary
- Remove unused core timer exports left over from #32543: `timerInsert`, `timerIncRefCount`, `timerDecRefCount`, `timerKRefed`, `TIMEOUT_MAX`
- Move WHATWG timer nesting depth tracking (`timerDepth`/`__setTimerDepth`/`getTimerDepth`) from core into `ext/web/02_timers.js` as local state
- Rewrite web timers (`setTimeout`/`setInterval`) to use Node's `Timeout` class from `ext:deno_node/internal/timers.mjs` via `createLazyLoader`, eliminating duplicate timer ID tracking and cancel/ref/unref logic
- Web timers remain thin wrappers adding only WHATWG-specific behavior: webidl type coercion, string callback eval, and timer nesting depth

## Test plan
- [x] `./x test-unit timers` passes
- [x] `./x test-unit abort` passes
- [x] `./x test-spec timers` passes
- [x] Smoke tested: setTimeout, setInterval, clearTimeout, clearInterval, string eval callbacks, AbortSignal.timeout, Node.js timers

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR refactors Deno’s timer surface by removing legacy core timer exports and reimplementing web timers (`setTimeout`/`setInterval`) as thin WHATWG wrappers on top of the Node-compatible `Timeout` implementation, with corresponding updates to leak-sanitizer spec outputs.

**Changes:**
- Remove unused core timer exports and timer-depth plumbing from `libs/core/01_core.js`.
- Rework `ext/web/02_timers.js` to use Node’s internal `Timeout` + active-timer lookup for scheduling/cancel/ref/unref, while keeping WHATWG string-callback eval and nesting depth tracking.
- Update spec expected outputs to account for changed leak-tracing stack traces.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `libs/core/01_core.js` | Drops legacy timer exports/hooks from the core bootstrap surface. |
| `ext/web/02_timers.js` | Rebuilds web timers atop Node’s internal `Timeout` implementation and active timer registry. |
| `tests/specs/test/ops_sanitizer_unstable/ops_sanitizer_unstable.out` | Adjusts expected leak stack output for interval leaks. |
| `tests/specs/test/ops_sanitizer_multiple_timeout_tests/ops_sanitizer_multiple_timeout_tests.out` | Adjusts expected leak stack output for multiple timeout leaks. |

commits.message:
refactor(core): clean up timer exports after JS timer rewrite

Remove leftover timer infrastructure from core that was no longer
needed after #32543:

- Remove unused core exports: timerInsert, timerIncRefCount,
  timerDecRefCount, timerKRefed, TIMEOUT_MAX
- Move WHATWG timer nesting depth (timerDepth/__setTimerDepth/
  getTimerDepth) from core into ext/web/02_timers.js as local state,
  since it's only used there
- Remove stale "called from Rust" comment

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(web): build web timers on top of Node's Timeout class

Web timers (setTimeout/setInterval) now use the Node.js Timeout class
from ext:deno_node/internal/timers.mjs via lazy loading, instead of
calling core.createTimer directly. This eliminates the duplicate timer
ID tracking and cancel/ref/unref logic.

Web timers remain thin wrappers that add WHATWG-specific behavior:
- webidl type coercion
- string callback eval
- timer nesting depth tracking

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: update leak sanitizer expected output for Timeout stack frames

The stack traces now include Timeout constructor frames above
setTimeout/setInterval since web timers go through Node's Timeout.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: test: add per-file timeout and reduce CI job timeout
body:
## Summary
- Add a 2-minute per-file timeout wrapping the entire `runSingleTest` (including `generateBundle` fetch calls) using `Promise.race`, so the runner always moves on even if the WPT server becomes unresponsive

title: fix(ext/node): use constant-time comparison for GCM auth tag verification
body:
## Summary

- Replace direct `==` comparison with `subtle::ConstantTimeEq` for AES-GCM authentication tag verification in both AES-128-GCM and AES-256-GCM decrypt paths
- The standard `==` operator short-circuits on the first differing byte, making it susceptible to timing side-channel attacks
- This matches Node.js/OpenSSL behavior which uses `CRYPTO_memcmp` for AEAD tag verification
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node_crypto): use constant-time comparison for GCM auth tag verification

The AES-GCM decryption tag verification used direct equality (==) which
short-circuits on the first differing byte, making it susceptible to
timing side-channel attacks. Switch to subtle::ConstantTimeEq for both
AES-128-GCM and AES-256-GCM tag comparisons, matching the constant-time
behavior of Node.js/OpenSSL's CRYPTO_memcmp.

Fixes #32815

title: fix(ext/node): set kLastWriteWasAsync in JS write path to prevent double callback
body:
## Summary

- Set `streamBaseState[kLastWriteWasAsync] = 1` in the JS `writeBuffer` and `writev` fast path before returning, so `afterWriteDispatched` correctly identifies the write as async and defers the callback
- Without this, a preceding Rust sync write (e.g. TTY stdout) leaves `kLastWriteWasAsync=0`, causing a subsequent JS Pipe write to fire the callback twice → `ERR_MULTIPLE_CALLBACK`

Fixes #32803
Fixes #32813
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Reviewed the changes with full repo context. No issues found.
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): set kLastWriteWasAsync in JS write path to prevent double callback

The Rust write_buffer (for TTY/TCP via uv_compat) sets
streamBaseState[kLastWriteWasAsync] = 0 on synchronous success. The JS
writeBuffer (for Pipe/legacy streams) fires #write asynchronously but
did not set kLastWriteWasAsync = 1. After a Rust sync write left the
flag at 0, a subsequent JS Pipe write would cause afterWriteDispatched
to call the callback immediately (thinking it was sync), then #write
would call req.oncomplete again on completion — triggering
ERR_MULTIPLE_CALLBACK.

Fixes #32803

title: fix(core): implement select fallback on macOS
body:
On macos, certain files are incompatible with kqueue. Chief among them being some of the `/dev/` files. In libuv, there's code that checks if a file works with kqueue, and if it doesn't then it has a fallback which uses a background thread that polls via select and then signals readiness via a socket. We implement that fallback here to match libuv.

Fixes #31125.
author: nathanwhit

reviews.body:
The PR implements a select(2)-based fallback for TTY polling on macOS when kqueue rejects certain file descriptors. The main concerns are around memory ordering issues with atomics shared between the select background thread and the main async thread, which could cause missed updates or indefinite blocking on ARM (Apple Silicon) architectures. There's also a potential buffer overflow if FD numbers exceed FD_SETSIZE.

**[HIGH]** `libs/core/uv_compat/tty.rs:1907`: All atomic operations on `close` and `interest` shared between the select background thread and the main thread use `Ordering::Relaxed`. On ARM (Apple Silicon Macs - the primary target for this fallback), Relaxed provides no cross-thread visibility guarantees. The `close` flag stored with Relaxed in `shutdown_select_fallback` may never become visible to the select thread, causing `thread.join()` to deadlock. Similarly, `interest` updates may be invisible, causing the select thread to watch the wrong fd sets and miss readiness events.
> Suggestion: Use `Ordering::Release` when storing `close` and `interest`, and `Ordering::Acquire` when loading them. At minimum: `close.store(true, Ordering::Release)` in shutdown, `close.load(Ordering::Acquire)` in select_thread_main, `interest.store(..., Ordering::Release)` in update_select_interest, and `interest.load(Ordering::Acquire)` in select_thread_main.

**[MEDIUM]** `libs/core/uv_compat/tty.rs:1868`: select(2) uses fd_set bitmaps which have a fixed size of FD_SETSIZE (typically 1024). If the TTY fd or int_fd is >= FD_SETSIZE, `FD_SET` will write out of bounds, causing memory corruption. This can happen in processes with many open file descriptors.
> Suggestion: Check that both `fd` and `int_fd` are less than `libc::FD_SETSIZE as RawFd` before proceeding with setup_select_fallback. Return an appropriate error if they exceed the limit. Alternatively, use poll(2) instead of select(2) which has no fd number limitation.

**[MEDIUM]** `libs/core/uv_compat/tty.rs:1855`: Rac

Two medium-severity issues found in the macOS TTY select-fallback reactor implementation: (1) the write polling path discards the AsyncFd readiness guard without clearing it, which can cause busy-spinning, and (2) cross-thread atomic operations use Relaxed ordering on ARM64 where stronger guarantees are needed to ensure visibility after wake-up signals.

**[HIGH]** `libs/core/uv_compat/tty.rs:1549`: Write polling path discards the AsyncFd write readiness guard with `let _ = reactor.async_fd().poll_write_ready(cx)`. Unlike the read path which correctly captures the guard and calls `clear_ready()` on WouldBlock, the write path never clears AsyncFd readiness. After draining the signaling byte from fake_fd, `poll_write_ready` will return Ready immediately on subsequent calls (without a new signal from the select thread), and `drain_fd` will return false. This causes unnecessary busy-spinning in the poll loop until the write queue empties.
> Suggestion: Capture the `poll_write_ready` guard and call `guard.clear_ready()` after draining the signaling bytes, mirroring the read path's pattern. This ensures tokio only wakes when the select thread signals actual new readiness.

**[MEDIUM]** `libs/core/uv_compat/tty.rs:1962`: Cross-thread atomics (`interest`, `close`) between the main thread and the select background thread all use `Ordering::Relaxed`. On ARM64 (Apple Silicon — the primary target for this macOS code), Relaxed ordering provides no happens-before guarantee. The select thread could wake up from the interrupt write but still read a stale `interest` or `close` value. While syscalls like write/read may provide implicit barriers in practice, this is not formally guaranteed and constitutes a data race under the C++ memory model.
> Suggestion: Use `Ordering::Release` when storing `interest` and `close` from the main thread (in `update_select_interest` and `shutdown_select_fallback`), and `Ordering::Acquire` when loading them in `select_thread_main`. This establishes a p

title: fix(ext/node): emit request "close" before socket "free" in keep-alive path
body:
## Summary
Fixes a keep-alive socket lifecycle ordering bug where `socket.emit("free")` fired before `request.on("close")` handlers had a chance to run. This caused libraries like `node-fetch` that attach per-request listeners to the socket (and clean them up on request "close") to accumulate stale listeners across reused sockets, leading to `MaxListenersExceededWarning` and eventual `Premature close` errors under concurrent load.

The fix reorders operations in `#tryReturnSocket()` to mirror Node's `emitFreeNT` ordering:

1. Emit request "close" (userland cleanup runs here)
2. Clear socket references (prevents response "close" from touching the pooled socket)
3. Emit socket "free" (agent reuses socket — cleanup already happened)

Fixes #32780

## Test plan
  - [x] New spec test: `http_keepalive_close_before_free` — verifies per-request
    socket listeners are cleaned up before socket reuse across 15 sequential
    keep-alive requests
  - [x] Existing `http_agent_keepalive` tests pass
  - [x] Existing `http_agent_keepalive_stale_*` tests pass
  - [x] Verify that original repro (see #32780) is fixed
author: joshvfleming

reviews.body: Reviewed the changes with full repo context. No issues found.
Reviewed the changes with full repo context. No issues found.

The PR modifies HTTP client request lifecycle handling by manually setting destroyed/closed flags and emitting events. The main concerns are around event ordering and potential double-emission of the "close" event.

**[MEDIUM]** `ext/node/polyfills/http.ts:1313`: Setting `req.destroyed = true` and `req._closed = true` manually followed by `req.emit("close")` bypasses the standard stream teardown path. If `req.destroy()` is later called (e.g., on timeout or abort), the stream's own `_destroy` implementation may attempt to emit "close" again. This depends on whether the stream internals check `_closed` to guard against double-emit. Please verify this doesn't cause double "close" emission in edge cases (timeout, abort, premature socket close).
> Suggestion: Consider using `req.destroy()` instead of manually setting flags if the stream's destroy method properly handles the lifecycle ordering, or add an explicit guard like `if (!req._closed)` before the emit. At minimum, verify that Node's Writable base class respects the `_closed = true` flag to prevent re-emission.
Looks good, just one suggestion
Thanks, nice fix!

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): emit request "close" before socket "free" in keep-alive path (#32780)

Fixes a keep-alive socket lifecycle ordering bug where `socket.emit("free")`
fired before `request.on("close")` handlers had a chance to run. This caused
libraries like `node-fetch` that attach per-request listeners to the socket
(and clean them up on request "close") to accumulate stale listeners across
reused sockets, leading to `MaxListenersExceededWarning` and eventual
`Premature close` errors under concurrent load.

The fix reorders operations in `#tryReturnSocket()` to mirror Node's
`emitFreeNT` ordering:

1. Emit request "close" (userland cleanup runs here)
2. Clear socket references (prevents response "close" from touching the
   pooled socket)
3. Emit socket "free" (agent reuses socket — cleanup already happened)

Closes #32780.

title: fix(ext/node): fix send() validation in child_process IPC
body:
Two validation issues in the IPC send() function:

1. Used `new TypeError("ERR_MISSING_ARGS")` instead of the proper `ERR_MISSING_ARGS` error class, so the error had the code as its message instead of the correct message and code properties.

2. Missing type validation for the message argument. Sending a Symbol would hit the IPC serializer and throw an internal error instead of the expected ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE. Now validates that message is string, object, number, or boolean before attempting to send.

title: fix(ext/node): rewrite node:tty on top of uv compat
body:
Fixed https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/30075

A decent chunk of this is written by claude, mostly uv_tty.
author: nathanwhit

reviews.body:
This PR introduces libuv stream wrapping infrastructure with multiple critical memory safety issues. The primary concerns are use-after-free vulnerabilities stemming from raw pointers stored in callback data that can outlive their referents when GC occurs, and improper lifetime management between Rust structs and libuv handles. The most severe issue is that LibUvStreamWrap stores raw pointers to its internal fields in callback data passed to libuv, but these pointers can become dangling if the GarbageCollected object is freed while async operations are pending.

**[CRITICAL]** `ext/node/ops/stream_wrap.rs:147`: LibUvStreamWrap stores a raw pointer `stream: *const uv_stream_t` without any ownership tracking. If the underlying stream is freed (e.g., closed by uv_close) while this GarbageCollected object still holds the pointer, subsequent method calls will dereference a dangling pointer causing use-after-free.
> Suggestion: Track whether the stream handle is still valid (similar to HandleWrap's state tracking) and set the stream pointer to null when the handle is closed.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node/ops/stream_wrap.rs:302`: CallbackData contains `bytes_read: *const Cell<u64>` pointing to LibUvStreamWrap's internal field. If LibUvStreamWrap is garbage collected while reads are pending, the `on_uv_read` callback will dereference freed memory at line 262.
> Suggestion: Store bytes_read in a shared ownership structure (Arc) or ensure CallbackData prevents GC of the parent LibUvStreamWrap via v8::Global reference.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node/ops/stream_wrap.rs:579`: In write_buffer(), after partial uv_try_write(), a raw pointer to ArrayBuffer data is passed to async uv_write. If the ArrayBuffer is GC'd before the async write completes, this causes use-after-free. WriteCallbackData doesn't hold a reference to the buffer.
> Suggestion: Store a v8::Global reference to the buffer in WriteCallbackData, or copy the remaining data to owned memory.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node/ops/stream_wrap.rs:152`
Couple more comments

title: perf(ext/napi): use threadpool for async work instead of spawning threads
body:
## Summary

- Replace `std::thread::spawn` with `deno_core::unsync::spawn_blocking` (tokio's blocking threadpool) in `napi_queue_async_work`
- Threads are reused from a pool instead of creating a new OS thread per call, reducing overhead especially on Linux where `clone()` is expensive
- This is analogous to how Node.js uses libuv's `uv_queue_work` threadpool

## Context

PR #32560 fixed a deadlock bug by moving `execute` callbacks to worker threads via `std::thread::spawn`. This created a performance regression (#32773) because every async NAPI call spawns and tears down a fresh OS thread. The tokio blocking threadpool keeps threads alive for reuse (default 10s keep-alive), avoiding per-call thread creation overhead.

## Benchmark (macOS aarch64, napi-rs async add)

| Runtime | µs/call |
|---------|---------|
| Node.js | ~8.8 |
| Deno 2.7.5 (std::thread::spawn) | ~8.5-9 |
| Deno dev (spawn_blocking) | ~8.5 |

On macOS the steady-state is similar since thread creation is lightweight. On Linux (reporter's platform), `std::thread::spawn` is significantly more expensive due to `clone()` syscall cost — the reporter measured 62µs → 258µs regression.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test --test integration -- napi` — all NAPI tests pass (debug + release)
- [x] Benchmarked with napi-rs async functions — performance on par with Node.js

Closes #32773

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

perf(ext/napi): use threadpool for async work instead of spawning threads

Replace `std::thread::spawn` with `deno_core::unsync::spawn_blocking`
(tokio's blocking threadpool) in `napi_queue_async_work`. This reuses
threads from a pool instead of creating a new OS thread per call,
reducing overhead especially on Linux where thread creation via clone()
is expensive.

Fixes #32773

title: fix(ext/ffi): retain backing store references in nonblocking calls
body:
Nonblocking FFI calls extract raw pointers from ArrayBuffer backing stores and send them to a background thread via spawn_blocking, but without retaining a reference to the backing store. If V8's garbage collector runs before the background thread completes, the backing store can be freed, leaving the native code with a dangling pointer.

Fix by collecting SharedRef<BackingStore> references alongside the raw pointers for buffer, struct, and out_buffer arguments in nonblocking calls, and moving them into the spawn_blocking closure so they are kept alive for the duration of the native call.
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — solid fix for the UAF in nonblocking FFI

the `BackingStoreHolder` pattern is clean: grab `SharedRef<BackingStore>` before extracting raw pointers, move holder into the `spawn_blocking` closure, explicit `drop()` after the call to make intent clear

test coverage is good: fills buffer with known pattern, forces GC while native sleeps, validates checksum on return
lgtm

commits.message:
fix(ext/ffi): retain backing store references in nonblocking calls

Nonblocking FFI calls extract raw pointers from ArrayBuffer backing
stores and send them to a background thread via spawn_blocking, but
without retaining a reference to the backing store. If V8's garbage
collector runs before the background thread completes, the backing
store can be freed, leaving the native code with a dangling pointer.

Fix by collecting SharedRef<BackingStore> references alongside the raw
pointers for buffer, struct, and out_buffer arguments in nonblocking
calls, and moving them into the spawn_blocking closure so they are kept
alive for the duration of the native call.

title: fix(ext/node): fix multiple deepEqual comparison issues
body:
## Summary

Fixes `test-assert-deep.js` compat test from #32706.

- **Invalid dates**: Fix `NaN !== NaN` comparison by using `NumberIsNaN` for `Date.getTime()` results, so two invalid dates with the same properties are considered deeply equal
- **Date type check order**: Check `isDate(val2)` before calling `DatePrototypeGetTime(val2)` to prevent `TypeError` on non-Date objects with `[Symbol.toStringTag] = 'Date'`
- **Loose array comparison**: Allow `null` and `undefined` to be loosely equal in arrays by deferring to `innerDeepEqual` in loose mode instead of short-circuiting
- **Set comparison**: Fix `setObjectEquiv` to properly reject non-matching primitives in strict mode (was incorrectly continuing instead of returning false)
- **Map comparison**: Fix `mapObjectEquiv` with same pattern — check `b.has(key1)` before skipping primitive keys
- **Promise comparison**: Two different Promise instances are now only equal by reference, not structurally

### Enabled tests
- `test-assert-deep.js` (all 52 subtests pass)

## Test plan
- [x] `test-assert-deep.js` passes via `./x test-compat`
- [ ] CI passes

title: fix(ext/node): fix Buffer.concat, expose internal/buffer, implement markAsUntransferable
body:
## Summary

Fixes 3 Buffer compat tests from #32706 and fixes #31824.

- **Buffer.concat**: Use `TypedArrayPrototypeGetByteLength` instead of `.length` to prevent spoofed length getters from causing uninitialized memory exposure
- **internal/buffer**: Register as a requireable internal module and export `utf8Write`
- **markAsUntransferable**: Implement using V8's `set_detach_key` API. Applied to the Buffer pool so `ArrayBuffer.prototype.transfer()` throws `TypeError` instead of detaching the shared pool. This prevents the bug where transferring a Buffer's backing store would break all subsequent `Buffer.from()` calls (#31824)

### Enabled tests
- `test-buffer-concat.js`
- `test-buffer-write-fast.js`
- `test-buffer-pool-untransferable.js`

Closes #31824

## Test plan
- [x] All 3 newly enabled tests pass via `./x test-compat`
- [x] Verified #31824 repro no longer reproduces
- [ ] CI passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Reviewed with full repo context. Initial findings didn't hold up on closer inspection. Looks good.
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(node/buffer): use byteLength in Buffer.concat to prevent spoofed length

Use TypedArrayPrototypeGetByteLength instead of .length in
Buffer.concat() to prevent spoofed length getters from causing
uninitialized memory exposure.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(node/buffer): expose internal/buffer module and utf8Write

Register internal/buffer as a requireable internal module and export
utf8Write from it, enabling test-buffer-write-fast.js.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(node/buffer): implement markAsUntransferable and make pool non-transferable

Add op_mark_as_untransferable using V8's set_detach_key to make
ArrayBuffers truly non-transferable (both via postMessage and
ArrayBuffer.prototype.transfer()). Apply it to the Buffer pool and
implement worker_threads.markAsUntransferable().

title: fix(ext/otel): don't mark server spans as error for 4xx responses
body:
## Summary

- Per [OTel HTTP semantic conventions](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/http/http-spans/), server spans should only have ERROR status for 5xx responses. 4xx responses are client errors — the server handled the request correctly.
- Previously, `updateSpanFromResponse` treated all >= 400 responses as errors regardless of span kind, causing 404s to show as faults/errors in tracing backends (AWS X-Ray, Grafana Tempo, etc.).
- Split into `updateSpanFromClientResponse` (errors on >= 400) and `updateSpanFromServerResponse` (errors on >= 500) with a shared `setResponseAttributes` helper.

Fixes #29615

## Test plan

- [x] Added `http_server_status` spec test covering 200, 404, and 500 responses with both client and server spans
- [x] Server 404 span: no `error.type`, `status.code: 0` (UNSET)
- [x] Server 500 span: `error.type: "500"`, `status.code: 2` (ERROR)
- [x] Client 404 span: `error.type: "404"`, `status.code: 2` (ERROR) — unchanged
- [x] Existing `otel_basic::fetch` test passes (client-side behavior unchanged)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Reviewed the changes with full repo context. No issues found.

commits.message:
fix(ext/otel): don't mark server spans as error for 4xx responses

Per OTel HTTP semantic conventions, server spans should only have ERROR
status for 5xx responses. 4xx responses are client errors and should not
be marked as errors on the server side. Client spans correctly continue
to treat all >= 400 responses as errors.

Split `updateSpanFromResponse` into `updateSpanFromClientResponse` (errors
on >= 400) and `updateSpanFromServerResponse` (errors on >= 500).

Fixes #29615

title: fix(ext/console): make timer and counter state per-instance to fix OTEL console wrapping
body:
## Summary

- Fixes `console.time` / `console.timeEnd` / `console.count` / `console.countReset` producing spurious warnings when OTEL is enabled (`OTEL_DENO=true`)
- Root cause: `timerMap` and `countMap` were module-level singletons shared across all `Console` instances. When OTEL wraps the console via `wrapConsole`, both the OTEL Console and the original Console methods are called sequentially, but they were sharing the same timer/counter state, so the second call would see already-modified state.
- Fix: move `timerMap` and `countMap` to private instance fields (`#timerMap`, `#countMap`) so each `Console` instance maintains independent state.

Fixes #28818

## Test plan

- [x] Added `tests/specs/cli/otel_basic/console_time` spec test that exercises `console.time`/`console.timeEnd`/`console.count` with OTEL enabled
- [x] Existing console tests pass (`cargo test console`)
- [x] Existing OTEL spec tests unaffected (pre-existing metric test failures are unrelated)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Reviewed the changes with full repo context. No issues found.

commits.message:
fix(ext/console): make timer and counter state per-instance to fix OTEL console wrapping

When OTEL is enabled, `wrapConsole` calls both the OTEL Console and the
original Console methods sequentially. Since `timerMap` and `countMap` were
module-level singletons shared across all Console instances, the OTEL Console
would modify timer/counter state before the original Console ran, causing
spurious "Timer already exists" and "Timer does not exist" warnings.

Move `timerMap` and `countMap` to private instance fields so each Console
instance maintains independent state.

Fixes #28818

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

merge: resolve conflict with main, keep both test entries

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
more lax wiuldcard

fix: use #printFunc directly in stateful console methods

Console methods time/timeEnd/timeLog/count/countReset previously called
this.info()/this.warn() internally. Since these are arrow functions with
lexically captured `this`, they always invoked the original info/warn,
bypassing the callConsole wrapper. This meant otelLog was never called
for their output. Using this.#printFunc() directly ensures each Console
instance's own print function is invoked.

Also update console_time.out for log.iostream attribute from main.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): fix AbortController signal handling in child_process s…
body:
Two bugs in the abort listener for child_process.spawn():

1. Hardcoded "SIGKILL" instead of using the killSignal option (default "SIGTERM"), causing wrong signal to be sent to child.

2. AbortError was created without passing signal.reason as cause, so controller.abort(new Error('boom')) lost the abort reason.

Also fixes test-child-process-fork-abort-signal.js which had the same root cause.
author: fraidev

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): fix AbortController signal handling in child_process spawn

Two bugs in the abort listener for child_process.spawn():

1. Hardcoded "SIGKILL" instead of using the killSignal option
   (default "SIGTERM"), causing wrong signal to be sent to child.

2. AbortError was created without passing signal.reason as cause,
   so controller.abort(new Error('boom')) lost the abort reason.

Also fixes test-child-process-fork-abort-signal.js which had the
same root cause.

title: fix(ext/node): accept all TypedArrays in spawnSync input option
body:
`normalizeInput` only accepted `Uint8Array` and `DataView`, rejecting other TypedArrays like `Int8Array`. Node.js accepts any TypedArray.

Replaced the separate `instanceof Uint8Array` and `instanceof DataView` checks with a single `ArrayBuffer.isView()` that covers all TypedArray subtypes and DataView.
