title: fix(ext/napi): prevent spurious TypeError from napi_coerce_to_object on null/undefined
body:
Fixes #31306
Fixes #28682

- `napi_coerce_to_object(null/undefined)` was letting V8's `ToObject()` throw a TypeError, which got stored in `env.last_exception` by the `napi_wrap!` try-catch, then re-thrown by `call_fn` as a spurious unhandled rejection
- This masked real errors in NAPI modules like rolldown — plugin errors showed "Cannot convert undefined or null to object" instead of the actual error message
- Fix: check for null/undefined before calling `to_object()`, returning `napi_invalid_arg` without generating a V8 exception
- Added regression test that verifies `napi_coerce_to_object(null/undefined)` returns an error status without leaving a pending exception
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/napi): prevent spurious TypeError from napi_coerce_to_object on null/undefined

When `napi_coerce_to_object` was called with null or undefined, V8's
`ToObject()` would throw a TypeError ("Cannot convert undefined or null
to object"). The `napi_wrap!` macro's try-catch caught this and stored
it in `env.last_exception`. When the NAPI callback returned through
`call_fn`, this pending exception was re-thrown into V8, producing a
spurious unhandled rejection.

This caused real-world issues with NAPI modules like rolldown, where
plugin errors were masked by the spurious TypeError instead of showing
the original error message.

The fix checks for null/undefined before calling `to_object()`,
returning `napi_invalid_arg` without generating a V8 exception.

Closes #31306

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
fmt

- Adds `find_env_file()` helper that walks up parent directories when the specified env file isn't found at the given path, restoring the `dotenvy::from_filename()` behavior

commits.message:
fix: restore --env-file parent directory traversal

When dotenvy was replaced with deno_dotenv in #32263, the parent
directory traversal behavior of `dotenvy::from_filename()` was lost.
This caused `--env-file` to no longer find .env files in parent
directories when running from a subdirectory.

Adds `find_env_file()` which walks up parent directories looking for
the named file, restoring the previous behavior.

Closes #32683

title: feat(core): add cloneable resource registry for structured clone
body:
## Summary
- Adds infrastructure for custom JS objects to support structured cloning via `postMessage`/`MessageChannel`
- Objects set `[core.hostObjectBrand]` to a serializer function returning `{ type: "<name>", ...data }`, then register a deserializer via `core.registerCloneableResource(name, fn)`
- Marks `op_deserialize` as reentrant so deserializer callbacks can invoke ops
- Passes registered cloneable deserializers during message port deserialization

This enables objects like `CryptoKey` (#12734) and `X509Certificate` to be cloned across message ports, addressing #12067.

For reference, here's how this approach compares to Node.js's implementation of structured cloning for custom objects:

### Node.js approach
- Hooks into V8's serializer delegate at the **C++ level** (`src/node_messaging.cc` — `WriteHostObject`/`ReadHostObject`)
- Objects call `markTransferMode(this, cloneable, transferable)` which sets a **private symbol** with bitflags (`kCloneable = 0x1`, `kTransferable = 0x2`)
- During serialization, the delegate calls `obj[kClone]()` which returns `{ data, deserializeInfo: "module:ClassName" }` (e.g. `"internal/crypto/keys:InternalCryptoKey"`)
- During deserialization, uses **dynamic `require()` module loading** to discover the constructor from the `deserializeInfo` string — no explicit registry needed
- **Two-phase deserialization**: first creates an empty instance via the constructor, then calls `obj[kDeserialize](data)` to populate it
- Supports both C++ `BaseObject`s (via virtual methods) and JS objects (via symbols) in a unified framework

### Our approach
- Hooks into the **same V8 mechanism** but from the Rust/JS side — `[core.hostObjectBrand]` symbol triggers `write_host_object`/`read_host_object` in `ops_builtin_v8.rs` (this infrastructure already existed)
- Objects set `[core.hostObjectBrand]` to a **serializer function** that returns `{ type: "<name>", ...data }`
- Uses an **explicit registry** (`core.registerCloneableResource(name, deserializerFn)`) mapping type name → deserializer function
- **Single-phase deserialization**: the deserializer function creates and returns the final object directly (simpler, no separate create/populate phases)

Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12067
Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12734
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Reviewed the changes. No issues found.

commits.message:
feat(core): add cloneable resource registry for structured clone support

Adds infrastructure for custom JS objects to support structured cloning
via `postMessage`/`MessageChannel`. This enables objects like
`CryptoKey` and `X509Certificate` to be cloned across message ports.

The mechanism works by:
1. Objects set `[core.hostObjectBrand]` to a serializer function that
   returns `{ type: "<name>", ...data }`
2. Extensions register a deserializer via
   `core.registerCloneableResource(name, deserializerFn)`
3. During deserialization, the registry is consulted to reconstruct
   the original object

Changes:
- `libs/core/01_core.js`: Add `registerCloneableResource` /
  `getCloneableDeserializers` registry, auto-pass deserializers
  in `structuredClone`
- `libs/core/ops_builtin_v8.rs`: Mark `op_deserialize` as reentrant
  so deserializer callbacks can invoke ops
- `ext/web/13_message_port.js`: Pass cloneable deserializers during
  message deserialization

Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12067
Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12734

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

test(core): add tests for cloneable resource registry

Tests cover:
- structuredClone with a cloneable object
- serialize/deserialize round-trip with cloneable object

body:
## Summary
- Fixes multiple compatibility issues with `crypto.X509Certificate` to pass the Node.js `test-crypto-x509.js` test suite
- Adds `isX509Certificate()`, `signatureAlgorithm`, `signatureAlgorithmOid` getters
- Fixes `emailAddress` label, date timezone format, `toLegacyObject()` output (Buffer raw, null-prototype objects, infoAccess as structured object, RSA modulus/exponent format)
- Adds structured clone support for X509Certificate via MessagePort

Depends on #32672

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: `toLegacyObject().infoAccess` is at risk of being wrong or empty because the new AIA parser does not decode `GeneralName` correctly. It treats the `accessLocation` as if a URI were identified by tag value `6` alone, but X.509 encodes `uniformResourceIdentifier` as context-specific `[6]`, not the universal IA5String tag. This can cause valid OCSP/CA Issuers URIs to be skipped or misread, breaking Node compatibility for certificates that rely on `infoAccess`.
Reviewed the changes. No issues found.

commits.message:
feat(core): add cloneable resource registry for structured clone support

Adds infrastructure for custom JS objects to support structured cloning
via `postMessage`/`MessageChannel`. This enables objects like
`CryptoKey` and `X509Certificate` to be cloned across message ports.

The mechanism works by:
1. Objects set `[core.hostObjectBrand]` to a serializer function that
   returns `{ type: "<name>", ...data }`
2. Extensions register a deserializer via
   `core.registerCloneableResource(name, deserializerFn)`
3. During deserialization, the registry is consulted to reconstruct
   the original object

Changes:
- `libs/core/01_core.js`: Add `registerCloneableResource` /
  `getCloneableDeserializers` registry, auto-pass deserializers
  in `structuredClone`
- `libs/core/ops_builtin_v8.rs`: Mark `op_deserialize` as reentrant
  so deserializer callbacks can invoke ops
- `ext/web/13_message_port.js`: Pass cloneable deserializers during
  message deserialization

Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12067
Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/12734

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fixes multiple compatibility issues with `crypto.X509Certificate` to pass
Node.js test-crypto-x509.js:

- Add `isX509Certificate()` to `internal/crypto/x509` module
- Fix `emailAddress` label in subject/issuer (was `Email`)
- Fix `validFrom`/`validTo` timezone suffix (`+00:00` → `GMT`)
- Add `signatureAlgorithm` and `signatureAlgorithmOid` getters
- Fix `toLegacyObject()`: return Buffer for `raw`, null-prototype
  objects for `subject`/`issuer`/`infoAccess`, add `emailAddress`
  field and `infoAccess` as structured object
- Fix RSA modulus to strip ASN.1 leading zero byte
- Fix RSA exponent format to `0x`-prefixed hex
- Add structured clone support for X509Certificate via MessagePort
  by introducing a cloneable resource registry in `Deno.core` and
  marking `op_deserialize` as reentrant

title: fix(npm): handle read-only bin files when setting up node_modules/.bin
body:
## Summary

- Some npm tarballs ship bin files with read+execute but **no write** permission (e.g. `@formatjs/cli@2.15.0` uses mode `555`). `make_executable_if_exists()` was opening these files with `O_RDWR` unconditionally, causing `EACCES` ("Permission denied").
- Fix: open read-only first to check the execute bit. Only reopen with write if chmod is actually needed.

```
$ curl -sL https://registry.npmjs.org/@formatjs/cli/-/cli-2.15.0.tgz | tar tvzf - | grep bin/formatjs
-r-xr-xr-x  0 0      0          81 26 Oct  1985 package/bin/formatjs
```

Closes #29847
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, nice fix

opening read-only first to check permissions, then only reopening with write if chmod is needed, is the right approach.

the `drop(file)` before reopening is important — good that you included it.

solid test coverage for both the readonly case (mode 555) and the non-executable case (mode 644).
LGTM, but CI is failing

commits.message:
fix(npm): handle read-only bin files when setting up node_modules/.bin

Some npm tarballs ship bin files with read+execute but no write
permission (e.g. `@formatjs/cli@2.15.0` uses mode 555). The
`make_executable_if_exists()` function was opening these files with
O_RDWR unconditionally, which fails with EACCES on such files.

The fix opens the file read-only first to check if the execute bit is
already set. Only if the execute bit is missing does it reopen with
write access to chmod. This avoids the permission error for packages
that already ship executable bin files without write permission.

Closes #29847

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

- Non-executable file (mode 644): verifies execute bit is added

quinn-proto 0.11.14

@​jxs reported a denial of service issue in quinn-proto 5 days ago:

title: fix(console): prevent multi-line object values in console.table
body:
## Summary

- Objects in `console.table` could break the table layout when their `Deno.inspect` output exceeded the default `breakLength` (72 chars), causing some rows to wrap across multiple lines
- Fix: set `breakLength: Infinity` in the inspect options for table cell values, ensuring single-line rendering

Before:
```
│     1 │ "371fe41e-..." │ { id: "371fe41e-...",
  name: "Deutsche Bahn",
  countryCode: "DE" } │
```

After:
```
│     1 │ "371fe41e-..." │ { id: "371fe41e-...", name: "Deutsche Bahn", countryCode: "DE" } │
```

Fixes #18828

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit test in `console_test.ts` verifying objects with long values stay on single line

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(console): prevent multi-line object values in console.table

Objects in `console.table` could break the table layout when their
inspect output exceeded the default `breakLength`, causing some rows
to wrap across multiple lines while others stayed on one line.

Fix by setting `breakLength: Infinity` in the inspect options used
for table cell values, ensuring all values are rendered on a single
line regardless of width.

Fixes #18828

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): initialize debuglog testEnabled with safe default
body:
## Summary

- Initialize `testEnabled` and `debugImpls` in `debuglog.ts` with safe defaults (`() => false` and empty object) instead of leaving them deliberately uninitialized
- Fixes bootstrap crash `TypeError: testEnabled is not a function` that occurs when internal stream code triggers debug logging before `initializeDebugEnv()` has been called

This crash occurs when stdin is unavailable during Node process bootstrap — for example in compiled binaries run as Windows services (via nssm), or processes detached with `nohup`/`disown` on FreeBSD. In these cases, `initStdin()` creates a dummy Readable and calls `.push(null)`, which triggers `readableAddChunk` → `debug()` from the stream module's `debuglog("stream")`. If this happens before `initializeDebugEnv()` sets `testEnabled`, the process panics.

With this fix, early calls to `debuglog()` gracefully return a no-op logger instead of crashing. `initializeDebugEnv()` still overrides the defaults during normal bootstrap.

Closes #24208

## Test plan

- [x] Existing tests pass (`tools/format.js`, `tools/lint.js --js`)
- [ ] Verify on Windows with a compiled binary run as a service (nssm)
- [ ] Verify with `--watch` on a detached process (`nohup`/`disown`)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): initialize debuglog testEnabled with safe default

Fixes a crash where `testEnabled is not a function` is thrown during
bootstrap when internal stream code triggers debug logging before
`initializeDebugEnv()` has been called. This occurs when stdin is
unavailable, such as in compiled binaries run as Windows services
(via nssm) or processes detached with nohup/disown.

Initialize `testEnabled` and `debugImpls` with safe defaults
(`() => false` and empty object) so early calls to `debuglog()`
gracefully disable debug logging instead of crashing.

Closes #24208

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: refactor(napi): replace external napi-sys with in-tree napi_sys crate
body:
## Summary

- Forks `napi-sys` (v2.2.2) into `libs/napi_sys/` to avoid build-time dependency on Node.js binaries (required by `napi-sys` v3.x) and allow adding new Node-API symbols directly in-tree
- Removes all napi version feature gates — every symbol is always available
- Eliminates `napi-build` dependency by inlining the macOS linker flags into `tests/napi/build.rs`
- Adds new symbols that are needed by blocked PRs:
  - `node_api_create_property_key_latin1`, `node_api_create_property_key_utf8` (#32559)
  - `node_api_create_object_with_properties`, `node_api_create_object_with_named_properties` (#31443)
  - `node_api_symbol_for`, `node_api_create_property_key_utf16`

This unblocks #32559 and #31443 which currently can't build because the symbols they need aren't available in `napi-sys` v2.2.2, and upgrading to v3.x introduces a requirement for Node.js binaries during build.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p napi_sys -p test_napi` passes
- [x] napi integration tests pass locally
- [ ] CI passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, good call forking napi-sys to avoid the Node.js binary build dep in v3.x

the macro approach for windows vs non-windows is clean - windows loads symbols at runtime via libloading, unix/mac uses standard extern linkage

one minor thing: the `#[allow(unused_unsafe)]` in tests/napi/src/lib.rs — is that because the non-windows codepath now generates safe fns? might be worth a comment explaining why it's needed

Thanks for the review!

> the `#[allow(unused_unsafe)]` in tests/napi/src/lib.rs — is that because the non-windows codepath now generates safe fns?

Exactly — on non-windows, the `generate!` macro expands to `unsafe extern "C" { pub safe fn ... }`, which means the functions are callable without `unsafe`. The existing test code wraps calls in `unsafe { ... }` blocks (which were required when using the old `napi-sys` crate's plain `extern "C"` declarations). Rather than touching every test file, the `allow` keeps them compiling. Added a comment explaining this in bb29a92.

commits.message:
refactor(napi): replace external napi-sys/napi-build with in-tree napi_sys crate

Fork napi-sys into libs/napi_sys to avoid build-time dependency on Node.js
binaries (required by napi-sys v3.x) and allow adding new Node-API symbols
directly in-tree. This unblocks PRs that need newer symbols:
- #32559 (node_api_create_property_key_latin1/utf8)
- #31443 (node_api_create_object_with_properties)

All napi version feature gates have been removed — every symbol is always
available. The napi-build dependency is also eliminated by inlining the
macOS linker flags into tests/napi/build.rs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: allow unused_unsafe in test_napi for safe extern fn declarations

The in-tree napi_sys declares functions as `pub safe fn` inside
`unsafe extern "C"` blocks, so existing `unsafe { ... }` wrappers
around napi calls are now redundant. Allow them crate-wide rather
than touching every test file.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Regenerate

fix: fix Windows build for in-tree napi_sys

- Remove duplicate `load()` from functions.rs (the `generate!` macro
  already creates one)
- Add `unsafe` blocks inside the Windows `generate!` macro for Rust
  2024 edition compatibility (mutable static access, unsafe fn calls)
- Move host library creation into `setup()` in lib.rs

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

add comment explaining unused_unsafe allow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: add clippy safety annotations for Windows build

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: don't abort symbol loading on missing symbols (Windows)

The old napi-sys had separate modules each with their own load() call,
so a missing symbol only skipped that module. With everything in a
single generate! block, the early return on a missing symbol (e.g. a
new node_api_* function not yet exported by Deno) would abort loading
ALL subsequent symbols including core ones like napi_create_object.

Fix by continuing to load remaining symbols when one is missing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: refactor(ext/node): remove multipleResolves process event handling
body:
## Summary
- Remove the `notImplementedEvents` code path in `process.ts` that was only used for the `multipleResolves` event
- The `multipleResolves` event is End-of-Life ([DEP0160](https://nodejs.org/api/deprecations.html#DEP0160)) since Node.js v25.0.0, as it didn't work with V8 promise combinators
- The event is now treated like any other unknown event (passed through to `EventEmitter`), matching Node.js behavior
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — clean removal of dead code

`multipleResolves` was deprecated (DEP0160) and removed in Node v25, so the `notImplementedEvents` array and all the associated warning logic can go. events now just pass through to EventEmitter like any other unknown event

commits.message:
refactor(node): remove `multipleResolves` process event handling

The `multipleResolves` event has been End-of-Life (DEP0160) since
Node.js v25.0.0, as it didn't work with V8 promise combinators.
Remove the special `notImplementedEvents` code path that was only
used for this event.

title: refactor(ext/node): consolidate node:fs modules (part 5)
body:
## Summary

Follow-up to #32555. Consolidates four more `node:fs` polyfill modules into `fs.ts` and `internal/fs/promises.ts`:

- `_fs/_fs_mkdir.ts` → inlined `mkdir`, `mkdirSync` + helpers (`fixMkdirError`, `findFirstNonExistent`)
- `_fs/_fs_mkdtemp.ts` → inlined `mkdtemp`, `mkdtempSync` + helpers (`decodeMkdtemp`, `parseMkdtempEncoding`)

title: refactor: move Temporal types to separate lib.temporal.d.ts
body:
## Summary

Moves Temporal API type definitions from `lib.deno.unstable.d.ts` to a new `lib.temporal.d.ts` file, and references it from `lib.deno.window.d.ts` and `lib.deno.worker.d.ts`.

## Problem

When users explicitly set `compilerOptions.lib` in their `deno.json`, they lose Temporal types unless they also include `"deno.unstable"`. This is confusing because:
- Temporal is stable in Deno 2.x (no longer behind `--unstable-temporal`)
- The runtime always has Temporal available
- Users shouldn't need to opt into "unstable" for stable features

## Solution

Create a separate `lib.temporal.d.ts` that is auto-included via `lib.deno.window.d.ts` and `lib.deno.worker.d.ts`. This ensures Temporal types are always available when using the standard Deno libs.

This approach was suggested in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32468#issuecomment-4018071807 and aligns with TypeScript's plan to add `esnext.temporal.d.ts` in TS 6.0.

Closes #32468
author: kajukitli

commits.message:
refactor: move Temporal types to separate lib.temporal.d.ts

Moves Temporal API type definitions from lib.deno.unstable.d.ts to a
new lib.temporal.d.ts file, and references it from lib.deno.window.d.ts
and lib.deno.worker.d.ts.

This fixes the issue where users who explicitly set compilerOptions.lib
would lose Temporal types unless they also included "deno.unstable".
Since Temporal is now stable in Deno 2.x (no longer behind --unstable-temporal),
users should get Temporal types automatically when using deno.window.

Closes #32468

title: fix(ext/node): make TTY stdout/stderr indestructible
body:
## Summary

- Libraries like `mute-stream` (used by `@inquirer/prompts`) call `destroy()` or `end()` on `process.stdout`, which previously closed the underlying Deno resource (rid 1/2). This broke subsequent `console.log` calls with `BadResource: Bad resource ID` because `op_print` looks up the resource in the table.
- Match Node.js behavior where stdio streams are indestructible:
  - Override `_destroy` with a dummy that calls `_undestroy()` to revert stream state without closing the handle (same as Node.js's `dummyDestroy` in `is_main_thread.js`)
  - Override `_handle._onClose` to prevent the `end()` -> `_final` -> `shutdown` -> `_onClose` path from closing the resource

Closes #32513

## Test plan

- [x] New spec test `process_stdout_indestructible` verifies `console.log` works after `process.stdout.destroy()`
- [x] New PTY integration test `process_stdout_destroy_undestroy_pty` 
- [x] Manually verified with `@inquirer/prompts` reproduction from the issue

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): make TTY stdout/stderr indestructible

Libraries like mute-stream (used by @inquirer/prompts) call destroy()
or end() on process.stdout, which previously closed the underlying
Deno resource (rid 1/2). This broke subsequent console.log calls with
"Bad resource ID" because op_print looks up the resource in the table.

Match Node.js behavior where stdio streams are indestructible:
- Override _destroy with a dummy that calls _undestroy() to revert
  stream state without closing the handle
- Override _handle._onClose to prevent the end() -> _final -> shutdown
  -> _onClose path from closing the resource

Closes #32513

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: refactor(ext/node): consolidate fs modules and move promises to internal/fs
body:
## Summary

- **Simplify `internal/fs/streams.mjs`**: use `import * as fs from "node:fs"` instead of cherry-picking from individual `_fs_*.ts` files
- **Inline small fs modules into `fs.ts`**: `_fs_access.ts`, `_fs_appendFile.ts`, `_fs_chmod.ts`, `_fs_chown.ts`, `_fs_close.ts` — callback and sync versions now live directly in `fs.ts`
- **Create `internal/fs/promises.ts`**: mirrors Node.js's `lib/internal/fs/promises.js` structure — all promise-based fs APIs are assembled here, keeping `fs.ts` focused on callback/sync APIs
- **Update `cp/cp.ts` and `cp/cp_sync.ts`**: use `Deno.chmod`/`Deno.chmodSync` directly instead of importing from the now-deleted `_fs_chmod.ts`
- **Consolidate tests**: move tests from `_fs/_fs_access_test.ts`, `_fs/_fs_appendFile_test.ts`, `_fs/_fs_chmod_test.ts`, `_fs/_fs_chown_test.ts`, `_fs/_fs_close_test.ts` into `fs_test.ts`

This is the first in a series of PRs to reduce the number of small `_fs/_fs_*.ts` files by inlining them into `fs.ts`, matching Node.js's module structure more closely.

This series of PRs should improve startup time a little bit, because we're not gonna have ~30 small modules.

title: fix: prevent panic on dynamic import with non-string error name
author: kajukitli

commits.message:
fix: prevent panic on dynamic import with non-string error name

When a dynamic import rejects with an Error whose 'name' property is a
non-string type (e.g., Number, Object, Symbol), serde_v8::from_v8 would
fail and the subsequent .unwrap() would panic, terminating the entire
Deno process.

This changes the .unwrap() to .unwrap_or_default(), allowing the error
to be handled gracefully with a default NativeJsError (which has
name: None, message: None).

Security: GHSA-2f8r-ppr9-ff8f

fix: prevent panic when error property getter throws

When processing errorAdditionalPropertyKeys, if a property getter throws,
the .unwrap() calls on arr.get_index() and exception.get() would panic.

Replace unwrap() with let-else patterns that skip problematic properties
instead of crashing.

title: fix(ext/node): use sync writes for TTY stdout/stderr WriteStream
body:
## Summary

- After #32343 switched `process.stdout`/`process.stderr` to `tty.WriteStream` (extending `net.Socket`), writes became asynchronous through the `LibuvStreamWrap` handle
- Since `console.log` in Deno writes synchronously via `Deno.core.print()`, this created a race condition where async `process.stdout.write()` calls could complete after a subsequent synchronous `console.log`, causing output corruption (e.g. listr2 ANSI redraws overwriting later output)
- Fix by overriding `_write` for fd 1 and 2 to use `writeSync`, matching the behavior of the previous `createWritableStdioStream` implementation

Closes #32473
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): use sync writes for TTY stdout/stderr WriteStream

After #32343 switched process.stdout/stderr to tty.WriteStream (extending
net.Socket), writes became asynchronous through the LibuvStreamWrap handle.
Since console.log in Deno writes synchronously via Deno.core.print(), this
created a race condition where async process.stdout.write() calls could
complete after a subsequent synchronous console.log, causing output
corruption (e.g. listr2 ANSI redraws overwriting later output).

Fix by overriding _write for fd 1 and 2 to use writeSync, matching the
behavior of the previous createWritableStdioStream implementation.

Closes #32473

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: test: modernize "node_compat" test runner
body:
## Summary

Modernizes the node compat test runner (`tests/node_compat/mod.rs`) with three
improvements:

### 1. Simplified test filtering

Any test from the Node.js suite can now be run directly with a filter,
**regardless of whether it's listed in `config.jsonc`**:

```sh
# Run a specific test
cargo test --test node_compat -- test-assert-deep

# Run all tests in a directory
cargo test --test node_compat -- sequential/

# Run all tests matching a pattern
cargo test --test node_compat -- test-crypto
```

**How collection interacts with `config.jsonc`:**
- **With a filter** (`-- <pattern>`): all test files are collected from the
  full suite directory, then filtered by the pattern. This means you can run
  *any* test — even ones not yet in `config.jsonc` — which is useful when
  working on new Node.js compatibility fixes.
- **Without a filter**: only tests listed in `config.jsonc` are run. This is
  the CI behavior and ensures we only run the known-passing set.
- **With `--report`**: all tests are collected and run (for the daily
  compatibility report).

Previously, running a test not in `config.jsonc` required a separate
`--filter` flag with different semantics from the standard cargo test filter.
That custom flag has been removed.

### 2. Report module extraction

All report-related types (`TestReport`, `ErrorInfo`, `ResultInfo`,
`CollectedResult`) and functions (`generate_report`, `get_deno_version`,
`read_node_version`) have been moved to a new `report.rs` module. This keeps
the main runner focused on test collection and execution.

### 3. Deduplicated PTY/piped execution paths

Extracted a `TestSetup` struct that computes the shared test configuration
(deno args, env vars, V8 flags, timeout) once. Both `run_piped()` and
`run_pty()` methods consume this setup, eliminating ~50 lines of duplicated
command-building logic between the PTY and piped output code paths.
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
refactor(node_compat): simplify test collection and filtering

Always collect all tests from the filesystem, then filter based on context:
- With a CLI filter (e.g. `cargo test --test node_compat -- test-assert`),
  match against the full suite so any test can be run directly
- Without a filter, retain only tests listed in config.jsonc (same as before)
- Report mode runs everything (same as before)

Remove the redundant custom `--filter` CLI arg and `collect_tests_from_config`
in favor of the built-in file_test_runner filter mechanism.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(node_compat): extract report module and deduplicate PTY/piped paths

- Move all report-related types (TestReport, ErrorInfo, ResultInfo,
  CollectedResult) and functions (generate_report, get_deno_version,
  read_node_version) into a separate `report` module.

- Extract TestSetup struct that computes shared state (args, env vars,
  timeout, flags) once, then both run_piped() and run_pty() consume it.
  This eliminates the duplicated command-building logic between the two
  execution paths.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: Reland refactor(core): move nextTick/immediate queues into core, replace ops with shared buffers (#32440)
body:
## Summary
- The nextTick rewrite (#32440) moved unhandled rejection processing into `processTicksAndRejections` (JS-side `processPromiseRejections`), but `rejectionhandled` events were still only dispatched from Rust's `dispatch_rejections`, which runs **after** tick draining. This caused `rejectionhandled` to fire after `unhandledrejection` for later promises, breaking expected event ordering.
- Extracted `dispatch_handled_rejections` from `dispatch_rejections` and call it before both the tick drain in the main event loop and between timer callbacks in `dispatch_timers`.
- Fixes `deno_core_testing::integration::error_rejection_catch`.

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo nextest run -p deno_core` — all 354 tests pass
- [x] `cargo nextest run -p deno_core_testing` — all 54 tests pass (including previously failing `error_rejection_catch`)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

processTicksAndRejections (JS-side), but "rejectionhandled" events
were still only dispatched from Rust's dispatch_rejections, which
runs after tick draining. This caused rejectionhandled to fire after
unhandledrejection for later promises, breaking the expected ordering.

Fix by extracting dispatch_handled_rejections and calling it before

title: ci: fix deno_core changes check in shallow clones
body:
## Summary
- The previous shell one-liner used `git diff` against the PR base SHA, which often isn't available in the shallow clone (`fetch-depth: 5`). When the base SHA is missing, `git diff` fails silently, `grep` gets no input, and deno_core tests are incorrectly skipped.
- Fix by explicitly fetching the base SHA and replacing the fragile shell pipeline with a Deno script (`tools/check_deno_core_changes.js`) using `Deno.spawnAndWait()`.
- Added `installDenoStep` to the pre_build job since the script needs Deno.

## Test plan
- [ ] Verify CI runs deno_core tests on a PR that changes files in `libs/core/`
- [ ] Verify CI skips deno_core tests on a PR that only changes files outside core dirs

title: fix(ext/node): handle partial writes to process.stdout/stderr
body:
## Summary

- Fix silent data truncation when piping >64KB through `process.stdout` in Node compat mode
- The `_write` handler in `createWritableStdioStream` discarded the return value of `writer.writeSync()`, assuming all bytes were always written. When stdout is a pipe and the OS buffer is near capacity (~64KB), `writeSync` returns a short count and the remaining bytes were silently lost.
- The fix loops on partial writes until the entire buffer is flushed.

Closes #26784

## Test plan

- Added `stdoutPipePartialWriteNotTruncated`: verifies `fs.createReadStream().pipe(process.stdout)` with >64KB does not truncate
- Added `stdoutWriteMultipleChunksNotTruncated`: verifies multiple `process.stdout.write()` calls totaling >64KB are not lost
- `cargo test unit_node::child_process_test`

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm. classic partial write bug - easy to miss that writeSync can return less than the buffer size.

one edge case to consider: if `writeSync` returns 0 (no progress), this becomes an infinite loop. unlikely with stdout but could happen with a broken/wedged fd. might want `if (nwritten === 0) break;` as a safety valve, though tbh this is probably fine for stdout/stderr.
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): handle partial writes to process.stdout/stderr

The `_write` handler for `process.stdout`/`process.stderr` discarded the
return value of `writer.writeSync()`, assuming all bytes were always
written. When stdout is a pipe and the OS pipe buffer is near capacity
(~64 KB), `writeSync` can return a short count. The remaining bytes were
silently lost, truncating output for any subprocess piping >64 KB of
data through `process.stdout`.

This fixes the handler to loop on partial writes until the entire buffer
has been written.

Closes #26784

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: perf(install): improve parallelism of fetches during npm resolution
body:
disclosure: profiling and optimization idea by me, code changes (and the rest of this PR description) by claude

Before

  resolve_pending processes one parent node at a time sequentially. Inside resolve_next_pending,
  it uses FuturesOrdered to fetch package info for that single parent's dependencies concurrently
   — but there is no overlap between parents. If parent A has deps [x, y, z] and parent B has
  deps [p, q], we fetch x/y/z concurrently, wait for them all to be processed, then move to B and
   fetch p/q. Newly discovered children go to the back of the queue and can't start fetching
  until every previously-queued parent finishes.

  After

  drain_pending_parallel drains all pending parent nodes into a single FuturesUnordered, so all
  parents' dep fetches are in-flight simultaneously. Results are buffered per-parent and retired
  in FIFO order (by insertion order) to preserve deterministic resolution — even though network
  responses arrive out of order, we process parents in the same order the old code would have.

  When a parent is retired and its deps are processed, any newly discovered child nodes are
  immediately enqueued into the same FuturesUnordered. This means we don't wait for the entire
  current BFS level to complete before starting the next — child fetches overlap with sibling
  parent fetches, giving cross-BFS-level parallelism.

Results
---

```
Benchmark 1: /Users/nathanwhit/Documents/Code/deno/target/deno-baseline install
  Time (mean ± σ):      8.295 s ±  0.879 s    [User: 1.196 s, System: 3.599 s]
  Range (min … max):    7.028 s …  9.758 s    10 runs

Benchmark 2: /Users/nathanwhit/Documents/Code/deno/target/release-lite/deno install
  Time (mean ± σ):      3.342 s ±  0.344 s    [User: 0.981 s, System: 3.297 s]
  Range (min … max):    2.992 s …  4.138 s    10 runs
```
author: nathanwhit

reviews.body: LGTM. Nice!

Merge main into npm-resolution-concurrency

title: fix(ext/crypto): fix P-521 EC key export for all formats
body:
## Summary

- **Fixed P-521 key generation** to output PKCS8 DER (matching P-256/P-384 behavior) instead of raw scalar bytes, which broke JWK private, JWK public (from private handle), and SPKI exports
- **Added P-521 JWK private export arm** in the `export_key_ec` match, which was the only curve missing from the `JwkPrivate` handler
- Added regression test for P-521 JWK private key export round-trip

Closes #26431

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p deno_crypto` compiles with no warnings
- [x] All `unit_node::crypto` tests pass (10/10)
- [x] Verified all P-521 export formats work: SPKI, PKCS8, JWK public, JWK private

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm

root cause was `generate_key_ec_p521()` returning raw scalar bytes while the export code expected PKCS8 DER (like P-256/P-384). good catch adding the missing P-521 arm in the JwkPrivate match too.

test does a proper round-trip which is the right way to verify this.

code looks correct

**verified against Node.js:**
- JWK fields match: `kty`, `crv`, `x`, `y`, `d`
- lengths are correct (88 chars base64url for 66-byte P-521 values)
- round-trip import/export works identically

one minor suggestion: test could also verify the lengths are exactly 88 chars (catches potential padding bugs)

```ts
assertEquals(jwk.x?.length, 88);
assertEquals(jwk.y?.length, 88);
assertEquals(jwk.d?.length, 88);
```

but not a blocker, the round-trip test would catch most issues anyway
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/crypto): fix P-521 EC key export for all formats

P-521 key generation was storing raw scalar bytes instead of PKCS8 DER,
unlike P-256/P-384 which use aws_lc_rs to generate PKCS8. This caused
JWK private, JWK public (from private handle), and SPKI exports to fail
for generated P-521 keys. Fix by encoding the generated P-521 secret key
to PKCS8 DER format, and add the missing P-521 JWK private export arm.

Closes #26431

title: fix(ext/node): make `execPath` named export a real string
body:
## Summary

- The named ESM export `execPath` from `node:process` was a frozen object with `String.prototype` that pretended to be a string but had `typeof === "object"`. This broke libraries like execa that check `typeof execPath === "string"`, causing `nuxt preview` to fail with `The "nodePath" option must be a string or a file URL`.
- Now `execPath` is initialized as `""` and set to `Deno.execPath()` during `__bootstrapNodeProcess`, so both `process.execPath` and the named import are real strings.

Before:
```
import { execPath } from "node:process";
typeof execPath // "object"
```

After:
```
import { execPath } from "node:process";
typeof execPath // "string"
```

## Test plan
- [x] Verified `typeof execPath === "string"` for both named import and `process.execPath`
- [x] Verified both return the correct path
- [ ] Existing tests pass

Closes #25779

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): make `execPath` named export a real string

The named ESM export `execPath` from `node:process` was a frozen object
with `String.prototype` that pretended to be a string but had
`typeof === "object"`. This broke libraries like execa that check
`typeof execPath === "string"`, causing nuxt preview to fail.

Now `execPath` is initialized as an empty string and set to
`Deno.execPath()` during bootstrap, so both `process.execPath` and
the named import are real strings.

Closes #25779

title: fix(node): support ECDSA with secp256k1 in `node:crypto`
body:
## Summary

- Adds secp256k1 ECDSA support to `node:crypto` key generation, signing, verification, and key import/export (DER, PEM, JWK)
- Adds `Secp256k1` variant to `EcPrivateKey`/`EcPublicKey` enums using the existing `k256` crate
- Enables `ecdh`, `jwk`, `pem` features on `k256` workspace dependency

Closes #27523

## Details

The `k256` crate was already used for ECDH with secp256k1, but the ECDSA key generation/signing/verification paths only supported P-224, P-256, and P-384. This PR adds secp256k1 support across all EC code paths:

- **Key generation**: `generateKeyPair('ec', { namedCurve: 'secp256k1' })` now works
- **Signing/verification**: `sign()`, `verify()`, `createSign()`, `createVerify()` with secp256k1 keys
- **Key import/export**: PKCS#8, SEC1, SPKI (DER/PEM), and JWK formats
- **Key details**: Reports `namedCurve: "secp256k1"` in `asymmetricKeyDetails`
- **X.509**: Parsing secp256k1 public keys from certificates
- **DH**: `diffieHellman()` with secp256k1 KeyObject pairs

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit tests for key generation, sign/verify (one-shot and streaming), PEM export/import roundtrip, and async key generation
- [x] `cargo test unit_node::crypto::crypto_key_test` passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
Good start but **incomplete implementation**. This PR adds secp256k1 key support but misses critical signing/verification operations.

## ✅ What works
- Key generation: `generateKeyPair('ec', { namedCurve: 'secp256k1' })`
- Key import/export: PEM, DER, JWK formats  
- ECDH operations (existing support via k256 crate)
- Proper OID handling (1.3.132.0.10)

## ❌ What's missing
- **Signing/verification operations** - no secp256k1 case in `ext/node_crypto/sign.rs`
- `sign()`, `verify()`, `createSign()`, `createVerify()` will fail at runtime
- Pattern established for P224/P256/P384 but secp256k1 not added

## The gap

In `ext/node_crypto/sign.rs`, the `EcPrivateKey` match around line 175 is incomplete:

```rust
EcPrivateKey::P224(key) => { /* signing logic */ }
EcPrivateKey::P256(key) => { /* signing logic */ } 
EcPrivateKey::P384(key) => { /* signing logic */ }
// EcPrivateKey::Secp256k1(key) => { /* MISSING */ }
```

## Fix needed

Add secp256k1 signing support in `sign.rs`:

```rust
EcPrivateKey::Secp256k1(key) => {
  let signing_key = k256::ecdsa::SigningKey::from(key);
  let signature: k256::ecdsa::Signature = signing_key
    .sign_prehash(digest)
    .map_err(|_| KeyObjectHandlePrehashedSignAndVerifyError::FailedToSignDigest)?;
  dsa_signature(dsa_signature_encoding, signature)
}
```

Same pattern needed in the verification path for `EcPublicKey::Secp256k1`.

The k256 crate has the `ecdsa` feature enabled so the signing types should be available.

## Testing

The current tests likely only cover key generation. Need to verify actual sign/verify operations work end-to-end, not just key creation and export.

Thanks for the review! However, the signing/verification support is already implemented in this PR.

`sign.rs` already has both:
- **Signing** (line 200): `EcPrivateKey::Secp256k1` match arm with `k256::ecdsa::SigningKey`
- **Verification** (line 326): `EcPublicKey::Secp256k1` match arm with `k256::ecdsa::VerifyingKey`

The tests also cover actual sign/verify operations end-to-end — not just key generation:
- `ec secp256k1 sign and verify` — tests `sign()`/`verify()` one-shot API
- `ec secp256k1 createSign and createVerify` — tests streaming API
- `ec secp256k1 export and import PEM` — tests PEM roundtrip + signing with imported keys

**Found Node.js compatibility issue with asymmetricKeyDetails**

Tested against Node.js and found that Deno returns incorrect curve names in `asymmetricKeyDetails`. 

**Current behavior (Deno)**:
- P-256 → `{ named_curve: "p256" }`
- P-384 → `{ named_curve: "p384" }` 
- secp256k1 → `{ named_curve: "secp256k1" }`

**Expected behavior (Node.js)**:
- P-256 → `{ namedCurve: 'prime256v1' }`
- P-384 → `{ namedCurve: 'secp384r1' }`
- secp256k1 → `{ namedCurve: 'secp256k1' }`

Node.js uses OpenSSL standard curve names via [`OBJ_nid2sn(nid)`](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/src/crypto/crypto_ec.cc#L901).

**Required fix**: Update curve name mappings in `ext/node_crypto/keys.rs` lines 1852-1855 and 1906-1909:

```rust
// Lines 1852-1855
let named_curve = match key {
  EcPrivateKey::P224(_) => "secp224r1",    // was "p224"
  EcPrivateKey::P256(_) => "prime256v1",   // was "p256"
  EcPrivateKey::P384(_) => "secp384r1",    // was "p384"
  EcPrivateKey::Secp256k1(_) => "secp256k1", // correct
};
```

Otherwise this breaks Node.js compatibility for P-224/P-256/P-384 curves.

commits.message:
fix(node): support ECDSA with secp256k1 in `node:crypto`

Adds secp256k1 support for EC key generation, signing, verification,
and key import/export (DER, PEM, JWK) in the node:crypto polyfill.
Previously only ECDH was supported for secp256k1 via the k256 crate.

Closes #27523

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: rustfmt formatting

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use Node.js-compatible OpenSSL curve names in asymmetricKeyDetails

Node.js returns OpenSSL short names (prime256v1, secp384r1, secp224r1)
via OBJ_nid2sn() rather than NIST names (p256, p384, p224).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(node): preserve AsyncLocalStorage context in stream.finished callback
body:
## Summary
- Snapshots the async context when `eos()` (the implementation behind `stream.finished`) is called and restores it around the callback invocation
- In Node.js this context propagation happens automatically through the native `AsyncWrap`/`InternalCallbackScope` C++ layer (`src/api/callback.cc`), but Deno's ops don't flow through that path, so `AsyncLocalStorage.getStore()` returned `undefined` inside `stream.finished` callbacks
- Adds a unit test verifying the fix with an HTTP server scenario matching the issue reproduction

Closes #32327

## Test plan
- [x] Added `asyncLocalStoragePreservedInStreamFinished` unit test in `tests/unit_node/async_hooks_test.ts`
- [x] Verified the exact PoC from the issue now prints `OK: got expected value`
- [x] Existing `unit_node::async_hooks_test` suite passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(node): preserve `AsyncLocalStorage` context in `stream.finished` callback

Snapshot the async context when `eos()` is called and restore it around
the callback invocation so that `AsyncLocalStorage.getStore()` returns the
expected value inside `stream.finished` callbacks.

Node.js propagates async context automatically through its native
`AsyncWrap`/`InternalCallbackScope` C++ layer, but Deno's ops don't flow
through that path, so the context was lost by the time the stream emitted
`close`/`finish`/`end`.

Closes #32327

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(core): store Global<Context> ptr for libuv-compat callbacks
body:
## Summary

- Store a `v8::Global<Context>` via `Global::into_raw()` once during `register_uv_loop` instead of storing a `v8::Local<Context>` pointer at the start of each event loop tick
- V8 keeps the persistent-handle slot updated across GC cycles, so the pointer remains valid for the lifetime of the runtime
- The raw Global is reconstructed and properly dropped in `JsRealmInner::destroy`
- Remove the per-tick `transmute` + `const` size assert that was previously needed

Ported from https://github.com/denoland/deno_core/pull/1315

Original author: @littledivy

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p deno_core` passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(core): store Global<Context> ptr for libuv-compat callbacks

Instead of storing a `v8::Local<Context>` pointer in `loop_.data` at
the start of each event loop tick (which is only valid for that tick),
store a `v8::Global<Context>` via `Global::into_raw()` once during
`register_uv_loop`. V8 keeps the persistent-handle slot updated across
GC cycles, so the pointer remains valid for the lifetime of the runtime.
The raw Global is reconstructed and dropped in `JsRealmInner::destroy`.

Ported from https://github.com/denoland/deno_core/pull/1315

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): support process.stdout resize events
body:
## Summary

- Add Windows support for `Deno.addSignalListener("SIGWINCH", ...)` by polling console size changes every 250ms using `GetConsoleScreenBufferInfo`
- Wire `process.stdout.on("resize")` / `process.stderr.on("resize")` to emit on SIGWINCH, matching Node.js behavior
- SIGWINCH signal registration is lazy -- only created when a "resize" listener is actually added to a TTY stream, and removed when the last listener is removed. This avoids a persistent pending op that interferes with deno_core's stalled TLA detection.

The code here is rough in some places, it will get significantly simpler once we get proper support for TTY and `net.Socket`.

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/26671
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
chore: upgrade rustyline 13→17 and nix 0.27→0.30

Upgrade rustyline to v17.0.0 (with custom-bindings feature) and
rustyline-derive to v0.11.1. Upgrade nix to v0.30.1 to align with
rustyline 17's dependency. This is needed so rustyline no longer
swallows SIGWINCH, enabling process.stdout resize events.

API changes:
- highlight_char: bool → CmdKind parameter
- set_keyseq_timeout: i32 → Option<i32>
- ReadlineError: removed WindowResized/Decode/SystemError variants
- nix fcntl/fchown: now take AsFd instead of raw fd

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(ext/node): support `process.stdout.on("resize")` via SIGWINCH

Wire SIGWINCH signal to emit "resize" events on process.stdout and
process.stderr, matching Node.js behavior. When a terminal is resized,
the SIGWINCH signal triggers process.stdout.emit("resize") and
process.stderr.emit("resize") for TTY streams.

Also adds WriteStream._refreshSize() to node:tty for Node.js compat.

On Windows, SIGWINCH is not a real signal so the listener is silently
ignored (process.on filters it out). This matches Node.js behavior
where SIGWINCH on Windows is only delivered in specific scenarios
handled by libuv.

Closes #26671

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat(ext/signals): support SIGWINCH on Windows via console resize polling

Add Windows support for `Deno.addSignalListener("SIGWINCH", ...)` and
`process.stdout.on("resize")` by polling console size changes every 250ms
using `GetConsoleScreenBufferInfo`. The polling thread starts lazily when
the first SIGWINCH handler is registered.

- Add SIGWINCH (28) to Windows signal dictionary
- Add background polling thread that detects console resize on Windows
- Allow SIGWINCH through the Node.js process signal filters on Windows
- Update tests and error messages accordingly

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore: add safety comments to fix clippy undocumented_unsafe_blocks

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into feat/windows-sigwinch-polling

fix(ext/node): make SIGWINCH→resize wiring lazy to prevent hanging tests

The previous implementation unconditionally registered
process.on("SIGWINCH") during node bootstrap for every Deno process.
This created a persistent op_signal_poll op that, while unrefed,
was still counted by deno_core's stalled TLA detection
(has_pending_ops includes unrefed ops), preventing the runtime from
detecting unresolvable top-level awaits and causing processes to hang.

Fix by making the SIGWINCH registration lazy:
- Remove the unconditional process.on("SIGWINCH") from bootstrap
- In createWritableStdioStream (streams.mjs), patch on/off to lazily
  register/unregister SIGWINCH via Deno.addSignalListener when "resize"
  listeners are added/removed on TTY stdout/stderr
- In tty.WriteStream, add the same lazy pattern for direct WriteStream
  usage
- Track previous terminal size explicitly in streams.mjs since the
  columns/rows getters dynamically call Deno.consoleSize()

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(install): support `--node-modules-dir` for `-g` and use a manual node_modules dir for installing `npm:` specifiers
body:
## Summary

Create a persistent `.<name>/` config directory under the install bin dir (e.g., `~/.deno/bin/.cowsay/`) for every `deno install -g` command. For npm packages, this directory also contains `package.json` and `node_modules/`, enabling lifecycle scripts to run during global install.

### Behaviour

| Scenario | `nodeModulesDir` set? |
|---|---|
| `deno install -g npm:cowsay` | Yes (`manual`) |
| `deno install -g --allow-scripts jsr:@std/http/file-server` | Yes (`manual`) |
| `deno install -g --node-modules-dir=manual jsr:@std/http/file-server` | Yes (`manual`) |
| `deno install -g jsr:@std/http/file-server` | No |
| `deno install -g http://example.com/script.ts` | No |

title: test: add timeout support to spec tests
body:
## Summary

- Adds a `timeout` field (in seconds) to spec test `__test__.jsonc` files
- When a test step's process exceeds the timeout, it is killed and the test fails with a "Test command timed out" panic
- Timeout can be set at the multi-test level (propagates to all contained tests) or per individual test
- No default timeout — the watchdog thread is only spawned when `timeout` is explicitly configured
- Reduces CI test job timeout from 240 minutes to 30 minutes

Example usage in `__test__.jsonc`:
```jsonc
{
  "timeout": 60,
  "args": "run long_running.ts",
  "output": "expected.out"
}
```
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

feat(tests): add timeout support to spec tests

Adds a configurable `timeout` field (in seconds) to spec tests, defaulting
to 300 seconds (5 minutes). When a test step exceeds the timeout, its
process is killed and the test fails with a clear "timed out" message.

The timeout can be set at the multi-test level (propagated to all contained
tests) or per individual test in `__test__.jsonc`.

title: fix(ext/node): enable test-stdio-closed tests
body:
- Fix `child_process` shell command translation to handle shell redirections (`1>&-`, `< file`), piped commands (`cmd1 | cmd2`), and shell variable prefixes (`"$NODE"`, `${NODE}`). Previously shell operators were passed to the CLI arg translator, causing parse failures or incorrect output.
- Remove duplicate `transformDenoShellCommand` call from `normalizeSpawnArguments` — it was already called in `buildCommand`, and the double transformation corrupted POSIX `'\''` quoting.
- Add `wrap_eval` parameter to `op_node_translate_cli_args` so shell command contexts skip eval code wrapping (which introduces metacharacters unsafe for shell embedding).
- Map `BrokenPipe` to `EPIPE` (was incorrectly mapped to `EBADF`) in `stream_wrap`, and catch `BrokenPipe` in `process.stdout`/ `process.stderr` `writeSync` to emit it as an async error event.
- Add minimal `_handle` with `ref()`/`unref()`/`close()` to PIPE stdin. Intentionally omits `readStart`/`readStop`/`reading` — adding those resets `_readableState.reading`, which triggers duplicate `_read()` calls with orphaned reffed promises that prevent process exit. 
- Ref `io.stdin` in `_read()` and unref on pause when no `readStop` exists, matching Node.js event loop ref-counting behavior.
- Add platform-specific quoting in `transformDenoShellCommand`: POSIX uses single quotes with `'\''` escaping, Windows uses double quotes with `\\` doubling before `"`.
- Add Windows `cmd.exe` detection (`/d /s /c` pattern) in `buildCommand` for shell command transformation.
- Deduplicate `--unstable-*` flags in `fork()` between CLI translator and bootstrap args.

Enables 3 new node_compat tests:
  - `test-stdio-closed.js`
  - `test-stdout-close-catch.js`
  - `test-stdout-close-unref.js`


Needs: 
- [x] https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/32086
- [x] https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/32087
author: fraidev

make test-stdio-closed pass on Windows

When a non-deno process (e.g. Python) is spawned with Deno's execPath in
its args, set DENO_NODE_COMPAT=1 in the child's environment. This env var
is then read by cli/args/flags.rs to enable allow_all, bare_node_builtins,
and detect_cjs for bare-run scripts, allowing the child deno process to use
require() and other Node.js compat features even when invoked directly
without the usual node compat flags.

This fixes test-stdio-closed.js on Windows, where the test spawns Python
which closes fd 0/1/2 and then calls deno directly (bypassing child_process
arg translation), causing require() to be undefined and exit code 1.

Revert "make test-stdio-closed pass on Windows"

title: fix(audit): gracefully handle malformed response
body:
Fixes an issue where `deno audit` fails to deserialize npm registry responses when the `actions` field is missing.

## Changes Made

- Added `#[serde(default)]` attribute to the `actions` field in `AuditResponse` struct in `cli/tools/pm/audit.rs` - this makes the field default to an empty `Vec` when missing from the JSON response
- Added a unit test to verify the deserialization works when `actions` field is missing

## Fix Details

The issue was that the npm registry audit API response sometimes doesn't include the `actions` field. The Deno audit code was expecting this field to always be present, causing deserialization to fail with: `missing field 'actions' at line 1 column 33`

The fix adds `#[serde(default)]` to the `actions` field, which makes it default to an empty vector when the field is not present in the JSON response. This is the standard serde pattern for optional fields that should have a sensible default value.

body:
Closes #31793

## Summary
- Add deno create as a first-class CLI subcommand
- Route it to the existing init package path (`InitFlags.package`)
- Keep npm-only behavior for `create` (no JSR support yet).
- Keep `--npm` support (similar to `deno init`) while also supporting explicit `npm:` package input.
- Fix npm-prefixed normalization to avoid `npm:create-npm:*`

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo check -p deno` passes
- [x] `cargo test -p deno create --lib` passes
- [x] `cargo test -p deno npm_name_to_create_package_test --lib` passes

## Manual check
- `deno create --yes vite`
Parser error: Missing `npm:` prefix. For example: `deno create npm:vite`.
- `deno create --yes --npm vite`
Resolves to `create-vite`.
- `deno create --yes npm:vite`
Resolves to `create-vite`.
- `deno create --yes npm:vite my-project`
Parser error (missing `--` before package args).
- `deno create --yes npm:vite -- my-project`
Resolves to `create-vite` with forwarded arg `my-project`.
- `deno create --yes jsr:@std/http`
Parser error: JSR packages are not supported by `deno create` yet. Use npm: or --npm.
- `deno create --yes --jsr @std/http`
Parser error: unexpected argument `--jsr`.
- `deno create --yes npm:`
Parser error: Missing package name after `'npm:'` prefix.

title: fix(ext/node): preserve streaming body on stale keepalive retry
body:
Streaming writes (e.g. pipeline, node-fetch) bypass outputData and go directly to _bodyWriter, so they were lost when a stale keepalive connection triggered a retry. This caused timeouts for libraries like node-fetch and gaxios that use pipeline() to send request bodies.

Capture body data written via _writeRaw() on reused sockets so it can be restored to outputData on retry, and re-call end() to set up body writer close logic for the new attempt.


Related: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32006#issuecomment-3916719816

title: fix(ext/node): add worker_threads stdin support
body:
## Summary

- Adds `worker.stdin` support when `{ stdin: true }` is passed to the `Worker` constructor
- Parent creates a `Writable` stream (`worker.stdin`) that sends `WORKER_STDIN` / `WORKER_STDIN_END` messages to the worker via `postMessage`
- Worker replaces `process.stdin` with a `Readable` that receives data from those messages, using an early `addEventListener` with `stopImmediatePropagation()` to hide internal messages from user handlers
- Fixes stdout/stderr forwarding to preserve binary data (passes `chunk` directly instead of `String(chunk)`)
- Enables 3 new passing node_compat tests: `test-worker-stdio.js`, `test-worker-stdio-flush.js`, `test-worker-stdio-flush-inflight.js`

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter "test-worker-stdio"` — 3 new tests pass
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter "test-worker-no-stdin-stdout-interaction"` — existing test still passes
- [x] `tools/lint.js` passes
- [x] `tools/format.js` passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

title: fix(ext/node): make worker_threads ref/unref idempotent like Node.js
body:
Closes #31871

## Summary
- Changes `Worker.ref()`/`Worker.unref()` from a reference counter to an idempotent boolean toggle, matching Node.js semantics
- In Node.js, `ref()`/`unref()` are idempotent: calling `ref()` multiple times is the same as calling it once, and a single `unref()` is sufficient to allow the process to exit
- Deno was using a counter (`#refCount`) that started at 1 and incremented/decremented on each `ref()`/`unref()` call. Libraries like [comlink](https://github.com/GoogleChromeLabs/comlink) call `ref()` for each pending RPC request but only `unref()` once when all pending requests complete — this left the counter elevated and prevented the process from exiting

## Test plan
- [x] Verified with the [comlink reproduction repo](https://github.com/ignatij/deno-vitest-4-compatibility-issue) — process exits correctly after single `unref()` call
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-worker-ref-onexit` (passes)
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-worker-terminate-unrefed` (passes)
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-worker-unref-from-message` (passes)
- [x] `tools/lint.js` and `tools/format.js` pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

title: fix(ext/node): forward stdout from worker_threads to parent
body:
Closes #31889

## Summary
- Adds `stdout` and `stderr` as `Readable` streams on the `Worker` class, and `stdin` (null by default), matching the Node.js API surface
- Forwards `process.stdout.write()` and `process.stderr.write()` from worker threads to the parent via internal messages, so `worker.stdout`/`worker.stderr` emit data and support `.pipe()`
- Closes stdio streams with `push(null)` when the worker exits
- Fixes `execArgv` validation to skip non-flag arguments (e.g. `"node"` and `"development"` in `--conditions node --conditions development`)
- Enables `test-worker-no-stdin-stdout-interaction.js` node_compat test

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-worker-no-stdin-stdout-interaction.js`
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-worker-execargv` (no regression)
- [x] Vitest 4 threads pool works end-to-end with [reproduction repo](https://github.com/ignatij/deno-vitest-4-compatibility-issue)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: fix(ext/node): validate execArgv flags in worker_threads instead of rejecting all
body:
## Summary

- Validate `execArgv` flags against `process.allowedNodeEnvironmentFlags` and
  a set of worker-disallowed per-process flags, instead of rejecting all
  non-empty `execArgv` arrays
- Pass `execArgv` through worker metadata so `process.execArgv` is set
  correctly inside the worker
- Set `process.traceProcessWarnings` when `--trace-warnings` is present in
  `execArgv`
- Add `worker.stderr` forwarding from worker to parent via internal messages

title: fix(ext/web): prevent GC of AbortSignal.any()
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32095

Introduced with V8 bump https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/31873

The new exposed `AbortSignal.any()`, stores dependent signals via `WeakRef` in the source signal's `dependentSignals` set. When the dependent signal is created as a temporary (e.g. `AbortSignal.any([...]).addEventListener("abort", cb)`), there are no strong references to it. V8's GC can collect it, destroying the abort listener and leaving promises unresolvable.

The fix stores a strong reference to dependent signals on the source signal (via an `activeDependents` set) whenever `addEventListener` refs a source timer. The strong reference is cleaned up in `removeEventListener` and `runAbortSteps`.

title: fix(ext/napi): don't mark accessor properties as read-only in napi_define_class
body:
## Summary

- In `napi_define_class`, accessor properties (getter/setter) were incorrectly marked `READ_ONLY` when `napi_writable` was not set in the property attributes. Per the Node-API spec, `napi_writable` only applies to data properties — for accessor properties, writability is determined by the presence of a setter.
- Removed the incorrect `READ_ONLY` flag application for accessor properties in `napi_define_class`.
- Added a test class (`NapiAccessorObject`) with getter/setter properties using `napi_default` attributes to verify the fix.

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32100

## Test plan

- [x] New test `"napi class accessor property is writable"` verifies assignment through setter works on accessor properties defined with `napi_default` attributes
- [x] All existing NAPI tests pass (49 passed, 0 failed)

title: fix(ext/node): set exitCode/signalCode correctly for killed child processes
body:
## Summary
- When a child process was killed by a signal (e.g., SIGTERM from a timeout), `exitCode` was unconditionally set to the raw OS exit code (e.g., 143 for SIGTERM) instead of `null`
- In Node.js, `exitCode` and `signalCode` are mutually exclusive — when a process dies from a signal, `exitCode` is `null` and `signalCode` is the signal name
- The fix properly decomposes the process status in `ChildProcess`, also incorporating `status.signal` from Deno's `CommandStatus` to handle externally-killed processes
- Enables `test-child-process-exec-timeout-expire` and `test-child-process-exec-timeout-kill` node_compat tests

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-child-process-exec-timeout-expire` passes
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-child-process-exec-timeout-kill` passes
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter test-child-process-exec-timeout-not-expired` still passes
- [x] Other enabled child_process node_compat tests verified (exit-code, exec-kill-throws, detached, spawn-event, flush-stdio)

title: ci: add detailed Node compat test insights as Slack thread reply
body:
## Summary

- After posting the daily node compat summary message to Slack, posts a **threaded reply** with per-test insights:
  - **Newly passing tests** — tests that were failing in the previous run but pass today (with affected OSes)
  - **Newly failing tests** — tests that were passing in the previous run but fail today (with affected OSes)
  - **Flaky tests** — tests that both passed and failed at least 2 times each over the last 15 daily runs (with per-OS pass ratios)
- Fetches full daily reports from GCS (`dl.deno.land`) to compare per-test results across all 3 OSes (linux, windows, darwin)
- Historical reports for the last 15 days are fetched in parallel for flaky detection
- Each section is capped (30/30/20 items) to stay within Slack block size limits
- Thread posting failures are caught separately so the main summary message is never affected

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify the workflow runs successfully on the next scheduled run
- [ ] Check that the Slack thread reply appears under the daily summary message
- [ ] Verify newly passing/failing detection is correct by comparing with the full report viewer
- [ ] Verify flaky test detection surfaces known flaky tests

title: fix(ext/node): unref child_process stdio streams by default
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32064

PR #31975 made child process stdio streams into Socket instances backed by Pipe → StreamResource. When libraries like esbuild call `child.unref()` / `stdout.unref()` to signal the event loop should be free to exit, the call chain was Socket.unref() → Pipe.unref() → only unrefs `#listener` (null for non-server pipes) → StreamResource never gets unref'd → process hangs.

With this fix, Pipe.unref() also propagates to the stream in `[kStreamBaseField]`, so esbuild's unref calls reach the StreamResource and the event loop can exit properly.

When using `DENO_UNSTABLE_CONTROL_SOCK`, the env variables of the deno process might be changed using the `Start` socket message. In this scenario, if the `DENO_UNSTABLE_CRON_SOCK` env var value changes (or is newly set)  the handler must be reloaded. 

The reason not to use `lazy_init` in this case is that it could be preferable in some cases to eagerly connect to the cron socket before (or during) the deferred configuration of the runtime.

title: feat: External Socket-Based Cron Implementation
body:
Summary

 This PR adds support for delegating cron job management to an external service via socket communication. When the `DENO_UNSTABLE_CRON_SOCK` environment variable is set, Deno will connect
 to an external cron service instead of using the built-in local cron scheduler.

 This enables use cases where:
 - Cron scheduling needs to be centralized across multiple Deno instances
 - External systems need to control when cron jobs execute
 - Advanced scheduling features are required beyond Deno's built-in capabilities

 Key Features

 1. Socket-Based Cron Handler (ext/cron/socket.rs)

 A new SocketCronHandler that communicates with external cron services via sockets:

 Socket Types Supported:
 - TCP: tcp:<host>:<port> (e.g., tcp:localhost:9000)
 - Unix Domain Sockets: unix:<path> (e.g., unix:/var/run/cron.sock)
 - Vsock (Linux/macOS/Android): vsock:<cid>:<port> (e.g., vsock:-1:9000)

 Protocol:
 - Outbound messages (Deno → External Service):
   - `{"kind":"register","crons":[{"name":"...","schedule":"...","backoff_schedule":[...]}]}`
   - `{"kind":"result","name":"...","success":true/false}`
 - Inbound messages (External Service → Deno):
   - `{"kind":"invoke","name":"...", "traceparent": "..."}`
   - `{"kind":"reject-new-crons", "reason": "..."}`

 2. Handler Abstraction (ext/cron/handler_impl.rs)

 Introduced a unified CronHandlerImpl enum that switches between:
 - LocalCronHandler - Built-in local scheduler (default)
 - SocketCronHandler - External socket-based scheduler

 Selection is automatic based on the DENO_UNSTABLE_CRON_SOCK environment variable.
author: arnauorriols

title: fix: enable edns for Deno.resolveDns
body:
Currently when using `Deno.resolveDns` on a domain with many (large) records **and** using a DNS server that allows large replies over UDP, deno times out with the following error:

```ts
> await Deno.resolveDns('large-txt-records.0x76.dev', 'TXT')
dropped malformed message waiting for id: 23755 err: rdata length too large for remaining bytes, need: 1105 remain: 430
dropped malformed message waiting for id: 60217 err: rdata length too large for remaining bytes, need: 1105 remain: 430
dropped malformed message waiting for id: 53517 err: rdata length too large for remaining bytes, need: 1105 remain: 430
Uncaught TimedOut: proto error: request timed out
    at async Object.resolveDns (ext:deno_net/01_net.js:85:17)
    at async <anonymous>:1:22
```

This PR addresses that issue by enabling edns on the resolver, which includes support for larger UDP dns responses.

title: fix(ext/node): retry stale keepAlive connections in http client
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/31891

When a server closes an idle keepAlive connection, the client's HTTP agent may still have the socket pooled. 
Attempting to reuse this stale socket results in ECONNRESET ("socket hang up"). 
This matches Node.js behavior by transparently retrying the request on a fresh connection when a reused socket encounters a stale connection error.
author: fraidev

title: feat: add --inspect-publish-uid flag for VSCode debugging
body:
This commit adds `--inspect-publish-uid` flag.

This flag is hidden in the help output at the moment. This flag is getting
added because it allows us to properly implement certain debugging
workflows in VSCode. More concretely, the --inspect-publish-uid flag
is used by VSCode when running a debugger for Node and we would
like VSCode to use the same infrastructure for Node and Deno.

title: refactor: move inspector server implementation to separate crate, make server a global
body:
This commit moves the implementation of `InspectorServer` to 
a new `deno_inspector_server` crate, this change is required because
this struct is used in both `runtime/` and `ext/node/` crate which
requires to have a crate that both can use.

Additionally the inspector server has been made a singleton global that
can be created and retrieved by all crates using it, which is a prerequisite
for implementing https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/31863.
author: bartlomieju

title: feat(cli/tsc): show progress bar for TSGO downloads
body:
Fixes #31884 

## Problem
When using `DENO_UNSTABLE_TSGO=1 deno check`, users receive no feedback during the TypeScript Go compiler download, making them think the process is stuck or frozen.

## Solution
Added two console messages in `cli/tsc/go/setup.rs`:
- "Downloading TypeScript Go compiler..." displayed before the download starts
- "TypeScript Go compiler downloaded successfully." displayed after successful completion

These messages provide clear feedback to users about the download progress without being overly verbose.

## Changes
- Modified `cli/tsc/go/setup.rs` to add `eprintln!` messages around the download process
- Messages are displayed to stderr to avoid interfering with command output
- No functional changes to the download logic itself

## Testing
The changes compile successfully and preserve existing functionality while improving user experience for the experimental TSGO feature.

title: fix(ext/node): implement getValidStdio for child_process
body:
Implements the internal `getValidStdio` function used by Node.js's child_process module to validate stdio configurations. This function:

  - Validates stdio is a string ('ignore', 'pipe', 'inherit', 'overlapped') or array
  - Expands arrays to at least 3 elements
  - Converts stream objects (like process.stdin) to fd descriptors
  - Tracks IPC channel positions
  - Throws appropriate errors for invalid configurations

 Also, enables test-child-process-validate-stdio.js in node_compat.

title: fix(node/child_process): reject null bytes in arguments
body:
Add validation to reject null bytes (\0) in child_process arguments, matching Node.js behavior. Null bytes in command arguments can cause security issues as they may truncate strings at the OS level.

Validates null bytes in:
  - file/command argument
  - args array elements
  - options.cwd, argv0, shell, env keys/values
  - fork options: modulePath, execPath, execArgv

Also fixes execFile to pass through argv0 option to spawn.

title: fix(ext/node): use correct block sizes for HMAC algorithms
body:
The HMAC implementation was hardcoded to use 64-byte blocks for most algorithms and 128-byte blocks only for sha512/sha384. This is incorrect for many algorithms like sha3-* variants and blake2 which have different block sizes.

Add a lookup table with correct block sizes for all supported hash algorithms and throw an error for unsupported algorithms.

Fixes #31765

title: fix(ext/node): handle errors in sqlite session filter callback and ad…
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/31635

Fixes two issues in the `node:sqlite` module:

 1. The filter handler in `applyChangeset` was panicking when the JavaScript filter callback threw an exception. The code used `.unwrap()` on the result of `filter.call()`, which returns `None` when an exception is thrown. Added proper exception handling with `tc_scope` and a `has_caught` flag to preserve the first exception.

 2. The Session class was missing `Symbol.dispose` support, causing the "session supports ERM" test to fail when using the `using` keyword. Added `[Symbol.dispose]` to Session.prototype.

  - Error catching in callbacks that routes errors to the active domain

title: fix(install): wait for package installations before applying patches
body:
## Overview

In the current latest version of Deno (2.6.3), there is an issue where running the `deno install` command causes patched packages specified in the `links` property of `deno.json` to be overwritten with npm package sources. To reproduce this issue, first remove all node_modules directories using a command like `rm -r node_modules **/node_modules`, then run `deno install`.

With the following configuration, the files under `./patches/@deno/vite-plugin` are overwritten with the source code from npm:

```json
{
  "links": ["./patches/@deno/vite-plugin"]
}
```

This occurs because patch files are cloned using hard links.

Previously, only `./patches/@deno/vite-plugin/package.json` was being modified. This was because `clone_dir_recursive_except_node_modules_child` was calling `clone_dir_recursive` instead of itself during recursion, resulting in simple copies rather than hard links. As a result, the overwriting occurred silently and was difficult to notice. Since the intended behavior appears to be using hard links to allow direct modifications to the original files, I have updated the function to call itself recursively.

Additionally, we have added a regression test to check that files in the existing integrations tests (under `/tests/specs`) are not overwritten. I added it by looking at other tests, but please let me know if there is a more desirable testing method.


## Related issue

I couldn't find it, but if it was there, I'm sorry.

title: fix(ext/telemetry): otel baggage propagation
body:
There were three bugs in baggage propagation:

- `BaggageImpl` was not reading items from the map passsed in, instead always creating an empty baggage
- `Deno.serve` was reading out baggage and storing it in a context, but then not starting the callback in that context, but in the parent context, meaning the user did not have access to the baggage.
- The baggage was stored on the context with the wrong key.

The gist of it is that `propagation.getActiveBaggage()` works now.

Secondly, this adds a composite propagator into the global OTEL context, so that `propagation.extract()` and `propagation.inject()` start working.

Fixes #28085 and fixes #30064, towards #28082

answer: UTF-8
question: what if pointer points to `D0` `00` bytes (invalid UTF-8)?
