title: perf(ext/web): use simdutf for base64 encode/decode
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR improves base64 encode/decode performance across `deno_web` and the Node.js `Buffer` polyfill by switching from the standalone `base64-simd` crate to V8’s bundled `simdutf` base64 implementation (via the `v8` crate), and by tightening the Rust↔JS call path for hot operations.

**Changes:**
- Re-implement `deno_web` base64 ops using `v8::simdutf`, including optimized buffer allocation patterns and external V8 string creation for large outputs.
- Update the Node `Buffer` polyfill to call the base64 ops directly (instead of going through JS wrapper helpers).
- Bump the `v8` crate and enable the `simdutf` feature; remove `base64-simd` from `ext/web` dependencies.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `ext/web/lib.rs` | Switch base64 encode/decode ops to `v8::simdutf`; add external-string path for large base64 encodes. |
| `ext/web/Cargo.toml` | Drop `base64-simd` dependency from `deno_web`. |
| `ext/node/polyfills/internal/buffer.mjs` | Route base64 Buffer operations directly to core ops for lower overhead. |
| `Cargo.toml` | Upgrade `v8` crate and enable `simdutf` feature flag. |
| `Cargo.lock` | Lockfile updates reflecting `v8` bump and dependency graph changes. |
</details>



<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (1)</summary>

**ext/node/polyfills/internal/buffer.mjs:978**
* The comment says `op_base64_encode_from_buffer` uses `v8::String::new_external_onebyte`, but the Rust implementation only uses external strings when the *base64 output* exceeds 8KB; smaller outputs still go through the normal one-byte string path (copying into V8). Consider adjusting the comment to reflect the actual threshold/behavior so it doesn’t mislead future performance work.
```
    throw new codes.ERR_BUFFER_OUT_OF_BOUNDS("length");
  }

  return blit

commits.message:
perf(ext/web): use simdutf for base64 encode/decode

Replace base64-simd crate with V8's bundled simdutf library for base64
operations. This avoids potential C++ symbol clashes and provides
significant performance improvements, especially for decode and write
operations (2-3x faster than canary at large sizes).

Update v8 crate to 146.7.0 and enable the simdutf feature. Remove
base64-simd dependency from ext/web.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

perf(ext/web): use external strings for large base64 encode

Use v8::String::new_external_onebyte for base64 output >8KB to avoid
copying encoded data into V8's heap. V8 takes ownership of the buffer
and frees it on GC, matching Node.js's approach.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

perf(ext/web): avoid zeroing buffers in base64 encode/decode

Use MaybeUninit for stack buffers and Vec::with_capacity for heap
buffers to avoid unnecessary zeroing before simdutf overwrites them.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

perf(ext/node): call base64 ops directly from buffer.mjs

Remove indirection through forgivingBase64Encode/Decode/etc wrapper
functions in 00_infra.js and call ops directly from buffer.mjs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Merge remote-tracking branch 'upstream/main' into perf/base64-simdutf

# Conflicts:
#	Cargo.lock
#	Cargo.toml
Merge main into perf/base64-simdutf, update v8 to 146.9.0

address review: deduplicate maximal_binary_length call, consolidate imports

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: avoid UB from creating &mut [u8] over uninitialized memory

Address review feedback: creating `&mut [u8]` from uninitialized memory
(via `from_raw_parts_mut` on `Vec::as_mut_ptr()` or `MaybeUninit`) is
undefined behavior even if the consumer only writes.

Fix by re-declaring simdutf FFI functions directly with raw pointer
signatures (`*mut u8` instead of `&mut [u8]`), so we never need to
construct a reference to uninitialized memory. The wrapper
`simdutf_base64_encode` now takes `*mut u8` + length, and
`simdutf_base64_decode_to_vec` calls the FFI function directly.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: format ext/web/lib.rs

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): set fd property on TTY ReadStream and WriteStream
body:
## Summary

- Set `this.fd = fd` in tty `WriteStream` and `ReadStream` constructors
- When stdout/stderr is a TTY, `process.stdout.fd` was `undefined` because the TTY code path in `process.ts` creates `TTYWriteStream` directly, bypassing `createWritableStdioStream` which sets `stream.fd`
- In Node.js, `createWritableStdioStream` (in `lib/internal/bootstrap/switches/is_main_thread.js:107`) always sets `stream.fd` after constructing the stream, regardless of stream type
- This broke `fs.writeSync(process.stdout.fd, data)` used by `@google/gemini-cli` for terminal capability detection

## Test plan

instances in the bootstrap function, matching how Node.js sets stream.fd
in lib/internal/bootstrap/switches/is_main_thread.js.

set it. This broke fs.writeSync(process.stdout.fd, data) used by
@google/gemini-cli for terminal capability detection.

title: fix(ext/node): child_process improvements (internalMessage, argv0, DEP0190)
body:
## Summary

Three child_process Node.js compatibility fixes:

### 1. Emit `internalMessage` event for `NODE_` prefixed IPC messages

In Node.js, IPC messages with a `cmd` property starting with `NODE_` are internal messages emitted as `"internalMessage"` instead of `"message"`. Deno was silently dropping these messages.

### 2. Support `argv0` option in `spawn`/`spawnSync`

Pass `argv0` through a new internal `kArgv0` symbol down to the Rust op, which uses `std::os::unix::process::CommandExt::arg0()` to set `argv[0]` at the OS level via `execve`. This matches how Node.js handles it through libuv.

### 3. Emit DEP0190 warning for shell spawn with args

Emit a `DeprecationWarning` (DEP0190) when `child_process.spawn()` is called with both args and `shell: true`, matching Node.js behavior.

## Test plan

- [x] `./x test-compat test-child-process-internal.js` passes
- [x] `./x test-compat test-child-process-spawn-argv0.js` passes
- [x] `./x test-compat test-child-process-spawn-shell.js` passes
- [x] Format and lint clean

author: bartlomieju

as "internalMessage" with proper prototype restoration.

Enables parallel/test-child-process-internal.js compat test.

title: fix(npm): create shims for all bin entries during global npm install
body:
Fixes #26133

When running `deno install -g npm:pyright`, only the first bin entry (`pyright`) got a shim. The second entry (`pyright-langserver`) was missing. This affects any npm package with multiple `bin` entries in `package.json`.

### Changes

- **Name resolution for multi-bin packages** — `resolve_name_from_npm_package_info()` now returns the bin name matching the package name (or unscoped name for `@scope/pkg`) when multiple entries exist, instead of returning `None`
- **Extra bin entry resolution** — `resolve_all_bin_entries_from_npm()` on `BinNameResolver` returns all `(name, script_path)` pairs; `BinaryNameAndUrl::resolve()` returns extra entries with proper `npm:pkg@version/script_path` URLs
- **Shared config directory** — Extra bin shims reference the primary entry's config dir via a `config_name` field on `BinaryNameAndUrl`, so all shims from the same package share one `.pkg/` config directory with `deno.json`, `package.json`, and `node_modules`
- **Install** — `install_global()` creates shims for all entries and persists extra names to `extra_bin_entries.json` in the config dir
- **Rollback on failure** — If an extra bin shim fails to install (e.g., name conflict without `-f`), the primary shim and any already-created extra shims are cleaned up to avoid partial installs
- **Uninstall** — `uninstall()` reads `extra_bin_entries.json` and removes all associated shims
- **Skipped when `--name` is provided** — explicit naming implies single-entry intent

### Test coverage

- New `@denotest/multi-bin` test package with two bin entries
- Spec test: install creates both shims, both execute correctly, uninstall removes both
- Unit tests for multi-bin name inference and entry resolution
author: copilot-swe-agent

reviews.body:
lgtm, good fix for pyright and other multi-bin npm packages

the approach is solid:
- primary bin is inferred by matching package name (or unscoped name for `@scope/pkg`)
- extra bin entries get shims with URLs like `npm:pkg@version/script_path`
- all shims share one config dir via `config_name` field
- `extra_bin_entries.json` persisted for clean uninstall
- rollback on failure prevents partial installs
- skipped when `--name` is provided (explicit naming = single-entry intent)

nice test coverage with the `@denotest/multi-bin` package.

one minor edge case: if two packages have conflicting extra bin names (e.g., both have a `server` bin), the second install will fail. but that's probably the right behavior — users should use `--name` or uninstall the conflicting package.
Reviewed the changes. No issues found.

## Pull request overview

Fixes `deno install -g npm:<pkg>` for npm packages that expose multiple `bin` entries by generating shims for all bin entries and ensuring they share a single config directory for node_modules/config.

**Changes:**
- Extend npm bin name resolution to infer the primary bin name for multi-bin packages and expose all `(bin_name, script_path)` pairs.
- Update global install to create shims for extra bin entries and persist their names for uninstall cleanup.
- Add spec + registry fixtures for a new `@denotest/multi-bin` npm package to validate install/run/uninstall behavior across platforms.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 11 out of 11 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `cli/tools/installer/global.rs` | Creates extra shims, shares config dir via `config_name`, persists extra names for uninstall, and removes extra shims on uninstall. |
| `cli/tools/installer/bin_name_resolver.rs` | Resolves all npm bin entries and improves primary-name inference for multi-bin packages (incl. scoped packages). |
| `tests/specs/install/global/npm_multiple_bins/__test__.jsonc` | New spec test covering install, executing both shims, and uninstall verification. |
| `tests/specs/install/global/npm_multiple_bins/*.out` | Expected outputs for multi-bin install/run/uninstall steps. |
| `tests/specs/install/global/npm_multiple_bins/assert_uninstalled.js` | Ensures both shims are removed after uninstall. |
| `tests/registry/npm/@denotest/multi-bin/1.0.0/*` | Adds a fixture npm package with two bin entries used by the spec test. |
</details>

title: fix(ext/node): pass uid/gid to spawn and implement process.getgroups
body:
Two fixes for child_process uid/gid support:

1. `uid` and `gid` options were destructured but not passed to `Deno.Command` in async `spawn()` (they were already passed in `spawnSync`). Also converts `PermissionDenied` errors to `EPERM` and throws synchronously, matching Node.js behavior.
2. Implements `process.getgroups()` using libc `getgroups()` syscall, which was missing entirely.
author: fraidev

reviews.body:
The PR adds `os.getgroups()` functionality for Node.js compatibility. The implementation has a critical type safety issue where a `Vec<u32>` buffer is cast to `*mut libc::gid_t`, but `gid_t` may not be 32 bits on all Unix platforms, potentially causing memory corruption. There's also a race condition between the two `getgroups` calls and silent error handling that could hide system errors.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node/ops/os/mod.rs:269`: Type mismatch when casting u32 buffer to libc::gid_t pointer. On some Unix platforms, libc::gid_t may be a different size than u32 (e.g., u16 or u64). The code allocates Vec<u32> but casts to *mut libc::gid_t, which could cause memory corruption or incorrect values.
> Suggestion: Use Vec<libc::gid_t> for the buffer, then convert to u32 when returning: `let mut groups: Vec<libc::gid_t> = vec![0; ngroups as usize];` followed by `Ok(groups.iter().map(|&g| g as u32).collect())`

**[MEDIUM]** `ext/node/ops/os/mod.rs:265`: Race condition: The number of groups can change between the first getgroups(0, null) call and the second call with the buffer. If groups increase, the second call may fail or truncate results.
> Suggestion: Consider using a retry loop if the second call indicates more groups than expected, or allocate a larger buffer upfront.

**[LOW]** `ext/node/ops/os/mod.rs:263`: Silently returning empty vector on getgroups error hides legitimate system errors from the caller.
> Suggestion: Consider returning an error when ngroups < 0 from the first call instead of silently returning an empty vector.

Two verified findings: a critical bug in the PermissionDenied error handler that breaks the error handling flow by using `throw` instead of reassigning `e`, and a medium-severity inconsistency where `process.getgroups` is set to `undefined` on Windows instead of being deleted like other process identity methods.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node/polyfills/internal/child_process.ts:583`: PermissionDenied handler uses `throw` instead of reassigning `e`, breaking the error handling flow. When `Deno.errors.NotFound` is caught, the code reassigns `e = _createSpawnError(...)` and falls through to set up stdio streams (stdin/stdout/stderr pipes) before emitting the error asynchronously via `queueMicrotask`. The new `PermissionDenied` handler instead does `throw _createSpawnError(...)`, which skips all stdio pipe setup and the deferred error emission. This means the ChildProcess will have null stdin/stdout/stderr when uid/gid permission errors occur, and the error won't be emitted via the normal `'error'` event pathway — it will throw synchronously instead.
> Suggestion: Change `throw _createSpawnError("EPERM", command, args.slice(1));` to `e = _createSpawnError("EPERM", command, args.slice(1));` to match the pattern used for the ENOENT case, allowing the catch block to fall through and properly set up stdio streams and emit the error asynchronously.

**[MEDIUM]** `ext/node/polyfills/process.ts:859`: `process.getgroups` is set to `undefined` on Windows via a ternary, meaning `'getgroups' in process` returns `true` and `process.getgroups` is `undefined`. This is inconsistent with both Node.js (where the property doesn't exist on Windows) and the other process identity methods in this file (getgid, getuid, getegid, geteuid) which are assigned unconditionally then `delete`d on Windows around line 1044-1048.
> Suggestion: Assign `process.getgroups` unconditionally as `() => op_getgroups()`, then add `delete process.getgroups;` in the `if (isWindows)` block alongside the other deletions (ar

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): pass uid/gid to spawn and implement process.getgroups

Two fixes for child_process uid/gid support:

1. `uid` and `gid` options were destructured but not passed to
   `Deno.Command` in async `spawn()` (they were already passed in
   `spawnSync`). Also converts `PermissionDenied` errors to `EPERM`
   and throws synchronously, matching Node.js behavior.

2. Implements `process.getgroups()` using libc `getgroups()` syscall,
   which was missing entirely.
fix: throw ENOTSUP for uid/gid on Windows, fix non-ASCII comment

fix: address review comments for getgroups

- Use Vec<libc::gid_t> instead of Vec<u32> for the buffer to avoid
  type mismatch on platforms where gid_t is not u32
- Return errors instead of silently returning empty vec when
  getgroups syscall fails
- Change return type from PermissionCheckError to OsError to
  support reporting IO errors

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use consistent patterns for getgroups and spawn error handling

- Change `throw` to `e =` in PermissionDenied catch handler so the
  error flows through existing stdio setup and #_handleError path
- Assign process.getgroups unconditionally and delete on Windows,
  matching getgid/getuid/getegid/geteuid pattern
- Use EPERM instead of ENOTSUP for uid/gid on Windows

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: revert ENOTSUP back for Windows uid/gid

Node.js expects ENOTSUP on Windows per test-child-process-uid-gid.js.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: restore throw for EPERM in spawn error handler

Node.js throws EPERM synchronously from spawn() for uid/gid permission
errors (unlike ENOENT which is emitted as an error event). The throw
is needed for assert.throws() in test-child-process-uid-gid.js to work.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): sqlite prepare() options, defensive default, iterator invalidation
body:
## Summary

Fixes 3 SQLite Node.js compat tests from #32706:

- **`prepare()` options**: Support `readBigInts`, `returnArrays`, `allowBareNamedParameters`, and `allowUnknownNamedParameters` as second argument to `DatabaseSync.prepare(sql, options)`
- **Defensive mode default**: Changed from `false` to `true` to match Node.js
- **Iterator invalidation**: Calling `get()`, `all()`, `run()`, or `iterate()` on a statement now invalidates any active iterator, causing `next()` to throw `ERR_INVALID_STATE` with "iterator was invalidated"

### Enabled tests
- `test-sqlite-config.js`
- `test-sqlite-named-parameters.js`
- `test-sqlite-statement-sync.js`

## Test plan
- [x] All 3 newly enabled tests pass via `./x test-compat`
- [x] Existing sqlite compat tests still pass
- [ ] CI passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
This PR introduces iterator support for SQLite statements but has critical memory safety issues. There's a confirmed memory leak where `IteratorContext` is never freed after being allocated with `Box::into_raw()`. Additionally, the raw pointer to `StatementSync` stored in the iterator context can become dangling if the statement is garbage collected before the iterator, creating a use-after-free risk. These issues need to be addressed before merging.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node_sqlite/statement.rs:687`: Memory leak: `IteratorContext` allocated with `Box::into_raw()` is never freed. Every call to `iterate()` leaks memory since `Box::from_raw()` is never called to deallocate the context.
> Suggestion: Add cleanup logic to free the context. Options include: (1) calling `Box::from_raw()` in `iterate_return` when the iterator completes, (2) using V8's weak callback mechanism on the external to clean up when garbage collected, or (3) tracking allocated contexts in `StatementSync` and freeing them on drop.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node_sqlite/statement.rs:693`: Use-after-free risk: `IteratorContext` stores a raw pointer `*const StatementSync`, but nothing prevents the `StatementSync` from being garbage collected while the JavaScript iterator still exists. The safety comment is not enforced by the type system.
> Suggestion: Use `Rc<RefCell<StatementSync>>` or similar reference-counted wrapper instead of a raw pointer to ensure the statement outlives all iterators. Alternatively, store a weak reference and check validity before each access.

**[MEDIUM]** `ext/node_sqlite/statement.rs:275`: Integer overflow handling not explicit: `iter_generation` increments without explicit overflow handling. While practically unlikely to overflow, using wrapping arithmetic would make the intent clear and prevent potential undefined behavior in debug builds.
> Suggestion: Use `self.iter_generation = self.iter_generation.wrapping_add(1);` to make overflow behavior explicit.

## Pull request overview

This PR updates the Node.js SQLite compatibility layer to match Node 25 behavior closely enough to enable previously failing node_compat SQLite tests.

**Changes:**
- Enabled three previously-disabled Node SQLite compat tests in `tests/node_compat/config.jsonc`.
- Added `DatabaseSync.prepare(sql, options)` support for `readBigInts`, `returnArrays`, `allowBareNamedParameters`, and `allowUnknownNamedParameters`.
- Aligned SQLite defensive mode default and implemented iterator invalidation semantics for `StatementSync.iterate()` when other statement methods are called.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| tests/node_compat/config.jsonc | Re-enables the SQLite Node compat tests covered by this PR. |
| ext/node_sqlite/database.rs | Adds per-`prepare()` options parsing and changes defensive mode default to match Node. |
| ext/node_sqlite/statement.rs | Adds iterator invalidation tracking for statement iterators and related lifecycle handling. |

commits.message:
fix(node/sqlite): prepare() options, defensive default, iterator invalidation

- Support `readBigInts`, `returnArrays`, `allowBareNamedParameters`,
  and `allowUnknownNamedParameters` as options in `prepare()`
- Default defensive mode to `true` (matching Node.js)
- Implement iterator invalidation: calling `get()`, `all()`, `run()`,
  or `iterate()` on a statement invalidates any active iterator,
  causing `next()` to throw `ERR_INVALID_STATE`

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(node/sqlite): fix iterator memory leak and improve safety

- Free IteratorContext allocations by tracking them in StatementSync and
  cleaning up in iterate_return() and Drop
- Use wrapping_add for iter_generation to make overflow behavior explicit
- Improve safety comments documenting __statement_ref GC prevention

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/node-compat-sqlite

fix(ext/node_sqlite): fix iterator invalidation after exhaustion

An exhausted iterator should return { done: true, value: null } even if
the statement was subsequently reset by get/all/run/iterate. This adds a
per-iterator `finished` flag to track completion independently from the
statement's generation counter.

Also applies cargo fmt formatting fixes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(ext/node_sqlite): set finished flag in iterate_return

When an iterator is exited early via return() (e.g. break in for-of),
the per-iterator finished flag must be set so subsequent next() calls
return { done: true, value: null } instead of throwing an invalidation
error.

title: fix(npm): `approve-scripts` detects packages when lockfile is disabled
body:
## Summary
- Fix `deno approve-scripts` failing to detect packages with lifecycle scripts when `"lock": false` is set in `deno.json`
- The issue was that `approve-scripts` queried the npm resolution snapshot without first resolving packages — with no lockfile, the snapshot was empty
- Fix by calling `ensure_top_level_package_json_install()` before querying the snapshot

Closes #32781

## Test plan
- [x] Added spec test `approve_scripts_no_lock` with two cases:
  - `approve_scripts_explicit_package` — verifies explicit package approval works with `lock: false`
  - `approve_scripts_detects_packages` — verifies the no-args path finds packages (previously returned "nothing to approve")
- [x] Verified test fails without the fix (outputs "No npm packages with lifecycle scripts need approval" instead of detecting candidates)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(npm): `approve-scripts` detects packages when lockfile is disabled

When `lock: false` is set in deno.json, `deno approve-scripts` (without
args) would report "No npm packages with lifecycle scripts need approval"
even though `deno install` correctly warned about packages with scripts.

The issue was that `approve-scripts` queried the npm resolution snapshot
without first resolving packages. With no lockfile, the snapshot was empty.
Fix by calling `ensure_top_level_package_json_install()` before querying.

Closes #32781

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(ext/crypto): implement P-521 sign, verify, and ECDH derive
body:
## Summary

- Adds P-521 (secp521r1) ECDSA sign/verify and ECDH deriveBits/deriveKey to WebCrypto, completing the P-521 curve support per [W3C Web Crypto spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/WebCryptoAPI/#dfn-EcKeyGenParams)
- Key generation and import/export were already implemented; this fills in the remaining operations
- Handles short hash digests (SHA-1, SHA-256) that are smaller than P-521's `bits2field` minimum requirement by left-padding

Closes #13449
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, completes P-521 support for WebCrypto

the padding logic for short hashes is correct — P-521's `bits2field` requires at least ceil(521/2) = 261 bits ≈ 33 bytes, so SHA-1 (20 bytes) and SHA-256 (32 bytes) need left-padding.

nice that this removes 154 lines of expected failures from the WPT expectations.

one minor duplication: the prehash computation + padding logic is repeated in both sign and verify. could extract to a helper, but it's not a blocker.
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/fetch): preserve user-provided Content-Length for stream bodies

When fetch() is called with a ReadableStream body and an explicit
Content-Length header, Deno was unconditionally stripping the header and
falling back to chunked transfer encoding. This caused failures with
APIs (like GitHub's) that require Content-Length. Now the user-provided
Content-Length is preserved when Deno cannot determine the body size
automatically (i.e. for streaming bodies).

Closes #24197

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat(ext/crypto): implement P-521 sign, verify, and ECDH derive

Adds support for P-521 (secp521r1) curve operations that were previously
missing from WebCrypto: ECDSA sign/verify and ECDH deriveBits/deriveKey.
Key generation and import/export were already implemented.

- Add P521 variant to CryptoNamedCurve enum with aws_lc_rs mappings
- Add P-521 ECDSA sign/verify using p521 crate's PrehashSigner/Verifier
- Add P-521 ECDH key agreement using p521::ecdh::diffie_hellman
- Enable ecdsa and pkcs8 features on p521 dependency
- Handle short hash digests (SHA-1, SHA-256) for P-521's larger field size

Closes #13449

title: fix: make flamegraph SVG fill full browser viewport
body:
Fixes #32725

The flamegraph SVG had a fixed pixel `height` attribute, which combined
with the `viewBox` aspect ratio, caused the browser to constrain the
rendered width — leaving the right side of the viewport empty.

- Changed `height="{image_height}"` → `height="100%"` on the `<svg>` element
- Added `fg:content_height="{image_height}"` to `<g id="frames">` so the
  JS resize logic can still reference the original content height
- Updated `flamegraph.js` to read `orig_height` from the new attribute
- Added test assertions for the new attributes

fills the entire browser viewport (both width and height). Store the
original content height as fg:content_height on the frames element so
the interactive JavaScript can still reference it for layout calculations.

title: feat(ext/telemetry): copy relevant span attributes to HTTP metrics
body:
## Summary

Supersedes #28476. Reimplemented from scratch on current `main` following the same design proposed in #28445.

- Create the HTTP server OTel span in Rust and store it on `HttpRecord`, so it can be shared with JavaScript
- Expose the span to JS via `op_http_get_span` — frameworks/users can set attributes like `http.route` on it
- Before the span ends, `op_http_copy_span_attributes_to_otel_info` copies `http.route` from the span to `OtelInfo` for metrics
- Add `http.route` to the HTTP server duration histogram and other metric attributes

This enables the workflow described in #28445: a router sets `http.route` on the auto-instrumented span, and that attribute automatically appears on `http.server.request.duration` and other HTTP metrics.

### Key changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ext/telemetry/lib.rs` | Make `OtelSpan` `Clone`-able (`Rc`-wrapped), public; export `OtelSpanState`, `SpanData`, `SpanContext`, etc. |
| `ext/http/service.rs` | Create span in `handle_request`, store on `HttpRecord`, add `get_otel_span` + `copy_span_attributes_to_otel_info` methods |
| `ext/http/http_next.rs` | Add `op_http_get_span` and `op_http_copy_span_attributes_to_otel_info` ops |
| `ext/http/lib.rs` | Add `http.route` field to `OtelInfoAttributes`, include in histogram attributes |
| `ext/http/00_serve.ts` | Use Rust-created span when available, call copy op before span end |
| `ext/telemetry/telemetry.ts` | Export `Span` class and `SPAN_KEY` |

Closes #28445

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing `otel_basic` spec tests pass (HTTP metrics still work)
- [ ] Manual test: set `http.route` on span in handler, verify it appears in metric attributes
- [ ] Add spec test for `http.route` in metrics

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
feat(http): copy relevant span attributes to HTTP metrics

Allow users to set attributes like `http.route` on the automatically
instrumented HTTP server span, and have those attributes reflected in
the HTTP server metrics (duration histogram, request/response size).

The HTTP server span is now created in Rust and stored on HttpRecord,
then exposed to JavaScript via `op_http_get_span`. When the response
is finalized, `op_http_copy_span_attributes_to_otel_info` reads
`http.route` from the span and copies it to OtelInfo for metrics.

Key changes:
- Make OtelSpan cloneable (Rc-wrapped) and public
- Export OtelSpanState, SpanData, SpanContext, etc. from deno_telemetry
- Create HTTP span in Rust (service.rs handle_request)
- Add ops: op_http_get_span, op_http_copy_span_attributes_to_otel_info
- Add http.route to OtelInfoAttributes and histogram metrics
- Export Span class and SPAN_KEY from telemetry.ts

Closes #28445

title: refactor(core): move timer processing from Rust to JavaScript
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
big refactor, overall looks solid. some observations:

**architecture:**
- moving timer management to JS (linked lists + priority queue) matches Node.js and makes phase ordering easier to control
- keeping system timers (AbortSignal.timeout) on the Rust `WebTimers` path is the right call
- shared Int32Array for timer ref count is clever — avoids crossing the FFI boundary for ref/unref

**potential issues:**

1. **`processTimers` return value semantics** — returning positive for refed, negative for unrefed timers is a bit subtle. easy to get wrong. consider returning an object `{ nextExpiry, hasRefedTimers }` instead? though i understand the perf motivation.

2. **timer ID collision** — PR mentions "fixed ID collision between web timer IDs and system timer IDs" but i don't see explicit separation of ID spaces. `nextTimerId` in JS starts at 1, what's the Rust side doing? might want a comment or explicit id space partitioning (e.g., JS uses positive, Rust uses negative).

3. **`runNextTicks()` called between timers** — this matches Node.js behavior where nextTick callbacks run between timer callbacks. but it means a long-running timer callback + many nextTicks could delay other timers. probably fine, just noting it.

4. **`op_timer_schedule` magic values** — `-1` for ref, `-2` for unref is also subtle. consider named constants or an enum.

5. **`L_remove` null checks** — you check `if (item._idleNext)` before assigning `item._idleNext._idlePrev`, but the linked list is circular. if the item is in the list, these should never be null. if it's not in the list, both are null. the null checks are defensive but the pattern is slightly inconsistent with Node.js's implementation which doesn't check.

6. **test coverage** — tests pass but i notice the stats_test.ts changes removed timer depth tracking tests. make sure the new behavior is still tested somewhere.

**minor:**
- `compareTimersLists` could just use numeric subtraction for the id comparison too (unless there's a ris

lgtm — this is a solid architectural refactor aligning with Node.js timer internals

key changes:
- JS now owns timer data structures (linked lists bucketed by duration, binary min-heap priority queue) matching `lib/internal/linkedlist.js` and `lib/internal/priority_queue.js`
- Rust provides minimal `UserTimer` (single-deadline wake mechanism) via `op_timer_schedule`
- `processTimers(now)` called from Rust at event loop phase 1c, returns next expiry info
- system timers (`AbortSignal.timeout`) stay on existing Rust path

the separation is clean:
- JS manages all timer state, ID assignment, bucketing, ref counting
- Rust just says "wake me at time T" and calls `processTimers` when it fires

this sets up for Phase 2 where timers fire in correct event loop phase order

-403 lines net, nice cleanup of the Rust timer code

The PR moves timer implementation from Rust to JavaScript. The most critical issues are: (1) timer nesting depth (`__setTimerDepth`) is not restored in a `finally` block, so any throwing callback permanently corrupts the WHATWG 4ms clamping depth for all subsequent timers; (2) one-shot timer cleanup (`MapPrototypeDelete`) is outside the `try/finally`, causing memory leaks when callbacks throw; (3) several defensive checks are missing in the new timer infrastructure (destroyed timer guards, overflow handling).

**[HIGH]** `ext/web/02_timers.js:56`: Timer nesting depth (`core.__setTimerDepth(prevDepth)`) is placed after `ReflectApply` but NOT inside the `finally` block. If the setTimeout callback throws, the nesting depth is never restored, permanently corrupting the WHATWG 4ms clamping behavior for all subsequent timers in this isolate.
> Suggestion: Move `core.__setTimerDepth(prevDepth)` into the `finally` block alongside `setAsyncContext(oldContext)`.

**[HIGH]** `ext/web/02_timers.js:93`: Same nesting depth restoration bug in setInterval's wrappedCallback. If the interval callback throws, `core.__setTimerDepth(prevDepth)` is skipped, permanently corrupting timer nesting depth.
> Suggestion: Move `core.__setTimerDepth(prevDepth)` into the `finally` block.

**[HIGH]** `ext/web/02_timers.js:60`: One-shot timer cleanup (`MapPrototypeDelete(activeTimers, webTimerId)`) is placed after the `try/finally` block. If the callback throws, this line is never reached (the exception propagates out of the wrappedCallback), causing the timer entry to leak in the `activeTimers` map permanently.
> Suggestion: Move `MapPrototypeDelete(activeTimers, webTimerId)` into the `finally` block to ensure cleanup regardless of whether the callback succeeds or throws.

**[MEDIUM]** `ext/web/02_timers.js:45`: Web timer ID counter (`nextWebTimerId`) increments without bound, but `clearTimeout`/`clearInterval` use `webidl.converters.long` which converts to a 32-bit signed integer. Once `nextWebTim
lgtm
LGTM

commits.message:
refactor(core): move timer processing from Rust to JavaScript

Move user timer management (setTimeout/setInterval) from Rust's BTreeSet
to a JavaScript-side implementation matching Node.js's timer architecture:
linked lists bucketed by duration, binary min-heap priority queue, and a
single native wake mechanism.

Rust now provides only a simple single-deadline `UserTimer` ("wake me at
time T"), while JS manages all timer data structures and scheduling via
`processTimers(now)`. System timers (e.g. AbortSignal.timeout) remain on
the existing Rust `WebTimers` path.

This is Phase 1 of aligning Deno's timer architecture with Node.js to
ensure timers fire in the correct event loop phase.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(node): simplify Node timer polyfills to use core timers directly

Node's Timeout class now directly creates core timer objects via
core.createTimer() instead of going through the web timer layer
(ext/web/02_timers.js). This removes an unnecessary intermediary:

Before: Node Timeout → web setTimeout_ → core.createTimer → op_timer_schedule
After:  Node Timeout → core.createTimer → op_timer_schedule

- Timeout stores the core timer object directly (_timer field)
- ref/unref/refresh/destroy use core timer APIs (refTimer2, etc.)
- Removes dependency on ext:deno_web/02_timers.js from Node polyfills
- Async context preservation handled directly in the Timeout wrapper

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(core): remove op_timer_queue and update core_testing

op_timer_queue is no longer used in production — all user timers now go
through the JS-managed core.createTimer() path. This commit:

- Removes op_timer_queue op (Rust function + registration + JS binding)
- Updates core_testing timer module to use core.createTimer/cancelTimer2
- Adds exception reporting to processTimers so timer callback errors are
  dispatched via the uncaught exception handler (matching old behavior)
- Removes timer depth test from core_testing (depth tracking is now a
  web timer concern, not core infrastructure)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(web): convert AbortSignal.timeout to use core.createTimer

AbortSignal.timeout now uses core.createTimer() instead of
core.queueSystemTimer(), matching Node.js's approach of using a regular
unrefed timer. Also removes dead code fallbacks to old timer ops from
ext/web/02_timers.js and updates stats tests to reflect that timers are
now fully JS-managed.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(core): remove old WebTimers infrastructure

Remove the old Rust-side timer system (WebTimers, dispatch_timers,
op_timer_queue_system, op_timer_cancel, op_timer_ref, op_timer_unref)
which is now fully superseded by the JS-managed timer infrastructure.
Only the UserTimer single-deadline mechanism remains in Rust.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(core): rename timer2 APIs to drop the "2" suffix

Now that the old ID-based timer ops are removed, rename cancelTimer2,
refTimer2, unrefTimer2 to cancelTimer, refTimer, unrefTimer.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(core): restore timer leak detection and fix CI failures

- Add op_timer_track/op_timer_untrack ops so Rust can track active
  JS-managed timers for the leak sanitizer
- Add op_leak_tracing_submit calls in createTimer for stack traces
- Clean up activity traces in op_timer_untrack
- Add has_pending_timers check in TLA stall detection so unrefed
  timers don't cause false "promise never resolved" errors
- Make Timeout._timer non-enumerable to fix node_prefix_missing test
- Fix .out file whitespace (trailing spaces on ERRORS/FAILURES lines)
- Update stack trace expectations for new timer call stack

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
fmt

fix(core): restore system timer exclusion from leak sanitizer

AbortSignal.timeout() timers were being tracked by the leak sanitizer
after the timer refactor, causing false positive leaks in tests. The old
Rust timer system had a "system timer" concept (op_timer_queue_system)
that excluded these from sanitizer stats.

- Add is_system parameter to op_timer_track and skip system timers in
  stats capture
- Mark AbortSignal.timeout() timers as system timers
- Fix Socket._destroy to use node's kDestroy instead of web clearTimeout
  for cleaning up node Timeout objects (ID space mismatch after refactor)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into refactor/js-timer-processing

fix(ext/node): use process.nextTick instead of setTimeout in randomBytes

The async randomBytes callback was using globalThis.setTimeout just to
defer execution, creating a tracked timer visible to the leak sanitizer.
Use process.nextTick instead, which matches Node.js behavior (callbacks
fire via libuv, not timers) and avoids false timer leak detection.

title: fix(ext/node): improve node:dns module compatibility
body:
## Summary
- Support `--dns-result-order` flag via `NODE_OPTIONS` env var by parsing it in the node_options binding and making `dnsOrder` initialization lazy (evaluated at runtime instead of snapshot time)
- Replace `node:punycode` import with `internal/idna` in `dns/promises` to avoid spurious punycode deprecation warning
- Export `UV_ENOMEM` from uv binding

The PR changes DNS hostname resolution to use `domainToASCII` from a new internal idna module instead of `toASCII` from node:punycode. The critical concern is whether the imported module actually exists, as its creation is not shown in the diff.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node/polyfills/internal/dns/promises.ts:67`: Import references 'ext:deno_node/internal/idna.ts' which is not shown in the PR diff. If this module doesn't exist or doesn't export 'domainToASCII', the import will fail at runtime.
> Suggestion: Verify that 'ext:deno_node/internal/idna.ts' exists and exports 'domainToASCII'. If it's being added as part of this PR, ensure the file is included in the changeset.

The PR adds DNS result ordering support (`--dns-result-order` flag and `dns.setDefaultResultOrder`/`dns.getDefaultResultOrder` APIs). The findings are mostly low severity. The medium finding about potential double stack trace capture in `dnsException` warrants a closer look but is likely benign given how `hideStackFrames` works in Deno's Node compat layer. The unused imports are minor cleanup items.

**[MEDIUM]** `ext/node/polyfills/internal/errors.ts:407`: `ErrorCaptureStackTrace(ex, dnsException)` is called where `dnsException` is the result of `hideStackFrames(...)`. Depending on the Deno implementation of `hideStackFrames`, this may result in double stack trace capture or incorrect frame exclusion if `hideStackFrames` already handles `ErrorCaptureStackTrace` internally.
> Suggestion: Verify that Deno's `hideStackFrames` implementation doesn't already call `ErrorCaptureStackTrace`. If it does, this explicit call is redundant and could produce unexpected stack traces.

**[LOW]** `ext/node/polyfills/internal_binding/node_options.ts:73`: `SafeRegExp` is imported from primordials but does not appear to be used in this file.
> Suggestion: Remove the unused `SafeRegExp` import.

**[LOW]** `ext/node/polyfills/internal_binding/node_options.ts:76`: `StringPrototypeSplit` is imported from primordials but does not appear to be used in this file.
> Suggestion: Remove the unused `StringPrototypeSplit` import.

Thanks for the review! Addressing each point:

**`ext:deno_node/internal/idna.ts` import** — This module already exists at `ext/node/polyfills/internal/idna.ts` and exports `domainToASCII`. No issue here.

**`ErrorCaptureStackTrace` in `dnsException`** — This is correct as-is. Deno's `hideStackFrames` (in `internal/hide_stack_frames.ts`) only renames the function via `ObjectDefineProperty(fn, "name", ...)` — it does not call `ErrorCaptureStackTrace` itself, so there's no double capture.

**Unused `SafeRegExp` and `StringPrototypeSplit` imports** — Already removed in cfdde5f7e7.

**CI failure** — The failing test `integration::lsp::lsp_node_modules_dir` is an unrelated flaky LSP test on macos-x86_64.
LGTM

title: fix(watch): support graceful shutdown via SIGTERM dispatch
body:
## Summary

- When watch mode restarts (file change) or exits (Ctrl+C), synthetically dispatch SIGTERM to any registered signal handlers before cancelling the running operation
- If SIGTERM handlers are registered, wait up to 10 seconds for the operation to finish gracefully before force-cancelling
- If no SIGTERM handlers are registered, restart/exit happens immediately (preserves existing behavior)
- Added `deno_signals::raise()` public API to synthetically trigger signal handlers
- This enables async cleanup patterns like closing DB connections, releasing resources, and running Effect TS finalizers

Closes #30912

## Example

```ts
const interval = setInterval(() => {}, 1000);

Deno.addSignalListener("SIGTERM", () => {
  console.log("Shutting down gracefully...");
  db.close().then(() => clearInterval(interval));
});
```

On file change with `deno run --watch`:
```
Watcher Process started.
Shutting down gracefully...
Watcher Waiting for graceful termination...
Watcher Restarting! File change detected: "main.ts"
```

## Known limitation

`process.exit()` / `Deno.exit()` calls `std::process::exit()` which terminates the entire Deno process, including the watcher. Scripts using SIGTERM handlers in watch mode should perform cleanup and let the event loop drain naturally (e.g. clear intervals/timeouts, close servers) rather than calling `process.exit()`. This is a pre-existing limitation unrelated to this change — it also affects the existing `unload` event behavior in watch mode.

## Test plan

- [x] All 42 existing watcher integration tests pass
- [x] Manually verified: SIGTERM handler fires on file change, async cleanup runs, watcher restarts
- [x] Manually verified: SIGTERM handler fires on Ctrl+C, async cleanup runs, process exits
- [x] Manually verified: scripts without SIGTERM handlers restart immediately (no grace period)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — nice feature for graceful shutdown in watch mode

implementation is clean:
- `deno_signals::raise(SIGTERM)` dispatches to registered handlers
- only waits if handlers actually exist (returns true)
- 10 second timeout prevents hanging forever
- works for both restart (file change) and exit (Ctrl+C)

the known limitation about `process.exit()` killing the watcher is fine — that's expected behavior and documented

one thought: might be worth a future enhancement to also dispatch SIGINT on Ctrl+C in addition to SIGTERM, since some libraries only listen for SIGINT. but SIGTERM is the standard "please shut down" signal so this is good as-is

This PR implements synthetic signal raising for Deno's file watcher to ensure both SIGINT and SIGTERM handlers fire when Ctrl+C is pressed. The implementation has a medium-severity concern about cross-platform compatibility on Windows and a minor code clarity issue with the use of bitwise OR.

**[MEDIUM]** `cli/util/file_watcher.rs:408`: On Windows, synthetically raising SIGINT may behave differently than on Unix since Windows doesn't have native POSIX signal support. While SIGINT is listed in the Windows signal_dict, the behavior of the synthetic raise should be verified on Windows.
> Suggestion: Verify that synthetically raising SIGINT works correctly on Windows, or consider platform-specific handling if issues arise.

**[MEDIUM]** `ext/signals/lib.rs:254`: The `raise` function calls `handle_signal(signal)` directly. Ensure that `handle_signal` is designed to be called from an async context (not just from actual signal handler callbacks) and properly synchronizes with any signal handler state.
> Suggestion: Verify that `handle_signal` is safe to call directly outside of a signal handler context.

**[LOW]** `cli/util/file_watcher.rs:408`: The bitwise OR (`|`) is used instead of logical OR (`||`) to ensure both `raise(SIGINT)` and `raise(SIGTERM)` execute without short-circuiting. While this is likely intentional, it may not be immediately clear to readers.
> Suggestion: Consider adding a brief comment explaining that bitwise OR is used intentionally to ensure both signals are always raised, e.g., `// Use bitwise OR to ensure both signals are raised regardless of return values`

commits.message:
feat(watch): support graceful shutdown via SIGTERM dispatch

When watch mode restarts due to file changes or exits due to Ctrl+C,
synthetically dispatch SIGTERM to registered signal handlers before
cancelling the running operation. This gives JavaScript code a chance
to perform async cleanup (e.g. closing DB connections, releasing
resources, running finalizers).

If no SIGTERM handlers are registered, the restart/exit happens
immediately as before. If handlers are registered, a grace period of
up to 10 seconds is given for the operation to finish.

Closes #30912

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat(watch): dispatch SIGINT on Ctrl+C and add integration tests

- Dispatch both SIGINT and SIGTERM on Ctrl+C so libraries that only
  listen for SIGINT also get notified for graceful shutdown
- Add test for SIGTERM dispatch on watch restart (file change)
- Add test for SIGINT+SIGTERM dispatch on Ctrl+C in watch mode

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

merge main into feature/watch-graceful-shutdown

Resolve conflicts in tests/integration/watcher_tests.rs by keeping
both the upstream unload/process-exit tests and adding back the PR's
SIGTERM/SIGINT tests with #[cfg(unix)] to fix Windows CI hangs.

Also reduce GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT from 10s to 5s.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: support SIGTERM signal listeners on Windows

Add SIGTERM to the Windows signal dictionary so that
Deno.addSignalListener("SIGTERM", ...) works on Windows. libuv already
defines SIGTERM on Windows (from the CRT), and Deno's signal init()
already maps CTRL_LOGOFF_EVENT and CTRL_SHUTDOWN_EVENT to SIGTERM.
This was the only missing piece preventing SIGTERM handlers from being
registered on Windows.

This also fixes the run_watch_sigterm_on_restart test on Windows CI —
the test no longer needs #[cfg(unix)] since SIGTERM handlers now work
cross-platform.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: update signal test for SIGTERM support on Windows

Update the signalsNotImplemented test to match the new error message
that includes SIGTERM, and remove SIGTERM from the unsupported list.
Add a test verifying SIGTERM can be registered on Windows.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

docs: address review comments on signal raise behavior

- Clarify that `raise()` works on all platforms (including Windows)
  because it invokes JS handlers directly rather than OS-level signals
- Document that bitwise OR is intentional to avoid short-circuiting
- Add doc comment on `raise()` explaining it's safe from any context

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): validate process.exitCode and fix process constructor name
body:
## Summary
- Validate `process.exitCode` setter to reject non-integer values with `ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE` (matching Node.js behavior)
- Simplify `process.exit()` to delegate validation to the exitCode setter
- Fix `invalidArgTypeHelper` to format numbers as `Received <value>` instead of `Received type number (<value>)`
- Set `Process.prototype.constructor` name to `"process"` so V8 error messages show `#<process>` instead of `#<EventEmitter>`

## Test plan
- [x] `test-process-exit-code-validation.js` now passes (added to config.jsonc)
- [x] Existing `test-process-exit-*` tests still pass
- [x] `tools/format.js` and `tools/lint.js --js` pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Reviewed the changes with full repo context. No issues found.

Minor behavioral change in `process.exit()` for non-number/non-string truthy values. The change from `if (code || code === 0)` to `if (code !== undefined)` means values like `false`, `true`, or objects now flow through to the exitCode setter (which validates types) instead of being silently ignored. This appears intentional to better match Node.js behavior but is a subtle breaking change.

**[LOW]** `ext/node/polyfills/process.ts:119`: Behavior change: `process.exit()` with non-number/non-string truthy values (e.g., `false`, `true`, objects) now throws `ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE` instead of being silently ignored. The old guard `if (code || code === 0)` filtered out falsy non-zero values; the new `if (code !== undefined)` passes everything except `undefined` to the exitCode setter, which validates types. This is likely intentional to match Node.js, but worth confirming edge cases like `process.exit(false)` and `process.exit(true)` behave the same as Node.js.
> Suggestion: Consider adding test cases for `process.exit(false)`, `process.exit(true)`, and `process.exit({})` to document the intended behavior and ensure parity with Node.js.

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): validate process.exitCode and fix process constructor name

- Validate `process.exitCode` setter to reject non-integer values with
  ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE (matching Node.js behavior): non-integer numbers,
  non-numeric strings, booleans, bigints, objects, etc.
- Simplify `process.exit()` to delegate validation to the exitCode setter
- Fix `invalidArgTypeHelper` to format numbers as `Received <value>`
  instead of `Received type number (<value>)` to match Node.js
- Set `Process.prototype.constructor` name to "process" so V8 error
  messages show `#<process>` instead of `#<EventEmitter>`

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: revert invalidArgTypeHelper change, use ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE for non-integer numbers

The invalidArgTypeHelper change for numbers broke error messages across
the Node compat layer (e.g. test-heapdump.js). Instead, use
ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE for non-integer numbers in process.exitCode which
already produces the correct "Received 2.1" format.

title: fix(ext/node): skip normalization in path.win32.join for reserved device names
body:
## Summary

- When a joined path contains a Windows reserved device name with a colon (e.g. `CON:`, `PRN:`, `NUL:`), skip `normalize()` and only convert `/` to `\`, matching Node.js behavior
- This prevents `..` segments from being resolved through device name boundaries (e.g. `CON:..\..` would otherwise traverse up)
- Enable `test-path-join.js` node compat test

**Before:** `path.win32.join("\\server\\uploads", "CON:..\\..\\private")` → `\server\private` (incorrect, resolves `..` through `CON:`)

**After:** `path.win32.join("\\server\\uploads", "CON:..\\..\\private")` → `\server\uploads\CON:..\..\private` (matches Node.js)

## Test plan

- [x] `test-path-join.js` node compat test passes (newly enabled)
- [x] All 17 path compat tests pass
- [x] `./tools/format.js` and `./tools/lint.js --js` pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Updates Deno’s Node.js `path.win32.join()` polyfill to match Node behavior when encountering Windows reserved device names (eg. `CON:`), preventing normalization from resolving `..` segments across the device-name boundary and enabling the upstream Node compat test for this case.

**Changes:**
- Detect reserved device-name components during `path.win32.join()` and, when present, skip `normalize()` while still converting `/` to `\`.
- Enable the Node compat test `parallel/test-path-join.js` in the node_compat configuration.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| tests/node_compat/config.jsonc | Enables `parallel/test-path-join.js` in the node compat suite. |
| ext/node/polyfills/path/_win32.ts | Adds reserved-device-name detection in `join()` and changes behavior to skip normalization in that case. |

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): skip normalization in path.win32.join for reserved device names

When a joined path contains a Windows reserved device name with a colon
(e.g. CON:, PRN:, NUL:), skip normalize() and only convert forward
slashes to backslashes. This matches Node.js behavior and prevents `..`
segments from being resolved through device name boundaries.

For example, `path.win32.join("\\server\\uploads", "CON:..\\..\\private")`
now correctly returns `\server\uploads\CON:..\..\private` instead of
incorrectly resolving to `\server\private`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use O(n) slice-based scan and split/join for reserved name check

Address review feedback: replace character-by-character string
concatenation with segment start/end index tracking and
StringPrototypeSlice for the reserved name scan. Use split/join
instead of char-by-char concatenation for forward slash replacement.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): improve process.title and support --title flag
body:
- Default `process.title` to `process.execPath` instead of hardcoded `"deno"`, matching Node.js behavior
- Make `process.title` setter functional instead of a no-op
- Parse `--title=<value>` from `NODE_OPTIONS` and set `process.title` during bootstrap
- Add `--title` flag handling in node compat test runner

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8592
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/31368
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
not comfortable approving this as-is

main issue: the `NODE_OPTIONS` parsing for `--title=` is too naive.

right now it does:

```ts
const args = nodeOptions
  ? StringPrototypeSplit(nodeOptions, new SafeRegExp("\\s"))
  : [];
```

then checks `startsWith("--title=")`.

that means quoted values with spaces won't parse correctly, even though `NODE_OPTIONS='--title=hello world'` / `NODE_OPTIONS='--title="hello world"'` style inputs are exactly where this option becomes useful. you'll either truncate at the first space or include broken quoting.

node's `NODE_OPTIONS` parsing is shell-like, not plain whitespace splitting, so this is going to diverge in a pretty user-visible way.

also, the linux/mac argv overwrite logic assumes the argv/env memory is one contiguous writable buffer ending at `argv[argc-1]`. that's the classic trick, but it's pretty invasive and deserves stronger justification/tests before we take it. especially since the PR summary is partly about `process.title` semantics, but this code is also changing OS-visible process naming behavior.
a couple comments

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): default process.title to execPath for Node.js compatibility

Change process.title from hardcoded "deno" to defaulting to
process.execPath, matching Node.js behavior. Also make the setter
functional instead of a no-op. Enable sequential/test-process-title.js
node compat test.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(ext/node): support --title flag for process.title via NODE_OPTIONS

Parse --title=<value> from NODE_OPTIONS and set process.title during
bootstrap. Also add --title flag handling in the node compat test
runner. Enable test-process-title-cli.js node compat test.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/node-compat-next-tick-error-spin

fix(ext/node): implement OS-level process.title setting via syscalls

Previously process.title only stored the value in a JS variable without
making OS-level changes. Node.js (via libuv) overwrites argv[0] so that
`ps` shows the new title. This adds op_node_process_set_title with
platform-specific implementations:

- macOS: uses _NSGetArgv()/_NSGetArgc() to overwrite argv[0] buffer,
  plus pthread_setname_np for Activity Monitor visibility
- Linux: uses .init_array constructor to capture argv at startup,
  overwrites argv[0] buffer, plus prctl(PR_SET_NAME) for /proc/self/comm
- Windows: uses SetConsoleTitleW

Enables the node_compat test-setproctitle.js test.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: address CI failures for process.title PR

- Add explicit unsafe blocks in argv_store for Rust 2024 edition
- Use primordials (StringPrototypeStartsWith/Slice) in node_options.ts
- Update process.title unit test to match new execPath default behavior

CI uses a newer Rust toolchain that requires `#[unsafe(link_section)]`
instead of bare `#[link_section]`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
safety comment
safety comments

fix: use quote-aware parser for NODE_OPTIONS and add argv overwrite justification

Address review feedback: replace naive whitespace splitting of NODE_OPTIONS
with a shell-like tokenizer that handles single/double quoted values
(e.g. --title="hello world"). Add comments explaining the argv buffer
overwrite technique with references to libuv/Node.js. Add spec tests
for process.title including quoted NODE_OPTIONS values.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge remote-tracking branch 'origin/main' into fix/node-compat-next-tick-error-spin

fix: address review feedback on process.title

- Replace `static mut` + `Once` with `OnceLock<ArgvInfo>` struct per
  nathanwhit's suggestion — cleaner, no unsafe statics
- Fix macOS bug: save argv buffer bounds once via OnceLock (previously
  recomputed on every call, so after the first title set zeroed the
  buffer, subsequent calls would compute a smaller buf_size)
- Add `Mutex` around the argv buffer write path so concurrent workers
  can't race on the shared buffer (matching libuv's approach)
- Unify Linux and macOS into shared `ArgvInfo` / `overwrite_argv_buffer`
  / `compute_argv_info` helpers, reducing duplication

title: fix(ext/node): support interface option for IPv6 multicast membership
body:
Fixes the IPv6 multicast interface option which was hardcoded to `0`.

## Problem

The `interface` option for `addMembership`/`dropMembership` was ignored for IPv6 sockets:

```ts
op_node_udp_join_multi_v6(this.#rid, multicastAddress, 0);  // hardcoded!
```

## Solution

Following libuv's approach ([uv-common.c](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/src/uv-common.c#L265), [udp.c](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/src/unix/udp.c#L731)):

1. Pass interface address from TypeScript to Rust
2. Add `resolve_ipv6_interface()` to convert addresses to interface indices:
   - Parse zone ID from address (e.g. `fe80::1%eth0` or `::1%1`)
   - Use `if_nametoindex` for interface names on Unix
   - Match interface by address via `getifaddrs` as fallback

## Changes

- `ext/node/ops/udp.rs`: Change `op_node_udp_{join,leave}_multi_v6` to take `Option<String>` interface address, add `resolve_ipv6_interface()`
- `ext/node/polyfills/internal_binding/udp_wrap.ts`: Pass `interfaceAddress ?? null` instead of `0`
author: kajukitli

reviews.body: Does this fix any test?

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): support interface option for IPv6 multicast membership

Previously, the interface parameter for addMembership/dropMembership
was hardcoded to 0 for IPv6 sockets, ignoring any interface address
passed by the user.

This change:
- Passes the interface address from TypeScript to Rust for IPv6
- Adds resolve_ipv6_interface() to convert interface addresses to
  interface indices, following libuv's approach:
  - Parse zone ID from address (e.g. 'fe80::1%eth0')
  - Use if_nametoindex for interface names
  - Match interface by address using getifaddrs on Unix

Ref: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/src/uv-common.c#L265
Ref: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/src/unix/udp.c#L731

title: perf(ext/webidl): optimize dictionary converters
body:
## Summary

Optimizes the `createDictionaryConverter` hot path and related type converters:

- **Pre-compute member context strings** at converter creation time instead of allocating template literals per call per field
- **Eliminate `ObjectAssign({}, defaultValues)`** — only copy individual defaults for fields not present in input
- **Remove `opts = { __proto__: null }` default parameters** from integer, longlong, DOMString, and dictionary converters — each was allocating a new object per call even when no caller passed opts
- **Inline `typeof` checks** instead of calling `type()` and comparing returned strings

### Benchmark: release build vs system Deno 2.7.5

| Benchmark | System Deno | This PR | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TextDecoder.decode(buf, {stream})` — 1-field dict | 844 ns | 128 ns | **6.6x** |
| `new Response(null, {status})` — 3-field dict | 207 ns | 50 ns | **4.1x** |
| `new Response(null, {})` — empty dict overhead | 135 ns | 45 ns | **3.0x** |
| `new Headers(pairs)` — sequence converter | 1.8 µs | 503 ns | **3.6x** |
| `new Headers(record)` — record converter | 1.6 µs | 370 ns | **4.3x** |

Closes #18431

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test -p specs_tests -- fetch` (13 tests passed)
- [x] Manual correctness tests covering TextDecoder, Response, Request, Blob, Headers, performance.mark, structuredClone, URLPattern, and crypto roundtrip

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

perf(ext/webidl): optimize dictionary converters

title: fix(ext/node): fix 8 Node.js compat test failures
body:
## Summary

Fixes 8 Node.js compat tests that were failing after the node_test submodule update to Node.js 25.8.1 (#32705). Closes part of #32706.

- **`events`**: `listenerCount()` now handles `EventTarget` instances by delegating to `getEventListeners()`
- **`worker_threads`**: `cpuUsage()` accepts `NaN`/`null`/`undefined` as "no previous value" (matching Node.js behavior)
- **`process`**: add `quic: false` to `process.features`
- **`util`**: `parseEnv()` returns a null-prototype object
- **`net`**: fix `autoSelectFamilyAttemptTimeout` default from 250 to 500 (matching Node.js)
- **`streams`**: pass `readableType`/`type` options through `Duplex.toWeb()` to create byte streams for BYOB readers
- **`fs`**: handle `ENOTDIR` in `globSync` `getDirentSync()` instead of throwing

### Enabled tests
- `test-child-process-spawn-timeout-kill-signal.js`
- `test-child-process-fork-timeout-kill-signal.js`
- `test-worker-cpu-usage.js`
- `test-process-features.js`
- `test-util-parse-env.js`
- `test-net-autoselectfamily-attempt-timeout-default-value.js`
- `test-stream-duplex.js`
- `test-fs-glob.mjs`

## Test plan

- [ ] CI passes

author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
The PR contains a logical error in the type validation for `prevValue`. The `NumberIsNaN()` check is semantically incorrect when applied to an object - it will always return `false` for objects since `NaN` is a numeric concept. The check is redundant and misleading, as the subsequent `validateObject` call handles the actual type validation.

**[HIGH]** `ext/node/polyfills/worker_threads.ts:665`: Incorrect type check: `NumberIsNaN(prevValue)` always returns `false` for objects. This check is semantically wrong since `prevValue` is expected to be an object with `user` and `system` properties, not a number. The condition `!NumberIsNaN(prevValue)` is always true for any object, making it redundant and confusing.
> Suggestion: Simplify the condition to just `prevValue != null` and remove the `NumberIsNaN` check, since `validateObject` on line 666 already handles the actual type validation.

title: perf(ext/web): optimize URLPattern ops to reduce serde overhead and GC pressure
body:
## Summary

- **`op_urlpattern_process_match_input`**: Replace `#[serde] Option<(MatchInput, Inputs)>` return with `#[string] Option<String>` + `#[buffer] &mut [u32]`. The 8 URL component values are concatenated into a single string with offsets written to a shared `Uint32Array(9)` buffer. The `Inputs` return (which just echoed back the caller's arguments) is eliminated entirely and reconstructed on the JS side.

- **`op_urlpattern_parse`**: Return a lean struct that excludes the unused `matcher` field (containing `prefix`, `suffix`, and `InnerMatcher` sub-objects) from each `UrlPatternComponent`.

- **JS side**: Components stored as indexed array instead of keyed object. Match values accessed by index. Inner loop variable shadowing fixed.

## Benchmark

| Benchmark | Baseline | Patched | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| **test()** | | | |
| test(string) cached | 3.96M ops/s | 5.20M ops/s | **+31%** |
| test(string) rotating 1k | 1.90M ops/s | 2.65M ops/s | **+39%** |
| test(string) no match | 5.40M ops/s | 6.59M ops/s | **+22%** |
| test(string, baseURL) | 676K ops/s | 942K ops/s | **+39%** |
| test(init) | 257K ops/s | 342K ops/s | **+33%** |
| test(string) complex | 2.22M ops/s | 3.18M ops/s | **+43%** |
| **exec()** | | | |
| exec(string) cached | 1.22M ops/s | 1.96M ops/s | **+60%** |
| exec(string) rotating 1k | 933K ops/s | 1.47M ops/s | **+58%** |
| exec(string) no match | 2.84M ops/s | 4.92M ops/s | **+73%** |
| exec(string, baseURL) | 481K ops/s | 713K ops/s | **+48%** |
| exec(init) | 198K ops/s | 289K ops/s | **+46%** |
| exec(string) complex | 1.03M ops/s | 1.46M ops/s | **+42%** |
| **Property getters** | | | |
| read all 8 properties | 7.81M ops/s | 11.63M ops/s | **+49%** |

## Test plan

- [x] `./x test-unit urlpattern` passes
- [x] WPT urlpattern suite: 347 passed, 0 failed, 7 expected failures (unchanged)
- [x] Manual sanity test of `test()`, `exec()`, property getters, string/init/baseURL inputs

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
perf(ext/web): optimize URLPattern ops to reduce serde overhead and GC pressure

Replace serde-serialized return values in URLPattern ops with leaner
alternatives:

- `op_urlpattern_process_match_input`: return a concatenated string +
  u32 offset buffer instead of `#[serde] Option<(MatchInput, Inputs)>`.
  The `Inputs` return (which just echoed back the caller's arguments)
  is eliminated entirely and reconstructed on the JS side.

- `op_urlpattern_parse`: return a lean struct that excludes the unused
  `matcher` field (with its prefix/suffix/InnerMatcher sub-objects)
  from each UrlPatternComponent.

On the JS side, components are stored as an indexed array instead of
a keyed object, and match values are accessed by index.

title: feat(core): update v8 to 146.8.0 with foreground task ownership
body:
## Summary

- Updates the `v8` crate from 146.5.0 to 146.8.0 (includes [rusty_v8#1934](https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8/pull/1934))
- Updates `DenoPlatformImpl` to receive `v8::Task` / `v8::IdleTask` ownership from the C++ custom platform, instead of tasks being queued in `DefaultPlatform`
- Tasks are queued per-isolate and drained/run directly on the event loop, removing the need for `PumpMessageLoop`
- Follows up on #32450 by adapting deno_core to the new rusty_v8 task ownership API

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check` passes (full workspace)
- [x] `tools/format.js` clean
- [x] `tools/lint.js` clean
- [ ] CI: existing `test_pump_message_loop` and `atomics_wait_async_notify` spec tests validate foreground task delivery

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
feat(core): update v8 to 146.8.0 with foreground task ownership

Updates the v8 crate to 146.8.0 which includes rusty_v8#1934. The
custom V8 platform now receives ownership of foreground tasks via
v8::Task and v8::IdleTask types, instead of tasks being queued in the
default platform. This eliminates the need for PumpMessageLoop and lets
deno_core run V8 foreground tasks directly on the event loop.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

simplify: spawn foreground tasks directly on tokio runtime

Instead of manually queuing tasks in a registry and draining them
in the event loop, spawn them directly onto the isolate's tokio
runtime handle. This removes the timer thread, task queues, and
the PumpMessageLoop call entirely.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: add multi-worker Atomics.waitAsync spec test

Verifies that V8 foreground tasks are delivered to the correct
isolate when multiple workers use Atomics.waitAsync concurrently.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use Handle::try_current() for non-tokio contexts

Some unit tests and snapshot creation run JsRuntime without a tokio
runtime. Use try_current() to gracefully skip waker registration
instead of panicking.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: drop idle tasks instead of running with zero deadline

V8 idle tasks check `deadline > MonotonicallyIncreasingTime()`, so
passing 0.0 makes them no-ops. Node also ignores idle tasks entirely.
Just drop them without running.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: make post_idle_task unreachable

Idle tasks are not used in server runtimes — Node also marks this
as UNREACHABLE(). Panic if V8 ever posts one unexpectedly.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: queue immediate tasks, spawn only delayed tasks

Immediate foreground tasks must run synchronously during the event
loop poll to preserve microtask/timer ordering and ensure the event
loop sees their effects before checking pending state. Delayed tasks
use tokio::spawn with tokio::time::sleep.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor: remove pump_v8_message_loop and share task queue via Arc

- Remove `pump_v8_message_loop` from `PollEventLoopOptions` — foreground
  tasks are now always drained unconditionally on every event loop tick.
- Share the `ForegroundTaskQueue` (Arc<Mutex<Vec<v8::Task>>>) between
  `JsRuntimeState` and the global platform registry so the event loop
  drains tasks from its local Arc without touching the global map.
- Delayed tasks now queue into the shared Vec after sleeping instead of
  running directly on a tokio worker thread, ensuring they execute on
  the isolate's foreground thread.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: format and clippy fixes for PollEventLoopOptions

Remove needless `..Default::default()` now that the struct has only
one field.

title: fix(ext/node): fix TLA stall with native TTY handles and make stdio indestructible
body:
## Summary

- Fix TLA (top-level await) stall detection to account for active native
  libuv-compat TTY handles. Without this, any `await` on readline/stdin
  with a non-stdout output stream (like `@inquirer/prompts`' MuteStream
  pattern) would immediately error with "Top-level await promise never
  resolved".
- Make TTY `process.stdout`/`process.stderr` indestructible at the process
  level, matching Node.js's `dummyDestroy` behavior. Sets `_isStdio = true`
  to prevent `Stream.pipe()` from ending stdout when a piped source ends.
- Add PTY integration tests for consecutive readline prompts (both direct
  stdout and PassThrough/MuteStream patterns).

Fixes #32782
Fixes #30747

## Test plan

- [x] Manually tested `@inquirer/prompts` `select()` consecutive calls
- [x] Manually tested reproduction from #30747 (top-level await with readline)
- [x] PTY integration tests: `readline_multi_prompt_pty`, `readline_muted_multi_prompt_pty`
- [x] Existing `process_stdout_indestructible` spec test passes
- [ ] CI

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): fix TLA stall with native TTY handles and make stdio indestructible

Two fixes for issues introduced by the TTY rewrite (#32777):

1. The TLA stall detection in `poll_event_loop_inner` didn't consider
   `has_uv_alive_handles`, so active native TTY handles (e.g. stdin
   reading via readline) couldn't prevent the "Top-level await promise
   never resolved" error. This broke any code using `await` with
   readline/stdin when output was piped through a PassThrough stream
   (like @inquirer/prompts does).

2. TTY `process.stdout`/`process.stderr` were not made indestructible
   at the process level. Libraries like mute-stream call `destroy()`
   on stdout between prompts. Without the `dummyDestroy` override and
   `_isStdio` flag, the underlying TTY handle would be closed, breaking
   subsequent I/O. This matches Node.js's behavior in
   `lib/internal/bootstrap/switches/is_main_thread.js`.

Fixes #32782
Fixes #30747

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/fetch): retry on stale pooled HTTP/1.1 connections
body:
## Summary

Fixes #31955

When a server shuts down and restarts on the same port, the fetch client's
connection pool may still hold a stale keep-alive connection. Reusing it causes
`connection closed before message completed` errors on every other request.

The existing `is_error_retryable()` only handled HTTP/2 GOAWAY and
REFUSED_STREAM. This PR extends it to also retry on:

- `hyper::Error::is_incomplete_message()` — connection closed before the
  message completed (the main error from the issue)
- `io::ErrorKind::ConnectionReset` — TCP RST from a stale pooled connection

Both are safe to retry because the server never received/processed the request.
The retry is limited to one attempt (enforced by the existing `Retried` marker
extension) and only applies to requests with clonable bodies (not streaming).

Note: despite being reported as a regression in 2.6.6, this issue reproduces all
the way back to Deno 1.46.0 — it was a long-standing bug, not a regression.
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, good fix for a frustrating issue

the retry logic is sound:
- `is_incomplete_message()` catches the "connection closed before message completed" case
- `ConnectionReset` / `ConnectionAborted` covers TCP RST from stale connections
- both are safe to retry since the server never processed the request

the existing `Retried` marker ensures we don't retry infinitely, and the body-clonability check prevents retrying streaming requests.

nice that you also fixed the unit test to accept connections in a loop — otherwise the retry would hit a closed listener and fail differently.

one thought: might be worth adding a brief comment in the test explaining *why* we loop on accept (so future readers understand it's for retry coverage, not just cleanup).
Reviewed the changes with full repo context. No issues found.

commits.message:
fix(ext/fetch): retry on stale pooled HTTP/1.1 connections

When a server shuts down and restarts on the same port, the fetch
client's connection pool may still hold a stale keep-alive connection.
Reusing it causes "connection closed before message completed" errors.

Extend `is_error_retryable()` to also retry on:
- `hyper::Error::is_incomplete_message()` (connection closed mid-request)
- `io::ErrorKind::ConnectionReset` (TCP RST on stale connection)

These are safe to retry because the server never received the request.

Closes #31955

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: address CI failures from stale connection retry

- Fix clippy collapsible_if lint errors
- Handle ConnectionAborted (Windows os error 10053) in addition to
  ConnectionReset for cross-platform retry support
- Fix errorMessageIncludesUrlAndDetailsWithTcpInfo unit test to accept
  connections in a loop so retries hit the same error

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/fetch-retry-stale-connection

title: feat(eval): auto-detect CJS vs ESM in deno eval
body:
Working on Node compat with AI agents it's often frustrating, because they try to reproduce issue by using `deno eval 'const fs = require("fs"); ...'. Also it would be helpful in most situations to be able to use ESM/CJS interchangeably. So this PR adds auto-detect whether code passed to `deno eval` is CommonJS or ESM.

Uses `deno_ast::parse_program` + `compute_is_script()` to check if the code contains `import`/`export` declarations. If it does, treat as ESM (`.mjs`); otherwise treat as CJS (`.cjs`).

This means `deno eval "require('fs')"` now works without needing `--ext=cjs`, and `deno eval "import { ok } from 'node:assert'; ..."` continues to work as ESM.

Add `-e/--eval` flag to the top-level `deno` command so that

The CJS/ESM auto-detection was running for both `deno -e` and

because CJS mode makes setTimeout return a Timeout object instead of
