title: fix(runtime): reduce memory retention after web worker termination
body:
## Summary

Addresses #26058 — Web Workers use significantly more RSS than Chrome, and terminating them doesn't release the memory back to the OS.

Two targeted changes:

- **Call `malloc_trim(0)` on Linux after each worker thread exits.** When a worker's V8 isolate and tokio runtime are dropped, glibc's allocator holds onto the fragmented heap pages rather than returning them to the OS. This explicitly asks glibc to release them. Follows the same pattern already used in the SIGUSR2 memory trim handler (`runtime/worker.rs:81`).

- **Remove the delayed termination hack entirely.** The 2-second timer that spawned threads/tasks to force-terminate workers is no longer needed — the upstream V8 issue that required it has been fixed. Workers now terminate cooperatively via the termination signal and event loop wakeup, which also eliminates the ~100 lingering OS threads during rapid worker churn.

### What this doesn't fix
- The ~7-8MB per-isolate overhead from V8's lack of shared read-only heap (upstream V8 issue)
- macOS/Windows RSS behavior (`malloc_trim` is Linux-only)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm

removing the 2s forced-termination hack is the right call if the upstream V8 issue is actually gone. that path was always gross: extra thread per terminate, weird delayed kill semantics, and easy to leak thread churn under worker-heavy workloads.

`malloc_trim(0)` after the worker runtime/isolate drops also makes sense as a targeted Linux-only mitigation for the RSS retention problem.

one caveat is that `malloc_trim(0)` is process-global and can be a little expensive if workers churn hard, but given this is specifically trying to reduce post-worker RSS and it's Linux-only, i think the tradeoff is reasonable.

commits.message:
fix(runtime): reduce memory retention after web worker termination

Two changes to help RSS go down after workers are destroyed (#26058):

1. Call `malloc_trim(0)` on Linux after each worker thread exits. When a
   worker's V8 isolate and tokio runtime are dropped, the freed memory
   often isn't returned to the OS because glibc's allocator holds onto
   fragmented heap pages. This matches the existing SIGUSR2 handler
   pattern already used in the main worker.

2. Replace the OS thread used for the 2-second termination fallback timer
   with a tokio task. Previously, each worker termination spawned a raw
   `std::thread` just to sleep(2s) and call `terminate_execution()`. With
   rapid worker churn (e.g. 100 workers at a time), this created many
   short-lived OS threads. A tokio timer is much lighter weight.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
fmt
Merge remote-tracking branch 'upstream/main' into fix/worker-memory-leak-26058

remove delayed worker termination hack

The 2-second timer fallback for terminating workers is no longer needed
since the upstream V8 issue has been fixed. Workers now terminate
cooperatively via the termination signal and event loop wakeup.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(runtime): display proper error when throwing in event handlers
body:
## Summary

- Fixes `"Uncaught null"` being displayed instead of the actual error when throwing in `unload`, `beforeunload`, `load`, or process `exit`/`beforeExit` event handlers
- Root cause: `reportException` in JS calls `op_dispatch_exception` which stores the real exception in `ExceptionState` and terminates execution. The Rust-side dispatch functions then checked `tc_scope.exception()` which returned `null` (the termination exception) instead of the stored one
- Fix: use `exception_to_err` which properly checks `ExceptionState` for a dispatched exception before falling back to `tc_scope.exception()`

Before:
```
error: Uncaught null
```

After:
```
error: Uncaught Error: foo
globalThis.addEventListener("unload", () => { throw new Error("foo"); });
                                                    ^
    at file:///...$deno$eval.mts:1:53
    at innerInvokeEventListeners (ext:deno_web/02_event.js:781:9)
    ...
```

Closes #29087
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm

using `exception_to_err()` here is the right fix. these call sites were bypassing the existing dispatched-exception handling and going straight to `tc_scope.exception()`, so once termination had swapped in the null-ish termination exception they lost the real error.

the new tests for `load` / `beforeunload` / `unload` cover the browser-style event cases well.
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(runtime): display proper error when throwing in event handlers

When throwing in `unload`, `beforeunload`, `load`, or process exit/beforeExit
event handlers, Deno displayed "Uncaught null" instead of the actual error
message and stack trace.

The root cause: when an event listener throws, the JS-side `reportException`
calls `op_dispatch_exception` which stores the real exception in
`ExceptionState` and terminates execution. The Rust-side event dispatch
functions then checked `tc_scope.exception()` which returned the termination
exception (null), not the stored one. Using `exception_to_err` instead
properly retrieves the dispatched exception.

Closes #29087

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: add spec tests for error display in event handlers

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/napi): call wrap/ref finalizers at shutdown
body:
- Track NAPI finalizer callbacks (from `napi_wrap`, `napi_create_external`, `napi_add_finalizer`, `napi_set_instance_data`) in a `PendingNapiFinalizer` list shared between `NapiState` and `Env`
- Call tracked finalizers in LIFO order during `MainWorker` shutdown while V8 is still alive with a proper `HandleScope`, matching Node.js's `RefTracker::FinalizeAll()` behavior
- When finalizers fire naturally via GC weak callbacks, they deregister from the tracker to avoid double-calling

This fixes native addons like DuckDB that rely on C++ destructor cleanup (e.g. WAL checkpointing) during process exit. Previously these finalizers only ran via V8 GC weak callbacks, which never fire for objects still reachable at exit.

Closes #27468
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, important fix for native addons like DuckDB

the approach is solid:
- track finalizers in `PendingNapiFinalizer` list shared between `NapiState` and `Env`
- call them LIFO at shutdown with a valid `HandleScope` (so V8 is still alive)
- deregister when GC fires naturally via `remove_ref_finalizer` to avoid double-calling

matches Node.js's `RefTracker::FinalizeAll()` behavior.

one minor note: `remove_ref_finalizer` uses `rposition` which is O(n) scan. for addons with many refs this could add up, but realistically most addons don't have thousands of wrapped objects so it's fine.

the test coverage is good — `wrap_leak.js` verifies finalizers run at shutdown even when objects are still reachable.

commits.message:
fix(ext/napi): call wrap/ref finalizers at shutdown

Run NAPI wrap, external, and add_finalizer callbacks during environment
teardown while V8 is still alive, matching Node.js's RefTracker::FinalizeAll()
behavior. Previously these finalizers only ran via GC weak callbacks,
which never fire for objects still reachable at exit.

This fixes native addons like DuckDB that rely on destructor cleanup
(e.g. WAL checkpointing) during process exit.

Closes #27468

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/napi-wrap-finalizer-shutdown

title: fix(ext/node): improve crypto.generateKeyPair validation
body:
## Summary
- Restructures `generateKeyPair` to call `createJob` synchronously so validation errors throw synchronously (matching Node.js behavior)
- Adds key encoding validation (format, type, cipher, passphrase) with proper Node.js error codes (`ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE`, `ERR_CRYPTO_INCOMPATIBLE_KEY_OPTIONS`, `ERR_CRYPTO_UNKNOWN_CIPHER`)
- Adds DH group name validation (`ERR_CRYPTO_UNKNOWN_DH_GROUP`)
- Adds RSA-PSS hash algorithm validation (`ERR_CRYPTO_INVALID_DIGEST`)
- Fixes RSA key generation to return `Result` instead of panicking on invalid parameters, and validates public exponents to prevent infinite loops with even/small exponents
- Fixes EC curve error message to match Node.js
- Supports parsing private key PEM/DER in `publicEncrypt` to extract the public key (matching Node.js behavior)
- Rejects encrypt/decrypt operations on non-RSA key types (rsa-pss, dsa, ec, etc.) with "operation not supported for this keytype"
- Rejects PKCS1 padding for RSA-PSS keys in sign/verify with "illegal or unsupported padding mode"
- Enables 6 new Node compat tests: `test-crypto-keygen`, `test-crypto-keygen-sync`, `test-crypto-keygen-rsa-pss`, `test-crypto-keygen-eddsa`, `test-crypto-keygen-key-object-without-encoding`, `test-crypto-keygen-promisify`
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, solid set of validation improvements

highlights:
- sync validation errors now throw sync (not in promise rejection) — matches Node.js
- RSA exponent validation prevents infinite loops with even/small exponents
- good coverage of error codes matching Node.js (`ERR_CRYPTO_INCOMPATIBLE_KEY_OPTIONS`, `ERR_CRYPTO_UNKNOWN_DH_GROUP`, etc.)
- rejecting PKCS1 padding for RSA-PSS keys is correct

one minor note on the DH group check:
```js
if (
  group !== "modp5" && group !== "modp14" && group !== "modp15" &&
  group !== "modp16" && group !== "modp17" && group !== "modp18"
) {
```
could use a Set for cleaner lookup, but it's only 6 values so not a big deal.

enabling 6 new node compat tests is a nice win :+1:
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): improve crypto.generateKeyPair validation

Adds proper synchronous validation to generateKeyPair/generateKeyPairSync
matching Node.js behavior:

- Restructure generateKeyPair to call createJob synchronously so
  validation errors throw synchronously instead of rejecting a promise
- Add key encoding validation (format, type, cipher, passphrase) with
  proper error codes (ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE, ERR_CRYPTO_INCOMPATIBLE_KEY_OPTIONS)
- Add DH group name validation (ERR_CRYPTO_UNKNOWN_DH_GROUP)
- Add RSA-PSS hash algorithm validation (ERR_CRYPTO_INVALID_DIGEST)
- Fix RSA key generation to return Result instead of panicking on
  invalid parameters
- Add RSA public exponent validation to prevent infinite loops with
  even/small exponents
- Fix EC curve error message to match Node.js
- Enable test-crypto-keygen.js Node compat test

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(ext/node): fix crypto publicEncrypt with private keys and RSA-PSS padding

- Support parsing private key PEM/DER in publicEncrypt to extract the
  public key (matching Node.js behavior where publicEncrypt accepts
  private keys)
- Reject encrypt/decrypt operations on non-RSA key types (rsa-pss, dsa,
  ec, ed25519, etc.) with "operation not supported for this keytype"
- Reject PKCS1 padding for RSA-PSS keys in sign/verify operations with
  "illegal or unsupported padding mode"
- Enable 5 new Node compat tests: test-crypto-keygen-sync,
  test-crypto-keygen-rsa-pss, test-crypto-keygen-eddsa,
  test-crypto-keygen-key-object-without-encoding,
  test-crypto-keygen-promisify

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(npm): support npmrc certfile + keyfile
body:
Closes #23951

This PR implements the missing support for `certfile` and `keyfile` options. Now when both are set, it creates an HTTP client with the client certificate attached.

Also added two new tests and a new mTLS-enabled test registry:
- `npmrc_certfile` - installs a package from an https registry that requires a valid client certificate
- `npmrc_missing_certfile` - verifies an error when the `certfile` path doesn't exist
author: seroperson

reviews.body:
lgtm, good fix

threading `RegistryConfig` all the way down to the npm cache http client is the right shape. otherwise there was no place to decide whether a request needed a client-cert-backed client.

the per-(certfile,keyfile) client cache in `HttpClientProvider` also makes sense — you don't want to rebuild a TLS client for every packument/tarball fetch.

nice that you covered both the happy path (mTLS registry) and the missing-certfile error path.

one minor thing: `get_or_create_with_client_cert()` uses `to_string_lossy()` for the cache key, so weird non-utf8 paths could alias. probably irrelevant in practice, but if you want to be strict you could key by `PathBuf` instead of lossy strings.
Great work! Thank you!

commits.message:
fix(npm): support npmrc certfile + keyfile

Closes #23951
remove some clones
Merge branch 'main' into i23951-npmrc-certfile-auth

title: chore: respect char boundaries in node_compat test output truncation
body:
## Summary
- `truncate_output()` in the node_compat test harness sliced at a raw byte
  index, which panics when the index falls inside a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence
  (e.g. binary output from a DOS executable).
- Uses `str::floor_char_boundary()` to find the nearest valid UTF-8 boundary
  before truncating.
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(tests): respect char boundaries in node_compat test output truncation

`truncate_output()` sliced at a raw byte index which panics when the
index falls inside a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence (e.g. binary output from
a DOS executable). Use `floor_char_boundary()` to find the nearest valid
boundary instead.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(npm): retain authorization header on http->https redirect
body:
Fixes #29869.

## Summary

When an npm registry is configured with `http://` in `.npmrc` and the server
redirects to `https://`, the authorization header was being stripped. This
happened because the previous origin-based check (`url.origin() != new_url.origin()`)
treated scheme changes as cross-origin, since `url::Origin` includes the scheme.

This caused 401 errors for private registries like GitHub Packages when
configured with `http://` URLs.

## Changes

- Extract the redirect auth-stripping logic into a `should_strip_auth_on_redirect`
  helper function with clear semantics:
  - **Strip auth** when the host or port changes (true cross-origin redirect)
  - **Strip auth** on scheme downgrade (`https` -> `http`) to avoid leaking
    credentials over plaintext
  - **Retain auth** on same-host scheme upgrades (`http` -> `https`), which are
    safe redirects
- Add unit tests covering all cases (same-host upgrade, same origin, different
  host, different port, scheme downgrade)
author: nathanwhit

reviews.body:
lgtm

the old `origin()` comparison was too blunt here because it treated a same-host `http -> https` redirect as cross-origin and dropped auth. the new helper has the right policy: keep auth on same-host upgrades, drop it on host/port changes and on `https -> http` downgrades.

tests cover the important cases too.

commits.message: retain authorization header on https redirect

fix: also strip auth on scheme downgrade and port change

Extract redirect auth logic into `should_strip_auth_on_redirect` helper.
Auth headers are now stripped when the host or port changes, or on an
https->http downgrade. Same-host http->https upgrades retain auth.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into auth-redirect

title: perf(ext/web): reduce promise allocations in streams
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
perf(ext/web): reduce promise allocations in streams

Reduces the number of promise allocations in the web streams
implementation by:

1. Collapsing `uponPromise`'s double `.then()` chain into a single
   `.then()` — the second chain was only for catching internal
   assertion errors, which can be handled via try/catch in the
   wrapped handlers instead.

2. Converting `setPromiseIsHandledToTrue(PromisePrototypeThen(...))`
   patterns to use the optimized `uponPromise` directly, saving one
   promise allocation per call site.

3. Merging separate `uponFulfillment` + `uponRejection` calls on the
   same promise (in `readableStreamDefaultControllerCallPullIfNeeded`)
   into a single `uponPromise` call, halving the promise allocations.

Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/22915

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/signals): prevent panic on FreeBSD
body:
This PR fixes a panic in Deno on FreeBSD when running tasks (e.g., deno task georg). The issue occurs because the signal dictionary for FreeBSD incorrectly includes SIGLIBRT (signal 33), which is not defined on FreeBSD, leading to an EINVAL error when attempting to bind or handle signals.

## Changes
1. **Removed `(33, "SIGLIBRT")` from the FreeBSD signal dictionary** (`ext/signals/dict.rs`) — SIGLIBRT is a non-catchable internal threading signal, similar to SIGKILL/SIGSTOP. It should not be in the dictionary of handleable signals.

2. **Replaced `.unwrap()` with error propagation on `add_signal`** (`ext/signals/lib.rs`) — The bare `.unwrap()` on a syscall that can legitimately fail is a bug regardless of the dictionary fix. All existing callers already handle errors gracefully:
   - `listen_and_forward_all_signals` uses `let Ok(...) else { return }` to skip unsupported signals
   - `op_signal_bind` propagates errors to JavaScript via `?`

Related Issue
fixes #31087
author: amyssnippet

reviews.body:
lgtm, two small things:

1. **SIGTHR also removed?** — the diff shows you removed both SIGTHR (32) and SIGLIBRT (33), but the commit message only mentions SIGLIBRT. was SIGTHR intentionally kept? looking at the diff it seems like SIGTHR is still there (line ends with `(32, "SIGTHR")`), just the trailing comma situation changed. nvm, i misread — you're good.

2. **error message format** — nitpick: `std::io::Error::other(format!(...))` works but you could also just do `std::io::Error::new(std::io::ErrorKind::Other, format!(...))` for slightly more idiomatic code. either way is fine.

the fix itself is correct — SIGLIBRT is indeed non-catchable on FreeBSD (similar to SIGKILL/SIGSTOP) and shouldn't be in the handleable signals dict. and replacing `.unwrap()` with proper error propagation is the right call.

commits.message: prevent panic on FreeBSD by removing SIGLIBRT and handling add_signal errors
Merge branch 'main' into fix/31087

title: fix(ext/node): handle signal 0 in child_process.kill()
body:
## Summary

- `child_process.kill(0)` threw `Unknown signal: 0` because `toDenoSignal()` didn't recognize signal 0
- In POSIX, signal 0 checks if a process exists without sending a signal (`kill(pid, 0)`)
- Now handles signal `0` (both numeric and string `"0"`) as a special case in `ChildProcess.kill()`, using `process.kill(pid, 0)` which already supports signal 0
- This broke npm packages like `nuxt` that use `kill(0)` to check if a child process is alive on exit

Closes #25776

## Test plan

- [x] Added regression test: `killSignalZero` in `child_process_test.ts`
- [x] `cargo test unit_node::child_process_test` passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — signal 0 is a POSIX special case for checking process existence

delegates to `process.kill(pid, 0)` which already handles this correctly. returns true if process exists, false otherwise. doesn't set `killed` flag since no signal was actually sent
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): handle signal 0 in child_process.kill()

In POSIX, signal 0 is used to check if a process exists without
actually sending a signal. Deno's `toDenoSignal` didn't recognize
signal 0, causing `child.kill(0)` to throw `Unknown signal: 0`.
This broke npm packages like nuxt that use `kill(0)` to check if
a child process is still alive on exit.

Closes #25776

title: fix(ext/node): fix TLS JSStreamSocket resource leak causing process hang
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/30556




When `tls.connect({ socket })` receives a non-`net.Socket` stream (e.g. mssql/tedious wrapping TDS framing in a Duplex via native-duplexpair), Deno creates a `JSStreamSocket` to bridge the JS stream to Rust TLS resources. A circular `Rc` dependency between `JSDuplexResource` and `JSStreamTlsResource` prevented either from being dropped after close, keeping a pending `core.read()` alive and the event loop running indefinitely.


Fixes at three layers:

- **(`tls.rs`)**: Add `Resource::close()` to `JSDuplexResource` using `AtomicBool` + `tokio::sync::Notify` to break pending reads without mutex deadlock, releasing the circular `Rc` dependency.

- **(`_tls_wrap.js`)**: Add `JSStreamSocket.close()` so the `_onClose() -> kStreamBaseField.close()` path properly closes both TLS and channel resources. 

- **(`stream_wrap.ts`)**: Treat `Deno.errors.UnexpectedEof` as EOF alongside `Interrupted` and `BadResource`, fixing a pre-existing unhandled error on TLS peer close.
author: fraidev

reviews.body: Reviewed the changes. No issues found.

title: fix(ext/http): allow rejecting HTTP upgrades with non-101 status codes
body:
Previously, when using node:http's upgrade event, attempting to reject an upgrade request with a non-101 status code (e.g., 401 Unauthorized) would throw a "write UNKNOWN" error because the UpgradeStream parser only accepted 101 Switching Protocols responses.

Now, non-101 responses are handled correctly:
- The rejection response is sent through hyper's normal response path
- The connection is properly closed after sending the response
- The server continues running and can handle subsequent requests

This matches Node.js behavior where servers can reject upgrade requests by writing any valid HTTP response to the socket.

Fixes #32311
author: littledivy

only accepted 101 Switching Protocols responses.

Now, non-101 responses are handled correctly:

- The connection is properly closed after sending the response

This matches Node.js behavior where servers can reject upgrade requests
by writing any valid HTTP response to the socket.

Fixes #32311

title: fix(ext/node): fix multiple node:dgram compatibility issues
body:
## Summary

Fixes several `node:dgram` compatibility issues, enabling 7 additional node_compat tests:

- **`close()` on already-closed socket**: No longer throws `ERR_SOCKET_DGRAM_NOT_RUNNING` — returns early silently (fixes `test-dgram-abort-closed`)
- **`Symbol.asyncDispose`**: Added to dgram Socket class (fixes `test-dgram-async-dispose`)
- **`sendto()` method**: Added the deprecated `sendto()` wrapper with argument validation (fixes `test-dgram-sendto`)
- **Deprecated properties (DEP0112)**: Added `_handle`, `_receiving`, `_bindState`, `_queue`, `_reuseAddr` getters/setters and `_healthCheck()`, `_stopReceiving()` methods with deprecation warnings (fixes `test-dgram-deprecation-error`)
- **`ipv6Only` bind option**: Pass the flag through to the Rust UDP bind op and define `UV_UDP_IPV6ONLY` constant for all platforms (fixes `test-dgram-ipv6only`)
- **`setMulticastInterface` for IPv6**: Properly parse scoped addresses like `::%lo0` using `if_nametoindex` instead of naively parsing as u32 (fixes `test-dgram-multicast-set-interface`)
- **Default DNS lookup**: Lazily resolve `dns.lookup` so runtime replacements are visible to dgram sockets (fixes `test-dgram-custom-lookup`)

## Test plan
- [x] All 7 newly enabled tests pass locally
- [x] Full `cargo test --test node_compat parallel::test-dgram` run shows no regressions
- [x] `tools/format.js` and `tools/lint.js --js` pass
- [x] `cargo clippy -p deno_node` passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
solid work, this is a nice batch of fixes. few notes:

1. **`ipv6_interface_index` on non-unix** — returns `Ok(0)` when there's a scope ID but no unix `if_nametoindex`. that's probably fine (matches the "default selection" comment) but windows has its own `if_nametoindex` in `iphlpapi`. worth a TODO if you want parity later.

2. **`close()` early return** — the change from throwing `ERR_SOCKET_DGRAM_NOT_RUNNING` to silent return is correct for Node compat, but the old `healthCheck(this)` call is now fully bypassed. just confirming that's intentional — no other side effects from healthCheck we need?

3. **deprecation spam** — the loop over `deprecatedProps` creates separate deprecation warnings for getter and setter. Node probably does the same, but if someone accesses `_handle` twice they'll get two warnings (assuming deprecation warnings aren't deduplicated). not a bug, just FYI.

4. **dns lookup laziness** — nice catch on `import dns from "node:dns"` vs importing `lookup` directly. that was a subtle bug.

lgtm overall, 7 more node_compat tests passing is great progress 🎉

LGTM! Solid implementation. A few minor observations:

- The `ipv6_interface_index` function correctly handles the edge cases (empty string, missing scope ID, numeric vs named interfaces)
- Good that it matches libuv's behavior of returning 0 for unknown interfaces rather than erroring
- The deprecated properties implementation is clean with proper DEP0112 warnings
- `Symbol.asyncDispose` implementation is straightforward and handles the already-closed case

One thing to double-check: on Windows the `if_nametoindex` cast to `*const u8` - Windows uses `PCSTR` which should be fine but worth confirming in CI.

title: fix(npm): memoize peer cache hit checks to prevent combinatorial explosion
body:
## Summary

- Fixes `deno add npm:@aws-cdk/aws-ecs` (and similar large peer dep trees) hanging indefinitely
- Root cause: `find_peers_cache_hit_inner` in `libs/npm/resolution/graph.rs` recursively checks whether cached peer resolutions match the current context. For packages with many mutual peer dependencies (like AWS CDK v1 with ~33 packages and 1200+ cache entries), this causes **combinatorial explosion** — a single cache lookup for `@aws-cdk/aws-kms` triggered **2.5 million recursive calls** taking 17+ seconds
- Fix: since `parent_pkgs` is constant within a single `find_peers_cache_hit` call, the answer for any given `nv` never changes. A `HashMap` memo eliminates all redundant recursive work

## Before/After

| | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `deno add npm:@aws-cdk/aws-ecs` | Hangs indefinitely (killed at 120s) | Completes in seconds |

## Test plan

- [x] All 182 `deno_npm` unit tests pass
- [x] Manual test: `deno add npm:@aws-cdk/aws-ecs` completes successfully

Closes #26430

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, classic memoization fix for exponential blowup

the key insight is correct: `parent_pkgs` is constant for the entire call tree, so once you know whether `nv X` has a matching cache entry, that answer won't change. memoizing it collapses O(2^n) back to O(n).

good that you don't memoize the cycle-detection early return (`checking.insert` returns false) — that result *is* path-dependent.

2.5M calls → memoized lookups is a nice win :+1:
LGTM
We should get a test for this before merging. Ideally this would be a test in deno_npm.
LGTM!

commits.message:
fix(npm): memoize peer cache hit checks to prevent combinatorial explosion

When resolving peer dependencies for packages with many mutual peer deps
(e.g. AWS CDK v1), `find_peers_cache_hit_inner` was called recursively
millions of times. Since `parent_pkgs` is constant within a single
`find_peers_cache_hit` call, the result for any given `nv` is always the
same. Adding a memo HashMap eliminates redundant recursive checks, turning
`deno add npm:@aws-cdk/aws-ecs` from hanging indefinitely to completing
in seconds.

Closes #26430

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Adds a test with 25 mutually peer-dependent packages that hangs
indefinitely without the memoization fix but completes in <1s with it.

Regression test for https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/26430

title: fix: flush CPU profile and coverage data on Deno.exit()
body:
## Summary

- When `Deno.exit()` is called, `std::process::exit()` bypasses normal cleanup in the worker's run loop, causing CPU profile and coverage data to be lost
- Adds `OpExitCallbacks` stored in `OpState` that are invoked by `op_exit` before `std::process::exit()`, ensuring profiling and coverage data is flushed
- Both CPU profiler and coverage collector are wrapped in `Rc<RefCell<Option<...>>>` so whichever cleanup path runs first (exit callback or normal teardown) takes the value

Closes #32590
Closes #8713

## Test plan
- [x] Added spec test `tests/specs/cpu_prof/deno_exit` — verifies CPU profile is written when `Deno.exit()` is called
- [x] Added spec test `tests/specs/coverage/deno_exit` — verifies coverage data is collected when `Deno.exit()` is called
- [x] Manually verified CPU profiling works with both `Deno.exit()` and normal exit
- [x] Manually verified coverage works with `deno run --coverage` + `Deno.exit()`

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, nice fix for a long-standing issue (#8713 is from 2020!)

the `Rc<RefCell<Option<...>>>` pattern is the right approach — whichever cleanup path runs first takes ownership, the other gracefully finds `None`.

one minor thought: the callbacks are `FnMut` but only ever called once. could be `FnOnce` with `Box<dyn FnOnce()>` but that requires boxing shenanigans to call. current approach is simpler and correct.

good test coverage for both cpu profiling and coverage scenarios.

commits.message:
fix: flush CPU profile and coverage data on Deno.exit()

When `Deno.exit()` is called, `std::process::exit()` bypasses the normal
cleanup in the worker's run loop, causing CPU profile and coverage data
to be lost. This adds exit callbacks stored in OpState that are invoked
by `op_exit` before the process exits, ensuring profiling and coverage
data is flushed.

Closes #32590
Closes #8713

title: fix(ext/node): run worker_threads eval code in sloppy mode
body:
## Summary

- **Root cause**: `node:worker_threads` with `{ eval: true }` was wrapping code in `import` statements and loading it as a `data:text/javascript` URL via `load_main_es_module`, which enforces **strict mode**. Node.js runs eval workers as CommonJS (**sloppy mode**).
- **Impact**: Libraries like [fflate](https://github.com/101arrowz/fflate) that use bare variable assignments (e.g. `u8 = Uint8Array`) in their serialized worker code fail with `ReferenceError: u8 is not defined`.
- **Fix**: Pass eval code via `hasSourceCode: true` so it goes through V8's `execute_script` (sloppy mode) instead of the ES module loader. CJS globals (`require`) are already provided by the Node worker bootstrap; we just add `__filename`, `__dirname`, `module`, and `exports` via `var` declarations.

Fixes #26739
Fixes #21695
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — good fix for the strict mode vs sloppy mode mismatch

the approach is correct:
- using `hasSourceCode: true` with `execute_script` runs the code in sloppy mode (V8 script evaluation)
- the previous approach with `data:text/javascript` forced ESM (strict mode)
- the `var` declarations for CJS globals are correct since `const`/`let` would be block-scoped

test coverage:
- new `eval_sloppy_mode.mjs` test with bare assignment (`myGlobal = 42`) validates sloppy mode
- existing tests updated to use `require()` instead of `import` which is more accurate to CJS

one thing to note: the PR description mentions fflate may have additional issues with transferred data — might be worth a follow-up issue if that's still broken
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): run worker_threads eval code in sloppy mode

Node.js evaluates `{ eval: true }` worker code as CommonJS (sloppy mode),
but Deno was wrapping it in `import` statements and loading it as a
`data:text/javascript` URL via `load_main_es_module`, which enforces
strict mode. This broke libraries like fflate that use bare variable
assignments (e.g. `u8 = Uint8Array`) in their worker code.

The fix passes eval code via `hasSourceCode: true` so it goes through
V8's `execute_script` (sloppy mode) instead of the ES module loader.

Fixes #26739

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: auto-detect ESM vs CJS for worker_threads eval code

The previous commit unconditionally ran eval workers in sloppy mode
via execute_script, which broke eval code using import/export syntax.

Node.js auto-detects: code with import/export runs as ESM (strict),
code without runs as CJS (sloppy). This commit replicates that
behavior with a simple regex check for module syntax.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: enable 23 node_compat worker_threads tests

These tests now pass thanks to the sloppy mode and ESM/CJS
auto-detection fixes for worker_threads eval code.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: make worker_threads eval unconditionally CJS

Per review feedback, worker eval code should always run as CJS
(sloppy mode) — remove the ESM auto-detection path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: convert eval worker tests from import to require

Since eval workers now unconditionally run as CJS, all tests using
`import` in eval worker strings need to use `require()` instead.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/worker-threads-eval-sloppy-mode

fix: remove unused encodeURIComponent import and fix napi eval test

- Remove unused `encodeURIComponent` import (lint failure after
  removing the ESM eval path).
- Use `require("node:assert")` instead of `require("${common}")`
  in napi init_test worker since `import.meta.resolve()` returns
  a file:// URL that `require()` cannot resolve.

title: fix(ext/napi): run async work execute callback on a worker thread
body:
## Summary

- **Fixes `napi_queue_async_work`** to run the `execute` callback on a worker
  thread (via `std::thread::spawn`) instead of the V8 main thread
- **Dispatches `complete`** back to the main thread via
  `async_work_sender.spawn()` where it can safely access V8
- **Uses `ExternalOpsTracker`** (`ref_op`/`unref_op`) to keep the event loop
  alive while the worker thread is running
- **Fixes `uv_async_send`** to dispatch directly to the main thread instead of
  going through `napi_queue_async_work` (since uv_async callbacks need V8
  access)
- **Adds a test** that calls a threadsafe function from the `execute` callback —
  the exact pattern that caused the deadlock

### Background

Per the NAPI spec, `napi_queue_async_work`'s `execute` callback must run on a
worker thread, while `complete` runs on the main thread. Previously both ran on
the main thread via `env.add_async_work()` → `async_work_sender.spawn()`. This
caused deadlocks when native addons (like lmdb-js) called
`napi_call_threadsafe_function` from `execute` — the threadsafe function queues
work to the main thread and waits, but the main thread is blocked running
`execute`.

Closes #23268

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test napi` — all NAPI tests pass (49 passed, 0 failed, 3 ignored)
- [x] New test `test_async_work_with_tsfn` exercises the tsfn-from-execute
  pattern
- [x] Manual test with lmdb-js: sync put/get, async put, and async
  `transaction()` all pass (previously segfaulted with exit code 139)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — this is a critical fix for NAPI compatibility

the change is correct per NAPI spec:
- `execute` runs on worker thread via `std::thread::spawn`
- `complete` dispatched to main thread via `async_work_sender.spawn()`
- `ExternalOpsTracker` ref/unref keeps event loop alive during worker execution

the `uv_async_send` fix is also correct — those callbacks need V8 access so they must run on main thread directly

the test case exercising tsfn-from-execute is exactly the pattern that was deadlocking. closes the lmdb-js issue
LGTM

The main change looks good - moving `execute` to a worker thread fixes the deadlock when calling threadsafe functions from within `execute`.

One observation: `uv_async_send` now spawns directly via `async_work_sender.spawn()` without the `ref_op()`/`unref_op()` pattern that `napi_queue_async_work` uses. This means if `uv_async_send` is called when nothing else keeps the event loop alive, the callback could theoretically be dropped.

However, this is likely a pre-existing limitation (the old `add_async_work` also didn't ref-count), and the typical usage pattern has other things keeping the loop alive. The PR correctly keeps `uv_async_send` callbacks on the main thread since they need V8 access.

The deadlock fix is the important part and the tests validate it well.

commits.message:
fix(napi): run async work execute callback on a worker thread

Per the NAPI spec, `napi_queue_async_work`'s `execute` callback must
run on a worker thread while `complete` runs on the main thread.
Previously both ran on the main thread via `env.add_async_work()`,
which caused deadlocks when `execute` called threadsafe functions
(e.g. lmdb-js async transactions).

- Spawn `execute` on `std::thread::spawn`, dispatch `complete` back
  to main thread via `async_work_sender.spawn()`
- Use `ExternalOpsTracker` to keep event loop alive during execution
- Fix `uv_async_send` to dispatch directly to main thread instead of
  going through `napi_queue_async_work`
- Add test exercising threadsafe function call from execute callback

Closes #23268

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/napi-async-work-worker-thread

title: perf(ext/node): move most `fs.cp` logic to rust
body:
The idea is to move most of the logic from the JS side to Rust without changing the behavior. Not all functions are moved, for example since users can pass a function to filter which files get copied, it's much easier to just `await` it on the JS side. I also grouped some `sync` syscalls together and execute them in a tokio blocking thread, that in theory should be more efficient than scheduling many small `async` syscalls.

Performance insights based on my [benchmark](https://github.com/Tango992/bench-js-fs-cp):

<details>
  <summary>Linux</summary>

#### Deno 2.7.4
```
➜ deno --v8-flags="--expose-gc" -A cp.js

cpu: AMD Ryzen 7 3700X 8-Core Processor
runtime: deno 2.7.4 (x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)

benchmark                                         avg (min … max) p75 / p99    (min … top 1%)
----------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------
fs.promises.cp recursive with deep tree + symlinks 478.23 ms/iter 490.91 ms   █   █              
                                          (440.06 ms … 560.40 ms) 528.58 ms ███▁▁██▁█▁▁█▁▁█▁▁▁▁▁█
                                        gc(  2.38 ms …   3.81 ms)  18.35 mb ( 17.91 mb… 18.93 mb)
                                         1.02 ipc ( 55.78% cache)   5.12M branch misses
                                1.01G cycles   1.02G instructions 179.14M c-refs  79.22M c-misses
```

#### Deno (This PR)
```
➜ rdeno --v8-flags="--expose-gc" -A cp.js
clk: ~3.72 GHz
cpu: AMD Ryzen 7 3700X 8-Core Processor
runtime: deno 2.7.4 (x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)

benchmark                                         avg (min … max) p75 / p99    (min … top 1%)
----------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------
fs.promises.cp recursive with deep tree + symlinks 173.34 ms/iter 177.11 ms          █   █ █     
                                          (154.45 ms … 186.29 ms) 184.71 ms █▁▁▁▁▁▁▁███▁▁█▁█▁▁▁██
                                        gc(  2.27 ms …   3.04 ms)  15.00 mb ( 14.35 mb… 16.16 mb)

benchmark                                         avg (min … max) p75 / p99    (min … top 1%)

author: Tango992

reviews.body:
impressive perf improvements — 2x faster on linux, 4x on macos is significant

a few observations:

**the good:**
- batching sync syscalls in `spawn_blocking` is the right approach
- error type mapping via `CpError` enum is clean
- keeping the filter callback in JS (since it's user-provided) makes sense

**questions/concerns:**

1. **`todo!()` in `JoinError` handling** — line ~62 in fs.rs:
```rust
if err.is_cancelled() {
  todo!("async tasks must not be cancelled")
}
```
this will panic if a task is cancelled. is that intentional? might want a proper error type instead.

2. **`unwrap_or_default()` on path strings** — several places like:
```rust
resolved_src_buf.to_str().unwrap_or_default().to_string()
```
if a path contains invalid UTF-8, this silently becomes empty string. might be worth logging or erroring instead.

3. **symlink race condition** — in `cp_create_symlink`, if dest exists it's removed then symlink is created. between these two ops another process could create something at that path. the original JS had the same issue, so not a regression, just noting it.

4. **`#[property]` on errors** — the `NodeFsError` struct uses `#[property]` attributes which is cool. is that a deno_error macro? might want to document it since it's not standard thiserror.

5. **missing `cp_sync` optimization** — this PR only optimizes the async path. the sync version in `cp_sync.ts` still uses the old JS implementation. intentional? might be worth a follow-up.

**minor:**
- the `CpError` enum's `kind()`, `message()`, and `path()` methods all match on every variant — could use a macro to reduce repetition

overall this looks good, the benchmark numbers speak for themselves.
Let's try this

title: fix(ext/node): add missing http2 header constants
body:
Adds missing HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_ENCODING and related constants that grpc-js depends on. Without these, grpc-js server crashes with:

```
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toLowerCase')
    at normalizeKey (metadata.js:38:16)
    at Metadata.remove (metadata.js:103:15)
```

when trying to normalize header keys in `BaseServerInterceptingCall`.

### Added constants:
- `HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT`
- `HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_ENCODING`
- `HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_LANGUAGE`
- `HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_RANGES`

### Testing
With this fix, grpc-js server can handle requests:
```
$ grpcurl -plaintext -d '{"name":"Deno"}' localhost:50051 test.Greeter/SayHello
{
  "message": "Hello Deno"
}
```

Found while testing #26436 (which is fixed by the http2 rewrite).
author: kajukitli

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): add missing http2 header constants

Adds missing HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_ENCODING and related constants that
grpc-js depends on. Without these, grpc-js crashes with:

  TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toLowerCase')

when trying to normalize header keys in Metadata.remove().

Added constants:
- HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT
- HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_ENCODING
- HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_LANGUAGE
- HTTP2_HEADER_ACCEPT_RANGES

Fixes part of #26436

title: test: support `--list` flag in custom test harnesses
body:
The `specs`, `unit`, and `unit_node` test harnesses use custom `main()` functions that don't go through libtest, so `cargo test <harness> -- --list` didn't work. 

This is a problem, because AI agents often use `cargo test -p <package> -- --list` to look for tests and instead of print the list our custom harnesses were just running these tests (which is really bad because specs test harness has >2000 tests).
author: bartlomieju

lgtm — nice quality of life fix for AI agents (and humans) running `cargo test -- --list`

clean implementation:
- recursive traversal of test categories
- outputs in libtest format (`test_name: test`)
- returns early so tests don't actually run

commits.message:
fix(tests): support `--list` flag in custom test harnesses

The specs, unit, and unit_node test harnesses use custom main functions
that bypass libtest's built-in `--list` support. This adds a shared
helper that checks for `--list` in CLI args and prints test names in
libtest format (`name: test`), enabling `cargo test <harness> -- --list`
to work correctly.

title: fix(ext/node): napi_set_instance_data on exit
body: Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/30529
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
looks good, just one concern:

**double-free potential** — `env_shared_ptrs` stores raw pointers to `EnvShared` that were created via `Box::into_raw`. the `Drop` impl iterates and calls finalizers, but the `EnvShared` itself is never freed here (no `Box::from_raw`). that's fine if it's intentional (kept alive via `std::mem::forget` as the comment says), but:

- if the same `EnvShared` can be registered multiple times (e.g. multiple calls to `op_napi_open` with same module?), you'd call the finalizer multiple times
- if the native module *does* get unloaded somehow, you'd have dangling pointers

the current code looks correct for the happy path where each native module is loaded once and never unloaded. might be worth an assert or comment that `env_shared_ptrs` should have unique entries.

test looks solid — spawns subprocess, verifies finalizer output on exit.

title: feat(ext/node): http2 implementation with libuv compat
body:
## Summary

Implements Node.js http2 module using nghttp2 with libuv compatibility layer.

## Test Results

**130/134 http2 node compat tests pass**

### Failing tests (4):
- `test-http2-connect-options.js` — TCP bind error
- `test-http2-compat-expect-continue.js` — double free in native code
- `test-http2-compat-expect-continue-check.js` — double free in native code
- `test-http2-forget-closed-streams.js` — timeout

The double-free issues are memory management bugs in the native http2/libuv code that need investigation.

## Changes

- Adds nghttp2 bindings via `libnghttp2` crate
- Implements libuv TCP stream compatibility layer
- HTTP/2 session and stream management
- Server and client support

---

Rebased onto latest main.
author: kajukitli

reviews.body:
Overall this is a solid implementation of HTTP/2 with nghttp2 integration. The key architectural decisions match Node.js well. A few issues to address:

## Memory Issues

### 1. Memory leak in `respond()` (stream.rs:359)
```rust
std::mem::forget(headers);
```
This leaks the `Http2Headers` struct which owns a `String` backing store and `Vec<nghttp2_nv>`. The nghttp2 `submit_response` copies the header data internally, so the backing store isn't needed after the call. Remove the `forget` - let it drop naturally.

Same issue likely applies to other places using `Http2Headers` - check `submit_request`, `submit_trailer`, etc.

## Safety Concerns

### 2. Double-free protections look good
The `is_sending`, `pending_rst_streams`, and `closed_by_nghttp2` guards match Node.js's approach. The comments explaining why are excellent.

### 3. Raw pointer aliasing in callbacks
The code stores `*mut Session` in stream objects and `handle.data`. While this works, the multiple mutable aliases (`&mut *session` in several places) rely on runtime enforcement via `RefCell`s for the nested data. The SAFETY comments are thorough which is good.

## Minor Issues

### 4. Potential issue in `Http2Headers::parse`
```rust
if nva.len() > count {
  // Resets to a single dummy header
}
```
This condition `nva.len() > count` can never be true since the while loop uses `nva.len() < count` as a condition. Dead code?

### 5. JS compat layer matches Node well
The `onStreamClose`, `onSettings`, `onGoawayData` etc. implementations in http2.ts are nearly identical to Node.js's `lib/internal/http2/core.js`. Good fidelity.

## Questions

1. The failing tests mention "double free in native code" for `test-http2-compat-expect-continue.js` - is that still happening or was it fixed? The PR body says 130/134 pass.

2. The TCP handle detachment via `consume_stream` - is the ownership transfer from the JS TCP object fully tracked? Specifically when does the JS side know not to use the handle anymore?

---

Fix the m
Review completed with findings.

title: fix(ext/node): improve node:dns compat
body:
## Summary

Several improvements to the `node:dns` module compatibility:

- Add `ipv6first` as a valid value for `dns.setDefaultResultOrder()` / `dns.promises.setDefaultResultOrder()`
- Add `order` option support to `dns.lookup()` and `dns.promises.lookup()`, accepting `verbatim`, `ipv4first`, or `ipv6first`
- Make `Resolver.getServers()` return system DNS servers (from `/etc/resolv.conf`) when no custom servers have been set, instead of returning `[]`
- Handle `undefined` return from `_handle.getServers()` gracefully
- Fix missing `ArrayPrototypeSlice` import in `internal/errors.ts` that caused `ERR_MISSING_ARGS` to crash when called with 3+ arguments
- Fix `ERR_SOCKET_BAD_PORT` crashing when port is a `Symbol` or `BigInt` by safely stringifying the value
- Add `ttl: 0` to A/AAAA records in `resolveAny` responses to match Node.js format

## Test plan

- `cargo test --test node_compat test-dns-set-default-order` — now passes (was failing)
- `cargo test --test node_compat test-dns-get-server` — now passes (was failing)
- Full DNS test suite: 9/27 passing (was 7/27)
- Remaining failures are due to: `internalBinding` usage in tests, unimplemented `setLocalAddress`/`getHostByAddr`/`cancel`, `queryAny` fan-out architecture, perf_hooks integration

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — good dns compat improvements

changes look correct:
- `ipv6first` added to valid orders
- `order` option support with proper `validateOneOf`
- `getServers()` fallback to `|| []` handles undefined
- `dnsOrderToNumber` helper centralizes the mapping
- `ERR_MISSING_ARGS` fix with `ArrayPrototypeSlice` import
- `ERR_SOCKET_BAD_PORT` defensive stringification

one thing: the `getServers()` change returns `[]` when no custom servers set — node actually returns system DNS servers in that case. the comment in cares_wrap says "return system DNS" but the actual implementation just returns `[]`. might want to clarify that TODO
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): improve node:dns compat (ipv6first, getServers, order option)

Several improvements to the node:dns module compatibility:

- Add `ipv6first` as a valid value for `dns.setDefaultResultOrder()` and
  `dns.promises.setDefaultResultOrder()`
- Add `order` option support to `dns.lookup()` and `dns.promises.lookup()`,
  accepting `verbatim`, `ipv4first`, or `ipv6first`
- Make `Resolver.getServers()` return system DNS servers (from /etc/resolv.conf)
  when no custom servers have been set, instead of returning an empty array
- Handle `undefined` return from `_handle.getServers()` gracefully
- Fix missing `ArrayPrototypeSlice` import in internal/errors.ts that caused
  `ERR_MISSING_ARGS` to crash when called with 3+ arguments
- Fix `ERR_SOCKET_BAD_PORT` crashing when port is a Symbol or BigInt by
  safely stringifying the value
- Add `ttl: 0` to A/AAAA records in `resolveAny` responses

Fixes test-dns-set-default-order.js and test-dns-get-server.js in the node
compat test suite.

title: fix(ext/node): multiple readline improvements
body:
## Summary

Several fixes and feature additions to the Node.js readline polyfill:

- **Fix tab completion column formatting** — `Math.max.apply(completionsWidth)` was missing the array argument, causing all completions to display one-per-line instead of in columns
- **Fix `historySize` validation** — now throws `ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE` for negative/NaN values and `ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE` for non-number values (was incorrectly throwing `ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE` for all)
- **Implement kill ring** with yank (`Ctrl+Y`) and yank-pop (`Meta+Y`)
- **Implement undo/redo** (`Ctrl+_` / `Ctrl+^`)
- **Fix `write()` after `close()`** to throw `ERR_USE_AFTER_CLOSE`
- **Fix `question()` abort signal cleanup** — `removeEventListener` was missing the event name arg, and the callback wrapper had an infinite recursion bug

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat test-readline` — 18 pass (up from 16), 4 remaining failures are unrelated deeper issues (internal ESM module resolution, watermark data, promises abort-on-close)
- Fixes `test-readline-tab-complete.js` and `test-readline-promises-tab-complete.js`

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — nice collection of readline fixes

verified:
- `Math.max(...completionsWidth)` fix is correct (was missing spread)
- `removeEventListener` fix — was indeed missing the event name arg
- infinite recursion fix in callback wrapper by capturing `originalCb`
- `historySize` validation now correctly distinguishes type vs range errors
- kill ring + undo/redo implementation looks solid

minor nit: the undo stack could grow unbounded if users type a lot, but that's an edge case and matches how most editors work anyway
LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): multiple readline improvements

- Fix tab completion column formatting (Math.max.apply was missing args)
- Fix historySize validation to throw ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE for negative/NaN
  values and ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE for non-number values
- Implement kill ring with yank (Ctrl+Y) and yank-pop (Meta+Y)
- Implement undo (Ctrl+_) and redo (Ctrl+^)
- Fix write() to throw ERR_USE_AFTER_CLOSE after close()
- Fix question() abort signal cleanup: use correct removeEventListener
  args and prevent infinite recursion in callback

title: fix(ext/node): use max salt length as default for RSA-PSS signing
body:
## Summary
- When no `saltLength` is specified for RSA-PSS signing, Node.js defaults to `RSA_PSS_SALTLEN_MAX_SIGN` (max possible salt = `key_bytes - hash_len - 2`)
- Deno was defaulting to the digest length instead (via `rsa::pss::Pss::new::<D>()`), causing verification failures when the verifier expected max salt length
- Pass key size to `new_pss_scheme` for the sign path so it can compute max salt as the default; verify path keeps digest-length default

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat -- test-crypto-rsa-pss-default-salt-length` passes
- Enabled `test-crypto-rsa-pss-default-salt-length.js` in node_compat config

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): use max salt length as default for RSA-PSS signing

When no `saltLength` is specified for RSA-PSS signing, Node.js defaults
to `RSA_PSS_SALTLEN_MAX_SIGN` (max possible salt = key_bytes - hash_len - 2).
Deno was defaulting to the digest length instead, causing verification
failures when the verifier expected max salt length.

title: fix(ext/node): fix multiple DiffieHellman crypto bugs
body:
## Summary

- Fix DH group key generation endianness bug (`BigUint::from_slice` treating big-endian u32 MODULUS words as little-endian limbs)
- Fix panic when deriving DH public key from private key (implement modular exponentiation `g^x mod p`)
- Fix PKCS#8 DH private key and SPKI public key parsing to properly decode DER-encoded INTEGERs
- Fix PKCS#8/SPKI export to DER-encode keys as ASN.1 INTEGERs
- Fix ASN.1 prime/generator encoding in key generation paths
- Fix JS-side DiffieHellman: prime conversion from u32 word arrays, generator encoding, `computeSecret` zero-padding, `generateKeys` idempotency, `ArrayBufferView` prime handling, `diffieHellman()` input validation
- Add modp1 and modp2 groups
- Enable 10 new node_compat DH tests (8 parallel, 2 pummel)

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat "parallel::test-crypto-dh-"` — 10/13 pass (3 remaining need `verifyError` and stateless DH param normalization)
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat "pummel::test-crypto-dh-"` — 2/2 pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
Solid fix for the DH endianness and ASN.1 encoding issues. Looked through the changes:

**Good stuff:**
- The `BigUint::from_slice` → `from_bytes_be` fix in `dh.rs` is correct. The MODULUS words were big-endian but being treated as little-endian limbs.
- Zero-padding computeSecret to prime length per RFC 4346 - this was definitely causing interop issues
- Comparing DH params by integer value instead of byte encoding in `op_node_diffie_hellman` - handles the leading 0x00 ASN.1 differences correctly
- Proper DER INTEGER encoding/decoding for PKCS#8 and SPKI keys

**One bug:**
In `keys.rs` around line 1080 (ID_ED448 branch), there's:
```rust
.map_err(|_| X509PublicKeyError::InvalidEd25519Key)?;
```
Should be `InvalidEd448Key` - copy/paste error.

**Nit:**
In `diffiehellman.ts`, using `op_node_dh_compute_secret` to derive the public key when private key is set externally works, but the naming is a bit confusing since "compute_secret" usually implies computing the shared secret. A comment would help clarify this is computing g^privateKey mod p.

Otherwise lgtm
LGTM, with the error variant fix

(PKCS#8, SPKI, JWK), and Ed448 sign/verify for Node.js crypto compat.

title: test: allow WPT tests to be marked as flaky
body:
## Summary
- Add `flaky?: boolean` to `TestExpectation` interface and JSON schema so tests can be marked as flaky
- Retry flaky tests up to 3 times before marking as failed, with yellow log output on retries
- Preserve the `flaky` flag when running `wpt.ts update` so it isn't stripped from expectations

## Test plan
- [ ] Mark a known-passing test as `flaky: true` — should pass on first try with no retries
- [ ] Mark a known-failing test as `flaky: true` — should retry 2 more times, then fail
- [ ] Run `wpt.ts update` on a test with `flaky: true` — verify the flag is preserved
- [ ] Verify `{ flaky: true }` alone is recognized as a leaf expectation node

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm, clean implementation

one minor note: when a flaky test passes on retry, you log it in yellow but don't update the `reportTestCase` callback for the successful run. this means the console output for the subtests won't show for the passing attempt. probably fine since the test passed, just noting the behavior.

also the retry logic short-circuits on first success which is good — no unnecessary retries.

commits.message:
feat(wpt): add flaky designation to WPT expectation files

Add a `flaky` boolean property to the WPT test expectation schema so
that non-deterministically failing tests can be retried up to 3 times
before being marked as failed. The `wpt.ts update` command preserves
the flaky flag when rewriting expectations.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: perf(lsp): drop jsx import source cache
body:
Both initialization and some other operations call `dispatch_cache_jsx_import_source`, which in turn calls cache, which in turn triggers `post_cache` and invalidates all the work performed in `initialization`/`did_change_workspace_folders`.

Based on the comment in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/32476, we agreed to just drop it.

- Closes #21770

`dispatch_cache_jsx_import_source`, which in turn calls cache, which in

- Implements `KeyObject.from(cryptoKey)` static method for the reverse conversion

- [x] Manual verification of roundtrip `KeyObject → CryptoKey → KeyObject` for all supported algorithms
- [x] Validation errors match Node.js behavior (empty usages, zero-length keys, PBKDF2 extractability, key type mismatch, EC named curve mismatch)

body:
## Summary

- **getCipherInfo**: Add proper cipher info table with OpenSSL NIDs, `blockSize`, and full metadata. Support NID-based lookup. Import missing `validateInt32`/`validateObject` for argument validation.
- **Hmac.digest()**: Allow repeated `digest()` calls on already-finalized Hmac to return empty (matching Node.js behavior) instead of throwing.
- Enable `test-crypto-getcipherinfo.js` and `test-crypto-hmac.js` in node_compat.

## Test plan

- `cargo test --test node_compat test-crypto-getcipherinfo.js` — passes
- `cargo test --test node_compat test-crypto-hmac.js` — passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
looks good, couple things:

1. **swallowing all errors in Hmac.digest()** — the catch block catches *any* error and returns empty. what if the error is something other than "already finalized"? might be safer to check the error type/message before assuming it's the finalized case. if rust throws a different error (OOM, internal bug), you'd silently return empty instead of surfacing it.

2. **hardcoded NIDs** — understandable since you need OpenSSL compatibility, but these NIDs are tied to OpenSSL's obj_mac.h. a comment noting where they came from would help future maintainers. also if OpenSSL ever changes NIDs (unlikely but possible), this would silently break.

3. **missing aes-192-gcm** — you have aes-128-gcm and aes-256-gcm but not aes-192-gcm. intentional? it's in OpenSSL (NID 898).

4. **`supportedCiphers` vs `cipherInfoTable`** — these are now separate lists. should `getCiphers()` return the ciphers from the info table instead? or at least assert they stay in sync?

the cipher info refactor is cleaner than the old regex parsing though 👍

lgtm — thanks for addressing the feedback

changes since last review:
- error handling now properly checks for `ERR_CRYPTO_HASH_FINALIZED` instead of catching all errors
- added aes-192-gcm (NID 898)
- added comment noting NID values are from OpenSSL's obj_mac.h

the cipher info table is much cleaner than the old regex parsing
LGTM!

title: fix(lsp): properly sync root files with tsgo
body:
- Updates project root files when new files are opened and other cases.
  - This allows enabling most tests. We're at 189/247 enabled with tsgo now.
- Changes some doc formatting logic for tsjs to match tsgo, and update fixtures.
- Coupled with:
  - https://github.com/denoland/typescript-go/pull/28
  - https://github.com/denoland/typescript-go/pull/29
  - https://github.com/denoland/typescript-go/pull/30
- Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32425
author: nayeemrmn

reviews.body:
looks solid overall. a few things:

1. **`PendingChangeTracker::take_change` logic** — when `valid_config` exists and files changed (opened/closed), you clone the config, mutate it, then compare via `fill_project_files`. if the hash doesn't change, you still return `Some(TsGoWorkspaceChange { new_configuration: None })` which is correct, but the clone was wasted. minor, but could early-exit before cloning if you tracked whether any opened/closed changes exist first.

2. **hash collision risk** — `FastInsecureHasher` for `project_files_hash` is fine for change detection, but the name says it all. if two different file sets hash to the same value, config updates get swallowed. extremely unlikely in practice, but worth a comment.

3. **test util expansion** — the `InitializeParamsBuilder` changes add a lot of hardcoded capability values (completion item kinds 1-25, etc). is there a reason to not derive these from the lsp_types enums to stay in sync automatically?

4. **`as_ts_script_kind` returns i32** — would an enum be cleaner here? you're mapping LanguageId → typescript's ScriptKind, having a named enum makes the intent clearer and prevents magic numbers.

5. **json patching for capabilities** — the `relatedInformation` and `tagSupport` insertion via nested `.unwrap()` chains is gnarly. comment says waiting on ls-types fix which is fair, but maybe a helper fn to make it less error-prone?

the core change (syncing root files on open/close, hashing to detect changes) makes sense and the test coverage bump to 189/247 is nice.

reviews.body:
lgtm — good ECDH fixes

key changes:
- `to_sec1_bytes()` → `to_encoded_point(false)` fixes the panic by producing uncompressed 65-byte output instead of compressed 33-byte
- hybrid format implementation is correct: uncompressed form with first byte modified (02/03 → 06/07)
- `setPrivateKey` string decoding fix looks right
- `convertKey` static method implementation is clean

one minor thing: the hybrid format logic is duplicated between `convertKey` and `getPublicKey` — could be extracted to a helper, but not blocking
LGTM

title: fix(ext/otel): remove panicking unwraps in telemetry code
body:
## Summary

- Replace `.unwrap()` calls on `OTEL_GLOBALS.get()` and isolate slot accesses
  with graceful error handling throughout `ext/telemetry/lib.rs`
- GC prologue/epilogue callbacks now return early if the isolate slot is missing
  (can happen during isolate teardown in watch mode restarts)
- `op_otel_enable_isolate_metrics` / `op_otel_collect_isolate_metrics` return
  early if globals or slots are not available
- `OtelTracer::builtin()`, `OtelTracer::start_span()`,
  `OtelTracer::start_span_foreign()`, and `OtelMeter::new()` return JS errors
  instead of panicking

## Context

These unwraps could panic in watch mode when:
1. V8 GC callbacks fire during isolate teardown after slots have been cleaned up
2. OTEL globals are accessed before initialization completes

Refs: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/29478
Refs: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/30567

## Test plan

- Existing otel spec tests pass (confirmed no regressions in passing tests)
- Manual testing with `OTEL_DENO=true deno run --watch` + rapid file changes shows no panics
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
lgtm — good defensive fix for watch mode crashes

all the changes are straightforward:
- `.unwrap()` → `.ok_or_else()` with proper JS error for ops
- `.unwrap()` → early return for GC callbacks (can't propagate errors from V8 callbacks anyway)
- isolate slot access now guards against teardown race conditions

the GC callbacks returning early is the right approach since they're called from V8 and can't meaningfully report errors

commits.message:
fix(ext/otel): remove panicking unwraps in telemetry code

Replace `.unwrap()` calls on `OTEL_GLOBALS.get()` and isolate slot
accesses with graceful error handling. These unwraps could panic
during watch mode restarts when the isolate is being torn down
(GC callbacks fire after slots are cleaned up) or if OTEL globals
are not yet initialized.

- GC prologue/epilogue callbacks: return early if isolate slot is gone
- op_otel_enable_isolate_metrics: return early if OTEL_GLOBALS unset
- op_otel_collect_isolate_metrics: return early if slot is gone
- OtelTracer/OtelMeter constructors: return JS errors instead of panicking

Refs: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/29478
Refs: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/30567

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: refactor(core): clean up RecursiveModuleLoad API
body:
## Summary

Cleans up the `RecursiveModuleLoad` API in `deno_core` by addressing several long-standing design issues:

- **Remove `RefCell` hack**: `LoadInit::Side.code` was wrapped in `RefCell<Option<String>>` solely because `prepare(&self)` needed to `.take()` it. Changing `prepare` to `&mut self` removes the need for interior mutability.
- **Eliminate triple resolution**: The root specifier was resolved independently in `new()`, `prepare()`, and `poll_next(Init)`. Now it's resolved once in `new()` and cached in a `resolved_specifier` field, reused by the other two call sites.
- **Merge construct + prepare into async constructors**: `main()` and `side()` are now `async` and call `prepare()` internally, removing the need for the `ModuleMap::load_main` / `ModuleMap::load_side` wrapper functions.
- **Privatize leaked fields**: `id`, `root_module_id`, and `root_module_reference` were `pub` fields set as side effects. They're now private with `pub(crate)` getter methods.
- **Add `run_to_completion()` method**: Encapsulates the identical while-let-next pump loop that was copy-pasted across 3 call sites (`load_main_es_module_from_code`, `load_side_es_module_from_code`, `do_load_job`). The dynamic import path (`drain_dyn_imports`) remains poll-based and continues using the `Stream` directly.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p deno_core` — clean, no warnings
- [x] `cargo test -p deno_core` — 352 passed
- [x] `cargo test -p deno_core_testing` — 54 passed
- [x] Updated 3 dynamic-import test assertions for reduced resolve counts (triple → single resolution)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
Clean API cleanup. The triple-resolution elimination and `run_to_completion` helper are good wins.

A few things:

1. **`run_to_completion` consumes self**: The method signature `async fn run_to_completion(mut self, ...)` consumes the load. This is fine for the three call sites that were copy-pasting the while-let loop, but worth noting that callers can't do anything with the load after completion (not that they need to, but it's a departure from the previous mutable-borrow pattern).

2. **Resolved specifier caching**: Good that errors are deferred to `prepare()`/`poll_next()`, but:
```rust
let resolved_specifier =
  Self::resolve_root_from_init(&init, &module_map_rc).ok();
```
If resolution fails here, `resolved_specifier` is `None`, and you'll resolve again in `poll_next` (the `LoadState::Init` branch). So for error cases you still resolve twice — once in `new()` (which fails and is ignored), once in `poll_next()` (which fails and surfaces). Minor, but might be worth short-circuiting.

3. **Test assertion changes**: The resolve count reductions (4→2, 5→3) in the tests are the expected outcome of removing the triple resolution. Good that these are explicitly called out.

4. **TrackedFutures/TrackedVec**: I see this same pattern in #32440 — might be worth extracting to a shared utility if both PRs land, to avoid duplication.

LGTM overall.
LGTM

commits.message:
refactor(deno_core): consolidate pending-flag pattern in ModuleMap

Replace 4 pairs of `RefCell<Collection>` + `Cell<bool>` flags with
`TrackedFutures<F>` and `TrackedVec<T>` wrapper types that maintain
the pending flag automatically. This eliminates ~20 manual flag updates
and prevents bugs where a flag could get out of sync with its collection.

Also adds clearing of `pending_dyn_mod_evaluations` in `destroy()`,
which was previously missing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(deno_core): drop misleading Poll return types from drain methods

The three inner methods called by `poll_progress` (`poll_prepare_dyn_imports`,
`poll_dyn_imports`, `poll_code_cache_ready`) loop internally until they drain
everything ready, then always return `Poll::Ready`. This made their `Poll`
return type misleading — they are not suspendable.

Rename them to `drain_*`, change return types from `Poll<T>` to plain
`T` (or `()`), and remove the now-unnecessary `assert!(_.is_ready())`
calls in the parent method.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(deno_core): simplify drain methods and remove dead module field

- Remove unused `module` field from `DynImportModEvaluate`. It was only
  opened as `_module` in `evaluate_dyn_imports` and the module is already
  rooted via `ModuleMapData::handles`.

- Flatten `evaluate_dyn_imports`: replace `Option<Result<..>>` indirection
  with a direct match on promise state.

- Convert `drain_prepare_dyn_imports` and `drain_dyn_imports` from
  `loop`/`if let`/`continue`/`return` to `while let` loops.

- Flatten nested matches in `drain_dyn_imports` by matching
  `Some(Ok(..))`, `Some(Err(..))`, `None` directly.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(deno_core): clean up RecursiveModuleLoad API

- Remove RefCell hack: change prepare(&self) to prepare(&mut self),
  simplifying LoadInit::Side.code from RefCell<Option<String>> to
  Option<String>
- Cache resolved specifier: resolve root specifier once in new() and
  reuse in prepare()/poll_next(), eliminating triple resolution
- Merge construct + prepare: make main()/side() async constructors
  that call prepare() internally, removing ModuleMap::load_main/load_side
- Privatize fields: make id, root_module_id, root_module_reference
  private with pub(crate) getters
- Add run_to_completion(): encapsulates the repeated pump loop pattern,
  eliminating 3 copy-pasted while-let-next loops across callers

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(deno_core): cache resolution errors to avoid double resolution

Address review feedback: store the full Result from resolve_root_from_init
instead of discarding errors with .ok(). This avoids resolving twice in
error cases (once in new() where it fails and is ignored, once in
prepare()/poll_next() where it fails and surfaces).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

- [x] Add test in `tests/unit/event_test.ts` verifying `removeEventListener` works with empty object options
- [x] Verify fix works and existing tests pass (28/28 pass)

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> 
> ----
> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>removeEventListener fails to remove event handler if empty object option is specified</issue_title>
> <issue_description>```js
> const target = new EventTarget();
> target.addEventListener("foo", console.log)
> target.removeEventListener("foo", console.log, {})
> target.dispatchEvent(new Event("foo"));
> ```
>

title: refactor(ext/node): consolidate fs modules (batch 2)
body:
## Summary

Continues the consolidation started in #32502 by inlining 5 more small `_fs` modules into `fs.ts`:

- **`_fs_fsync.ts`** → `fsync`, `fsyncSync` inlined into `fs.ts`
- **`_fs_fdatasync.ts`** → `fdatasync`, `fdatasyncSync` inlined into `fs.ts`
- **`_fs_link.ts`** → `link`, `linkSync` inlined into `fs.ts`
- **`_fs_unlink.ts`** → `unlink`, `unlinkSync` inlined into `fs.ts`
- **`_fs_fchmod.ts`** → `fchmod`, `fchmodSync` inlined into `fs.ts`

Additional changes:
- **`internal/fs/promises.ts`**: inlined `link`/`unlink` promise versions
- **`internal/fs/handle.ts`**: imports `fchmod`/`fdatasync`/`fsync` from `node:fs` and creates promisified versions locally
- **`cp/cp.ts`** and **`cp/cp_sync.ts`**: use `Deno.remove`/`Deno.removeSync` directly instead of importing from the now-deleted `_fs_unlink.ts`
- **Tests**: consolidated from 4 separate test files into `fs_test.ts`

This removes 5 small modules from the module graph, continuing to reduce startup overhead.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p deno_node` passes
- [x] `tools/lint.js --js` passes
- [x] `tools/format.js` passes
- [ ] CI tests pass

title: fix(ext/node): implement proper `resourceLimits` for `node:worker_threads`
body:
## Summary

Implements proper `resourceLimits` support for `node:worker_threads`, matching Node.js behavior:

- **Individual V8 ResourceConstraints**: Uses `set_max_old_generation_size_in_bytes`, `set_max_young_generation_size_in_bytes`, `set_code_range_size_in_bytes`
- **Stack size**: Sets OS thread stack size via `std::thread::Builder::stack_size()` for `stackSizeMb`
- **Resolved limits**: Reads back V8 defaults for unspecified values via new `op_worker_get_resource_limits` op, so `resourceLimits` inside the worker always shows actual values (matching Node.js behavior)
- **OOM handling**: Installs near-heap-limit callback that terminates the worker with `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY` error code
- **Init ordering fix**: Stores resolved limits in op_state before `worker.bootstrap()` so the polyfill can read them during init

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/26156
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/10036
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
feat(ext/node): implement `resourceLimits` for `node:worker_threads`

Implements the `resourceLimits` option for `node:worker_threads` Worker
constructor, resolving #26156. Previously, resourceLimits was accepted
but silently ignored.

- Thread `resourceLimits` from JS through `op_create_worker` to the V8
  isolate's `CreateParams::heap_limits()`
- Install a near-heap-limit callback that gracefully terminates the
  worker instead of crashing the entire process with a fatal V8 OOM
- Emit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY` error with exit code 1, matching
  Node.js behavior
- Expose correct `resourceLimits` inside the worker via serialized
  metadata, and clear to `{}` after worker exits
- Enable `test-worker-resource-limits.js` node_compat test
