title: fix(core): switch to V8 Explicit microtask policy for correct nextTick ordering
body:
Closes #27281

## Summary
- Switches deno_core from V8's **Auto** microtask policy to **Explicit** so that `process.nextTick` callbacks run before `Promise.then` callbacks, matching Node.js behavior
- Guards pre-drain `perform_microtask_checkpoint()` calls with `has_tick_scheduled()` so microtasks are deferred to `processTicksAndRejections` when ticks are pending
- Removes redundant policy toggles in `exception_to_err` (error.rs) since policy is now always Explicit
- Adds explicit `perform_microtask_checkpoint()` after `module.evaluate()` in `mod_evaluate_sync` to ensure module evaluation promises resolve under Explicit policy

## How it works
Under Auto policy, V8 auto-drains microtasks after JS calls, running `.then` callbacks before deno_core can drain the nextTick queue. Under Explicit policy, microtask draining only happens at explicit `perform_microtask_checkpoint()` calls. By guarding these checkpoints with `has_tick_scheduled()`, we defer microtask draining to `__drainNextTickAndMacrotasks` → `processTicksAndRejections`, which drains nextTick first, then microtasks — matching Node.js.

I think at the end of all these refactors we might end up with something like `InternalCallbackScope` from Node.js, but for now, just moving to explicit microtask queue is a pretty big deal.
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: nice!

commits.message:
reland: refactor(core): move nextTick/immediate queues into core, replace ops with shared buffers (#32440)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(core): dispatch rejectionhandled before unhandledrejection

The nextTick rewrite moved unhandled rejection processing into
processTicksAndRejections (JS-side), but "rejectionhandled" events
were still only dispatched from Rust's dispatch_rejections, which
runs after tick draining. This caused rejectionhandled to fire after
unhandledrejection for later promises, breaking the expected ordering.

Fix by extracting dispatch_handled_rejections and calling it before
both the tick drain in the event loop and between timer callbacks.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
reset CI
fix

fix(core): switch to V8 Explicit microtask policy for correct nextTick ordering

Switch deno_core from V8's Auto microtask policy to Explicit policy so that
process.nextTick callbacks run before Promise.then callbacks, matching Node.js
behavior.

Under Auto policy, V8 drains promise microtasks at internal checkpoints before
deno_core gets a chance to drain the nextTick queue. With Explicit policy,
microtask checkpoints only happen when we explicitly call
perform_microtask_checkpoint(), allowing __drainNextTickAndMacrotasks (via
processTicksAndRejections) to interleave nextTick before microtasks.

Changes:
- Set Explicit microtask policy in create_isolate() (setup.rs)
- Remove redundant policy toggles in exception_to_err (error.rs)
- Guard pre-drain microtask checkpoints with has_tick_scheduled() so
  microtasks are deferred to the tick drain when ticks are pending
- Add perform_microtask_checkpoint() after module.evaluate() in
  mod_evaluate_sync to ensure module evaluation promises resolve under
  Explicit policy
- Guard module map dynamic import checkpoints with has_tick_scheduled()
- Add tests for nextTick vs Promise.then ordering

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into explicit-microtask-policy
Merge branch 'main' into explicit-microtask-policy

fix(ffi): flush microtasks in thread-safe callback before unblocking caller

With explicit microtask policy, microtasks queued by FFI thread-safe
callbacks don't run automatically. Add perform_microtask_checkpoint()
after the callback runs inside spawn_blocking so microtasks flush
before the calling C thread is unblocked.

fix: update expected stack traces for async ops test

The lazy and deferred async throw tests now include
processTicksAndRejections and __drainNextTickAndMacrotasks frames
that appear after moving tick/immediate queues into core.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use contains-based assertions for lazy/deferred async op stacks

The stack trace for lazy/deferred async ops is non-deterministic: when
no ticks are scheduled, the microtask runs inside op_run_microtasks()
(no processTicksAndRejections frame); when ticks are scheduled, it
runs inside processTicksAndRejections(). Use contains-based assertions
to handle both paths.

title: ci: Add GHA automation for daily issue and PR insights
body:
## Summary

Adds a daily GitHub Actions workflow that posts issue and PR insights to Slack,
helping the team stay on top of new activity and items that need attention.

### What's included

- **`tools/issue_pr_insights.ts`** — Deno script that:
  - Fetches new issues and PRs created since the last run (using
    [hashy](https://hashy.deno.deno.net) for timestamp persistence)
  - Identifies issues with zero comments and PRs with no comments or reviews
  - Posts a formatted summary to a configured Slack channel

- **`.github/workflows/issue_pr_insights.yml`** — GHA workflow that:
  - Runs on a daily schedule (9 AM UTC, Mon–Fri)
  - Supports manual trigger with a configurable `hours_lookback` input
  - Requires `SLACK_TOKEN` and `SLACK_CHANNEL` secrets

### How it works

1. On each run, the script reads the last-run timestamp from hashy (or
   computes one from the `HOURS_LOOKBACK` input for manual runs)
2. Fetches all issues and PRs created since that timestamp via the GitHub API
3. Filters for "no response" items: issues with 0 comments, PRs with no
   comments and no reviews
4. Posts a Slack message with counts and linked lists of no-response items
5. Saves the current timestamp to hashy for the next run

### Secrets required

| Secret | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `GITHUB_TOKEN` | Provided automatically by GHA |
| `SLACK_TOKEN` | Slack Bot OAuth token with `chat:write` scope |
| `SLACK_CHANNEL` | Slack channel ID to post to |

title: refactor(core): move nextTick/immediate queues into core, replace ops with shared buffers
body:
## Summary

Major refactoring of the nextTick/immediate queue infrastructure, moving it from Node polyfills into deno_core and replacing op-based communication with shared memory buffers.

### Move nextTick queue into core
- Remove `macrotaskCallback` array and the polling return-value protocol
- Collapse macrotask + nextTick callback arrays into a single `tickCallback` slot
- Simplify `__drainNextTickAndMacrotasks` to just call the tick callback + microtasks (no more while-loop polling)
- Move `FixedQueue`, tick queue, and drain loop (`processTicksAndRejections`, `runNextTicks`, `queueNextTick`) from `ext/node/polyfills/_next_tick.ts` into `libs/core/01_core.js`
- `ext/node/polyfills/_next_tick.ts` becomes a thin wrapper: `nextTick()` handles Node-specific validation, async hooks, and exit checks, then delegates to `core.queueNextTick()`
- `setTickHook()` allows Node polyfills to inject per-tick async hook behavior without core depending on Node internals
- `process.ts` no longer registers `setTickCallback`/`setMacrotaskCallback` — just calls `enableNextTick()` which sets up the tick hook

### Move immediate queue into core
- Move `setImmediate`/`clearImmediate` queue management from `ext/node/polyfills/timers.ts` into `libs/core/01_core.js`
- Add async hooks support (`emitInit`/`emitBefore`/`emitAfter`/`emitDestroy`) for immediates via `setImmediateHook()`
- Interleave promise rejection processing with nextTick drain loop
- Catch errors in nextTick callbacks and route through `uncaughtException`

### Replace ops with shared typed array buffers
- Replace `op_has_tick_scheduled` / `op_set_has_tick_scheduled` with a shared `Uint8Array` (`tickInfo`) backed by `ContextState::tick_info: Box<[u8; 2]>`
- Replace `op_immediate_count` / `op_immediate_ref_count` / `op_immediate_set_has_outstanding` / `op_immediate_has_ref_count` with a shared `Uint32Array` (`immediateInfo`) backed by `ContextState::immediate_info: Box<[u32; 3]>`
- JS reads/writes the shared buffers directly — no JS-to-Rust boundary crossing needed
- Shared buffers are passed from Rust to JS via init-only `__setTickInfo` / `__setImmediateInfo` setter functions during `store_js_callbacks`
- `__drainNextTickAndMacrotasks` now takes no arguments (reads `tickInfo` directly)

### Wire up `hasRejectionToWarn`
- Set `tickInfo[1]` in `promise_reject_callback` (Rust) when `PromiseRejectWithNoHandler`
- `runNextTicks()` now checks `!hasTickScheduled() && !hasRejectionToWarn()`, so pending promise rejections are processed even when no ticks are queued (matching Node.js behavior)
- `processPromiseRejections()` clears the flag after draining

refactor(core): move nextTick queue into core, remove macrotask/callback indirection

Phase 2: Remove macrotaskCallback array and the polling return-value protocol.

Simplify __drainNextTickAndMacrotasks to just call the tick callback + microtasks.

Phase 3: Move FixedQueue, tick queue, and drain loop (processTicksAndRejections,
runNextTicks, queueNextTick) from ext/node/polyfills/_next_tick.ts into
libs/core/01_core.js. Core now owns the queue directly —
__drainNextTickAndMacrotasks calls processTicksAndRejections inline.

ext/node/polyfills/_next_tick.ts becomes a thin wrapper: nextTick() handles
Node-specific validation, async hooks (emitInit/emitBefore/emitAfter/emitDestroy),
and exit checks, then delegates to core.queueNextTick(). The tick hook mechanism
(setTickHook) allows Node polyfills to inject per-tick async hook behavior
without core depending on Node internals.

process.ts no longer registers setTickCallback/setMacrotaskCallback — just
calls enableNextTick() which sets up the tick hook.

that get drained in the same cycle.

Errors thrown in nextTick callbacks were propagating out of

title: fix(ext/node): support HTTP over Windows named pipes in node:http
body:
Add Windows Named Pipe support to Deno's Node.js HTTP compatibility layer, enabling libraries like `dockerode`/`docker-modem` that use `http.request({socketPath: "//./pipe/docker_engine"})` to work on Windows.

Add `WindowsPipe` variant to `NetworkStream` with proper handling across HTTP server, request properties, and Node HTTP client ops.

Implement EBUSY (ERROR_PIPE_BUSY / os error 231) retry logic in `Pipe.connect()` for Windows, emulating libuv's `WaitNamedPipeW` behavior with polling retries.
author: fraidev

reviews.body:
overall looks good - nice to get dockerode working on windows.

couple things:

1. **EBUSY retry loop** (line 439): parsing error messages for `"os error 231"` is brittle. if the error message format changes, this breaks silently. ideally rust would expose the raw os error code properly. that said, if this is how the error surfaces today, it's pragmatic.

2. **50 retries × 100ms = 5s max wait** - libuv uses `WaitNamedPipeW(30000)` which blocks up to 30s. might want to bump this or document why 5s is sufficient.

3. **`tokio::task::yield_now().await`** (http.rs line 346): this is a bit fragile - you're hoping one yield is enough for the cancelled ops to be polled and drop their Rc refs. in theory this should work but if there's ever a case where it doesn't, this fails with a confusing BadResourceId. might be worth a comment explaining the invariant.

4. **WindowsPipeListener stub** - the accept/local_addr methods just return errors. would `unimplemented!()` or `panic!()` be clearer that these should never be called? returning `Unsupported` suggests it's a recoverable error when really it's a bug if they're ever invoked.

5. no tests in this PR - is there a way to test this in CI without docker? even a basic named pipe echo test would catch regressions.

lgtm, you addressed my feedback.

the retry loop now matches libuv's 30s timeout, the yield invariant has a good comment explaining when/why it's safe, and the test is a nice minimal repro. good work.

re: WindowsPipeListener - your call on error vs panic. returning an error is more defensive i suppose, especially if something unexpected invokes it. not worth blocking on.

commits.message: fix(ext/node): support HTTP over Windows named pipes in node:http
lint
retry on ebusy
add test
use 30s like node
add comment to yield
format
fix test
fix settimeout
check error by code not msg

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): fix `dgram.addMembership` EINVAL error

Fix two bugs causing `addMembership` to always throw EINVAL:

1. `op_net_validate_multicast` incorrectly required the interface address
   to be a multicast address. The interface address is a local address
   (e.g. 0.0.0.0 for INADDR_ANY), not a multicast address.

2. When `interfaceAddress` is omitted (the common case), `undefined` was
   passed to the Rust op which expects a string. Now defaults to "0.0.0.0"
   for IPv4 and interface index 0 for IPv6, matching Node.js behavior.

Closes #31059

title: fix(ext/node): make UDP socket ops synchronous, implement missing dgram methods
body:
## Summary

- Creates a new `NodeUdpSocketResource` in `ext/node` that stores `tokio::net::UdpSocket` directly (no `AsyncRefCell`), making all socket config operations synchronous
- Replaces `udp_wrap.ts`'s dependency on `ext:deno_net/01_net.js` DatagramConn with direct sync ops
- Implements previously unimplemented methods: `setTTL`, `setMulticastInterface`, `addSourceSpecificMembership`, `dropSourceSpecificMembership`
- Removes artificial 64KB cap on `recvBufferSize`/`sendBufferSize` options

### Why

Node.js expects `addMembership`, `setBroadcast`, `setMulticastTTL`, etc. to be synchronous. The previous implementation wrapped Deno's `DatagramConn` which stored the socket in `AsyncRefCell`, forcing all socket config to be async. This caused `addMembership` to be fire-and-forget (never awaited), silently swallowing errors and breaking multicast (e.g. SSDP discovery).

### New ops in `ext/node/ops/udp.rs`

**Sync ops** (socket config):
- `op_node_udp_bind` — bind with socket2, returns `(rid, hostname, port)`
- `op_node_udp_join_multi_v4/v6`, `op_node_udp_leave_multi_v4/v6`
- `op_node_udp_set_broadcast`, `op_node_udp_set_multicast_loopback`, `op_node_udp_set_multicast_ttl`
- `op_node_udp_set_ttl`, `op_node_udp_set_multicast_interface`
- `op_node_udp_join_source_specific`, `op_node_udp_leave_source_specific`

**Async ops** (data transfer):
- `op_node_udp_send`, `op_node_udp_recv`

Supersedes #32287.

Fixes #18324
Fixes #28421
Fixes #30486

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo check -p deno_node` — compiles cleanly, no warnings
- [x] New unit tests for `addMembership`/`dropMembership`/`setBroadcast`/`setMulticastTTL`
- [x] New unit tests for `setTTL`, `setMulticastInterface`, source-specific multicast
- [x] New unit test for large `recvBufferSize`/`sendBufferSize` (4MB)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

solid fix. storing the socket directly without AsyncRefCell and making the config ops sync is the right call for Node compat.

a couple minor things:

1. `op_node_udp_set_multicast_interface` line 286 - the `.unwrap_or(0)` silently swallows parse errors for ipv6 interface index. might want to return an error instead so users know their input was invalid.

2. `setTTL` validation is done on the TS side (1-255) but the rust side takes u32 - not a bug, but could add validation in rust for defense in depth.

tests look good. nice that you covered the edge cases like invalid TTL values and large buffer sizes.

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): make UDP socket ops synchronous for Node.js compat

Node.js expects multicast join/leave, setBroadcast, setMulticastTTL,
and setMulticastLoopback to be synchronous. The previous implementation
wrapped Deno's DatagramConn which stored the socket in AsyncRefCell,
forcing all socket config to be async and silently swallowing errors.

Adds a new NodeUdpSocketResource that stores tokio::net::UdpSocket
directly (no AsyncRefCell needed since all config methods take &self).
New sync ops for socket config, async ops for send/recv.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat(node/dgram): implement setMulticastInterface, setTTL, and source-specific multicast

Implements the remaining unimplemented UDP socket methods:
- setMulticastInterface using socket2::SockRef::set_multicast_if_v4/v6
- setTTL using socket2::SockRef::set_ttl
- addSourceSpecificMembership/dropSourceSpecificMembership using
  libc::setsockopt with IP_ADD/DROP_SOURCE_MEMBERSHIP

All ops are synchronous, going through NodeUdpSocketResource directly.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
fmt

fix: make source-specific multicast ops cross-platform

Remove #[cfg(unix)] from SSM ops and add Windows support using
windows-sys WinSock setsockopt. Also run formatter.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(node/dgram): allow large recvBufferSize and sendBufferSize

Remove the artificial UDP_DGRAM_MAXSIZE (64KB) cap on buffer sizes in
bufferSize(). Node.js allows setting large SO_RCVBUF/SO_SNDBUF values
(e.g. 4MB) via recvBufferSize/sendBufferSize options without error.

Fixes #30486

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: address CI failures - clippy casts and EINVAL validation

Remove unnecessary `as i32` casts flagged by clippy on Windows, and add
upfront address validation in addMembership/dropMembership so invalid
multicast addresses return EINVAL even on unbound sockets (matching
Node.js behavior).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: validate addresses before rid check in source-specific membership ops

Add IPv4 address validation to addSourceSpecificMembership and
dropSourceSpecificMembership so that invalid addresses (like '0')
return EINVAL even on unbound sockets, matching Node.js behavior.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(npm): NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY now correctly overrides .npmrc registry
body: Fixes #31392. Previously .npmrc registry had higher priority than NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY env var, which contradicts npm behavior.
author: kajukitli

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(npm): NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY now correctly overrides .npmrc registry

Fixes #31392

Previously, the .npmrc registry setting had higher priority than the
NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY environment variable, which contradicts npm's
documented behavior and user expectations.

Changes:
- Modified registry_url_and_config_for_maybe_scope() to check
  NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY before falling back to .npmrc registry
- Added as_resolved_with_env() method to pass environment access
- Updated resolver to use new method with environment variable access
- Added comprehensive unit tests and integration test to verify the priority order

The priority is now correctly:
1. Scoped registries (for scoped packages)
2. NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY environment variable
3. .npmrc registry setting
4. Default registry fallback

address review: clarify env_registry_url handling

The issue is that env_registry_url already contains NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY
if set, but we need to distinguish between 'env var was set' vs 'using
default fallback' to determine priority over .npmrc registry setting.

Added clear comments explaining the approach.
fix: format long assert_eq! lines properly

refactor: use NpmRegistryUrl struct instead of callback

Address review feedback: instead of passing a callback to check
NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY, return a struct from npm_registry_url() that
tracks whether the URL came from an env var.

title: fix(ext/node): support SEC1 EC private key import/export in `node:crypto`
body:
## Summary

- Fix SEC1 EC private key import failing with "malformed or missing named curve in ec parameters" when the optional `parameters` field is absent
- Fix SEC1 EC private key export to include the curve OID in the `parameters` field, matching OpenSSL/Node.js behavior
- The `elliptic-curve` crate's `to_sec1_der()` omits the optional parameters field, so Deno-exported keys couldn't be re-imported

### Import fix
When the curve OID is missing from the PKCS#8 `AlgorithmIdentifier`, fall back to:
1. Extracting it from the inner SEC1 `ECPrivateKey.parameters` field
2. Inferring the curve from the private key byte length (28→P-224, 32→P-256, 48→P-384)

### Export fix
After calling `to_sec1_der()`, re-encode with the curve OID added to the `parameters` field.

Closes #22920

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit test covering SEC1 PEM/DER round-trip for all four EC curves (P-224, P-256, P-384, secp256k1)
- [x] `cargo test unit_node::crypto` passes
- [x] Manual verification with reproduction script from the issue

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
the len-to-oid inference is sketchy:

1. 32-byte ambiguity between P-256 and secp256k1 - you just "prefer P-256" and try both. but if someone has a valid secp256k1 key, it'll silently get parsed as P-256 first (which might succeed with garbage output depending on the bytes)

2. the fallback `or_else` only triggers if P-256 parsing *fails* - but both curves accept any 32-byte value as a valid scalar (as long as it's < curve order). so secp256k1 keys will almost always get misidentified as P-256

3. no P-521 support in the inference (65-byte keys)

the real fix is to require the curve OID or fail. inferring from length is just asking for silent data corruption on secp256k1 keys
nice, requiring the curve OID instead of inferring from length is the right call. lgtm :+1:
lgtm

commits.message:
fix(node): support SEC1 EC private key import/export in `node:crypto`

SEC1 EC private keys without the optional `parameters` field (curve OID)
could not be imported, failing with "malformed or missing named curve in
ec parameters". This happened because the `elliptic-curve` crate omits
the parameters field in `to_sec1_der()`, and the import path required it.

Fix import by falling back to the inner SEC1 key's parameters or
inferring the curve from private key byte length. Fix export by
re-encoding with the curve OID included to match OpenSSL/Node.js.

Closes #22920

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: require curve OID for SEC1 EC key import instead of inferring from length

Inferring the curve from private key byte length is unreliable since
P-256 and secp256k1 both use 32-byte keys, which could lead to silent
misidentification. Require the curve OID or fail.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: perf(install): split tarball extraction into decompress + write, limit write concurrency
body:
Split tarball extraction into a CPU-bound decompression phase and an I/O-bound filesystem write phase. Gate the write phase with a semaphore to reduce fs contention.

This is on a create-next-app project.
```
❯ hyperfine --warmup 1 --prepare "deno clean; rm -rf node_modules deno.lock" "deno install" "~/Documents/Code/deno/target/release-lite/deno install"
Benchmark 1: deno install
  Time (mean ± σ):     13.662 s ±  0.823 s    [User: 3.586 s, System: 7.008 s]
  Range (min … max):   12.364 s … 14.531 s    10 runs

Benchmark 2: ~/Documents/Code/deno/target/release-lite/deno install
  Time (mean ± σ):     12.889 s ±  0.926 s    [User: 2.524 s, System: 3.318 s]
  Range (min … max):   11.487 s … 14.400 s    10 runs

Summary
  ~/Documents/Code/deno/target/release-lite/deno install ran
    1.06 ± 0.10 times faster than deno install
```
Note both wall time and especially system time is decreased, since it's not trying to write a ton of files at the same time

Before submitting a PR, please read https://docs.deno.com/runtime/manual/references/contributing

    - fix(std/http): Fix race condition in server

  Examples of bad title:

6. Ensure `./tools/lint.js` passes.
7. Open as a draft PR if your work is still in progress. The CI won't run

perf(npm): split tarball extraction into decompress + write phases

Split tarball extraction into a CPU-bound decompression phase and an
I/O-bound filesystem write phase. Gate the write phase with a semaphore
(4 permits) to reduce filesystem contention on macOS APFS, which has an
internal mutex that causes heavy contention with many parallel file ops.

title: fix(ext/node): CBC decipher setAutoPadding(false) incorrectly strips bytes
body:
## Summary

- `Decipheriv.final()` unconditionally stripped PKCS7 padding from decrypted output regardless of the `#autoPadding` flag, corrupting data when `setAutoPadding(false)` was used
- Made the PKCS7 padding stripping conditional on `this.#autoPadding`
- Added tests for `aes-256-cbc` and `aes-128-cbc` roundtrips with `setAutoPadding(false)`

Closes #28381

## Test plan

- [ ] `aes-256-cbc` encrypt/decrypt roundtrip with `setAutoPadding(false)` returns original plaintext
- [ ] `aes-128-cbc` encrypt/decrypt roundtrip with `setAutoPadding(false)` returns original plaintext
- [ ] Existing cipher tests still pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
the fix looks correct - PKCS7 padding removal should only happen when autoPadding is true.

one thing to double-check: what happens if `buf.at(-1)` is 0 when autoPadding is true? that would make `buf.subarray(0, 16)` which keeps the full buffer - seems fine but might be worth understanding. corrupted padding could have values outside 1-16 range. might be worth adding validation that the padding byte is in valid range (1-16) to catch invalid padding early, though that's technically a separate issue from this fix.

tests look good - roundtrip is the right approach for verifying this.

lgtm - clean fix. the PKCS7 unpadding was unconditionally applied even when `setAutoPadding(false)` was set, which would strip legitimate data bytes.

one edge case worth noting: when `autoPadding=false` and the last byte of plaintext happens to be a small value (like 0x01-0x0f), the old code would've silently corrupted the output by treating it as padding length. good catch.

fix(ext/node): CBC decipher `setAutoPadding(false)` incorrectly strips bytes

`Decipheriv.final()` unconditionally stripped PKCS7 padding from decrypted
output regardless of the `#autoPadding` flag, corrupting data when
`setAutoPadding(false)` was used. Make the stripping conditional on
`#autoPadding`.

length. While the Rust layer already validates padding via
decrypt_padded_b2b_mut::<Pkcs7>, this guards against silent data

title: fix(ext/node): implement KeyObject.equals, fix createCipheriv with KeyObject/null IV
body:
## Summary

Builds on top of #32267 (must be merged first). Implements `KeyObject.prototype.equals()` and fixes `createCipheriv`/`createDecipheriv` to accept `KeyObject` keys and `null` IVs.

Closes #27211
Closes #27295
Closes #28405

## What changed

### `KeyObject.prototype.equals` (new)

- Added `PartialEq` implementations for all key types in the Rust layer (`EcPublicKey`, `EcPrivateKey`, `RsaPssPublicKey`, `RsaPssPrivateKey`, `DhPublicKey`, `DhPrivateKey`, `AsymmetricPublicKey`, `AsymmetricPrivateKey`, `KeyObjectHandle`)
- Added `op_node_key_equals` op that compares two `KeyObjectHandle` cppgc references
- Wired up the JS `equals()` method to call the new op instead of throwing `ERR_NOT_IMPLEMENTED`

### `createCipheriv` / `createDecipheriv` fixes

- `toU8()` in cipher.ts now handles `KeyObject` inputs by extracting secret key bytes via `op_node_export_secret_key`
- `toU8()` now handles `null` IV (used by ECB mode) by converting to an empty `Uint8Array`
- This fixes the `@aws-crypto/client-node` crash reported in #28405

### Other fixes

- `symmetricKeySize` now returns **bytes** instead of bits, matching Node.js behavior
- `symmetricKeySize` returns `undefined` for asymmetric keys instead of throwing `notImplemented`
- `KeyObject` constructor now validates the `handle` argument type

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build` succeeds
- [x] `cargo check -p deno_node_crypto` passes
- [x] All 85 crypto key unit tests pass
- [x] Verified #27211 repro script works (Ed25519 X509 publicKey + equals)
- [x] Verified #28405 repro (`createCipheriv`/`createDecipheriv` with null IV)
- [ ] Full CI

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
looks good overall. adds comprehensive `PartialEq` impls and the p521 support is a nice bonus.

one issue:

**`symmetricKeySize` behavior change looks unintentional?**

the PR description says it fixes `symmetricKeySize` to return bytes instead of bits - but Node.js docs say it should return bytes (which is now correct). however, the base `KeyObject` class getter was changed to just return `undefined` unconditionally:

```ts
get symmetricKeySize(): number | undefined {
  return undefined;  // always returns undefined now
}
```

this means `SecretKeyObject` still works (it has its own getter calling `op_node_get_symmetric_key_size`), but if someone ever calls `symmetricKeySize` on the base class directly, it'll silently return `undefined` instead of throwing `notImplemented`. probably fine since `SecretKeyObject` is the only one that should use it, but worth noting.

rest looks solid :+1:

Thanks for the review!

Re: `symmetricKeySize` — the change is intentional. In Node.js, the base `KeyObject` class doesn't define a `symmetricKeySize` getter at all — only `SecretKeyObject` has it. So accessing `.symmetricKeySize` on a `PublicKeyObject` or `PrivateKeyObject` in Node.js returns `undefined` (property not found). Returning `undefined` from the base class getter matches that behavior. The previous `notImplemented()` throw was incorrect — it would blow up on any asymmetric key access, which Node.js doesn't do.
LGTM

title: fix(ext/node): support Ed25519, X25519, and P-521 keys in X509Certificate.publicKey
body:
## Summary

- Fixes `X509Certificate.prototype.publicKey` throwing "unsupported x509 public key type" for **Ed25519** and **X25519** certificates
- Fixes `X509Certificate.prototype.publicKey` throwing "unsupported ec named curve" for **P-521 (secp521r1)** EC certificates
- Adds full **P-521** EC key support across the `node:crypto` compat layer

Ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/27211
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/22879

## What changed

### Ed25519 / X25519 in X509Certificate.publicKey

`KeyObjectHandle::new_x509_public_key()` relied on x509-parser's `spki.parsed()` which returns `PublicKey::Unknown` for Ed25519/X25519 keys (the library doesn't have specific variants for these). The catch-all `_` arm now checks the algorithm OID and constructs the appropriate key objects.

### P-521 support (new)

Added P-521 (`secp521r1`) support everywhere P-384 was already handled:

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `Cargo.toml` | Added `ecdh` and `jwk` features to the `p521` workspace dep |
| `ext/node_crypto/Cargo.toml` | Added `p521.workspace = true` |
| `ext/node_crypto/keys.rs` | `EcPublicKey::P521` / `EcPrivateKey::P521` variants, OID constant, key parsing (PKCS8/SEC1/SPKI/X509), JWK import/export, DER export, key generation, key details |
| `ext/node_crypto/sign.rs` | ECDSA signing and verification with P-521 |
| `ext/node_crypto/lib.rs` | ECDH operations (encode, generate, compute secret, compute public key, diffie-hellman) |
| `ext/node_crypto/x509.rs` | X509 key info extraction for secp521r1 curve |

### Tests

Added two unit tests in `tests/unit_node/crypto/crypto_key_test.ts`:
- Ed25519 X509Certificate.publicKey extraction and comparison with standalone SPKI
- P-521 X509Certificate.publicKey extraction with key type/details verification

## What's still not implemented

The issue reporter also mentions `createPublicKey` failing for these certificate types — that path is separate from the X509 path and may need its own fix (untested here).

Other gaps found during this work:
- `KeyObject.prototype.equals` is not implemented (returns `ERR_NOT_IMPLEMENTED`)
- X25519 X509 certificates are handled but there are no known test certs to validate against

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build` succeeds
- [x] `cargo test -p deno_node_crypto` passes
- [x] Unit tests pass: `deno test --no-check --allow-all --config tests/config/deno.json tests/unit_node/crypto/crypto_key_test.ts --filter "X509Certificate publicKey for"`
- [ ] Full CI

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

looks solid. the X509 parsing fix for Ed25519/X25519 is clever - using the fallback `_` branch to check algorithm OIDs when x509-parser returns `PublicKey::Unknown`. 

P-521 support is comprehensive - added everywhere the other curves are handled (key gen, sign/verify, ECDH, import/export). proper OID (1.3.132.0.35) and consistent patterns.

tests look good - real cert data for both cases. the Ed25519 test even validates the extracted key matches standalone SPKI which is nice.

only nit - the P-521 test uses lowercase "p521" for namedCurve but other tests use "secp521r1". might want to check if that's consistent with node behavior
lgtm - straightforward P-521 addition and Ed25519/X25519 handling for X509. no issues.
LGTM

title: fix(node/crypto): handle empty input in decipher final with auto_pad=false
body:
## Summary

Fixes a panic in `node:crypto` when using `createDecipheriv` with `setAutoPadding(false)` (denoland/deno#31957).

Two cases caused panics in `DecipherContext::final`:
1. **Empty input** — `GenericArray::from_slice` panicked on a 0-length slice when it expected 16 bytes. Now returns `Ok(())` early.
2. **Invalid block length** (not a multiple of 16 bytes) — the assertion inside `generic-array` panicked. Now uses the existing `assert_block_len!` macro to return `DecipherError::InvalidFinalBlockLength` instead.

Affected cipher modes: `Aes128Cbc`, `Aes128Ecb`, `Aes192Ecb`, `Aes256Ecb`, `Aes256Cbc`.

## Changes

- `ext/node/ops/crypto/cipher.rs` — added empty-input guard and `assert_block_len!` validation for all 5 affected `(cipher, auto_pad=false)` match arms
- `tests/unit_node/crypto/crypto_cipher_test.ts` — regression tests for both empty input and invalid block length scenarios

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests for empty input with `setAutoPadding(false)` across all affected ciphers
- [x] Unit tests for invalid block length (10 bytes) returning `RangeError` instead of panicking
- [ ] `cargo test -p deno_node` passes

Fixes #31957

lgtm :+1:

title: fix(npm): peerDependency resolution leading to multiple versions being installed + hanging
body:
There are still weekly reports of Fresh users having errors because of duplicated `preact` or `@preact/signals` packages. So I spend the day hunting down various peer dependency installation issues.

Essentially, this PR rewrites our dependency resolution code to use a two-phase approach, which fixes duplicate peer dependencies or hanging installation.

Peer deps were resolved inline during BFS traversal, making results dependent on traversal order. If package lib (which peers with `react`) was encountered before `react` was added to the graph by a sibling, it would auto-resolve `react@18.2.0 (latest)`. When encountered again in a subtree where `react@18.3.0` already existed, it got a different resolution, which creates a duplicate lib entry. With patterns like Expo/React Native (100+ plugins all peering with `react`, `react-native`, and `expo`), this caused exponential blowup.

This issue affects practically every frontend project as the framework is always a peerDependency (`react`/`preact`/`vue`/etc) and most of them have some form of singletons which only work when one copy of the framework is installed.

Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/31179
Fixes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/21394
author: marvinhagemeister

reviews.body:
Amazing work!! This is going to be so much better.

Thanks a lot for looking into this.

commits.message: fix(install): don't install multiple versions of peerDependency packages

fix(install): use 2 phase install algorithm to fix broken peer dependency installations

We had a bunch of issues where we were either repeatedly re-resolving the same dependencies over and over again when cyclic peer dependencies were present, or growing module ids unboundly which also caused hangs.

This was all caused by using a 1 pass algorithm prior to this change, whereas proper peer dependency resolution requires a 2 pass algorithm. The reason for that is that peer dependency resolution is based around the "peer context" that is passed around and can influence which version a package is resolved to. We need to be super careful when those should bubble up to parents.

Pure dependencies without any peers should be resolved from a local cache, then check if we resolved a dep with the same peer context and lastly do the expensive thing of checking directly.

title: fix(ext/node): support DER-encoded keys in publicEncrypt/privateDecrypt
body:
## Summary

- `publicEncrypt`, `privateDecrypt`, and `privateEncrypt` only accepted PEM-encoded (text) keys. When passed DER (binary) encoded keys, they failed with `invalid utf-8 sequence of 1 bytes from index 1` because the code tried to convert the binary key data to a UTF-8 string before parsing as PEM.
- This broke npm packages like `openpgp` which pass PKCS#1 DER-encoded public keys to `crypto.publicEncrypt`.
- Now all three functions detect key format automatically: try PEM if UTF-8, otherwise try DER. For both paths, SPKI/PKCS#8 is tried first with PKCS#1 as fallback.

Closes #26099

## Test plan

- [x] Added tests for `publicEncrypt`/`privateDecrypt` with SPKI/PKCS#8 DER keys
- [x] Added tests for `publicEncrypt`/`privateDecrypt` with PKCS#1 DER keys
- [x] Existing PEM-based tests continue to pass
- [ ] `cargo test unit_node::crypto::crypto_cipher_test`

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

fix(ext/node): support DER-encoded keys in publicEncrypt/privateDecrypt

`publicEncrypt`, `privateDecrypt`, and `privateEncrypt` only accepted
PEM-encoded keys, failing with "invalid utf-8 sequence" when passed
DER (binary) encoded keys. This broke npm packages like `openpgp` that
pass PKCS#1 DER keys to `publicEncrypt`.

Now tries SPKI/PKCS#8 format first, then falls back to PKCS#1 format,
for both PEM and DER encoded keys.

Closes #26099

title: feat(ext/node): implement missing X509Certificate methods
body:
## Summary
- Implements 9 previously stubbed `crypto.X509Certificate` methods: `toString()`, `raw`, `subjectAltName`, `checkIP()`, `checkIssued()`, `checkPrivateKey()`, `verify()`, `infoAccess`, and `toLegacyObject()`
- Adds proper IPv4/IPv6 formatting for Subject Alternative Name IP addresses
- Adds new Rust ops in `ext/node_crypto/x509.rs` and wires them to the JS polyfill

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28494

## Test plan
- [x] Added spec test at `tests/specs/node/crypto_x509_methods/` covering all implemented methods
- [x] `cargo check -p deno_node_crypto` passes
- [x] `./tools/format.js && ./tools/lint.js` pass
- [x] Manual verification with test certificates (agent1-cert.pem, ca1-cert.pem)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
Reviewed against Node.js reference implementation - found several behavioral differences that need alignment:

## ❌ checkIP return behavior discrepancy

**Node.js behavior:**
- Returns the IP string on match
- Returns undefined on no match  
- Throws ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE on invalid IP format or null bytes

**Current implementation:**
- Returns undefined for null bytes instead of throwing
- Need to verify Rust op returns string not boolean

## ❌ verify method signature mismatch

**Node.js allows both public and private keys:**
```javascript
// From node/src/crypto/crypto_x509.cc:473-474
// A Public Key can be derived from a private key, so we allow both.
CHECK_NE(key->Data().GetKeyType(), kKeyTypeSecret);
```

**Current implementation incorrectly rejects private keys:**
```typescript
if (pkey.type !== 'public')
  throw new ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE('pkey', pkey);
```

Should be:
```typescript
if (pkey.type === 'secret')
  throw new ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE('pkey', pkey);
```

## ❌ Missing options parameter

**Node.js signature:** `checkIP(ip, options)` - options currently unused but parsed for future OpenSSL compatibility

**Current implementation:** `checkIP(ip)` - missing options parameter entirely

## ✅ Other methods look correct

- checkIssued, checkPrivateKey follow Node.js patterns correctly
- Input validation and error types match
- toString/toJSON/raw/subjectAltName implementations appear sound

The core implementation is solid but these edge cases need fixing for full Node.js compatibility.
LGTM

commits.message:
feat(ext/node): implement missing X509Certificate methods

Implements the following previously stubbed methods on crypto.X509Certificate:
- toString() / toJSON() - Return PEM encoding
- raw - Return DER-encoded Buffer
- subjectAltName - Return formatted SAN string with proper IP formatting
- checkIP(ip) - Check IP address against SAN entries
- checkIssued(otherCert) - Check if cert was issued by another cert
- checkPrivateKey(privateKey) - Check if private key matches cert
- verify(publicKey) - Verify cert signature with public key
- infoAccess - Return Authority Information Access string
- toLegacyObject() - Return PeerCertificate object

from spec tests to unit tests in crypto_key_test.ts. Fix the existing
X509Certificate inspect test to match the updated infoAccess output.

title: fix(test): dedupe discovered workspace test modules
body:
This PR should address #25949.

I fixed duplicate test module execution caused by overlapping discovery entries (e.g., workspace + `deno test .`).

IMHO this is a suboptimal but safer fix. The cleaner solution would be to make discovery non-overlapping at member-pattern expansion time, but changing that area seems more likely to introduce breaking behavior for existing workflows. Given the small scope of the bug, I preferred the lower-risk fix.

Note: this adds a small discovery-time overhead (an extra map pass and some allocations for deduplication). IMHO it’s acceptable for now, but I’d appreciate feedback on whether a follow-up should move deduplication earlier in discovery.
author: pmarchini

title: fix(ext/node): make `setAutoPadding(false)` a no-op for GCM ciphers
body:
## Summary

- GCM is a stream cipher mode that doesn't use block padding. In Node.js, `setAutoPadding()` is a no-op for GCM modes, but Deno was throwing `setAutoPadding(false) not supported for Aes128Gcm/Aes256Gcm yet` when called on GCM deciphers.
- This broke npm packages like `ssh2` that set auto-padding to false by default.
- Merges the `auto_pad=false` arms into the existing `auto_pad=true` arms (using `_` wildcard) for both `Aes128Gcm` and `Aes256Gcm` in `Decipher::final()`, and removes the now-unused error variants.

Closes #29425
Closes #28589
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): make `setAutoPadding(false)` a no-op for GCM ciphers

GCM is a stream cipher mode that doesn't use block padding. In Node.js,
`setAutoPadding()` is a no-op for GCM modes, but Deno was throwing an
error when `setAutoPadding(false)` was called on GCM deciphers. This
broke npm packages like `ssh2` that set auto-padding to false by default.

title: perf(install): request abbreviated packuments from npm registry
body:
Here's the speedup with a clean cache against an astro starter repo

```bash
Benchmark 1: ~/Documents/Code/deno/target/release-lite/deno install
  Time (mean ± σ):      6.535 s ±  0.578 s    [User: 1.417 s, System: 7.037 s]
  Range (min … max):    5.783 s …  7.443 s    10 runs

Benchmark 2: ~/Documents/Code/deno/target/deno-baseline install
  Time (mean ± σ):      8.555 s ±  1.429 s    [User: 2.159 s, System: 6.828 s]
  Range (min … max):    6.736 s … 11.942 s    10 runs
  ```

Any package that has a large packument should see a noticeable speedup, since the abbreviated versions tend to be 2-5x smaller.

One complication is that the time field is not present in abbreviated metadata, so if you are using the minimum-dependency-age resolution option, then we have to request the full metadata. Then that full metadata gets written to the fs cache. So basically if you're using minimum-dependency-age you won't see a benefit, which is unfortunate but no good way around it at this point.

Disclosure: the idea and direction came from me, claude did the code changes, but i've reviewed every line in the PR
author: nathanwhit

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
feat(npm): request compact packuments from npm registry

Request the abbreviated install manifest format (application/vnd.npm.install-v1+json)
which is 2-5x smaller than the full packument. Add has_install_script field to
NpmPackageVersionInfo to support the hasInstallScript field from the abbreviated
format.

title: fix(coverage): correct line and branch counts in coverage reports
body:
## Summary

Fixes three bugs in `deno coverage` that caused incorrect LCOV output (closes #9865):

- **Line counts were inflated** due to summing all covering V8 ranges instead of using the innermost (most specific) range. V8 coverage ranges are nested, and the smallest range that fully covers a line gives the correct execution count. For example, a line inside an if-body previously showed `count = function_count + branch_count` instead of just `branch_count`.
- **Branch coverage only reported one arm** of if/else statements (`BRF:1` instead of `BRF:2`). Now detects implicit complement branches by computing `parent_count - range_count`, and groups explicit branches by their source-mapped line.
- **Source-mapped line deduplication summed counts** when multiple compiled JS lines mapped to the same TypeScript source line, further inflating counts. Now uses `max()` instead of `+=`.
- **Negative BRDA counts for branches inside loops** — the complement calculation used the function-level call count instead of the enclosing (parent) range count. For example, an if/else inside a for-loop that runs 5 times now correctly computes complements relative to the loop iteration count, not the function call count.

### Before (from the issue)
```
BRDA:3,1,0,1
BRF:1          ← only 1 branch found
BRH:1
DA:2,3         ← inflated counts
DA:3,3
DA:4,4
DA:5,8         ← else line hit 8 times?
```

### After
```
BRDA:3,1,0,1
BRDA:3,1,1,1   ← both branches detected
BRF:2           ← 2 branches found
BRH:2
DA:2,2          ← correct: function called 2 times
DA:3,2
DA:4,1          ← correct: if-branch taken once
DA:5,1          ← correct: else-branch taken once
```

### Comparison with c8 (Node.js V8-based coverage)

Tested against [c8](https://github.com/bcoe/c8), the standard V8-based coverage tool for Node.js, using a shared test suite with various branch patterns (if/else, nested if, ternary, switch, logical operators) and loop constructs (for, while, for-of with early return).

**Branch totals now match c8 for loop-heavy code:**
| File | c8 (Node) | Deno (after) |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| `loops.js` | BRF:10, BRH:8 | BRF:10, BRH:8 |

**Remaining differences (by design):**
- **Complement branches**: Deno synthesizes both arms of a branch (e.g. `BRDA:5,1,0,2` + `BRDA:5,1,1,1` for an if/else), while c8 reports only the V8 range (`BRDA:5,3,0,2`). This gives Deno slightly higher BRF counts for pure branch code (e.g. `branches.js`: Deno BRF:20 vs c8 BRF:17) but more complete coverage information.
- **Function-entry branches**: c8 counts function entries as branches; Deno does not.
- **Line hit counts**: Deno uses the innermost V8 range for line counts, correctly marking untaken branch bodies as 0. c8 marks some structural lines (e.g. closing braces, function signatures) as hit even in untaken branches, resulting in higher LH counts.

## Test plan

- [x] All existing coverage integration tests updated and passing (21/21)
- [x] All existing coverage spec tests updated and passing
- [x] Added new spec test `tests/specs/coverage/branch_and_line_counts/` — reproduces the exact scenario from issue #9865
- [x] Added new spec test `tests/specs/coverage/no_negative_branch_counts/` — regression test for branches inside loops
- [x] Manual comparison against c8 confirms branch totals match for loop constructs
- [x] No negative BRDA values in any test output

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(coverage): correct line and branch counts in coverage reports

Fixes three bugs in `deno coverage` that caused incorrect LCOV output:

1. Line counts were inflated due to summing all covering V8 ranges instead
   of using the innermost (most specific) range. V8 coverage ranges are
   nested, and the smallest range that covers a line gives the correct count.

2. Branch coverage only reported one arm of if/else statements (BRF:1)
   instead of both (BRF:2). Now detects implicit complement branches by
   computing parent_count - range_count, and groups explicit branches by
   their source-mapped line.

3. Source-mapped line deduplication summed counts when multiple compiled JS
   lines mapped to the same source line, further inflating counts. Now uses
   max() instead.

Closes #9865

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
fmt

fix(coverage): fix expected test output and negative branch counts

Update coverage_summary.out to expect 50% branch coverage (was 0%),
matching the corrected branch counting from the previous commit.

Fix negative BRDA counts for branches inside loops by detecting when
a range's count exceeds the enclosing function's count (indicating a
loop body rather than a branch arm) and skipping complement generation.

Add regression test for no_negative_branch_counts.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(coverage): use parent range count for branch complements

Instead of always using the function-level count (block_hits) to
compute branch complements, find the smallest enclosing range for
each sub-range and use its count. This gives correct complement
values for branches inside loops — e.g. an if/else in a for-loop
now uses the loop iteration count as the parent, not the function
call count.

title: fix(ext/node): make fsPromises.watch() a proper AsyncIterable with close()
body:
## Summary
- The watcher returned by `node:fs/promises` `watch()` was a plain object with only `[Symbol.asyncIterator]`, missing `next()` and `return()` methods
- The returned object now properly implements the `AsyncIterator` protocol, making it directly usable with `for await...of` and cleanly stoppable via `break` or `AbortSignal`
- Improved abort signal handling (respects already-aborted signals, uses `{ once: true }`)

Fixes #23562

- [x] Verified `for await (const event of watcher)` works correctly
- [x] Verified `watcher.next()` and `watcher.return()` are available
- [x] Verified `Symbol.asyncIterator` is present on the returned object

fix(ext/node): make fsPromises.watch() a proper AsyncIterable with close()

The watcher returned by `fsPromises.watch()` was missing `next()`,
`return()`, and `close()` methods that Node.js provides. This made the
returned object not properly usable as an async iterator and prevented
closing the watcher programmatically.

Fixes #23562

title: fix(jupyter): handle shutdown and interrupt requests per protocol
body:
## Summary

- Send `shutdown_reply` on the control channel before exiting, with the `restart` field echoed back from the request
- Handle `interrupt_request` by calling `v8::IsolateHandle::terminate_execution()` to actually interrupt running JS code (e.g. infinite loops), then send `interrupt_reply`
- Clear the V8 termination flag (`cancel_terminate_execution()`) before each evaluation so the kernel remains usable after an interrupt
- Implement `is_complete_request` with bracket/brace/paren balancing instead of always replying "complete" — also handles unterminated strings, template literals, and comments
- Add integration tests for shutdown reply, interrupt reply, and interrupt of running code with subsequent execution
- Add 16 unit tests for `is_complete` logic

Verified against the [kernel-testbed](https://github.com/runtimed/kernel-testbed) conformance suite: **26/26 (100%)**, up from 92%.

Closes #32357

## Test plan

- [x] `jupyter_shutdown_reply` — sends shutdown, verifies reply content and clean process exit
- [x] `jupyter_shutdown_restart_reply` — verifies `restart: true` is echoed back
- [x] `jupyter_interrupt_reply` — verifies reply on control channel
- [x] `jupyter_interrupt_running_code` — starts `while (true) {}`, interrupts it, verifies the kernel can still execute code afterward
- [x] 16 unit tests for `check_is_complete` covering balanced/unbalanced brackets, strings, comments, template literals, and edge cases
- [x] All pre-existing jupyter tests continue to pass
- [x] Full kernel-testbed conformance suite passes 26/26

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(jupyter): handle shutdown and interrupt requests per protocol

The Jupyter kernel was not sending replies for `shutdown_request` and
`interrupt_request` messages on the control channel, causing conformance
test timeouts. Now the kernel sends proper `shutdown_reply` (with the
`restart` field echoed back) before exiting, and `interrupt_reply` after
terminating V8 execution via `IsolateHandle::terminate_execution()`.
Before each subsequent evaluation, `cancel_terminate_execution()` clears
the termination flag so the kernel remains usable after an interrupt.

Closes #32357

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(jupyter): implement is_complete_request with bracket balancing

Instead of always replying "complete", check whether the code has
balanced brackets, braces, parens, and terminated strings/comments.
Returns "incomplete" for unterminated constructs and "invalid" for
mismatched delimiters.

title: fix(ext/node): "node:readline" handles emoji width correctly
body:
## Summary
- Characters with the Unicode `Emoji_Presentation` property (like ⚡ U+26A1) are rendered as width 2 in terminals, but `getStringWidth` was returning 1 since they aren't classified as East Asian Wide. This caused cursor positioning issues in `node:readline` when emoji were used in prompts.
- Added a `\p{Emoji_Presentation}` regex check in `getStringWidth` to properly detect emoji that should be wide
- Extended `isFullWidthCodePoint` to cover missing SMP emoji ranges (Transport/Map Symbols 0x1F680-0x1F6FF, Supplemental Symbols 0x1F900-0x1F9FF, Chess Symbols 0x1FA00-0x1FA6F, Symbols Extended-A 0x1FA70-0x1FAFF)

Closes #30042

## Test plan
- [x] Verified `⚡` prompt now reports correct cursor position (cols=3 for "⚡ " instead of cols=2)
- [x] Verified other Emoji_Presentation=Yes characters (🚀, 🤔, ⌚, 🩺) also report width 2
- [x] Verified non-emoji-presentation characters (like ☀ without VS16) still report width 1
- [x] Verified ASCII and CJK characters are unaffected
- [ ] Run existing readline spec tests
- [ ] Run `test-icu-stringwidth` node compat test

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(node): handle emoji width correctly in readline

Characters with the Unicode `Emoji_Presentation` property (like ⚡) are
rendered as width 2 in terminals, but `getStringWidth` was returning 1
for them since they aren't classified as East Asian Wide. This caused
cursor positioning issues in `node:readline` when emoji were used in
prompts.

Add a `\p{Emoji_Presentation}` regex check alongside the existing
`isFullWidthCodePoint` check, and extend `isFullWidthCodePoint` to
cover missing SMP emoji ranges (Transport/Map Symbols, Supplemental
Symbols, Chess Symbols, Symbols Extended-A).

Closes #30042

title: fix(ext/node): provide CJS globals in worker_threads eval mode
body:
## Summary
- When using `new Worker(code, { eval: true })` in `node:worker_threads`, Node.js evaluates the code as CommonJS, making `require()` available. Deno was wrapping the code in a `data:text/javascript` URL (ESM), causing `require is not defined` errors.
- Wraps eval worker code with CJS globals (`require`, `__filename`, `__dirname`, `module`, `exports`) by prepending ESM imports from `node:module` and `node:process`.
- Adds a spec test verifying `require("worker_threads")` works in eval workers.

Closes #27181
Closes #27167

## Test plan
- [x] New spec test `eval_cjs_require` passes — creates an eval worker using `require("worker_threads")` and verifies it can communicate back via `parentPort`
- [x] All existing `worker_threads` spec tests continue to pass
- [x] Manually verified the exact reproduction from the issue works

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): provide CJS globals in worker_threads eval mode

When using `new Worker(code, { eval: true })`, Node.js evaluates the code
as CommonJS, making `require()` available. Deno was wrapping the code in a
`data:text/javascript` URL which loads as ESM, causing `require is not
defined` errors. This wraps eval code with CJS globals (require, __filename,
__dirname, module, exports) so that CommonJS code works in eval workers.

Closes #27181

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(node/vm): support vm.constants.DONT_CONTEXTIFY in createContext
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/31192

Adds support for creating vanilla (non-contextified) V8 contexts via vm.createContext(vm.constants.DONT_CONTEXTIFY). This creates a context without property interceptors, where globalThis is reference-equal to the returned object and Object.freeze(globalThis) works correctly.

This is needed by libraries like jsdom that require contexts without interceptor overhead. The feature was added in Node.js v22.8.0.

Enables node_compat test: test-vm-context-dont-contextify.js
author: fraidev

commits.message: fix(node/vm): support vm.constants.DONT_CONTEXTIFY in createContext

title: fix(lsp): tsgo typings for bytes/text imports
body:
- Blocked on https://github.com/denoland/typescript-go/pull/25
- Adds synthetic TS documents for files imported with type bytes/text.
  - E.g. `deno:/text-import-types/file:///path/to/file.txt`
- Adds a normalization layer to convert those document URIs back to the actual resource URI and zero out locations pointing within them.
  - `deno:/text-import-types/file:///path/to/file.txt (0, 7)` -> `file:///path/to/file.txt (0, 0)`
  - This applies to goto-definition responses etc. which now work better than our ts-js integration.

title: fix(install): global install should not warn about using node_modules dir for npm specifiers
body:
Previously:

```
> deno install -g --allow-scripts npm:dprint
╭ Warning
│
│  Ignored build scripts for packages:
│  npm:dprint@0.51.1
│
│  Lifecycle scripts are only supported when using a `node_modules` directory.

title: fix(node/buffer): fix base64 decoding for strings with hyphens
body:
## Summary
- Fix `Buffer.from(str, "base64")` throwing on strings containing `-` characters (e.g. `"base64-encoded-bytes-from-browser"`)
- Two bugs in `base64ToBytes` / `base64clean`:
  1. Base64url character replacements (`-`→`+`, `_`→`/`) were applied **after** `base64clean`, so the padding calculation used the wrong string length
  2. `base64clean` appended `===` when `length % 4 == 1`, which is invalid base64 padding — now drops the trailing character to match Node.js behavior

## Test plan
- Added regression test in `tests/unit_node/buffer_test.ts` verifying:
  - `Buffer.from("base64-encoded-bytes-from-browser", "base64")` produces correct output matching Node.js
  - `Buffer.from("not-valid-base64!!!", "base64")` doesn't throw
  - `Buffer.from("A", "base64")` returns empty buffer (single char too short)
- Verified output matches Node.js exactly for all test cases

Closes #24908

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(node/buffer): fix Buffer.from with base64 strings containing hyphens

Fixes base64 decoding for strings containing `-` and `_` characters
(base64url chars). Two bugs: (1) base64url replacements happened after
base64clean, causing wrong padding calculation, and (2) base64clean
appended `===` for length%4==1 which is invalid base64 — now drops the
trailing char like Node does.

Closes #24908

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(permissions): allow /proc/pressure/* files with --allow-read
body:
The /proc/pressure/* files (memory, cpu, io) are read-only system monitoring files that only expose PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics. These files are safe to read and commonly used for system monitoring and performance analysis.

Previously, these files required --allow-all permission which was overly restrictive. This change allows them to be read with just --allow-read permission while maintaining security for other potentially dangerous /proc files.

Closes: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/30779
author: jfernandez

reviews.body: Seems reasonable to me!

## Pull request overview This PR relaxes the permission requirements for reading PSI (Pressure Stall Information) files in `/proc/pressure/` from `--allow-all` to just `--allow-read`. PSI files (`/proc/pressure/memory`, `/proc/pressure/cpu`, `/proc/pressure/io`) are read-only system monitoring files that expose performance metrics and are commonly used for system monitoring. **Changes:** - Modified permission check logic to allow `/proc/pressure/*` files with `--allow-read` permission - Added test coverage for all three PSI files (memory, cpu, io) ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | runtime/permissions/lib.rs | Added conditional logic to allow /proc/pressure/* files with --allow-read instead of requiring --allow-all | | tests/specs/permission/special/main.js | Added test cases to verify /proc/pressure/* files can be read with --allow-read permission | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(permissions): allow /proc/pressure/* files with --allow-read

The /proc/pressure/* files (memory, cpu, io) are read-only system
monitoring files that only expose PSI (Pressure Stall Information)
metrics. These files are safe to read and commonly used for system
monitoring and performance analysis.

Previously, these files required --allow-all permission which
was overly restrictive. This change allows them to be read with
just --allow-read permission while maintaining security for other
potentially dangerous /proc files.

Closes: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/30779

Signed-off-by: Jose Fernandez <me@jrfernandez.com>

title: fix(ext/node): `createServer().listen().address()` returns port 0
body:
## Summary

- Bind the TCP/TLS port synchronously in `Server.listen()` so that `address()` immediately returns the OS-assigned port, matching Node.js behavior
- Previously the port binding was deferred to `nextTick` via `Deno.serve()`, causing `address()` to return port 0 when using port 0 (auto-assign)
- Introduces `_listen()` method on `ServerImpl` (overridden by HTTPS `Server` for TLS) that calls `Deno.listen()`/`listenTls()` synchronously, then passes the pre-bound listener to `serveHttpOnListener()` in `_serve()`
- Errors (e.g. `EADDRINUSE`) are caught and emitted asynchronously via `nextTick`, matching Node.js behavior

Fixes #31758
Fixes #24427

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit test for HTTP: `address()` returns non-zero port immediately after `listen(0)`
- [x] Added unit test for HTTPS: same behavior with TLS server
- [x] All existing `unit_node::http_test` tests pass (77 total)
- [x] All existing `unit_node::https_test` tests pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: Makes sense to me

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): `createServer().listen().address()` returns port 0

Bind the TCP/TLS port synchronously in `Server.listen()` so that
`address()` immediately returns the OS-assigned port, matching Node.js
behavior. Previously the port binding was deferred to `nextTick` via
`Deno.serve()`, causing `address()` to return port 0.

Fixes #31758

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(compile): add `--self-extracting` flag
body:
Adds a `--self-extracting` flag to `deno compile`. Instead of serving files from the in-memory virtual file system, the compiled binary extracts all embedded files to disk on first run and uses real file system operations at runtime.

This unlocks full Node API support for compiled binaries, including native addons that need real files on disk, and fixes scenarios where native code reads relative files that previously only existed in memory.

The extraction directory is chosen in order of preference:

1. `<exe_dir>/<exe_name>.fs/<hash>/` (next to the compiled binary)
2. Platform data directory fallback:
   - Linux: `$XDG_DATA_HOME/<exe_name>/ or ~/.local/share/<exe_name>/<hash>`
   - macOS: `~/Library/Application Support/<exe_name>/<hash>`
   - Windows: `%LOCALAPPDATA%\<exe_name>\<hash>`

## Advantages

1. More code will work because it extracts and runs on the real file system.

## Disadvantages

1. Initial startup time cost to extract the directory.
2. More space used on the file system.
3. More memory usage than not using it because the binary and text can't be static references anymore.
4. User can more easily tamper with the extracted contents, changing how it works or other code might tamper with it.

Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28918
author: dsherret

reviews.body: LGTM

title: fix(node/buffer): fix latin1Slice and hexSlice returning wrong results
body:
## Summary
- Fix `Buffer.prototype.latin1Slice` and `Buffer.prototype.hexSlice` having an extraneous `string` parameter that shifted all arguments passed to the underlying `_latin1Slice`/`_hexSlice` functions
- Fix `_latin1Slice` not handling missing `start`/`end` arguments, causing it to return empty strings when called with no arguments
- Add unit tests for both methods

Fixes #32276

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo test unit_node::buffer_test` passes
- [x] Manually verified `Buffer.of(1,2,3,0xff).latin1Slice()` returns a 4-character string (previously returned empty string)
- [x] Manually verified `hexSlice()` and `hexSlice(start, end)` return correct results

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(node/buffer): fix latin1Slice and hexSlice returning wrong results

Both `latin1Slice` and `hexSlice` had an extraneous `string` parameter
that shifted all arguments, causing the underlying slice functions to
receive wrong values. Additionally, `_latin1Slice` didn't handle missing
`start`/`end` arguments, resulting in empty strings when called with no
arguments.

Fixes #32276

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(check): add `--check-js` flag
body:
## Rationale

it was not possible to typecheck javascript files with deno without modifying source code by either doing one of:
- create deno.json with compiler flag `checkJs: true`
- adding `// @ts-check` at the source file.

this makes quick typechecking awkward for javscript-only project that doesn't use package.json (e.g https://github.com/simpelplant/charasort-bluearchive)

## Summary
- Add `deno check --check-js` and force JS type-checking for this command.
- Add e2e spec coverage for with/without flag behavior.

## Verification

```
cargo test --package deno args::flags::tests::check -- --nocapture
cargo test specs::check::check_js_flag -- --nocapture
cargo build --package deno
```

```sh
~/r/deno/deno feat/check-check-js-flag✎ | 1 ❱ cat foo.js                                                           
Math.pow("")
~/r/deno/deno feat/check-check-js-flag | 127 ❱ deno check foo.js                                                   
~/r/deno/deno feat/check-check-js-flag✎ ❱ ./target/debug/deno check foo.js                                         
~/r/deno/deno feat/check-check-js-flag✎ ❱ ./target/debug/deno check --check-js foo.js                              
Check foo.js
TS2554 [ERROR]: Expected 2 arguments, but got 1.
Math.pow("")
     ~~~
    at file:///run/media/home/scarf/repo/deno/deno/foo.js:1:6

    An argument for 'y' was not provided.
        pow(x: number, y: number): number;
                       ~~~~~~~~~
        at asset:///lib.es5.d.ts:744:20

error: Type checking failed.
```

## Credit

commit made using gpt-5.3 codex high
author: scarf005

reviews.body: LGTM, thanks!

title: feat(unstable): add `Deno.spawn()`, `Deno.spawnAndWait{Sync}()`
body:
## Summary

- Add `Deno.spawn(cmd, options?)` / `Deno.spawn(cmd, args, options?)` as shorthand for `new Deno.Command(cmd, options).spawn()`
- Add `Deno.spawnAndWait(cmd, options?)` / `Deno.spawnAndWait(cmd, args, options?)` as shorthand for `new Deno.Command(cmd, options).output()`
- Add `Deno.spawnAndWaitSync(cmd, options?)` / `Deno.spawnAndWaitSync(cmd, args, options?)` as shorthand for `new Deno.Command(cmd, options).outputSync()`
- All three support a 3-argument overload where args are passed separately, and throw if `options.args` is also provided
- All APIs are marked as unstable for now

title: fix(ext/node): enable test-zlib-invalid-input-memory and test-zlib-un…
body:
Fixes two previously disabled zlib Node.js compatibility tests.

  - **`parallel/test-zlib-invalid-input-memory.js`** — enabled and passing.
    Root cause was a two-layer problem:
    1. `AsyncResource` in `async_hooks.ts` did not call `emitInit()` in its constructor, so `createHook({ init })` callbacks never fired for `AsyncResource` instances. The test's `init` hook assumed any new async resource was the tracked GC object, and when Deno's streams machinery created `TickObject` resources during error handling, the init callback threw an `AssertionError` from inside a `process.nextTick`, silently swallowing the error event.
    2. In `zlib.js`, `processCallback` skipped calling the Transform write `cb()` when `kError` was set, preventing the stream's destroy sequence from running and thus the error event from being emitted.

  - **`parallel/test-zlib-unused-weak.js`** — enabled and passing. The test passes the V8 flag `--no-concurrent-array-buffer-sweeping` to ensure deterministic GC accounting. Deno's node-compat test runner's `parse_flags` function did not recognise this flag and silently dropped it, so it was never forwarded. Added the flag to `parse_flags` in `mod.rs`.

  - **`abort/test-zlib-invalid-internals-usage.js`** — added to `config.jsonc` as disabled. This test uses `internalBinding('zlib').Zlib` (a Node.js C++ internal) which Deno does not implement and cannot reasonably emulate.
author: fraidev

reviews.body: Great!

title: fix(ext/node): implement timeout and killSignal for spawn()
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/19517

Fixes the `timeout` and `killSignal` options for `child_process.spawn()` (and `fork()`, which delegates to `spawn()`). Previously these options were accepted but silently ignored.

The fix sets a `setTimeout` after spawning that kills the child with the specified `killSignal` (default: `"SIGTERM"`) if the process hasn't exited within `timeout` ms. The timer is cleared if the process exits naturally before the deadline.
author: fraidev

reviews.body: Nice fix

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): implement timeout and killSignal for spawn()

Fixes the `timeout` and `killSignal` options for `child_process.spawn()`
(and `fork()`, which delegates to `spawn()`). Previously these options
were accepted but silently ignored.

The fix sets a `setTimeout` after spawning that kills the child with the
specified `killSignal` (default: `"SIGTERM"`) if the process hasn't
exited within `timeout` ms. The timer is cleared if the process exits
naturally before the deadline.

title: feat(fmt): add --fail-fast flag to deno fmt --check
body:
issue no : 
#26585

Adds a `--fail-fast` flag to `deno fmt --check` that stops checking files immediately after finding the first formatting error, matching the behavior of `deno test --fail-fast`.


When running `deno fmt --check` on large codebases, developers often want immediate feedback on the first formatting issue rather than waiting for all files to be checked. This flag enables:

- Faster feedback loop when fixing formatting issues incrementally
- Reduced output noise by stopping at the first error
- Consistency with `deno test --fail-fast` behavior
- Time savings on large codebases

title: fix(ext/node): handle shell redirections in child_process exec
body:
When child_process.exec() runs a shell command like "deno" "script.js" < "input.txt", transformDenoShellCommand failed to add run -A because shell redirections (<, >, |) were included in the args passed to the CLI parser, triggering the metacharacter safety bail-out.


 Enables node_compat tests: test-stdin-from-file.js and test-stdin-from-file-spawn.js.
author: fraidev

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Improves Deno’s Node-compat `child_process.exec()` shell-command translation so Deno-invoking commands containing shell redirections/pipes can still be rewritten (eg adding `run -A`) without shell operators confusing the CLI arg parser. Also adds the legacy `process.openStdin()` API and enables the related Node-compat stdin tests. **Changes:** - Add `splitShellSuffix()` and integrate it into `transformDenoShellCommand()` to separate shell operators from CLI args before translation, then re-append the suffix. - Expand the shell variable detection regex to match `"$VAR"` in addition to `"${VAR}"` / `${VAR}` / `$VAR`. - Implement `process.openStdin()` and enable stdin-from-file node_compat tests in config. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | tests/node_compat/config.jsonc | Enables two stdin-from-file Node-compat tests. | | ext/node/polyfills/process.ts | Adds `process.openStdin()` implementation for legacy API compatibility. | | ext/node/polyfills/internal/child_process.ts | Adds shell-suffix splitting and improves env-var Deno-path detection to allow arg translation with redirections/pipes present. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
I asked @dsherret to take a look at this PR - feels to me we might want to use `deno_task_shell` to perform this parsing instead of hand-rolling it.

Discussed with David and while it's not necessary to use `deno_task_shell`, I got Claude to highlight a few potential issues in the current implementation:
```
  Potential issues:

  1. No backslash escape handling. splitShellSuffix doesn't handle \< or \>. A command like echo hello \< world would
  incorrectly split at the escaped <. This is likely rare in child_process.exec() usage but worth noting.
  2. & as first operator vs &&. If the command is script.js & echo done, the function splits at & — which is correct (it's a
   shell operator). But consider script.js --flag=a&b (no spaces around &): this would incorrectly split at &. Again,
  uncommon in practice since shell commands typically have spaces around operators.
  3. Semicolons in URLs or arguments. A command like deno run script.ts --url="http://example.com" is fine (semicolons
  inside quotes are handled), but deno run script.ts --separator=; without quotes would split at ;. This is actually the
  correct shell behavior though — an unquoted ; is a shell operator.

  Shell variable regex fix

  // Old: /^(?:"\$\{([^}]+)\}"|\$\{([^}]+)\}|\$([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*))/
  // New: /^(?:"\$\{([^}]+)\}"|\"\$([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)\"|\$\{([^}]+)\}|\$([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*))/

  Adds matching for "$VARNAME" (quoted, no braces). Group indices updated correctly from 3 to 4.

```

So maybe worth trying out with `deno_task_shell` parsing?

## Pull request overview Copilot reviewed 9 out of 10 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
LGTM

improve splitShellSuffix
use deno_task_shell instead
fix quotes
escaped posix shell vars
fix windows
format
remove deno_task_shell again
use &str instead

title: feat(cli): add `--save-exact`/`--exact` flag to `deno add` and `install`
body:
<!--
Before submitting a PR, please read https://docs.deno.com/runtime/manual/references/contributing

1. Give the PR a descriptive title.

  Examples of good title:
    - fix(std/http): Fix race condition in server
    - docs(console): Update docstrings
    - feat(doc): Handle nested reexports

  Examples of bad title:
    - fix #7123
    - update docs
    - fix bugs

2. Ensure there is a related issue and it is referenced in the PR text.
3. Ensure there are tests that cover the changes.
4. Ensure `cargo test` passes.
5. Ensure `./tools/format.js` passes without changing files.
6. Ensure `./tools/lint.js` passes.
7. Open as a draft PR if your work is still in progress. The CI won't run
   all steps, but you can add '[ci]' to a commit message to force it to.
8. If you would like to run the benchmarks on the CI, add the 'ci-bench' label.
-->

Fixes #30803

### Description

This PR introduces the `--save-exact` flag (and its alias `--exact`) to the `deno add`. rn, deno defaults to prepending a caret (`^`) to installed versions (e.g., `^1.0.0`). This flag allows users to opt-out of this behavior and save the exact version string (e.g., `1.0.0`) to `deno.json` or `package.json`, aligning with the behavior found in `npm`, `pnpm`, and `yarn`.

### Changes

- Updated `AddFlags` in `cli/args/flags.rs` to include `save_exact`.
- Updated `cli/tools/pm/mod.rs` to pass this flag to the version selection logic.
- Implemented logic to return an empty prefix string `""` instead of `^` when the flag is present.
