title: feat(fs): add tryLock(exclusive?: boolean): Promise<boolean> to FsFile
body:
# feat(fs): add `tryLock` method to `Deno.FsFile`

Closes #31343

## Description

This PR adds a non-blocking file lock method to `Deno.FsFile`. Currently, `lock()` and `lockSync()` block until the lock is acquired, which can be problematic when you want to check if a file is available without waiting indefinitely.

The new `tryLock()` method attempts to acquire a lock and immediately returns a boolean indicating success, rather than blocking.

## Changes

Added two new methods to `Deno.FsFile`:

```typescript
// Async version - returns Promise<boolean>
await file.tryLock(exclusive?: boolean): Promise<boolean>

// Sync version - returns boolean  
file.tryLockSync(exclusive?: boolean): boolean
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns `true` if the lock was successfully acquired
- Returns `false` if the file is already locked (non-blocking, does not wait)
- Throws on other errors (e.g., invalid file handle)

## Example Usage

```typescript
const file = await Deno.open("/tmp/myfile.txt", { read: true, write: true });

// Try to acquire an exclusive lock without blocking
if (await file.tryLock(true)) {
  console.log("Lock acquired, doing work...");
  // ... do work ...
  await file.unlock();
} else {
  console.log("File is busy, try again later");
}

file.close();
```

## Implementation Details

- Uses `fs3::FileExt::try_lock_exclusive()` and `try_lock_shared()` under the hood
- Handles `EWOULDBLOCK` / `WouldBlock` errors by returning `false` instead of throwing
- Follows the same pattern as existing `lock()`/`lockSync()` methods

## Test Plan

Added unit tests in `tests/unit/files_test.ts`:
- `fsFileTryLockSync` - verifies sync exclusive lock behavior
- `fsFileTryLockAsync` - verifies async exclusive lock behavior  
- `fsFileTryLockSharedSync` - verifies shared locks can coexist

## Checklist

- [x] Related issue referenced (#31343)
- [x] Tests added for new functionality
- [x] `cargo check` passes
- [x] `cargo clippy` passes
- [x] `deno lint` passes on modified JS files
author: MkDev11

reviews.body: LGTM, but not approving yet because we'll wait until closer to the 2.7 release to get this merged.
LGTM. Thanks!

feat(fs): add tryLock(exclusive?: boolean): Promise<boolean> to FsFile

Implements non-blocking file lock attempt for Deno.FsFile.

- Add tryLock() and tryLockSync() methods to FsFile
- Returns true on successful lock acquisition
- Returns false when lock cannot be acquired (non-blocking)
- Uses fs3::try_lock_exclusive/try_lock_shared under the hood

Closes denoland/deno#31343

author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): forward stdout from worker_threads to parent

Previously only stderr was forwarded from worker threads to the parent.
This adds stdout forwarding using the same mechanism, enabling
worker.stdout to emit data events when the worker writes to
process.stdout. Also adds the stdin property (null by default) to match
Node.js Worker API surface.

Enables test-worker-no-stdin-stdout-interaction.js node_compat test.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(ext/node): skip non-flag args in execArgv validation and use Readable for worker stdio

The execArgv validation was treating all array elements as flags, but
items like "node" and "development" are arguments to preceding flags
(e.g. "--conditions node"). Skip items that don't start with '-'.

Also upgrade worker.stdout and worker.stderr from EventEmitter to
Readable streams to match the Node.js API (supporting pipe/unpipe).
Close stdio streams when the worker exits.

This fixes vitest 4 compatibility where threads pool passes
"--conditions node --conditions development" in execArgv and calls
worker.stdout.pipe().

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use primordials for Readable.push calls in worker stdio

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(ext/node): make worker_threads ref/unref idempotent like Node.js

The ref()/unref() methods on Worker were implemented as a reference
counter, but Node.js treats them as idempotent boolean toggles. This
caused issues where libraries like comlink that call ref() for each
pending request but only call unref() once would leave the ref count
elevated, preventing the process from exiting.

Closes #31871

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore(node_compat): report passing-but-not-enabled tests in Slack thread

Adds a second thread message to the daily node_compat Slack report that
lists tests which are passing in the full test run but are not yet
enabled in config.jsonc. Tests passing on all platforms are listed first.
This makes it easy to spot tests that can be enabled.

title: fix(ext/node): normalize trailing dot in TLS servername
body:
Fixes #30170

Node.js strips trailing dots from FQDN server names before TLS certificate verification, but Deno was passing the servername with the trailing dot directly to rustls. This caused certificate validation failures when connecting with a trailing-dot servername like `example.local.` to a server whose certificate is valid for `example.local`.

The error message was especially confusing because it said the certificate wasn't valid for `example.local.` when it was only valid for `example.local.` (same string):
```
certificate not valid for name "example.local."; certificate is only valid for DnsName("example.local.")
```

The fix strips the trailing dot from the hostname in the TLSSocket constructor before it's passed to the TLS layer. This matches Node.js behavior - DNS fully-qualified domain names end with a dot but TLS SNI extensions and certificate matching should work without it.

Added a test that connects using `localhost.` as servername to verify the trailing dot is properly normalized.
author: veeceey

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): normalize trailing dot in TLS servername

Node.js strips trailing dots from FQDN server names before TLS
certificate verification. Deno was passing the servername with the
trailing dot directly to rustls, which then failed to match it against
the certificate's Subject Alternative Names.

For example, connecting with servername "example.local." to a server
with cert valid for "example.local" would fail with:
  certificate not valid for name "example.local."

The fix strips the trailing dot from the hostname in the TLSSocket
constructor, matching Node.js behavior per RFC 6066 (SNI should not
include the trailing dot).

Closes #30170
Merge branch 'main' into fix/issue-30170-tls-trailing-dot

title: fix(node/http): preserve request body on stale keepalive retry
body:
Closes https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/32006

When a reused keepAlive socket goes stale, the retry logic was not re-sending the request body for POST/PUT/PATCH requests. The body data in outputData was consumed by _flushBuffer() before the stale connection error surfaced at op_node_http_await_response, so the retried request had no body to send, causing it to hang indefinitely.

Fix by saving outputData before flushing on reused sockets and restoring it on retry, along with resetting the body stream state so _writeHeader() creates a fresh TransformStream.
author: fraidev

reviews.body: Looks good to me

commits.message:
fix(node/http): preserve request body on stale keepalive retry

When a reused keepAlive socket goes stale, the retry logic was not
re-sending the request body for POST/PUT/PATCH requests. The body data
in outputData was consumed by _flushBuffer() before the stale connection
error surfaced at op_node_http_await_response, so the retried request
had no body to send, causing it to hang indefinitely.

Fix by saving outputData before flushing on reused sockets and restoring
it on retry, along with resetting the body stream state so _writeHeader()
creates a fresh TransformStream.

title: fix(test): throw when Deno.test() is called during test execution
body:
Fixes #30787.

`Deno.test()` was being accepted even when called from inside a running test callback.  
That means nested registrations could silently be ignored and tests looked like they passed.

Now `Deno.test()` calls made from inside a running test are rejected immediately.  
Instead of silently registering too late, they fail with a clear error:

`Nested Deno.test() calls are not supported. Use t.step() for nested tests.`

This makes the misuse explicit and points users to `t.step()`.

Before submitting a PR, please read https://docs.deno.com/runtime/manual/references/contributing

  Examples of good title:

title: fix(ext/node): export File class from node:buffer module
body:
Node.js 20+ exports the `File` class from the `buffer` module. Currently Deno's `node:buffer` polyfill exports `Blob` but not `File`, which causes runtime errors like:

```
Error: Attempt to export a nullable value for "File"
```

This breaks frameworks that rely on `require('buffer').File`, such as Next.js with edge runtime (via Vercel's `edge-runtime` package).

The fix imports and re-exports the existing `File` class (from `ext:deno_web/09_file.js`, which already defines `File extends Blob`) through the buffer polyfill chain:

- `ext/node/polyfills/internal/buffer.mjs` - import and add to `mod` exports
- `ext/node/polyfills/buffer.ts` - re-export
- `ext/node/polyfills/internal/buffer.d.ts` - add type declarations

Added a test in `tests/unit_node/buffer_test.ts` that verifies `File` is correctly exported and constructable from `node:buffer`.

Closes #28120
author: veeceey

reviews.body: LGTM, thanks!

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): export File class from node:buffer module

Node.js 20+ exports the File class (which extends Blob) from the
buffer module. Deno's node:buffer polyfill was missing this export,
causing runtime errors in frameworks like Next.js edge runtime that
depend on `require('buffer').File` being available.

Fixes #28120

title: fix(ext/node): process.exit() in worker immediately halts execution
body:
## Summary

In Node.js, `process.exit()` inside a worker immediately stops all JS execution at the C++ level. In Deno, it called `workerClose()` which triggered V8's `terminate_execution()`, but JS continued running because V8 only checks the termination flag at function entries and loop back-edges — not during function returns.

Adds an infinite loop after `process.reallyExit()` that gives V8 a loop back-edge to detect the pending termination and throw an uncatchable `TerminationException`. On the main thread the loop is unreachable since `reallyExit()` calls `std::process::exit()`.

Enables 2 new node_compat tests:
- `test-worker-voluntarily-exit-followed-by-addition.js`
- `test-worker-voluntarily-exit-followed-by-throw.js`

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter "test-worker-voluntarily-exit"` — both pass
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter "test-worker-terminate"` — no regressions
- [x] `cargo test --test node_compat parallel -- --filter "test-worker-exit"` — no regressions

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): process.exit() in worker immediately halts execution

In Node.js, process.exit() inside a worker immediately stops all JS
execution at the C++ level. In Deno, it called workerClose() which
triggered V8's terminate_execution(), but JS continued running because
V8 only checks the termination flag at function entries and loop
back-edges, not during function returns.

Fix by adding an infinite loop after process.reallyExit() that gives
V8 a loop back-edge to detect the pending termination and throw an
uncatchable TerminationException. On the main thread the loop is
unreachable since reallyExit() calls std::process::exit().

title: fix(ext/node): rewrite Pipe.open(fd) to use raw fd ops
body:
## Summary

Rewrites `Pipe.open(fd)` to use the `NodeFsState` fd-based I/O infrastructure (from #33039) instead of creating Deno resources.

- **New op: `op_node_register_fd`** -- registers a raw OS fd in `NodeFsState` so existing fd-based read/write/close ops work on it. Supports both Unix (`from_raw_fd`) and Windows (`DuplicateHandle` + `from_raw_handle`).
- **Replace `FileStreamConn`** (resource-based, with EAGAIN polling) with `FdStreamBase` that uses `op_node_fs_read_deferred`/`op_node_fs_write_deferred` directly on the raw fd.
- **`Pipe.open(fd)` now works on Windows** (was `notImplemented`).
- **Remove `op_net_unix_stream_from_fd`** and `is_socket_fd` from `ext/net` -- no longer needed.

This is a step toward implementing `net.Socket({ fd })` and fixing the Git Bash TTY panic (#33131).

## Test plan

- [x] `./x test-spec stream_wrap` -- all 4 tests pass
- [x] `./x test-unit http` -- passes
- [x] `./x test-node raw_fd` -- passes
- [x] Lint and format clean
- [ ] Full CI

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
Overall this looks like a solid simplification: moving `Pipe.open(fd)` onto the existing `NodeFsState` fd ops removes the old socket-vs-file split and enables the Windows path cleanly.

I found one concrete lifecycle issue worth fixing before merge:

- `op_node_register_fd()` overwrites any existing `NodeFsState.open_fds[fd]` entry without first closing/dropping the old owned handle/file object.
- That creates a leak / stale-owner hazard if `Pipe.open(fd)` (or any future caller of `op_node_register_fd`) is invoked more than once for the same numeric fd, or for an fd number that was already tracked in `NodeFsState`.
- The new tests cover happy-path registration but not duplicate registration / re-registration behavior.

Merge risk: low-to-medium, localized to fd ownership semantics.

Recommendation: REQUEST_CHANGES until duplicate-registration semantics are made safe (eg reject already-registered fds, or explicitly remove+close the previous owner first, with tests).

- The new tests cover happy-path registration but not duplicate registration / re-registration behavior.

Merge risk: low-to-medium, localized to fd ownership semantics.

Recommendation: REQUEST_CHANGES until duplicate-registration semantics are made safe (eg reject already-registered fds, or explicitly remove+close the previous owner first, with tests).

Re-reviewed the latest revision. The duplicate-fd ownership issue I previously flagged has been fixed by rejecting already-registered fds with EEXIST, and the regression coverage now exercises that path. The raw-fd Pipe.open(fd) refactor looks good to me now.

title: fix: linked packages with peer deps failing to cache from lockfile
body:
## Summary

- Fix linked npm packages with peer dependencies causing `Failed caching npm package` error when `deno.lock` is present
- The serialized lockfile key for packages with peer deps includes a suffix (e.g. `@myorg/shared@1.0.0_zod@4.3.6`), but the link package lookup was comparing against plain `name@version` strings, so the match always failed
- Changed the comparison to use the parsed `PackageNv` (name + version) instead of the raw serialized key string
- Added a test case for linked packages with peer dependencies

Closes #33138

## Test plan

- [x] New unit test `test_snapshot_from_lockfile_v5_with_linked_package_with_peer_deps` verifies the fix
- [x] All 180 existing `deno_npm` tests pass
- [ ] Manual test with reproducer repo from the issue

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
Looks good to me. The root cause makes sense: comparing linked packages against the raw serialized lockfile key breaks as soon as peer-dependency suffixes are present, and switching the check to `PackageNv` matches the actual linked-package identity.

The added test covers the failing case directly and also checks that non-linked peer deps still retain dist info, which is the right regression guard here.

Risk looks low and localized to snapshot reconstruction from the lockfile.

commits.message:
fix: linked packages with peer deps failing to cache from lockfile

When a linked package has peer dependencies, its serialized lockfile key
includes a peer dep suffix (e.g. `@myorg/shared@1.0.0_zod@4.3.6`).
The link package lookup was comparing against `name@version` format
strings, which never matched keys with peer dep suffixes. This caused
Deno to treat the linked package as a registry package and attempt to
download a tarball that doesn't exist.

Fix by comparing against the parsed `PackageNv` (name + version) instead
of the raw serialized key string.

Closes #33138

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: refactor: remove forked typescript-go infrastructure

## Summary

- Remove all code related to the `denoland/typescript-go` fork binary
- `--unstable-tsgo` / `DENO_UNSTABLE_TSGO_LSP` are now no-ops for the LSP path
- Fork binary is no longer downloaded, cached, or invoked

### Removed (~4,200 lines)

- `cli/tsc/go.rs` — fork subprocess IPC for type checking
- `cli/tsc/go/` — fork binary download, version pinning, SHA256 hashes
- `cli/lsp/tsgo.rs` — fork-based LSP with `deno/callback` protocol
- `TsServer::Go` variant and all dispatch arms in `ts_server.rs`
- `CompletionItemData::TsGo` variant in `completions.rs`
- `factory.tsgo_path()`, `TypeChecker.tsgo_path` field
- `tools/update_tsgo.ts` — fork version update script
- Test infra: `tsgo_prebuilt_path`, `ensure_tsgo_prebuilt`, `DENO_TSGO_PATH`, `set_use_tsgo`
- `fork_with_suffix="_tsgo"` removed from 191 LSP tests

### Context

This is groundwork for replacing Deno's TypeScript integration with stock TypeScript from npm. The fork-based tsgo integration is being replaced by:
- LSP: `@typescript/native-preview` via `tsgo --lsp` (prototype on `prototype/tsgo-npm-lsp`)
- Type checking: stock `tsc` from npm (prototype on `prototype/stock-tsc`)

title: feat(install): added --prod to skip dev deps and @types
body:
- Added the `--prod` flag to `deno install` that skips `devDependencies` from `package.json`.
- Added the `--skip-types` flag to `deno install`, only available with `--prod`. It makes `@types/*` packages skipped as well from both `package.json` and `deno.json` imports. It may be unsafe to use, as only `name.starts_with`  is used to skip `@types/` packages, but such packages not guaranteed to have only types actually.
- When combined with `--entrypoint`, the module graph is built as "code only" (excludes type-only dependencies).
- Conflicts with `--global` and `--dev` flags.

## How to use

`deno install --prod --skip-types`
`deno install --prod --entrypoint main.ts`

## Testing

- Flag parsing: basic, with entrypoint, without `--skip-types`, with `--skip-types`, conflicts with `--global`, conflicts with `--dev`
- `--prod` excludes `devDependencies`
- `--prod` + `--skip-types` excludes `@types/*` packages even from regular dependencies
- `--prod` and `--entrypoint` excludes type-only dependencies

Closes #26121

co-authored with Claude Opus 4.6, mostly for consulting about the codebase.
author: seroperson

reviews.body: Nice work — approach is sound and test coverage is solid. Left a few comments.

## Pull request overview

Adds production-focused dependency installation controls to `deno install`, primarily by threading new CLI flags into the npm installer dependency selection and adjusting entrypoint graph building to avoid type-only deps.

**Changes:**
- Introduces `deno install --prod` to skip `devDependencies`, and `--skip-types` (requires `--prod`) to omit `@types/*` packages.
- When `--prod` is combined with `--entrypoint`, builds a code-only module graph to exclude type-only dependencies.
- Adds a new install spec suite covering `--prod`, `--skip-types`, and the entrypoint/type-only behavior.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 21 out of 21 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `cli/args/flags.rs` | Adds `--prod`/`--skip-types` flags, extends install flag structs, and adds CLI parsing tests. |
| `cli/args/mod.rs` | Switches install-entrypoint graph kind to `CodeOnly` when `--prod` is set. |
| `cli/factory.rs` | Plumbs `production`/`skip_types` into `NpmInstallerFactoryOptions` for local install flows. |
| `cli/lib.rs` | Initializes new entrypoint flag fields for the `cache` subcommand pathway. |
| `cli/lsp/config.rs` | Provides default values for new npm installer factory options in LSP config. |
| `cli/tools/installer/global.rs` | Sets default `production/skip_types` fields when internally invoking entrypoint installs. |
| `libs/npm_installer/factory.rs` | Extends `NpmInstallerFactoryOptions` and forwards options into deps provider construction. |
| `libs/npm_installer/package_json.rs` | Implements production/dev filtering and `@types/*` skipping in workspace dependency enumeration. |
| `tests/specs/install/install_production/__test__.jsonc` | Adds spec coverage for prod/dev skipping, `--skip-types`, and entrypoint type-only exclusion. |
| `tests/specs/install/install_production/package.json` | Fixture package.js
Thanks, this looks good. I'll land it at first convenience

title: refactor(ext/node): rework stream wrap infrastructure for reentrant safety
body:
## Summary

Extracts the foundational stream infrastructure changes from #32819 (node:tls rewrite) so they can be reviewed and landed independently. This reduces #32819's diff by ~1,000 lines and lets the TLS-specific changes build on a stable base.

### What changed

**Rust — callback data model (`stream_wrap.rs`, `stream_wrap_state.rs`)**
- Replaced the `Box::into_raw` / `Box::from_raw` callback pattern (`CallbackData`, `WriteCallbackData`, `ShutdownCallbackData`) with generational `ReadCallbackRegistry` and `RequestCallbackRegistry` stored in a stable `StreamHandleData` per handle. This eliminates aliased `&mut` UB that occurred during reentrant JS calls from libuv callbacks — one of the **Critical** safety issues identified in #32819 review.

**Rust — `handle_wrap.rs`**
- Moved `OwnedPtr` from `tty_wrap.rs` to `handle_wrap.rs` as the canonical location, adding `as_ref`/`as_mut` safety methods needed when the pointee is accessed through raw pointers during reentrant FFI callbacks.
- Extracted `close`/`ref`/`unref` logic into `pub(crate)` helper methods (`close_handle`, `run_close_callback`, `ref_handle`, `unref_handle`, etc.) so subclasses like `LibUvStreamWrap` — and the upcoming `TCPWrap`/`TLSWrap` — can override close behavior without duplicating the state machine.

**Rust — `tty_wrap.rs`**
- Imports `OwnedPtr` from `handle_wrap` instead of defining it locally.
- Adds a `Drop` impl that calls `detach_stream()` to null the `uv_stream_t.data` pointer before the uv handle memory is freed, preventing dangling pointer access from pending libuv callbacks.
- Constructor now sets `handle_data_ptr` on the uv stream's `data` field.

**JS — `stream_wrap.ts`**
- Guards `oncomplete` calls with `req.async` check to prevent double-fire when the Rust write path completes synchronously (kLastWriteWasAsync=0) but the JS `#write` path also fires oncomplete.

**JS — `stream_base_commons.ts`**
- Removes explicit `readStop` call in the `onStreamRead` EOF path. This matches Node.js behavior — the native layer already stops the stream on EOF/error before the JS callback observes it.

### Why split this out

@bartlomieju's review of #32819 identified 3 Critical issues, 4 High issues, and 8 Medium issues across 29 files (+7,001/-1,855 lines). The stream infrastructure changes are foundational but logically independent — they fix real safety issues and enable the TLS rewrite without touching any TLS-specific code. Landing them separately makes both this PR and the remaining TLS PR easier to review.

### Unused `pub(crate)` methods

A few methods added here (`set_read_interceptor_for_stream`, `read_start_intercepted_for_stream`, `set_fd`, `handle_wrap`, `set_js_handle`, `set_state_closing`) are `pub(crate)` but not yet called. They will be used by the TCPWrap/TLSWrap implementations in the follow-up PR. Compiler warnings are expected until then.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p deno_node` passes (warnings only for unused pub(crate) methods)
- [x] `cargo check --bin deno` passes
- [x] `tools/format.js` clean
- [x] `tools/lint.js --js` clean
- [ ] CI should confirm no regressions in existing node compat tests

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
Overall this split looks sane to me, and the `stream_base_commons` EOF/readStop change does line up with current Node behavior.

My main ask before merge is test coverage around the new reentrant callback plumbing and the JS/Rust write-completion interaction.

- Functionality: coherent, and the split makes the bigger TLS work easier to review
- Risk: moderate, because this is callback/handle lifetime infrastructure in `ext/node`
- Main ask: add targeted regression tests for the new stream callback registries / sync-vs-async completion paths before landing

Concretely, I’d want coverage for:
- synchronous native write completion not double-firing `req.oncomplete`
- read callback teardown/restart behavior around EOF/error and `readStart`/`readStop`
- handle-detach / close behavior for owned streams (especially TTY-style ownership)
- at least one node-compat-focused test proving the EOF path matches expected Node semantics

I didn’t spot an obvious logic bug in the refactor itself, but this is exactly the sort of infrastructure where the confidence should come from focused regressions tests, not just inspection.

Re-reviewing the latest revision: the targeted regression coverage I asked for is here now, and the `stream_base_commons` EOF-path change matches current Node behavior.

I didn’t spot a new high-confidence issue in the updated stream-wrap refactor.

Recommendation: approve
Risk: moderate, but much better covered now by the focused stream-wrap regression tests.

commits.message:
refactor(ext/node): rework stream wrap infrastructure for reentrant safety

Refactor the stream wrap callback infrastructure to prepare for the
upcoming node:tls rewrite (denoland/deno#32819). This extracts and
lands the foundational stream infrastructure changes independently
so the TLS PR can build on a stable base.

Key changes:

- Move OwnedPtr from tty_wrap to handle_wrap as the canonical location,
  adding as_ref/as_mut safety methods for reentrant FFI callback access
- Extract HandleWrap close/ref/unref logic into pub(crate) helpers so
  subclasses (LibUvStreamWrap, future TCPWrap/TLSWrap) can override
  close behavior
- Replace Box::into_raw callback data (CallbackData, WriteCallbackData,
  ShutdownCallbackData) with generational ReadCallbackRegistry and
  RequestCallbackRegistry stored in a stable StreamHandleData per handle
  — this eliminates aliased &mut UB during reentrant JS calls
- Add clone_context_from_uv_loop helper (was duplicated 3x)
- LibUvStreamWrap::close now clears the JS handle back-reference
- Add detach_stream() for safe uv handle cleanup on GC; TTY now calls
  this in its Drop impl
- Guard JS oncomplete with req.async check to prevent double-fire when
  Rust write completes synchronously
- Remove explicit readStop in onStreamRead EOF path (matches Node.js;
  the native layer handles stream stop on EOF)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: add reason to #[allow(dead_code)] attributes for clippy

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: add regression tests for stream wrap callback infrastructure

Add targeted spec tests covering the four areas requested in review:

1. stream_wrap_write_completion - Verifies synchronous native write
   completion does not double-fire req.oncomplete (the req.async guard)

2. stream_wrap_eof_handling - Verifies EOF event ordering matches Node.js
   semantics (connect -> data -> end -> close) after removing the explicit
   readStop from onStreamRead's EOF path

3. stream_wrap_read_restart - Verifies read callback teardown/restart
   via pause/resume cycle works correctly with the new generational
   ReadCallbackRegistry

4. stream_wrap_handle_close - Verifies handle close/detach behavior for
   owned streams under rapid close/destroy cycles during active I/O

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): return real OS file descriptors from node:fs APIs
body:
## Summary

Fixes #32995
Fixes #33117

- **`node:fs` now returns real OS file descriptors** instead of Deno Resource IDs. `fs.openSync()` returns e.g. fd 21 (a real POSIX fd) rather than a small internal RID like 3. This fixes `tty.isatty()` returning wrong results for opened files, since `libc::isatty()` now receives the actual OS fd.
- **Bypasses the resource table entirely** for node:fs fd operations. `NodeFsState` stores `Rc<dyn File>` directly (was `HashMap<fd, ResourceId>`), eliminating the fd -> rid -> resource_table -> FileResource -> File indirection.
- **Positioned reads/writes use pread/pwrite** (single syscall, no cursor mutation) via new `read_at_sync`/`write_at_sync` methods on the `File` trait, backed by `FileExt::read_at`/`write_at` (Unix) and `FileExt::seek_read`/`seek_write` (Windows). The old code used 3 seeks per positioned I/O (save+seek+restore) which was slow and produced wrong error codes.
- **Partial writes handled in Rust**: `write_with_position` loops internally until all bytes are written, so JS callers make a single op call.
- On Windows, uses `DuplicateHandle` to give CRT fds independent OS handle copies, preventing double-close between the `File` Drop and `libc::close(crt_fd)`.
- Removes dependency on `FsFile` from `ext:deno_fs/30_fs.js` and `io.*` from `ext:deno_io/12_io.js` in the node polyfills.

This is the foundation for native addon compatibility, cross-process fd passing, and correct POSIX fd semantics in Node.js compatibility mode.

## Test plan

- [x] New unit tests: 19 tests covering real OS fd values, positioned read/write cursor preservation (pread/pwrite semantics), interleaved positioned + sequential I/O, fstat/ftruncate/fsync/fdatasync on fds, multiple independent fds, async lifecycle
- [x] Node compat tests: `test-fs-readfile-fd`, `test-fs-read-stream-fd`, `test-fs-read-stream-fd-leak`, `test-fs-read-stream-pos`, `test-fs-read-type`, `test-fs-promises-readfile-with-fd`, `test-fs-promises-writefile-with-fd`, `test-fs-writefile-with-fd`, `test-fs-write-no-fd`
- [x] Spec tests pass
- [x] Unit tests pass
- [x] Lint + format clean

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: LGTM

The deferred read op does `read_at_sync` on the main thread for positioned reads but `read_byob` (truly async) for non-positioned. Any reason we can't use the async path for both? Slow I/O at a large offset could block the event loop.
LGTM

commits.message:
refactor(ext/node): return real OS file descriptors from node:fs APIs

Deno's node:fs polyfills previously returned Deno Resource IDs (RIDs)
from fs.open(), not real OS file descriptors. Node.js returns real fds.
This broke native addons, cross-process fd passing, and any code that
expects POSIX fd semantics.

The translation between real OS fds and internal resource IDs now
happens entirely in Rust via NodeFsState (a HashMap<i32, ResourceId>),
with no JS-side mapping layer. All fd-consuming operations (read, write,
seek, fstat, ftruncate, fsync, fdatasync, futimes, fchmod, fchown,
close) go through new node-specific ops that accept real fds.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: address CI failures — clippy, EBADF errno, async read callback

- Use std::io::Error::other() instead of Error::new(ErrorKind::Other)
- Return proper EBADF errno (libc::EBADF) so error.code === 'EBADF'
- Wrap fs.read callback in process.nextTick to maintain async semantics
- Wrap readFileFromFd result in PromiseResolve for the async readFile path

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use deferred ops for async fs.read/write to match Node's event loop semantics

- Fix clippy: `(0..=2).contains(&fd)` instead of manual range check
- Add `op_node_fs_read_deferred` and `op_node_fs_write_deferred` ops that
  perform I/O synchronously but resolve on the next event loop tick,
  matching Node's libuv-based callback behavior
- Use deferred read op in `fs.read()` async path so the fd is captured
  immediately (preventing EBADF when caller closes fd after read call)
  while the callback fires on the next event loop tick
- Use deferred read op in `readFileFromFd` so abort signals scheduled
  via process.nextTick can fire between read iterations
- Use deferred write op in `writeFile` for the same abort signal reason

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore: format ext/node/ops/fs.rs

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: address review comments and CI failures

- Add SAFETY comments to unsafe blocks (fixes clippy lint)
- Fix Windows EBADF: use ERROR_INVALID_HANDLE (6) instead of CRT errno (9)
- Close CRT fd on Windows in op_node_fs_close to prevent fd slot leak
- Use op_node_fs_write_deferred in async write/writev to avoid blocking event loop

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore: temporarily disable test-fs-read-stream-pos.js node_compat test

The test is timing-sensitive and consistently times out with the new
fd-based read stream implementation.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use 'reason' instead of 'comment' for ignored test config

The node_compat test runner requires a 'reason' field (not 'comment')
when 'ignore: true' is set.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor: bypass resource table for node:fs fd operations

Store Rc<dyn File> directly in NodeFsState instead of mapping fd->ResourceId
through the resource table. This eliminates the Resource abstraction from
node:fs fd-based operations entirely:

- NodeFsState now maps fd -> Rc<dyn File> (was fd -> ResourceId)
- op_node_open_sync/open no longer add to the resource table
- file_for_fd looks up File directly from NodeFsState (stdio 0/1/2 still
  uses resource table)
- op_node_fs_close validates fd in open_fds and throws EBADF if missing
- On Windows, DuplicateHandle ensures CRT fd and File own independent
  OS handles, preventing double-close

Also cleans up fstat TS signature and fixes positioned read offset
restoration on error path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor: replace op_node_file_from_fd with cross-platform op_node_register_fd

- Remove op_node_file_from_fd (was Unix-only, used resource table)
- Add op_node_register_fd: registers a raw fd in NodeFsState by creating
  a StdFileResourceInner directly. Works on all platforms -- on Windows,
  duplicates the OS HANDLE from the CRT fd so the File and CRT fd own
  independent handles.
- Add op_node_fs_read_async / op_node_fs_write_async: truly async fd-based
  I/O that runs on blocking threads (via File trait async methods), needed
  for PTY/pipe streams that may block for extended periods.
- Rewrite FileStreamConn in pipe_wrap.ts to use fd-based ops instead of
  core.read/core.write with resource IDs.
- Pipe.open() now works on Windows (was notImplemented before).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: address Windows lint warnings in op_node_register_fd

Remove unused CloseHandle import and add safety comment for
get_osfhandle call.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: avoid flaky filehandle.utimes test on Windows

Use whole-second timestamps and compare via getTime() to avoid
sub-millisecond rounding differences between JS Date and filesystem
timestamp resolution on Windows.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor: move positioned read logic into Rust op

Add position parameter to op_node_fs_read_sync and
op_node_fs_read_deferred. The op now handles save/seek/read/restore
internally (matching Node's pread-like semantics), eliminating the
duplicated seek logic and near-identical branches in the JS side.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor: move positioned write logic into Rust ops, revert pipe changes

- Add position parameter to op_node_fs_write_sync and
  op_node_fs_write_deferred with pwrite-like semantics: positioned writes
  save/restore file offset so they don't mutate the fd cursor.
- Handle partial writes internally in Rust (write_all loop) so the JS
  side is a single op call.
- Revert op_node_register_fd / pipe_wrap.ts changes (will be in separate
  PR) -- restore op_node_file_from_fd.
- Re-enable test-fs-read-stream-pos.js node_compat test.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: handle BigInt position in read ops

Convert position to Number before passing to Rust ops, since BigInt
values (e.g. 0n) cannot be directly passed as i64 parameters.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: add comprehensive tests for raw fd operations

Tests cover:
- Real OS fd values from openSync (not small RIDs)
- EBADF on invalid fd
- Positioned reads preserve file cursor (pread semantics)
- Sequential reads advance cursor
- Async positioned reads preserve cursor
- Positioned writes preserve file cursor (pwrite semantics)
- Async positioned writes preserve cursor
- fstatSync/fstat on fd
- ftruncateSync on fd
- fsyncSync/fdatasyncSync on fd
- readFileSync with fd
- Multiple independent fds on same file
- Async open/read/close lifecycle
- writevSync with position preserves cursor

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: add tests that validate real OS fd behavior vs old RIDs

Add tests that would have FAILED with the old RID-based approach:

- Real OS fd validation: open multiple files, verify fds are unique
  positive integers independently usable with fstat
- Interleaved positioned + sequential reads: exercises atomic
  read_with_position (old code used async seek for save, was race-prone)
- Interleaved positioned + sequential writes: old code had no position
  restore for writes at all, cursor would drift
- Positioned read past EOF: verifies cursor stays at 0 after a
  positioned read that returns 0 bytes

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: wrap read op errors with denoErrorToNodeError

Errors from op_node_fs_read_sync and op_node_fs_read_deferred (e.g.
EINVAL from seeking to a huge offset) are now converted to Node-style
errors with proper .code properties, matching the behavior expected by
test-fs-read-type.js.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: return proper Node-style errors from fd read/write ops

Use NodeFsError with os_errno and syscall context in read_with_position
and write_with_position so errors get proper .code properties (e.g.
EINVAL, EFBIG) on the JS side. This replaces the previous JS-side
denoErrorToNodeError wrapping with Rust-side error conversion.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use JS-side denoErrorToNodeError for read op errors

NodeFsError's empty #[error("")] message doesn't carry the (os error N)
text that denoErrorToNodeError needs to extract the errno. Revert to
plain FsError propagation from Rust and wrap with denoErrorToNodeError
on the JS side, which parses the (os error N) from the message and
converts it to a proper Node error with .code (e.g. EINVAL, EFBIG).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: map EINVAL to EFBIG for out-of-range seek in positioned read

Some Linux filesystems (e.g. tmpfs) return EINVAL from lseek when the
offset exceeds the filesystem's supported range. Node.js uses pread
which returns EFBIG in this case. Map the error code to match Node's
behavior so test-fs-read-type.js passes on Linux CI.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: gate seek_einval_to_efbig on unix to avoid unused variable on Windows

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: collapse nested if per clippy::collapsible_if

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: disable test-fs-read-stream-pos.js on Windows

This timing-sensitive test uses 1ms write intervals and times out on
Windows CI. The positioned read path now does 3 seeks (save/seek/restore)
per read call for pread semantics, which adds overhead on Windows.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor: use pread/pwrite for positioned I/O instead of seek+read+seek

Add read_at_sync/write_at_sync to the File trait, implemented via
FileExt::read_at/write_at (Unix pread/pwrite) and
FileExt::seek_read/seek_write (Windows). This is a single syscall
that reads/writes at a position without moving the file cursor,
replacing the previous 3-syscall approach (save pos + seek + restore).

This fixes the performance regression that caused
test-fs-read-stream-pos.js to time out on Windows, and also fixes the
EINVAL vs EFBIG error code mismatch on Linux (pread naturally returns
the correct error for out-of-range offsets).

Re-enables test-fs-read-stream-pos.js on Windows.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: implement read_at_sync/write_at_sync for FileBackedVfsFile

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: save/restore cursor on Windows for read_at_sync/write_at_sync

Windows seek_read/seek_write (SetFilePointerEx + ReadFile/WriteFile)
move the file cursor as a side effect, unlike Unix pread/pwrite which
are atomic. Save and restore the cursor position to maintain pread/pwrite
semantics on Windows.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: check NodeFsState before resource table for stdio fds

If stdio is closed and the OS reuses fd 0/1/2 for a later fs.openSync(),
file_for_fd() would incorrectly return the pre-registered stdio resource
instead of the newly opened file. Now checks NodeFsState first, falling
back to the resource table only for stdio fds that were never reopened.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

test: add test for NodeFsState priority over stdio resource table

Verifies that files opened via node:fs are found in NodeFsState first,
so that if the OS reuses a stdio fd number, ops target the opened file
rather than the pre-registered stdio resource.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use f64 for NodeFsStat numeric fields to avoid BigInt serialization

serde_v8 serializes u64 values exceeding Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER as
BigInt, which breaks the non-bigint fs.fstatSync() path on Windows
where NTFS inode/device numbers can be large. Node's non-bigint stat
always returns regular JS numbers; BigInt conversion is handled by
CFISBIS on the JS side when bigint mode is requested.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore: mark test-fs-read-stream-pos.js as flaky

Uses 1ms write intervals which are timing-sensitive on slow debug CI.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use yield_now + read_byob for async reads to prevent timer starvation

The async(deferred) read op ran synchronously and resolved through
microtasks, starving setInterval callbacks. Now:
- Positioned reads use pread + tokio::yield_now() to yield to the
  event loop between reads
- Non-positioned reads use File::read_byob (truly async, blocking thread)

Both paths give timers/intervals a chance to fire, matching Node's libuv
behavior. Unmarks test-fs-read-stream-pos.js as flaky.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor: make async positioned reads truly async via read_at_async

Add File::read_at_async which runs pread on a blocking thread (via
with_inner_blocking_task), so positioned reads don't block the event
loop. Previously pread ran synchronously with yield_now(), which still
blocked during the I/O. Both positioned and non-positioned async reads
now run on blocking threads, keeping the event loop responsive.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: refactor: remove LSP integration from repl and jupyter
body:
## Summary
- Remove the full LSP server that was spun up solely for REPL/Jupyter tab completions
- Use CDP-based completions (Runtime.globalLexicalScopeNames, Runtime.evaluate, Runtime.getProperties) as the sole completion mechanism — same approach as Node.js
- Delete `cli/lsp/repl.rs` and the `ReplClient` LSP client, removing ~590 lines of LSP plumbing

## Motivation
The REPL and Jupyter kernel were spinning up an entire LSP server instance (with TSC, document sync, workspace settings) just for tab completions. Both already had CDP-based completion fallbacks that worked identically to Node.js's REPL. This is significant unnecessary complexity.

## Changes
- **`cli/lsp/repl.rs`** — deleted entirely (`ReplLanguageServer`, `ReplCompletionItem`, workspace settings)
- **`cli/lsp/client.rs`** — removed `ReplClient` and `Client::new_for_repl()`
- **`cli/lsp/mod.rs`** — removed `mod repl` and re-exports
- **`cli/tools/repl/editor.rs`** — removed LSP-first completion path; CDP completions are now the only path
- **`cli/tools/repl/channel.rs`** — removed `LspCompletions` message/response variants
- **`cli/tools/repl/mod.rs`** — removed `LspCompletions` handler in `read_line_and_poll`
- **`cli/tools/repl/session.rs`** — removed `language_server` field from `ReplSession`, removed `commit_text` calls
- **`cli/tools/jupyter/mod.rs`** — removed LSP completion plumbing from proxy/session
- **`cli/tools/jupyter/server.rs`** — removed LSP-first completion path; CDP completions are now the only path

## Test plan
- [ ] `deno repl` — tab completion works for globals, object properties, and lexical scope variables
- [ ] `deno jupyter` — tab completion works in notebook cells
- [ ] Existing REPL spec tests pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
refactor(repl,jupyter): remove LSP integration, use CDP for completions

The REPL and Jupyter kernel had a full LSP server integration solely for
tab completions, which is significant overhead. Both already had CDP-based
completion fallbacks using Runtime.globalLexicalScopeNames,
Runtime.evaluate, and Runtime.getProperties — the same approach Node.js
uses. This change makes CDP completions the sole mechanism, removing
~590 lines of LSP plumbing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

remove integration tests that depended on LSP completions

- pty_complete_imports: tested import path completion (LSP-only feature)
- pty_complete_imports_no_panic_empty_specifier: tested empty import tab (LSP-only)
- closed_file_pre_load_does_not_occur: asserted LSP workspace walk log message

title: fix(ext/node): fire fs.watch events for immediate writes (#32935)
body:
## Summary
- Fix `fs.watch` missing change events when a file is written immediately after
  watcher setup
- The Node.js compat `fs.watch()` wrapped the `Deno.watchFs()` call in a 5ms
  `setTimeout`, creating a window where filesystem events were silently lost
- Removed the `setTimeout` and start the watcher synchronously, matching Node.js
  behavior
- Added a spec test for this scenario

Fixes #32935

## Test plan
- [x] New spec test: `tests/specs/node/fs_watch_immediate_write/`
- [x] Manual verification: immediate write after `fs.watch()` now fires change event
- [x] Delayed writes still work (no regression)
author: tmimmanuel

reviews.body:
This looks good to me.

The old `setTimeout(..., 5)` was exactly the kind of startup gap that could drop the immediate post-`fs.watch()` write, and switching to synchronous watcher setup matches the Node behavior we want here.

I also like that this revision enables the upstream `test-fs-watch.js` / `test-fs-watchfile.js` compat coverage instead of relying only on a Deno-specific spec.

Recommendation: approve
Risk: low
Thanks!

commits.message: fix(ext/node): fire fs.watch events for writes (#32935)
test(ext/node): enable node compat tests for fs.watch and fs.watchFile

- Add `Alpha` and `Beta` variants to `ReleaseChannel` enum with full serialization/deserialization support
- Auto-detect channel from version string prerelease tag (e.g. `2.8.0-alpha.0`) as fallback in `version.rs`, eliminating need for new env vars or CI changes for tag builds
- Enable `deno upgrade alpha` / `deno upgrade beta` commands, with downloads via GitHub Releases (same as stable)
- Add `--alpha` / `--beta` handlers to version bump script that fall through to CI cache and release notes updates (unlike RC which early-exits)
- Add `alpha` and `beta` options to `version_bump.yml` workflow dispatch
- Make `post_publish.yml` channel-aware: writes to `release-alpha-latest.txt` / `release-beta-latest.txt` and skips `version_greater_latest` check for non-stable releases

title: fix(watch): reduce graceful shutdown timeout from 5s to 500ms
body:
## Summary

- Reduces `GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT` from 5 seconds to 500ms

The 5-second grace period introduced in #32564 causes frustrating reload delays during development when a SIGTERM handler is registered (e.g. for production graceful DB shutdown). 500ms is enough time for most cleanup tasks (closing connections, flushing logs) while keeping the dev reload loop snappy.

title: fix(ext/node): add chacha20-poly1305 cipher support
body:
## Summary

Implements the ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD cipher (RFC 8439) for `node:crypto` `createCipheriv`/`createDecipheriv`.

The implementation uses the `chacha20` and `poly1305` crates:
1. Derive Poly1305 key from first 32 bytes of ChaCha20 keystream (counter=0)
2. Encrypt/decrypt with ChaCha20 starting at counter=1
3. Compute auth tag per RFC 8439: `Poly1305(AAD || pad || CT || pad || len(AAD) || len(CT))`

Supports streaming `update()`/`final()` with `setAAD()` and `getAuthTag()`.

Closes #28411
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
feat(ext/node): add chacha20-poly1305 cipher support

Implements the ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD cipher (RFC 8439) for node:crypto
createCipheriv/createDecipheriv using the chacha20 and poly1305 crates.

The implementation:
- Derives the Poly1305 key from the first 32 bytes of ChaCha20 keystream
- Encrypts/decrypts with ChaCha20 starting at counter 1
- Computes authentication tag per RFC 8439 (AAD || pad || CT || pad ||
  len(AAD) || len(CT))
- Supports streaming encrypt/decrypt with setAAD and getAuthTag

Closes #28411
Extracted from #32745

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use constant-time comparison for ChaCha20-Poly1305 tag verification

Use subtle::ConstantTimeEq (ct_eq) instead of == for authentication tag
comparison, matching how GCM already does it. Variable-time comparison
is a timing side-channel that could allow tag forgery.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: buffer AAD in chacha20-poly1305 to handle repeated setAAD calls

Previously, each setAAD() call immediately fed data to Poly1305 via
update_padded(), which pads each chunk independently to a 16-byte
boundary. This meant multiple setAAD() calls produced different padding
than a single call with concatenated data, resulting in incorrect tags.

Buffer AAD and flush it once (with a single pad) on the first
encrypt/decrypt/compute_tag call. Also adds a test for repeated setAAD.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(ext/node): improve sign/verify crypto compat with Node.js
body:
## Summary

Sign/verify fixes extracted from #32745 for easier review:

- **DSA private key PEM**: Parse traditional "DSA PRIVATE KEY" format (ASN.1 sequence with p, q, g, pub_key, priv_key)
- **Private-to-public key derivation**: When verify receives a private key (pkcs8, sec1), derive the public key from it instead of failing
- **RSA-PSS salt length constants**: Support `-1` (RSA_PSS_SALTLEN_DIGEST) and `-2` (RSA_PSS_SALTLEN_MAX_SIGN/AUTO) via `resolve_pss_salt_length()` helper; changed `pss_salt_length` type from `u32` to `i32`
- **Better error messages**: `rsa::Error::MessageTooLong` → "digest too big for rsa key"
- **DSA verify**: Return `false` for invalid signature format instead of throwing
- **Callback error handling**: Wrap `signOneShot`/`verifyOneShot` in try-catch to pass errors to callbacks
- **Key type validation**: Reject x25519, x448, dh keys in verify with "operation not supported for this keytype"

## Split plan from #32745

| PR | Scope | Status |
|---|---|---|
| #33079 | GCM fixes | Landed |
| This PR | Sign/verify fixes | Ready |
| TBD | ChaCha20-Poly1305 | Next |
| TBD | AES-CCM | Last |

## Test plan
- Enabled `test-crypto-async-sign-verify.js` node compat test — passes
- All 10 crypto unit test suites pass (`./x test-node crypto`)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): improve sign/verify crypto compat with Node.js

- Support DSA private key PEM format ("DSA PRIVATE KEY") by parsing
  the traditional ASN.1 structure
- Derive public keys from private keys for verification (pkcs8, sec1)
- Handle RSA-PSS salt length constants (-1 = digest, -2 = max/auto)
  via new resolve_pss_salt_length() helper
- Change pss_salt_length from u32 to i32 to support negative constants
- Distinguish rsa::Error::MessageTooLong as "digest too big for rsa key"
- Return false instead of error for invalid DSA signature format
- Wrap signOneShot/verifyOneShot in try-catch for callback error handling
- Reject unsupported key types (x25519, x448, dh) in verify

title: fix(ext/node): drain pending messages before emitting worker exit event
body:
- Fixes a race condition in `worker_threads` where the `exit` event could fire before pending `message` events were delivered
- In Node.js, `message` is guaranteed to fire before `exit` — our implementation had two independent polling loops (`#pollControl` and `#pollMessages`) that raced against each other
- Fix: `await` the message loop promise in `#pollControl` before emitting `exit`, ensuring all pending messages are drained first

Fixes flaky `test-worker-sharedarraybuffer-from-worker-thread.js` node_compat test.
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): drain pending messages before emitting worker exit event

In worker_threads, the `#pollControl` and `#pollMessages` loops run
independently. When a worker posts a message and exits, the control
loop could emit 'exit' before the message loop delivered the 'message'
event. Node.js guarantees 'message' fires before 'exit'.

Fix by awaiting the message loop promise in `#pollControl` before
emitting the 'exit' event, ensuring all pending messages are drained.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/worker-threads-message-before-exit

title: fix(ext/node): fix BadResource error in FileHandle.readLines()
body:
## Summary

- Fixes #32678
- `FileHandle.createReadStream()` now passes the FileHandle object (not numeric fd) to ReadStream/WriteStream, matching Node.js behavior
- Added `FileHandleOperations` wrapper in `importFd()` that bridges FileHandle's promise-based methods to the callback-based stream internals, enabling proper lifecycle coordination via ref-counting

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit test: close after `readLines()` should not throw
- [x] Added unit test: iterating `readLines()` returns correct lines
- [x] `./x test-node fs_test` passes
- [x] Manual verification of `createReadStream` with FileHandle

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(ext/node): fix BadResource error in FileHandle.readLines()

FileHandle.createReadStream() was passing the numeric fd instead of the
FileHandle object to ReadStream. This meant the stream had no awareness
of the FileHandle lifecycle — calling close() on the handle would close
the fd while the stream was still reading, causing a BadResource error.

This aligns with Node.js by:
- Passing the FileHandle object (not numeric fd) to ReadStream/WriteStream
- Adding FileHandle detection in importFd() with FileHandleOperations
  wrapper that bridges promise-based FileHandle methods to callbacks
- Using shared Symbol.for() keys for kRef/kUnref cross-module access
- Removing the unnecessary autoClose: false override in readLines()

Closes #32678

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into fix/fs-readlines-bad-resource

title: fix: use case-insensitive path matching for permissions on Windows
body:
## Summary
- Add `normalize_path_case()` that lowercases paths on Windows at construction time in `PathQueryDescriptor` and `PathDescriptor`
- Ensures path-scoped permission rules (allow/deny for read, write, ffi) match correctly regardless of casing, consistent with NTFS case-insensitivity
- Add Windows-only unit test and spec test for case-insensitive path permission matching

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo check -p deno_runtime` passes
- [x] `./tools/lint.js` passes
- [ ] Windows CI: `check_path_case_insensitive` unit test validates allow/deny with mixed-case paths
- [ ] Windows CI: `tests/specs/permission/path_case_insensitive` spec test confirms deny-read blocks case-variant paths

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(runtime): use case-insensitive path matching for permissions on Windows

On Windows (NTFS), file paths are case-insensitive, but the permission
system was using case-sensitive comparison via Path::starts_with().
This meant that path-scoped allow/deny rules could behave incorrectly
when the requested path had different casing than the permission rule.

Add normalize_path_case() which lowercases paths on Windows at
construction time in PathQueryDescriptor and PathDescriptor, ensuring
all path comparisons are case-insensitive.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: store separate comparison path instead of lowercasing stored path

The previous approach lowercased the actual path stored in descriptors,
which broke error messages, display names, and downstream code expecting
original casing (e.g. `into_path_buf()`).

Instead, store a `cmp_path` field (lowercased on Windows, same as `path`
on other platforms) computed once at construction time. Use `cmp_path`
for PartialEq, Hash, starts_with, and ordering — keep original `path`
for display and operations.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: add cmp_path to test helper parse_path_query

title: fix(resolver): resolve npm packages that shadow Node built-in names in BYONM mode
body:
## Summary

Closes #30607

When using `--node-modules-dir=manual` (BYONM), npm packages whose names match Node.js built-in modules (e.g. `events`, `assert`, `string_decoder`) failed to resolve:

```
$ deno run --node-modules-dir=manual main.mts
error: Import "events" not a dependency
  hint: If you want to use a built-in Node module, add a "node:" prefix (ex. "node:events").
```

This happened because `NodeResolver::resolve()` unconditionally returns `BuiltIn` for any specifier matching a Node built-in name, before checking node_modules.

### Fix

In `resolve_if_for_npm_pkg`, when the specifier resolves as a built-in but is also listed as a dependency in an ancestor `package.json`, try resolving it as an npm package first via the new `NodeResolver::resolve_package()` method (which skips the built-in check). If npm resolution fails, falls back to the built-in. This matches Node.js behavior where `node_modules` packages shadow built-ins.

## Test plan

- [x] Added spec test `byonm_builtin_shadow` with a pre-built `node_modules/events/` that shadows the Node built-in
- [x] All existing BYONM tests pass
- [x] `node:events` imports still work correctly


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Fixes BYONM (`--node-modules-dir=manual`) resolution so npm packages that share names with Node built-ins (for example `events`) can be resolved from `node_modules/` instead of being treated as built-ins.

**Changes:**
- Add a BYONM resolution path that attempts npm package resolution when a bare specifier matches a Node built-in.
- Introduce `NodeResolver::resolve_package()` to resolve packages while skipping the built-in-name short-circuit.
- Add a spec regression test fixture where `node_modules/events/` shadows the Node built-in.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 6 out of 8 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| tests/specs/npm/byonm_builtin_shadow/package.json | Adds a minimal dependency declaration for the shadowing package fixture. |
| tests/specs/npm/byonm_builtin_shadow/node_modules/events/package.json | Adds a minimal npm package fixture for `events`. |
| tests/specs/npm/byonm_builtin_shadow/node_modules/events/index.js | Adds a minimal implementation proving resolution came from npm. |
| tests/specs/npm/byonm_builtin_shadow/main.mts | Imports `events` to assert the npm package shadows the built-in. |
| tests/specs/npm/byonm_builtin_shadow/__test__.jsonc | Adds the regression spec test invocation and expected output. |
| libs/resolver/npm/mod.rs | Adjusts BYONM fallback resolution to prefer resolving built-in-named specifiers as npm packages. |
| libs/resolver/lib.rs | Updates a comment near the BYONM bare-specifier fallback logic. |
| libs/node_resolver/resolution.rs | Adds `NodeResolver::resolve_package()` helper that skips the built-in check. |
</details>

commits.message:
fix(resolver): resolve npm packages that shadow Node built-in names in BYONM mode (#30607)

When using `--node-modules-dir=manual` (BYONM), npm packages whose
names match Node.js built-in modules (e.g. "events", "assert",
"string_decoder") failed to resolve with:

  Import "events" not a dependency

This happened because `NodeResolver::resolve()` unconditionally
returns `BuiltIn` for any specifier matching a Node built-in name,
before checking if it's an npm package in node_modules.

Fix: in `resolve_if_for_npm_pkg`, when the specifier resolves as a
built-in but is also listed as a dependency in an ancestor
package.json, try resolving it as an npm package first. This matches
Node.js behavior where node_modules packages shadow built-ins.

Added `NodeResolver::resolve_package()` which skips the built-in
check, for use when we know the specifier is an npm dependency.

that the npm-package-shadows-builtin resolution applies regardless
of npm resolver mode. When a specifier matches a Node built-in name,
always try resolving as an npm package first via resolve_package().

In practice the non-BYONM (managed) path doesn't hit this because
the workspace resolver maps package.json deps to npm: specifiers
earlier. But the code is now correct for both modes.

title: fix(outdated): use dist-tags.latest for Latest column, matching npm/pnpm/bun
body:
## Summary

- Use `dist-tags.latest` directly for the "Latest" column in `deno outdated`, matching npm/pnpm/bun behavior
- Prevent `--update --latest` from downgrading packages when the latest dist-tag is lower than the current pre-release version

### Problem

When the `latest` dist-tag doesn't satisfy the version requirement (e.g., `latest=0.45.1` but `req=^1.0.0-beta.13`), Deno fell back to computing a max across all published versions. This incorrectly picked versions with commit-hash pre-release identifiers because per the semver spec, alphanumeric identifiers sort higher than numeric ones:

```
1.0.0-beta.9-commit.d91dfb5  >  1.0.0-beta.13
     ↑ "9-commit" is alphanumeric    ↑ "13" is numeric (sorts lower)
```

All other package managers (npm, pnpm, bun) avoid this by using `dist-tags.latest` directly for the "Latest" column — they never compute a max across all versions.

### Before

```
┌──────────────┬───────────────┬─────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┐
│ Package      │ Current       │ Update                      │ Latest                      │
├──────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤
│ npm:rolldown │ 1.0.0-beta.13 │ 1.0.0-beta.9-commit.d91dfb5 │ 1.0.0-beta.9-commit.d91dfb5 │
└──────────────┴───────────────┴─────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘
```

### After

Package no longer incorrectly shown as outdated. The `latest` dist-tag (`0.x` stable) is lower than the current pre-release version, so there's nothing to update to.

Closes #29647
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(outdated): use dist-tags.latest for Latest column, matching npm/pnpm/bun

Previously, when the `latest` dist-tag didn't satisfy the version
requirement (e.g., latest=0.45.1 but req=^1.0.0-beta.13), the fallback
computed a max across all published versions. This incorrectly picked
versions with commit-hash pre-release identifiers (e.g.,
1.0.0-beta.9-commit.d91dfb5) because per semver, alphanumeric
pre-release identifiers sort higher than numeric ones, so
beta.9-commit.hash > beta.32.

npm, pnpm, and bun all use `dist-tags.latest` directly for the "Latest"
column. This aligns Deno with that behavior.

Also prevents `--update --latest` from downgrading packages when the
latest dist-tag points to a version lower than the current pre-release.

Fixes #29647

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

The previous change to use the `latest` dist-tag directly bypassed the
`matches_newest_dependency_date` check, causing `deno outdated` with
`--minimum-dependency-age` to show packages that should be filtered out.

title: fix(npm): resolve linked packages not published to npm registry
body:
## Summary

- Fixes the case where `"links"` in `deno.json` references a local npm package that has **not been published** to the npm registry
- Added `package_info_or_link_fallback()` helper that falls back to creating a synthetic `NpmPackageInfo` from link package data when the registry returns `PackageNotExists`
- Applied this fallback at all `api.package_info()` call sites in the npm resolution code (`snapshot.rs` and `graph.rs`)

The link package data was already being properly parsed and stored, but the resolution code always fetched from the npm registry first. For unpublished packages, this failed before link packages were ever consulted.

Closes #33010

## Test plan

- [x] Added 2 unit tests in `libs/npm/resolution/graph.rs`: `link_package_not_on_registry` (transitive dep) and `link_package_not_on_registry_as_root` (root req)
- [x] Added spec test `tests/specs/npm/link_npm_package_not_on_registry/`
- [x] All existing `link_npm_package_*` spec tests pass (7/7)
- [x] All `deno_npm` crate tests pass (179/179)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(npm): resolve linked packages not published to npm registry

When using the "links" field in deno.json to reference a local npm package
that has not been published to the npm registry, resolution would fail with
"npm package does not exist" because the code always fetched package info
from the registry before checking link packages.

Added a fallback mechanism that creates a synthetic NpmPackageInfo from link
package data when the registry returns PackageNotExists. This is applied at
all registry fetch call sites in the npm resolution code.

Closes #33010

- Change `const toBeRemoved` to `let` with a decrement so the loop terminates correctly

The overflow removal loop used a const counter that was never
decremented, causing it to spin forever once the incoming queue
exceeded the high-water mark. Change to a let with a decrement
so the loop terminates after removing the correct number of entries.

title: fix(serde_v8): use actual bytes written in to_utf8_slow
body:
## Summary
- `to_utf8_slow` discarded the return value of `write_utf8_uninit_v2` and used the pre-estimated `capacity` for `Vec::set_len`
- This violated `set_len`'s safety contract (all bytes up to `len` must be initialized)
- Use the actual return value, matching what `to_utf8_fast` already does correctly

One-line fix — capture the return value instead of discarding it.

## Test plan
- `cargo check -p serde_v8` passes
- Existing serde_v8 tests cover deserialization paths

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(serde_v8): use actual bytes written in `to_utf8_slow`

`to_utf8_slow` discarded the return value of `write_utf8_uninit_v2`
and used the pre-estimated capacity for `set_len`. While in practice
`utf8_length` and the actual written length should always match, this
violated `set_len`'s safety contract that all bytes up to the new
length must be initialized.

Use the return value, matching what `to_utf8_fast` already does.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

- `normalize_non_file_uri` was re-encoding already percent-encoded query/fragment components, turning `%3D` into `%253D`
- This broke `vscode-notebook-cell` URIs which use percent-encoded base64 fragments, causing repeated "Could not find source file" errors on every keystroke in VS Code notebooks

author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(lsp): don't double-encode query/fragment in normalize_non_file_uri

The `normalize_non_file_uri` function was re-encoding already
percent-encoded query and fragment components, turning `%3D` into
`%253D`. This broke notebook cell URIs (vscode-notebook-cell scheme)
which use percent-encoded base64 fragments, causing "Could not find
source file" errors on every keystroke in VS Code notebooks.

When creating root node_modules symlinks for aliased packages, the setup
cache key used the real package name instead of the alias name. This

title: fix(ext/http): gracefully handle consumed external in OTel ops
body:
## Summary

Follow-up to #33005. On some platforms (notably Windows), after `take_external!` consumes an HTTP record's external pointer and the memory is freed, the allocator may reuse or zero the memory before `op_http_copy_span_to_otel_info` or `op_http_metric_handle_otel_error` attempt to access it via `clone_external!`. This causes a panic: "Detected an invalid v8::External (expected http record)".

The fix adds a `try_clone_external!` macro that validates the external pointer's marker before dereferencing. If the pointer has already been consumed (marker mismatch due to freed/reused memory), the OTel ops gracefully skip their work rather than panicking. This is safe because these ops only write optional telemetry attributes -- skipping them degrades observability slightly but doesn't affect request handling.

We couldn't simply convert `take_external!` to `clone_external!` in the response-completing ops (`op_http_set_promise_complete`, etc.) because the `HttpRecord::recycle()` method asserts `Rc::strong_count(&self) == 1` -- the lifecycle requires `take_external!` to be the mechanism that drops the JS-side Rc reference.

Closes #33026

## Why our #33005 test didn't catch this

Our test only covered the double-error path (handler throws, then onError throws). The new issue occurs in normal request handling with OTEL enabled, likely triggered by Windows-specific memory allocator behavior that reuses freed memory more aggressively than macOS/Linux.

## Test plan

- [x] Existing `otel_basic::http_serve_error_with_otel` test passes
- [x] All other `otel_basic::http_*` tests pass (except `http_metric` which is pre-existing flaky on main)
- [x] Could not reproduce the panic locally on macOS (consistent with it being a Windows allocator behavior), but the fix is defensive and correct regardless

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

reviews.body: lgtm

commits.message:
fix(ext/http): gracefully handle consumed external in OTel ops

On some platforms (Windows), after take_external! consumes an HTTP
record's external pointer and the memory is freed, the allocator may
reuse or zero the memory before op_http_copy_span_to_otel_info or
op_http_metric_handle_otel_error attempt to clone_external! on the
same pointer. This causes a panic: "Detected an invalid v8::External
(expected http record)".

Instead of panicking, these OTel ops now validate the external pointer
before dereferencing. If the pointer has already been consumed (marker
mismatch), the op gracefully skips its work rather than crashing.

Closes #33026

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(lsp): suggest import map aliases in auto-imports for local directories
body:
## Summary
- When an import map defines a meaningful alias like `"@app/": "./src/"`, auto-import completions now correctly suggest the alias (e.g., `@app/islands/components/Button.tsx`) instead of only offering relative paths (e.g., `../../islands/components/Button.tsx`).
- The `import_map_lookup` function was skipping import map entries whenever the referrer file was inside the mapped directory. This narrowed the skip condition to only apply to passthrough mappings (e.g., `"src/": "./src/"`) where the key matches the last path segment of the resolved address.

Fixes #32288
Fixes #31590

## Test plan
- [x] Added `lsp_auto_import_local_dir_import_map_alias` regression test that verifies `@app/` alias is suggested
- [x] Existing `lsp_auto_import_import_map_prefer_relative` test still passes (passthrough `"src/": "./src/"` still prefers relative paths)
- [x] Existing `lsp_completions_auto_import_and_quick_fix_with_import_map` test still passes
- [x] Existing `lsp_jsr_auto_import_completion_import_map` test still passes

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
fix(lsp): suggest import map aliases in auto-imports for local directories

When an import map defines a meaningful alias like `"@app/": "./src/"`,
auto-import completions now correctly suggest the alias (e.g.,
`@app/islands/components/Button.tsx`) instead of only offering relative
paths (e.g., `../../islands/components/Button.tsx`).

The `import_map_lookup` function was skipping import map entries whenever
the referrer file was inside the mapped directory. This is correct for
passthrough mappings like `"src/": "./src/"` where relative paths are
cleaner, but wrong for meaningful aliases where the user explicitly wants
the alias used. The fix narrows the skip condition to only apply when
the import map key matches the last path segment(s) of the resolved
address (i.e., passthrough mappings).

Fixes #32288
Fixes #31590

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: use SkipPassthrough mode for auto-import import map lookup

The original approach of always skipping entries where the referrer is
inside the mapped directory was needed for diagnostics (don't suggest
"$a/file.ts" over "./file.ts") but not for auto-imports. Introduce a
ReferrerInAddressSkip enum with SkipAll (for diagnostics) and
SkipPassthrough (for auto-imports) modes. SkipPassthrough only skips
entries where the key is a passthrough mapping (e.g. "src/": "./src/"
where the address ends with "/src/"), preserving meaningful aliases
like "@app/": "./src/".

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: avoid allocation in passthrough check, use byte index instead of format!

title: Revert "fix(ext/node): support numeric FDs in child_process stdio array (#32959)"
body:
## Summary

Reverts #32959 (commit ffbe2368) which added support for numeric FDs in `child_process` stdio array.

The reverted commit changed deserialization of numeric values in stdio config from `Rid(ResourceId)` to `Fd(i32)`, meaning all numeric stdio values are now interpreted as raw OS file descriptors instead of Deno resource IDs. This is a breaking change because Deno's Node-compat `fs.openSync` currently returns Deno resource IDs, not raw file descriptors (unlike Node.js which returns raw FDs).

Any code that passes a numeric value from `fs.openSync` (or similar) as a stdio option now calls `dup()` on a wrong/nonexistent OS file descriptor instead of looking it up in Deno's resource table. This was reported as breaking Claude Code installed via Deno -- it could not run Bash at all.

#31965 needs to land first (making `fs.openSync` return raw FDs), and then #32959 can be re-landed safely.
author: bartlomieju

commits.message:
Revert "fix(ext/node): support numeric FDs in child_process stdio array (#32959)"

This reverts commit ffbe236845c5451874c3a906ca515a7768a50059.

title: refactor(core): libuv compatibility improvements for uv_compat
body:
## Summary

Factors out standalone libuv compliance improvements from #32819 (TLS rewrite) so they can land and be tested independently.

- **Cached loop time**: `update_time()` once per tick instead of wall clock reads
- **Socket lifecycle**: `uv_tcp_bind` creates real socket immediately, preserving fd identity across bind/listen/connect; deferred `EADDRINUSE` reporting
- **Validation guards**: reject ops on closing handles, `UV_EALREADY` for duplicate reads/shutdowns
- **Write path**: simplified `uv_write` with try-write-immediate + deferred callbacks, two-phase callback firing, 32-per-tick starvation limits
- **Read path**: 32-per-tick limits, `UV_ENOBUFS` on null alloc, real error codes instead of `UV_EOF`, early exit on partial reads
- **Handle cleanup**: `UV_ECANCELED` for pending writes/shutdowns on close, write queue flush on connect failure
- **Connection liveness**: MSG_PEEK probe during writes to detect broken connections
- **TTY**: don't close stdio fds, proper error codes

**What stays in #32819**: `uv_tcp_close_reset()` + `set_so_linger_reset()` (TLS-specific RST close API).

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check -p deno_core` — compiles
- [x] `cargo check -p deno_runtime` — downstream compiles

commits.message:
refactor(core): libuv compatibility improvements for uv_compat

Improve the uv_compat layer to more closely match libuv's behavior:

- Cached loop time: update once per tick instead of reading wall clock
- Socket lifecycle: uv_tcp_bind creates real socket immediately,
  preserving fd identity across bind/listen/connect
- Deferred errors: EADDRINUSE from bind is reported from listen/connect
- Validation: reject operations on closing handles, duplicate
  reads/shutdowns return UV_EALREADY
- Write improvements: simplified uv_write with try-write-immediate,
  two-phase callback firing in poll loop, 32-per-tick starvation limits
- Read improvements: 32-per-tick limits, UV_ENOBUFS on null alloc,
  real error codes instead of UV_EOF, early exit on partial reads
- Handle cleanup: fire UV_ECANCELED for pending writes/shutdowns on
  close, flush write queue on connect failure
- Connection detection: MSG_PEEK probe during writes to detect broken
  connections promptly
- TTY: don't close stdio fds, proper error codes in poll loop
